2. Verify filling all fields and clicking "send to Verida" sends the request and shows a confirmation.
3. Verify Step 3 is marked as complete after successful submission.
4. Verify a "Transport Request Sent to Verida" entry appears in the Activity Timeline.

Negative:
7. Verify that submitting without required fields (e.g., patient address) shows validation errors.
8. Verify that if the Verida integration is down, an error message is shown with a retry option. ( optional )

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the transport request form has a clean layout with clearly labeled fields.
11. Verify the 5-step progress bar shows Step 3  is the current step.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify submitting a transport request with a very long address is accepted.

Security:
14. Verify that patient data in the transport request is transmitted securely to Verida.
15. Verify that only authorized providers can submit transport requests.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the transport request form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify submitting the request to Verida completes within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to add the assigned case manager's details to the Verida transport request
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to include the assigned case manager details in the Verida transport request within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When filling out the transportation request form (Step 4 of the ADH Documentation Stepper), the provider can add the case manager name, phone number, and email to the request. This information enables Verida to coordinate directly with the case manager regarding transportation logistics, schedule changes, or issues that arise. The case manager details auto-populate from the previously selected case manager in the documentation workflow, but can be manually adjusted if needed. Including the case manager as a contact on the transport request ensures continuity of care coordination and reduces the burden on the provider to relay transportation updates between Verida and the case manager.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider opens the transport request form, Then the system must auto-populate the case manager fields with details from the previously assigned case manager.

2. If a case manager was previously assigned, Then the name, phone, and email must be pre-filled.
   Else if no case manager was assigned, Then the fields must be empty for manual entry.

3. If the provider edits the auto-populated case manager details, Then the updated values must be used in the Verida request.

4. When the transport request is submitted, Then the case manager details must be included in the request payload.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the transport request form shows case manager fields (name, phone, email) auto-populated from the assigned case manager.
2. Verify the auto-populated details match the case manager selected in the earlier step.
4. Verify the submitted Verida request includes the case manager details.
5. Verify that with no previously assigned case manager, the fields are empty and editable.

Negative:
6. Verify that submitting with a case manager email in invalid format shows a validation warning.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the case manager section is clearly labeled within the transport request form.
8. Verify auto-populated fields are editable with clear input styling.
9. Verify the case manager fields are grouped together for easy identification.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify behavior when the case manager is changed after the transport request form is opened — the form should allow manual update.
11. Verify that clearing all case manager fields and submitting includes the request without case manager info.

Security:
12. Verify that case manager contact details are transmitted securely to Verida.
13. Verify that only authorized providers can view and modify case manager details on the transport form.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify case manager details auto-populate within 1 second of loading the form.
2. Verify the transport request form with case manager details loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to add an escalation flag or escalation / Urgent details to the Verida transport request
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to flag a Verida transport request as urgent or escalated and add escalation details within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. On the transport request form, the provider can toggle an escalation flag and enter urgent details explaining why the request needs priority handling (e.g., "Patient starts ADH program Monday — transport must be confirmed by Friday"). The escalation flag is transmitted to Verida along with the request, ensuring the transportation provider prioritizes the request appropriately. This is critical for time-sensitive situations where a patient start date is approaching and transportation has not yet been arranged, or when a schedule change requires immediate attention. The escalation status is visible in the documentation stepper and Activity Timeline for team awareness.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider opens the transport request form, Then the system must display an escalation flag toggle and details field.

2. If the provider enables the escalation flag and enters details, Then the escalation information must be included in the Verida request.
   Else if the flag is not enabled, Then the request must be sent as standard priority.

3. If the provider enables the escalation flag but does not enter details, Then the system must display "Please provide escalation details" and prevent submission.

4. When the request is submitted with escalation, Then the documentation stepper must show an escalation indicator on Step 3.

5. If the escalation flag is toggled on and then off before submission, Then the request must be sent as standard priority.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the transport request form displays an escalation flag toggle and details text field.
2. Verify toggling the escalation flag to "On" enables the details text field.
3. Verify entering "Patient starts ADH program Monday — transport must be confirmed by Friday" as escalation details saves correctly.
4. Verify the submitted Verida request includes the escalation flag and details.
5. Verify Step 3 shows an escalation indicator (e.g., red flag icon) after submission with escalation.
6. Verify submitting without escalation sends a standard priority request.

Negative:
7. Verify that enabling the escalation flag without entering details shows "Please provide escalation details".

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the escalation flag is a clearly labeled toggle switch.
9. Verify the details text field appears or becomes enabled only when the flag is toggled on.
10. Verify the escalation indicator on Step 3 is visually prominent (e.g., red icon).

Edge Cases:
11. Verify toggling the flag on and off multiple times before submission works without issues.
12. Verify that very long escalation details (1000+ characters) are accepted.

Security:
13. Verify that escalation details are transmitted securely to Verida.
14. Verify that only authorized providers can set escalation flags.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the escalation toggle responds within 0.5 seconds.
2. Verify submitting an escalated request completes within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to fill out state-provided details in the system, including CPT/ICD-10 codes, service frequencies, and validation of NPI numbers.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to enter state-provided clinical and billing details into the system as part of the Medicaid documentation workflow within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The provider fills out a form with CPT codes (Current Procedural Terminology codes for services rendered), ICD-10 codes (International Classification of Diseases codes for diagnoses), service frequencies (how often services are provided — e.g., daily, 3x/week), and NPI numbers (National Provider Identifier for verification). The system validates NPI numbers against the NPPES (National Plan and Provider Enumeration System) registry to ensure accuracy. These details are required for the Prior Authorization (PA) package submission to the state and for billing setup. Accurate coding is essential for Medicaid reimbursement — incorrect codes can result in claim denials and delayed payments.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider opens the state details form, Then the system must display fields for CPT codes, ICD-10 codes, service frequencies, and NPI number.

2. If the provider enters a valid CPT code, Then the system must show available CPT codes that matches the entered CPT code with its description.
   Else if the code is invalid, Then the system must display "Invalid CPT code".

3. If the provider enters a valid ICD-10 code, Then the system must show available ICD-10 codes that matches the entered ICD-10 code with its description.
   Else if the code is invalid, Then the system must display "Invalid ICD-10 code".

4. If the provider enters a valid NPI number, Then the system must show a green checkmark.
   Else if the NPI is invalid, Then the system must display "Invalid NPI number — please verify".

5. If any required field is missing, Then the system must prevent form submission with specific error messages.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the state details form displays fields for CPT codes, ICD-10 codes, service frequencies, and NPI number.
2. Verify entering CPT code "97110" validates successfully and shows "Therapeutic Exercises".
3. Verify entering ICD-10 code "G30.9" validates and shows "Alzheimer disease, unspecified".
4. Verify entering service frequency "3 times per week" saves correctly.
5. Verify entering a valid NPI number (e.g., "1234567893") shows a green validation checkmark.
6. Verify saving the complete form stores all data and allows step completion.

Negative:
7. Verify entering an invalid CPT code (e.g., "XXXXX") shows "Invalid CPT code".
8. Verify entering an invalid ICD-10 code shows "Invalid ICD-10 code".
9. Verify entering an invalid NPI number shows "Invalid NPI number — please verify".
10. Verify that saving without required fields shows specific validation errors.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify CPT and ICD-10 fields have search/autocomplete functionality.
12. Verify the NPI validation indicator (green checkmark or red X) is clearly visible.
13. Verify service frequency options are presented as dropdown or structured input.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify entering multiple CPT codes for the same patient works correctly.

Security:
16. Verify that NPI and medical coding data is transmitted securely.
17. Verify that only authorized providers can enter state-provided details.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify CPT/ICD-10 code validation completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify NPI number validation against NPPES completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to view the complete history of frequency changes
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view the complete history of service frequency changes for a lead/patient within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Service frequencies (how often services are provided, e.g., daily, 3x/week, 5x/week) may change over time based on patient needs, state authorization updates, or care plan revisions. The frequency change history shows a chronological log of all changes including the previous frequency, the new frequency, the date of change, the reason for the change, and who made the change. This history is important for compliance documentation, as Medicaid auditors may require proof that frequency changes were authorized and properly documented. The history is accessible from the lead/patient detail page and is read-only to prevent tampering.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider navigates to the frequency change history, Then the system must display all changes in reverse chronological order.

2. If frequency changes exist, Then each entry must show previous value, new value, date, reason, and author.
   Else if no changes exist, Then only the initial frequency setting must be displayed.

3. If a new frequency change is made, Then it must appear at the top of the history immediately.

4. The frequency history must be read-only — the provider must not be able to edit or delete history entries.

5. If the history has many entries (20+), Then pagination must be available.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the frequency change history section displays a chronological log.
2. Verify each entry shows previous frequency (e.g., "3x/week"), new frequency (e.g., "5x/week"), date, reason (e.g., "Increased per state authorization"), and author.
3. Verify a new frequency change appears at the top immediately after it is made.
4. Verify that a patient with no frequency changes shows only the initial setting.

Negative:
5. Verify that the frequency history entries cannot be edited or deleted.
6. Verify that unauthorized users cannot access the frequency history.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the history is displayed in a clean table format with clear column headers.
8. Verify entries are color-coded or labeled to distinguish increases from decreases.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify history with 50+ entries paginates correctly.
10. Verify that frequency changes made on the same day are ordered by time.

Security:
11. Verify the frequency history is immutable and cannot be tampered with.
12. Verify that history entries are included in compliance audit exports.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the frequency change history loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify pagination in the history works within 1 second per page.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to track PA submission status (Submitted/Under Review/Approved/Denied).
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track the Prior Authorization (PA) submission status for a Medicaid lead/patient within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The PA status progresses through stages: Submitted, Under Review, Approved, and Denied. The current PA status is displayed on the lead/patient detail page and in the documentation stepper, providing the provider with real-time visibility into where the authorization stands. When the state or insurance entity updates the PA status, the system reflects the change with a timestamp and notification. PA approval is a critical milestone — without it, Medicaid services cannot begin and billing cannot proceed. Tracking PA status helps the provider anticipate timelines, follow up on pending authorizations, and take corrective action if a PA is denied.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider views the PA status section, Then the system must display the current status with a timestamp.

2. If the PA status changes to "Approved", Then the system must mark the PA step as complete and allow progression.
   Else if the status changes to "Denied", Then the system must display the denial reason and offer a resubmission option.

3. If the PA status is "Under Review", Then the provider should see an estimated timeline if available.

4. When the PA status changes, Then the system must log it in the Activity Timeline and send a notification to the provider.

5. If the PA has not been submitted yet, Then the status must show "Not Submitted" with an option to submit.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the PA status section displays "Submitted" after the PA is submitted.
2. Verify the status updates to "Under Review" when the state begins review, with a timestamp.
3. Verify the status changes to "Approved" when approved, marking the PA step complete.
4. Verify the status changes to "Denied" with a denial reason when denied.
5. Verify PA status changes appear in the Activity Timeline.
6. Verify a notification is sent to the provider when PA status changes.

Negative:
7. Verify that a "Not Submitted" status prevents progression through the documentation stepper.
8. Verify that a "Denied" PA offers a resubmission option.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify PA status is displayed as a progress tracker (Submitted → Under Review → Approved/Denied).
10. Verify each status stage is color-coded (blue for Submitted, yellow for Under Review, green for Approved, red for Denied).
11. Verify the denial reason is displayed in a readable format.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify behavior when PA status changes rapidly (e.g., Submitted → Under Review → Denied in one day).
13. Verify that the PA status history shows all transitions, not just the current status.

Security:
14. Verify that PA status data is accessible only to authorized providers.
15. Verify that PA status changes are logged in an immutable audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify PA status updates reflect in the UI within 3 seconds of the change.
2. Verify the PA status section loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to resubmit updated document if got rejected from State
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to resubmit updated documents that were previously rejected by the state as part of the Medicaid authorization process within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When a document is rejected (e.g., PA package denied or specific documents flagged for issues), the system displays the rejection reason and allows the provider to upload a corrected version of the document. The resubmission form pre-populates with the original document details and highlights the fields or sections that need correction. After uploading the updated document, the provider submits it for re-review, and the status tracking updates to reflect the resubmission. This workflow is critical because document rejections are common in Medicaid processing, and timely resubmission prevents delays in patient onboarding and service start dates.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When a document is rejected by the state, Then the system must display the rejection with the reason and a "Resubmit" option.

2. If the provider clicks "Resubmit" and uploads a corrected document, Then the system must submit the updated document and change status to "Resubmitted".
   Else if the provider does not upload a file, Then the system must display "Please upload the corrected document" and prevent submission.

3. When a document is resubmitted, Then the system must log it in the Activity Timeline.

5. If the resubmission fails (network error), Then the system must display an error and retain the uploaded file for retry.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify clicking "Resubmit" opens a form for uploading an updated document.
2. Verify uploading a corrected document and clicking Submit changes status to "Resubmitted".
4. Verify a "Document Resubmitted" entry appears in the Activity Timeline.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the resubmission form has a file upload area with drag-and-drop support.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify resubmitting a document multiple times (rejected → resubmit → rejected → resubmit) logs each attempt.
12. Verify uploading a large document (50MB) is handled within file size limits.

Security:
13. Verify that uploaded documents are scanned for malware before processing.
14. Verify that resubmitted documents are transmitted over secure channels.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the resubmission form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify document upload and submission complete within 10 seconds for a 10MB file.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to add Billing and clearance details for patient
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to enter billing and clearance details for a patient as part of the documentation stepper workflow in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. In the ADH Documentation Stepper Step 5 (Billing Setup & Clearance) and ALF Documentation Stepper Step 4 (Billing Setup & Clearance), the provider fills in billing information including insurance details, billing codes, authorization numbers, clearance dates, and payment arrangements. This step ensures that all financial and insurance clearances are in place before the patient can be admitted and services can begin. The billing details are linked to the patient record and used for subsequent claim submissions to Medicaid or private insurance. Without completed billing setup, the facility cannot bill for services rendered, potentially resulting in unbilled care and revenue loss.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider reaches the Billing Setup & Clearance step, Then the system must display the billing form with all required fields.

2. If the provider fills all required billing fields and saves, Then the step must be marked as complete.
   Else if required fields are missing, Then the system must highlight them and prevent saving.

3. If the authorization number is invalid or expired, Then the system must display a warning.

4. If the provider needs to update billing details later, Then the form must pre-populate with existing data.

5. When billing details are saved, Then they must be linked to the patient record for billing operations.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Billing Setup & Clearance form displays fields for insurance details, billing codes, authorization number, clearance date, and payment arrangements.
2. Verify entering insurance ID "MED-2026-001234", authorization number "AUTH-5678", clearance date "2026-04-01" and saving stores the data.
3. Verify the Billing step is marked as complete after saving.
4. Verify billing information is accessible from the patient profile.
5. Verify reopening the billing form shows pre-populated data.

Negative:
6. Verify saving without required fields (e.g., authorization number) shows validation errors.
7. Verify entering an expired authorization number shows a warning.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the billing form has a clean layout with clearly labeled fields.
9. Verify the stepper progress bar shows the Billing step as current.
10. Verify date fields use date pickers.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify that updating billing details after initial save preserves the change history.
12. Verify behavior when billing codes are not recognized — the system should allow entry with a warning.

Security:
13. Verify that billing and insurance data is encrypted in transit and at rest.
14. Verify that only authorized providers can access billing details.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the billing form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify saving billing details completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to convert a Lead to 'New Arrival' status after all documentation steps are completed and PA package is received.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to convert a lead to "New Arrival" status once all documentation stepper steps are completed and the Prior Authorization (PA) package has been received and approved within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The conversion is a major milestone in the lead lifecycle, signifying that the lead has been fully qualified, all paperwork is complete, and the patient is cleared to begin receiving services at the facility. The system validates that all prerequisite steps are complete before allowing the conversion: all documentation steps must be marked as done, mandatory documents must be received and signed, and the PA must be in "Approved" status. Once converted, the lead status badge on the Lead Profile Header changes to "New Arrival" and the lead moves out of the active sales pipeline into the admissions workflow. This conversion is logged in the Activity Timeline and triggers any configured downstream processes.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider clicks "Convert to New Arrival", Then the system must validate all prerequisites before allowing conversion.

2. If all documentation steps are complete and PA is "Approved", Then the conversion must proceed and the status must change to "New Arrival".
   Else if any documentation step is incomplete, Then the system must display "Cannot convert — the following steps are incomplete: [list of steps]".
3. When the conversion is successful, Then the Activity Timeline must log the event.

4. If the conversion is successful, Then the lead must move from the sales pipeline to the admissions workflow.

5. If the provider attempts to convert a lead that is already "New Arrival", Then the system must display "Lead is already in New Arrival status."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that with all steps complete and PA "Approved", clicking "Convert to New Arrival" changes the status.
2. Verify the Lead Profile Header badge shows "New Arrival" after conversion.
3. Verify the Lead List Status column shows "New Arrival".
4. Verify a "Lead Converted to New Arrival" entry appears in the Activity Timeline.
5. Verify the lead moves from the sales pipeline to the admissions workflow.

Negative:
6. Verify that attempting to convert with incomplete steps shows "Cannot convert — the following steps are incomplete: [Step 3, Step 4]".
7. Verify that attempting to convert with PA status "Denied" shows "Cannot convert — PA status is Denied".
8. Verify that attempting to convert with PA status "Under Review" prevents conversion.
9. Verify that re-converting an already "New Arrival" lead shows "Lead is already in New Arrival status.".

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the "Convert to New Arrival" button is displayed only when prerequisites are met (or always displayed but disabled if not met).
11. Verify the error message clearly lists which prerequisites are not met.
12. Verify the "New Arrival" badge has a distinct color.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify that if a step becomes incomplete after the button is clicked (race condition), the system still validates.
14. Verify that the conversion action cannot be triggered multiple times simultaneously.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can convert leads to "New Arrival".
16. Verify the conversion action is logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the prerequisite validation check completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the conversion action and status update complete within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to convert a 'New Arrival' to 'Active' status after confirming arrival and completing service day setup.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to convert a patient from "New Arrival" status to "Active" status within the Nemi Care Facility Portal after the patient has physically arrived at the facility and the first service day setup has been completed. The conversion validates that the patient has confirmed their arrival, all pre-arrival preparations are finalized, and the service day (schedule, transportation, meals, activities) is configured. Once converted to "Active", the patient becomes a fully active resident in the system, and regular billing, scheduling, and charting workflows begin. The Active status badge appears on the patient profile and the patient moves from the admissions workflow to the active resident management area. This is the final status transition in the lead-to-resident lifecycle, completing the full conversion journey.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider clicks "Convert to Active", Then the system must validate that arrival is confirmed and service day setup is complete.

2. If all prerequisites are met, Then the status must change to "Active" and billing/scheduling workflows must activate.
   Else if arrival is not confirmed, Then the system must display "Cannot convert — arrival not confirmed".
   Else if service day setup is incomplete, Then the system must display "Cannot convert — service day setup is incomplete".

3. When conversion is successful, Then the patient must appear in the active residents list.

4. When conversion is successful, Then the Activity Timeline must log the event.

5. If the patient is already "Active", Then the system must display "Patient is already in Active status."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that with arrival confirmed and service day setup complete, clicking "Convert to Active" changes the status to "Active".
2. Verify the patient profile badge shows "Active" after conversion.
3. Verify the patient appears in the active residents list.
4. Verify a "Converted to Active" entry appears in the Activity Timeline.
5. Verify that billing and scheduling workflows activate after conversion.

Negative:
6. Verify that converting without confirmed arrival shows "Cannot convert — arrival not confirmed".
7. Verify that converting without complete service day setup shows "Cannot convert — service day setup is incomplete".
8. Verify that re-converting an already "Active" patient shows "Patient is already in Active status.".

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the "Convert to Active" button is visible on the "New Arrival" patient profile.
10. Verify the "Active" badge is styled with a distinct color (e.g., green).
11. Verify the error messages clearly list which prerequisites are not met.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify that converting a patient who arrived after hours logs the correct timestamp.
13. Verify that the conversion cannot be triggered multiple times simultaneously.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized providers can perform the Active conversion.
15. Verify the conversion is logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the prerequisite validation completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the conversion action completes within 3 seconds.

User Story:
As a provider, I should be able to view an activity timeline for a lead showing all activities with timestamps
- Lead Created
- Visit Done
- Phone Call
- SMS
- Visit Scheduled
- Frequency change etc.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive activity timeline for a lead that shows all activities and interactions with precise timestamps within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The Activity Timeline is a vertical timeline displayed on the lead detail page showing entries for Lead Created, Visit Done, Phone Call, SMS, and Visit Scheduled events, each with the exact date and time of occurrence. Each entry shows the activity type with a corresponding icon, the timestamp, and a "View Summary" expandable link for detailed information. The timeline presents activities in reverse chronological order (newest at top), giving the provider an immediate overview of the most recent interactions. This timeline aggregates all touchpoints from different communication channels (calls, SMS, visits, notes) into a single unified view. It is the primary tool for understanding the complete engagement history with a lead before making follow-up decisions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider views the Activity Timeline, Then the system must display all activities in reverse chronological order with timestamps.

2. If a "Lead Created" event exists, Then it must appear as the earliest entry in the timeline.

3. If the provider clicks "View Summary" on any entry, Then the system must expand the entry to show full details.
   If the provider clicks again, Then the entry must collapse.

4. If the lead has activities of all types (Created, Call, SMS, Visit Scheduled, Visit Done), Then all types must appear in the timeline.

5. If the lead has no activities beyond creation, Then only the "Lead Created" entry must be shown.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Activity Timeline shows "Lead Created" entry with the creation timestamp.
2. Verify a "Phone Call" entry appears with the call timestamp after a call is made.
3. Verify an "SMS" entry appears after an SMS is sent.
4. Verify a "Visit Scheduled" entry appears after a visit is scheduled.
5. Verify a "Visit Done" entry appears after a visit is completed.
6. Verify clicking "View Summary" on "Phone Call" shows call type, duration, notes, and AI summary.
7. Verify clicking "View Summary" on "SMS" shows message content and AI summary.
8. Verify all entries are in reverse chronological order.

Negative:
9. Verify a lead with only the creation event shows only "Lead Created".
10. Verify that timeline entries cannot be deleted by the provider.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the timeline uses a vertical layout with connecting lines between entries.
12. Verify each activity type has a distinct icon (phone for calls, envelope for SMS, calendar for visits, etc.).
13. Verify timestamps are formatted consistently (e.g., "Mar 25, 2026 10:30 AM").
14. Verify "View Summary" links are visually clickable.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify behavior with 200+ activity entries — pagination or lazy loading should handle it.
16. Verify that activities from the same minute are displayed in the correct order.

Security:
17. Verify that the timeline only shows activities the provider is authorized to view.
18. Verify that private notes do not appear in the timeline for unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Activity Timeline loads within 3 seconds for a lead with 100+ activities.
2. Verify expanding "View Summary" loads details within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on "View Summary" on activity to expand activity details
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to click on the "View Summary" link on any activity entry in the Activity Timeline to expand and view detailed information about that specific activity within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Each activity entry in the vertical timeline (Lead Created, Visit Done, Phone Call, SMS, Visit Scheduled) has a "View Summary" expandable link. When clicked, the entry expands inline to reveal the full details of the activity — for calls, this includes call type, duration, pre-call notes, in-call notes, and AI-generated summary; for SMS, the message content and AI summary; for visits, the visit type, date, time, location, and notes. Clicking "View Summary" again (or a collapse control) collapses the expanded view back to the compact timeline entry. This expand/collapse pattern keeps the timeline clean and scannable while providing on-demand access to detailed information without navigating away from the page.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "View Summary" on a collapsed entry, Then the entry must expand to show full details inline.
   If the provider clicks the collapse control on an expanded entry, Then it must collapse back to the compact view.

2. If the entry is a Phone Call, Then the expanded view must show call type, duration, pre-call notes, in-call notes, and AI summary.
   If the entry is an SMS, Then message content and AI summary must be shown.
   If the entry is a Visit, Then visit type, date, time, location, and notes must be shown.
   If the entry is "Lead Created", Then creation date, creator, and initial details must be shown.

3. If multiple entries are expanded simultaneously, Then the system must handle all expanded states correctly.

4. If the expanded details are still loading, Then a loading indicator must be shown within the expanded area.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify clicking "View Summary" on a Phone Call entry expands to show call type "Follow-up", duration "15 min", pre-call notes, in-call notes, and AI summary.
2. Verify clicking "View Summary" on an SMS entry expands to show message text and AI SMS summary.
3. Verify clicking "View Summary" on a Visit entry expands to show visit type "Facility Tour", date, time, location, and notes.
4. Verify clicking "View Summary" on "Lead Created" shows creation date, creator name, and initial details.
5. Verify collapsing an expanded entry returns it to compact view.
6. Verify expanding one entry does not affect the state of other entries.

Negative:
7. Verify that "View Summary" on an entry with no additional details shows a minimal expanded view (e.g., "No additional details").

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the expand/collapse animation is smooth (no flickering or jumping).
9. Verify the expanded area has clear visual boundaries distinguishing it from adjacent entries.
10. Verify a loading spinner appears if details take time to load.
11. Verify the "View Summary" link text changes to "Hide Summary" (or an icon changes) when expanded.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify expanding all entries on the page simultaneously works without display issues.
13. Verify rapid clicking (expand/collapse/expand) does not cause state inconsistencies.
14. Verify that expanded details retain their state during page scroll.

Security:
15. Verify that expanded details only show information the provider is authorized to view.
16. Verify that private note details are not exposed in expanded views for unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify expanding an activity entry loads the details within 1 second.
2. Verify collapsing an entry is instantaneous (under 0.5 seconds).

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to check bed availability for both private and semi-private against state license capacity in real-time.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to check real-time bed availability for both private and semi-private rooms against the state license capacity within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The bed availability check is part of the ALF Documentation Stepper Step (Check Bed Availability) and is also accessible from the general facility management area. The system displays total licensed capacity (as per state license), current occupied beds, available beds, and beds on hold, broken down by room type (private and semi-private). The data updates in real-time as beds are assigned, released, or placed on hold. This is critical for ALF compliance — exceeding state-licensed bed capacity is a regulatory violation that can result in fines and license revocation. The provider uses this information to determine whether they can proceed with a lead admission or need to place the lead on a waitlist.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider reaches ALF Stepper Step 4, Then the system must display real-time bed availability by room type against state license capacity.

2. If private rooms are available, Then the system must show the count and allow selection.
   Else if no private rooms are available, Then the system must show "No private rooms available" and suggest semi-private or waitlist.

3. If semi-private rooms are available, Then the system must show the count and allow selection.
   Else if none are available, Then the system must show "No semi-private rooms available".

4. If total occupancy equals license capacity, Then the system must display a capacity warning and prevent room assignment.

5. If bed data changes while the provider is viewing, Then the system must reflect updates on the next refresh or in real-time.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify ALF Stepper Step 4  displays total capacity (e.g., 50 beds), occupied (e.g., 42), available (e.g., 6), on hold (e.g., 2).
2. Verify private rooms show availability breakdown (e.g., 20 total, 18 occupied, 2 available).
3. Verify semi-private rooms show availability breakdown separately.
4. Verify that assigning a bed to another patient reduces the available count.
5. Verify that beds on hold are counted separately from occupied.

Negative:
6. Verify that when all beds are occupied (capacity reached), zero available is shown with a capacity warning.
7. Verify that the system prevents room assignment when at capacity.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the bed availability display uses color-coded indicators (green for available, red for occupied, yellow for on-hold).
9. Verify the display is organized by room type (private and semi-private sections).
10. Verify the state license capacity number is prominently displayed as the maximum.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify that two providers checking availability simultaneously see consistent data.
12. Verify behavior when a bed is released and immediately assigned — counts should stay consistent.
13. Verify that the capacity check accounts for beds on hold (total = occupied + on-hold + available).

Security:
14. Verify that bed availability data is accessible only to authorized providers.
15. Verify that the state license capacity cannot be modified by the provider.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify bed availability loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify real-time updates reflect within 3 seconds of a change.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view current census showing occupied beds, available beds, and beds on hold.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a current census dashboard showing the real-time status of all beds in the facility within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The census display shows occupied beds (currently assigned to active residents), available beds (unoccupied and ready for assignment), and beds on hold (reserved for incoming patients or temporarily unavailable). The census provides a facility-wide snapshot that helps the provider with capacity planning, admissions decisions, and regulatory compliance reporting. The data is presented in a clear visual format with counts and optionally a graphical representation (pie chart or bar chart). Accurate census data is required for daily state reporting in many jurisdictions and for internal operations management.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider navigates to the census view, Then the system must display occupied, available, and on-hold bed counts.

2. If the provider clicks on a census category, Then the system must show the list of beds in that category.

3. If a bed status changes, Then the census must reflect the update on the next refresh.

4. If the facility has no beds on hold, Then the on-hold count must show zero.

5. If the census data fails to load, Then the system must display "Census data unavailable" with a retry option.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the census view shows occupied beds (e.g., 42), available beds (e.g., 6), and beds on hold (e.g., 2).
2. Verify 42 + 6 + 2 = 50 matches the total facility capacity.
3. Verify that after a resident is discharged, the available count increases by 1.
4. Verify clicking "Available" shows a list of available rooms with room numbers.
5. Verify the visual chart matches the numeric values.

Negative:
6. Verify that if census data fails to load, "Census data unavailable" with retry is shown.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the census is displayed as a dashboard with distinct tiles for each category.
8. Verify color coding: green for available, red/blue for occupied, yellow for on-hold.
9. Verify the visual chart (pie or bar) is rendered correctly.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify census accuracy when multiple bed changes happen simultaneously.
11. Verify the census handles a facility with 0 available beds gracefully.

Security:
12. Verify that census data is accessible only to authorized providers.
13. Verify that census counts cannot be manually overridden.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the census view loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify census data refreshes within 2 seconds on manual refresh.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to filter a floor, choose a room type (private or semi-private), and assign a specific available room to a patient.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to filter available rooms by floor and room type, then assign a specific room to a patient within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. On the bed availability or room assignment screen, the provider can filter by floor (e.g., Floor 1, Floor 2, Floor 3) and by room type (Private, Semi-Private). The filtered results show specific available rooms with room numbers, and the provider can select one to assign to the patient. Once assigned, the room status changes from "Available" to "Occupied" and is linked to the patient record. This granular room assignment capability ensures that patients are placed in appropriate rooms based on their preferences and care needs, and that the facility floor plan is efficiently utilized. This is typically done as part of ALF Stepper Step 3 (Check Bed Availability).
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider selects a floor filter, Then only rooms on that floor must be displayed.

2. If the provider selects "Private" room type, Then only private rooms must be shown.
   Else if the provider selects "Semi-Private", Then only semi-private rooms must be shown.

3. If the provider selects an available room and confirms assignment, Then the room must be marked "Occupied" and linked to the patient.
   Else if the room becomes unavailable between selection and confirmation (assigned by another provider), Then the system must display "Room is no longer available. Please select another room.".

4. If no rooms match the filters, Then "No available rooms match the selected criteria" must be shown.

5. When a room is assigned, Then the census counts must update accordingly.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify filtering by "Floor 2" shows only Floor 2 rooms.
2. Verify filtering by room type "Private" shows only private rooms.
3. Verify combining Floor 2 and Private filters shows only private rooms on Floor 2.
4. Verify selecting "Room 205" and confirming assigns it to the patient.
5. Verify the room status changes from "Available" to "Occupied" after assignment.
6. Verify the census available count decreases by 1 after assignment.

Negative:
7. Verify that when no rooms match filters, "No available rooms match the selected criteria" is shown.
8. Verify that assigning a room that was just taken by another provider shows "Room is no longer available.".

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the floor filter is a dropdown with all facility floors listed.
10. Verify the room type filter has "Private" and "Semi-Private" options.
11. Verify available rooms are displayed as selectable cards or list items with room numbers.
12. Verify the selected room is visually highlighted.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify assigning the last available room on a floor updates the filter to show zero rooms.
14. Verify that unassigning a room (discharge) makes it available again in the filter results.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can assign rooms.
16. Verify that room assignment actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify floor and room type filtering returns results within 1 second.
2. Verify room assignment confirmation completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to move qualified resident to waitlist when no beds are available.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to move a qualified lead/resident to a waitlist when no beds are available in the facility within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When the provider completes the qualification process but finds that all beds are occupied during ALF Stepper Step 4 (Check Bed Availability), the system offers the option to add the lead to the waitlist instead of assigning a room. The waitlist captures the lead name, priority level, date added, and contact information. This ensures that qualified leads are not lost when the facility is at capacity — they are tracked and can be notified when a bed becomes available. The waitlist is prioritized, allowing the provider to manage which residents should be offered beds first based on urgency, wait time, and other criteria.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When no beds are available and the lead is qualified, Then the system must offer a "Move to Waitlist" option.

2. If the provider confirms "Move to Waitlist", Then the lead must be added to the waitlist with date, name, priority, and contact information.
   Else if the provider cancels, Then the lead must remain at the current step.

3. If the lead is waitlisted, Then the Lead Profile status must show "Waitlisted".

4. If a priority is set, Then the waitlist must be sorted by priority and date added.

5. If a bed becomes available later, Then the system must allow the provider to move the waitlisted lead to room assignment.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that when no beds are available, a "Move to Waitlist" option is displayed.
2. Verify clicking "Move to Waitlist" adds the lead to the waitlist with the current date.
3. Verify the waitlist shows the lead with name, priority, date added, and contact info.
4. Verify the Lead Profile status shows "Waitlisted".
5. Verify setting priority to "High" ranks the lead appropriately in the waitlist.

Negative:
6. Verify that canceling the "Move to Waitlist" action keeps the lead at the current step.
7. Verify that a lead cannot be waitlisted if they are not qualified.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the "Move to Waitlist" button is visible when no beds are available.
9. Verify the waitlist displays in a table format with sortable columns.
10. Verify priority options (High, Medium, Low) are selectable.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify adding multiple leads to the waitlist simultaneously works correctly.
12. Verify that removing a lead from the waitlist (e.g., lead withdraws) updates the list.

Security:
13. Verify that only authorized providers can manage the waitlist.
14. Verify waitlist data is accessible only to authorized staff.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify adding a lead to the waitlist completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the waitlist loads within 2 seconds with 50+ entries.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view waitlist list with resident priority, date added, and contact information.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view the complete waitlist showing all waitlisted residents with their priority level, date added, and contact information within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The waitlist is displayed as a table or list with columns for Resident Name, Priority (High, Medium, Low), Date Added, Phone, Email, and Service Type. The list is sorted by priority (highest first) and then by date added (oldest first within the same priority), ensuring fair and structured bed allocation when availability opens up. The provider can quickly scan the waitlist to identify which residents should be contacted first when a bed becomes available. The waitlist is a living document that updates as residents are added, removed, or have their priority adjusted. This view is accessible from the facility management or ALF lead management section.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider navigates to the waitlist, Then the system must display all waitlisted residents sorted by priority and date added.

2. If the provider clicks on a resident name, Then the system must navigate to the resident detail page.

3. If the waitlist is empty, Then the system must display "No residents on the waitlist.".

4. If a resident is removed from the waitlist, Then the list must update accordingly.

5. If the provider changes a resident priority, Then the waitlist order must re-sort.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the waitlist displays columns
2. Verify residents are sorted by priority (High → Medium → Low) then by date added (oldest first).
3. Verify clicking a resident name navigates to their detail page.
4. Verify phone and email are visible for each waitlisted resident.
5. Verify removing a resident from the waitlist removes them from the view.

Negative:
6. Verify an empty waitlist shows "No residents on the waitlist.".

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the waitlist is displayed in a clean table format with sortable columns.
8. Verify priority is displayed as a colored badge (red for High, yellow for Medium, green for Low).
9. Verify the waitlist has pagination if there are many entries.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify that residents with the same priority and date are displayed in a consistent order.
11. Verify that changing a resident priority from "Low" to "High" moves them up in the list.

Security:
12. Verify that the waitlist is accessible only to authorized providers.
13. Verify that contact information is not exposed to unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the waitlist loads within 2 seconds with 100+ entries.
2. Verify sorting the waitlist by different columns completes within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to notify waitlisted resident when bed becomes available.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to send a notification to a waitlisted resident when a bed becomes available in the facility within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When a bed is freed up (due to discharge, transfer, or room release), the provider can view the waitlist, identify the next resident based on priority, and send them a notification via email or SMS informing them of the bed availability. The notification includes details about the available room type and instructions for next steps. This notification functionality ensures that waitlisted residents are promptly informed of openings, reducing the time beds remain empty and maximizing occupancy. The notification action is logged in the Activity Timeline for both the provider and the resident record.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When a bed becomes available, Then the system must allow the provider to notify waitlisted residents via the "Notify" button.

2. If the provider clicks "Notify" and the resident has valid contact information, Then the system must send the notification and update the waitlist entry to "Notified".
   Else if the resident has no valid contact info, Then the system must display "Unable to notify — contact information is missing".

3. When the notification is sent, Then the Activity Timeline must log the action.

4. If the resident confirms and is assigned a room, Then the resident must be removed from the waitlist.
   Else if the resident declines, Then the provider must be able to notify the next resident on the list.

5. If multiple beds become available, Then the provider must be able to notify multiple residents.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify a "Notify" button is available next to each waitlisted resident when a bed is available.
2. Verify clicking "Notify" sends a notification and shows a success message.
3. Verify the Activity Timeline shows "Bed Availability Notification Sent".
4. Verify the waitlist entry updates to "Notified" with the notification date.
5. Verify assigning a room to the notified resident removes them from the waitlist.

Negative:
6. Verify that notifying a resident without contact information shows "Unable to notify — contact information is missing".
7. Verify that if notification delivery fails, an error is shown.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the "Notify" button is clearly visible and positioned in the waitlist row.
9. Verify the "Notified" status badge is visually distinct.
10. Verify the notification date is displayed alongside the status.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify notifying the same resident twice for different bed openings logs each notification.
12. Verify behavior when the bed is assigned to someone else after notification — the waitlisted resident should be notified of the change.

Security:
13. Verify that only authorized providers can send bed availability notifications.
14. Verify that notifications do not expose other residents information.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify sending a notification completes within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the waitlist with "Notified" status updates within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to track how long Leads have been on waitlist.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track the duration each lead has been on the waitlist within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The waitlist view displays a "Days on Waitlist" column that calculates the number of days since the lead was added to the waitlist (Date Added to current date). This metric helps the provider identify leads who have been waiting excessively long and may need priority attention, reassessment, or alternative arrangements. The wait time can be used to prioritize notifications when beds become available — longer-waiting leads may be offered beds first if priorities are equal. This tracking is also important for operational reporting and for identifying systemic capacity issues that may require expansion or process changes. Leads waiting beyond a configurable threshold (e.g., 30 days) can be flagged for follow-up.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider views the waitlist, Then each entry must display the calculated "Days on Waitlist" from the date added to the current date.

2. If a lead has been on the waitlist longer than the configurable threshold (e.g., 30 days), Then the entry must be visually flagged.

3. If the provider sorts by "Days on Waitlist", Then the list must sort by descending wait time.

4. If a lead is removed from the waitlist, Then the total wait duration must be recorded for reporting.

5. If all leads are within the acceptable wait threshold, Then no flags should be shown.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the waitlist shows a "Days on Waitlist" column with calculated values.
2. Verify a lead added 15 days ago shows "15 days" in the column.
3. Verify a lead added today shows "0 days" or "< 1 day".
5. Verify sorting by "Days on Waitlist" orders by longest wait first.

Negative:
6. Verify that a lead not on the waitlist does not have wait time tracked.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the "Days on Waitlist" column is clearly visible and formatted.
8. Verify flagged entries (over threshold) have a visual indicator (e.g., red text, warning icon).
9. Verify the column is sortable by clicking the header.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify that the day count updates correctly at midnight (day rollover).
11. Verify that leads added and removed on the same day show "0 days" in historical records.

Security:
12. Verify that waitlist duration data is accessible only to authorized providers.
13. Verify that the wait time calculation cannot be manipulated.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the "Days on Waitlist" calculation renders within 1 second for all entries.
2. Verify sorting by wait time completes within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to update the room status on hold and release the room
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to place a room on hold (temporarily reserving it) and subsequently release it back to available status within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When a room needs to be reserved for an incoming patient, maintenance, or other reasons, the provider can change its status to "On Hold" with an optional reason. When the hold is no longer needed, the provider can release the room, changing its status back to "Available" so it can be assigned to another patient. The on-hold status prevents other providers from assigning the room to a different patient during the hold period. Room status changes are reflected in the census (occupied, available, on-hold counts) in real-time and logged in the facility audit trail. This capability is essential for managing room turnover, maintenance schedules, and coordinating admissions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider selects "Place on Hold" on an available room, Then the system must change the room status to "On Hold".

2. If the provider enters a hold reason, Then the reason must be stored.
   Else if no reason is entered, Then the hold must be placed without a reason (reason is optional).

3. When the provider selects "Release Room" on an on-hold room, Then the status must change to "Available".

4. If another provider attempts to assign an on-hold room, Then the system must display "Room is currently on hold" and prevent assignment.

5. When room status changes, Then the census counts must update in real-time and the action must be logged.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify selecting "Place on Hold" on an available room changes its status to "On Hold".
2. Verify entering hold reason "Reserved for incoming patient John Doe" saves the reason.
3. Verify selecting "Release Room" on an on-hold room changes status back to "Available".
4. Verify the census on-hold count increases by 1 when a room is placed on hold.
5. Verify the census available count increases by 1 when a room is released.
6. Verify hold and release actions are logged in the audit trail with timestamps.

Negative:
7. Verify that assigning an on-hold room shows "Room is currently on hold" and prevents assignment.
8. Verify that placing an already on-hold room on hold again shows "Room is already on hold".

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify on-hold rooms are displayed with a yellow/orange indicator in the room list.
10. Verify the "Place on Hold" and "Release Room" actions are accessible from a room context menu.
11. Verify the hold reason is visible when viewing on-hold room details.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify placing a room on hold and releasing it multiple times logs each action.
13. Verify that a room placed on hold for a long period (30+ days) triggers a review notification.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized providers can place rooms on hold or release them.
15. Verify that room status changes are immutably logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify placing a room on hold updates the status within 1 second.
2. Verify releasing a room updates the census counts within 2 seconds.

User Story:
As a Provider, I should be able to view all residents in table/list format.
- All, Active, In-progress, New Arrivals and Discharged, Waitlist residents
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive list of all residents within the Nemi Care Facility Portal displayed in a table/list format with status-based filtering. The Residents List Screen features a blue navigation bar at the top and presents residents in a structured table with columns for resident Name, Status (Active, In-progress, New Arrival, Discharged, waitlist), and Service Type. Above the table, filter tabs labeled All, Active, In-progress, New Arrivals, and Discharged allow the provider to quickly narrow down the view to residents of a specific status category. The default view shows all residents under the "All" tab, displaying the complete census across every status. Each row in the table is clickable, allowing the provider to navigate to the individual resident detail page for further management. This centralized resident list view is essential for the provider to have an at-a-glance understanding of their facility census, track resident statuses, and efficiently locate specific residents for care coordination and administrative tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider navigates to the Residents List Screen, Then the system must load and display all residents in a table with columns Name, Status, and Service Type, with the "All" tab selected by default.

2. If the provider clicks the "Active" tab, Then only residents with "Active" status must be displayed in the table.
   Else if the provider clicks the "In-progress" tab, Then only residents with "In-progress" status must be displayed.
   Else if the provider clicks the "New Arrivals" tab, Then only residents with "New Arrival" status must be displayed.
   Else if the provider clicks the "Discharged" tab, Then only residents with "Discharged" status must be displayed.
  Else if the provider clicks the "waitlist" tab, Then only residents with "waitlist" status must be displayed.
   Else if the provider clicks the "All" tab, Then all residents must be displayed regardless of status.

3. If residents exist for the selected status filter, Then the table must show the matching resident rows.
   Else if no residents exist for the selected status, Then the table must display a "No residents found" message.

4. If the provider clicks on a resident row, Then the system must navigate to the selected resident's detail page.

5. If the Residents List Screen loads successfully, Then the blue navigation bar must be visible at the top of the page with the resident count for each tab.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that navigating to the Residents section loads the Residents List Screen with a table showing all the columns
2. Verify the "All" tab is selected by default and displays all residents across all statuses.
3. Verify clicking the "Active" tab filters the table to show only residents with "Active" status.
4. Verify clicking the "In-progress" tab filters the table to show only residents with "In-progress" status.
5. Verify clicking the "New Arrivals" tab filters the table to show only residents with "New Arrival" status.
6. Verify clicking the "Discharged" tab filters the table to show only residents with "Discharged" status.
7. Verify clicking the "All" tab after selecting another tab returns the view to all residents.
7. Verify clicking the "waitlist" tab after selecting another tab returns the view to all residents.
8. Verify clicking on resident "Maria Garcia" row navigates to her resident detail page.

Negative:
9. Verify that selecting a status tab with no matching residents displays "No residents found" message.
10. Verify that the table does not display duplicate entries for the same resident under any filter tab.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the blue navigation bar is displayed at the top of the Residents List Screen.
12. Verify the filter tabs (All, Active, In-progress, New Arrivals, Discharged) are visually distinct and show the active tab with a highlighted/selected state.
13. Verify the Status column displays status values with color-coded badges (e.g., green for Active, yellow for In-progress, blue for New Arrival, gray for Discharged).
14. Verify the table rows are clickable with a hover effect indicating interactivity.
15. Verify the table supports pagination or infinite scroll when the resident count exceeds the visible area.

Edge Cases:
16. Verify the resident list displays correctly when there is only one resident in the facility.
17. Verify the table handles a large number of residents (500+) without layout breaking or columns misaligning.
18. Verify switching between filter tabs rapidly does not cause display glitches or stale data.

Security:
19. Verify that only authenticated providers can access the Residents List Screen.
20. Verify that resident data displayed in the table is not accessible to unauthorized roles.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Residents List Screen loads and displays all residents within 3 seconds.
2. Verify switching between filter tabs updates the table within 1 second.
3. Verify the table renders correctly with 500+ resident records without significant lag.

User Story: As staff, I should be able to update resident profiles, payers, contacts and preferences
Description of User Story: This feature allows facility staff to update existing resident records in the Assisted Living Facility management system. Staff can modify resident demographic information such as name, date of birth, gender, and photo. The payer information section allows updating primary and secondary payer details including insurance information and payment methods. Emergency contacts and authorized contacts can be added, edited, or removed from the resident profile. Resident preferences including dietary restrictions, activity preferences, room temperature settings, and communication preferences can be updated at any time. All changes are saved with an audit trail showing who made the modification and when. The system validates all input fields to ensure data integrity and completeness.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the staff modifies a required field and leaves it blank, Then a validation error must appear and the save must be blocked.

2. If the staff updates payer information with valid data, Then the system must save the changes and display a success message.

3. If the staff tries to delete the last emergency contact, Then the system must warn that at least one emergency contact is required.

4. If the staff enters an invalid phone number format, Then the field must show a validation error.

5. If the profile update is successful, Then the audit trail must record the change automatically.

6. If a network error occurs during save, Then the system must display an error message and retain the unsaved changes in the form.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that staff can update resident demographic information (e.g., change last name from "Smith" to "Johnson") and the changes are saved correctly.
2. Verify that staff can update payer details including insurance ID and payment method and the update is confirmed.
3. Verify that staff can add a new emergency contact (e.g., "Jane Doe, 555-1234, Sister") and it appears in the contacts list.
4. Verify that staff can update resident dietary preferences (e.g., "Gluten-Free") and activity preferences and they are saved.
5. Verify that staff can edit an existing contact and the updated details are reflected.

Negative:
6. Verify that staff leaving a required field (e.g., Date of Birth) blank sees a validation error.
7. Verify that staff entering an invalid email format (e.g., "notanemail") is prevented from saving.
8. Verify that staff attempting to remove the only emergency contact is blocked by the system.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify that the resident profile page has clearly labeled tabs for Demographics, Payers, Contacts, and Preferences.
10. Verify that form fields display current values and are editable with proper input types (date picker, dropdown, text).
11. Verify that success and error toast notifications display correctly after save actions.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify that staff can update the profile with special characters in name fields (e.g., "O'Brien" or "Mary-Jane").
13. Verify that staff adding the maximum number of contacts allowed and then attempting to add one more is handled gracefully.
14. Verify that staff making changes and navigating away without saving are prompted about unsaved changes.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized staff can access and edit resident profiles.
16. Verify that sensitive fields like SSN are masked and encrypted in transit.
Performance Scenarios:
Verify that resident profile page should load with all tabs and data within 3 seconds.
Verify that saving profile updates should complete within 2 seconds under normal load.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to filter and Search residents list.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to filter and search through the residents list within the Nemi Care Facility Portal to quickly locate specific residents. The Residents List Screen includes a search bar prominently positioned above the table that allows the provider to type a resident name or other identifying information to narrow down results in real-time. Adjacent to the search bar, a filter button provides access to advanced filtering options that allow the provider to narrow the list by criteria such as service type, status, or other resident attributes. As the provider types in the search bar, the table dynamically updates to show only residents matching the search query. The combination of search and filter functionality enables providers to efficiently manage large resident populations by quickly finding specific individuals or subsets of residents based on multiple criteria. This is essential for day-to-day facility operations where providers need rapid access to resident information without scrolling through lengthy lists.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider types text into the search bar, Then the system must dynamically filter the resident table to show only rows where the resident name or identifiers contain the search text.

2. If the search query matches one or more residents, Then the matching residents must be displayed in the table.
   Else if the search query does not match any residents, Then the table must display "No residents found" message.

3. If the provider clicks the filter button, Then the system must display a filter panel with options for service type, status, and other resident attributes.

4. If the provider applies a service type filter of "ADH", Then only residents with service type "ADH" must be shown.
   Else if the provider applies a service type filter of "ALF", Then only residents with service type "ALF" must be shown.

5. If both a search query and filter are active, Then the table must show only residents matching both criteria simultaneously.
   Else if the provider clears the search but a filter is still active, Then only the filter must remain applied.
   Else if the provider removes the filter but the search is still active, Then only the search must remain applied.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that typing "John" in the search bar dynamically filters the table to show only residents with "John" in their name.
2. Verify that typing "Garcia" shows residents like "Maria Garcia" and "Carlos Garcia" in the results.
3. Verify that clearing the search bar resets the table to show all residents under the current status tab.
4. Verify that clicking the filter button opens a filter panel with options for Service Type and Status.
5. Verify that selecting Service Type "ADH" in the filter shows only ADH residents.
6. Verify that applying both a search for "Maria" and a filter for Service Type "ALF" shows only Maria residents with ALF service type.
7. Verify that removing all filters and clearing the search restores the full resident list.

Negative:
8. Verify that searching for a non-existent name like "XYZNONEXIST" displays "No residents found" message.
9. Verify that applying a filter with no matching residents displays "No residents found" message.
10. Verify that entering special characters in the search bar (e.g., "<script>alert(1)</script>") does not cause errors or XSS execution.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the search bar is prominently positioned above the resident table with a placeholder like "Search residents...".
12. Verify the filter button is clearly visible adjacent to the search bar with a filter icon.
13. Verify the filter panel opens as an overlay or slide-out panel with clearly labeled filter options.
14. Verify that the search results update dynamically as the provider types without requiring a manual submit action.
15. Verify that applied filters show visual indicators (e.g., badges or chips) so the provider knows which filters are active.

Edge Cases:
16. Verify that searching with a single character "M" returns all residents whose name contains "M".
17. Verify search is case-insensitive — "maria" and "MARIA" return the same results.
18. Verify that applying filters while on the "Active" status tab combines the tab filter with the additional filter correctly.
19. Verify that rapidly typing and deleting in the search bar does not cause stale or incorrect results.

Security:
20. Verify that the search and filter functionality is accessible only to authenticated providers.
21. Verify that search input is sanitized server-side to prevent SQL injection or NoSQL injection attacks.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that search results update within 500 milliseconds of typing each character.
2. Verify that applying filters returns results within 1 second on a list of 500+ residents.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to import & export residents list.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to import resident data from external files and export the current residents list from the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The Residents List Screen features Import and Export buttons positioned prominently in the toolbar area above the table, allowing the provider to perform bulk data operations. The Import function allows the provider to upload a file (e.g., CSV or Excel format) containing resident records, which the system parses and adds to the resident database after validation. The Export function generates a downloadable file containing the currently displayed resident list data, respecting any active filters or status tabs so the provider can export targeted subsets. This capability is essential for facility onboarding when migrating resident data from legacy systems, for sharing resident lists with external stakeholders or regulatory bodies, and for backup and reporting purposes. Without import/export, the provider would need to manually enter each resident record individually and would have no efficient way to extract data for offline analysis or compliance reporting.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. When the provider clicks the Import button, Then the system must display an import dialog with file upload capability supporting CSV and Excel formats.

2. If the provider uploads a valid file with all records passing validation, Then the system must import all records and display a success message with the count of imported residents.
   Else if some records fail validation, Then the system must import the valid records and display an error report listing the failed rows with reasons.
   Else if the file format is unsupported, Then the system must display an error "Unsupported file format. Please upload CSV or Excel file".

3. If the provider clicks the Export button with no filters active, Then the system must export all residents.
   Else if filters are active, Then the system must export only the filtered subset of residents.

4. If the imported file contains duplicate records matching existing residents, Then the system must flag the duplicates and allow the provider to skip or overwrite.

5. If the import file is empty (no data rows), Then the system must display "The uploaded file contains no data" and prevent the import.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Import and Export buttons are visible on the Residents List Screen toolbar.
2. Verify clicking Import opens an import dialog with a file upload area supporting CSV and Excel formats.
3. Verify uploading a valid CSV file with 10 resident records imports all 10 and displays a success message "10 residents imported successfully".
4. Verify uploading a valid Excel (.xlsx) file imports the records correctly.
5. Verify that after a successful import, the newly imported residents appear in the Residents List table.
6. Verify clicking Export downloads a file containing all residents when the "All" tab is selected.
7. Verify clicking Export with the "Active" tab selected downloads a file containing only Active residents.
8. Verify the exported file contains correct columns: Name, Status, Service Type.

Negative:
9. Verify uploading an unsupported file format (e.g., .pdf, .jpg) displays "Unsupported file format. Please upload CSV or Excel file".
10. Verify uploading an empty file displays "The uploaded file contains no data".
11. Verify uploading a file with invalid data (e.g., missing required name field) shows an error report with row-level error details.
12. Verify that importing a file with duplicate records matching existing residents flags the duplicates for provider review.

UI Scenarios:
13. Verify the Import button has an upload icon and the Export button has a download icon for visual clarity.
14. Verify the import dialog supports drag-and-drop file upload in addition to browse.
15. Verify a progress indicator is shown during file upload and processing.
16. Verify the import error report is displayed in a readable table with row numbers and error descriptions.

Edge Cases:
17. Verify importing a large file with 1000+ resident records processes without timeout.
18. Verify that importing a file with mixed valid and invalid rows imports only the valid ones and reports the invalid rows.
19. Verify exporting when the filtered list has zero residents either downloads an empty file with headers or shows a message "No data to export".

Security:
20. Verify that only authorized providers with import/export permissions can access the Import and Export functionality.
