User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to assign a room to a Respite Care patient using the standard room assignment flow
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to assign an available room to a Respite Care patient using the same standard room assignment flow that exists for other resident types within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident profile or during the admission process, the provider can access the Room Assignment interface which displays available rooms, their status (Available, Occupied, Under Maintenance), floor or wing information, and room type (Private, Semi-Private, Shared). The provider selects an available room and confirms the assignment, which updates both the resident record and the room status to Occupied. For Respite Care patients, the room assignment is inherently temporary and tied to the expected discharge date, after which the room should be released. This standardized flow ensures consistent room management across all care types and prevents double-booking of rooms. Without this feature, respite patients would have no formal room tracking, leading to potential conflicts with permanent residents and operational confusion.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Assign Room" on a Respite Care patient profile, Then the system must display the Room Assignment interface showing only rooms available for assignment.
   Else if no rooms are available, Then the system must display a message "No rooms currently available" with an option to view the waitlist.

2. If the provider selects an available room and confirms the assignment, Then the system must update the room status to "Occupied" and link it to the Respite Care patient record.
   Else if the selected room becomes unavailable between selection and confirmation (concurrent assignment), Then the system must display an error "Room is no longer available, please select another room".

3. If the provider attempts to assign a second room to a patient who already has an active room assignment, Then the system must prompt whether to transfer the patient to the new room or cancel the action.

4. If the room assignment is successful, Then the room must appear as "Occupied" on the Room Management dashboard with the patient name and expected discharge date.
   Else if the assignment fails due to a system error, Then the system must display an error message and the room status must remain unchanged.

5. If the Respite Care patient has an expected discharge date, Then the system must display this date alongside the room assignment for planning purposes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Assign Room" opens the Room Assignment interface showing available rooms.
2. Verify that selecting an available room and confirming assigns the room to the Respite Care patient.
3. Verify that the room status changes to "Occupied" on the Room Management dashboard after assignment.
4. Verify that the resident profile displays the assigned room number, floor, and room type.
5. Verify that the Room Management dashboard shows the expected discharge date for the assigned respite patient.

Negative:
6. Verify that attempting to assign an already occupied room displays an appropriate error message.
7. Verify that attempting to assign a room under maintenance status is not allowed.
8. Verify that submitting room assignment without selecting a room displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the Room Assignment interface displays rooms in a clear grid or list format with status indicators.
10. Verify available rooms are visually distinct from occupied and maintenance rooms (e.g., color coding).
11. Verify the room details (floor, wing, type) are clearly visible for each room entry.
12. Verify the "Confirm Assignment" button is enabled only when a room is selected.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify behavior when two providers attempt to assign the same room to different patients simultaneously.
14. Verify behavior when a Respite Care patient already has a room assigned and a new assignment is attempted.
15. Verify that room assignment works correctly when the facility has only one available room left.

Security:
16. Verify that only authorized providers can assign rooms to patients.
17. Verify that room assignment changes are recorded in the audit log.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Room Assignment interface loads the list of available rooms within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that confirming a room assignment updates the room status within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to collect an upfront deposit payment from a Respite Care patient at the time of admission
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to collect and record an upfront deposit payment from a Respite Care patient during the admission process within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. During admission or from the resident billing section, the provider can initiate a deposit collection by entering the deposit amount, selecting the payment method (Credit Card, Debit Card, Check, Cash, Electronic Transfer), and recording the transaction. The system generates a deposit receipt with a unique transaction ID, amount, payment method, date, and payer details. Respite Care stays are short-term by nature, so collecting a deposit at admission ensures financial security for the facility and sets clear payment expectations with the patient or their family. The deposit amount is tracked separately from regular billing and can be applied toward the final invoice or refunded upon discharge depending on facility policy. Without this feature, the facility would lack a structured way to capture and track upfront payments for temporary respite stays.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Collect Deposit" and enters a valid amount and payment method and clicks "Process Payment", Then the system must record the deposit transaction and generate a unique transaction ID.
   Else if the provider enters an invalid amount (zero, negative, or non-numeric), Then the system must display a validation error and prevent submission.

2. If the payment method is "Credit Card" or "Debit Card", Then the system must capture the last four digits of the card for reference.
   Else if the payment method is "Check", Then the system must capture the check number.
   Else if the payment method is "Cash", Then the system must record the cash amount and any change due.

3. If the deposit is processed successfully, Then the system must display a success message and generate a printable receipt.
   Else if the payment processing fails (e.g., card declined), Then the system must display an error message and allow retry.

4. If the facility policy requires a minimum deposit for respite care, Then the system must validate the entered amount against the minimum threshold.
   Else if no minimum is configured, Then any amount greater than zero must be accepted.

5. If a deposit has already been collected for the resident, Then the system must allow additional deposits and track each as a separate transaction.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Collect Deposit" opens the Deposit Payment Form with fields for amount, payment method, and payer details.
2. Verify that entering a valid deposit amount of $500, selecting "Credit Card", and clicking "Process Payment" records the transaction successfully.
3. Verify that the deposit appears as a line item in the resident billing section with correct amount, method, and date.
4. Verify that a receipt is generated with transaction ID, facility name, resident name, amount, and payment method.
5. Verify that multiple deposits can be collected for the same resident and each is tracked separately.

Negative:
6. Verify that entering a deposit amount of zero displays a validation error.
7. Verify that entering a negative deposit amount displays a validation error.
8. Verify that attempting to process payment without selecting a payment method displays a validation error.
9. Verify that entering non-numeric characters in the deposit amount field is rejected.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the Deposit Payment Form is clearly labeled and organized with amount, payment method, and payer sections.
11. Verify the payment method dropdown includes Credit Card, Debit Card, Check, Cash, and Electronic Transfer options.
12. Verify the receipt format is clean and printable with all required details.
13. Verify the success message is displayed prominently after a successful deposit collection.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when processing a very large deposit amount (e.g., $99,999.99).
15. Verify behavior when the payment gateway times out during credit card processing.
16. Verify that the deposit amount field handles decimal values correctly (e.g., $250.50).

Security:
17. Verify that payment card details are masked and not stored in plain text.
18. Verify that only authorized billing staff or providers can process deposit payments.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Deposit Payment Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that processing a deposit payment and generating a receipt completes within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to add Vitals for a Respite Care Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to add vital signs for a Respite Care Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal patient charting section. From the resident charting screen, the provider can access the Vitals module and record measurements including blood pressure (systolic/diastolic), heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation (SpO2), weight, and pain level. Each vitals entry is timestamped with the date and time of recording and associated with the logged-in provider who took the measurements. For Respite Care patients, regular vitals monitoring is essential during their temporary stay to detect any deterioration in health and ensure continuity of care when they return to their primary caregiver. The vitals data is stored in the resident medical record and can be viewed in a list or trend format for clinical decision-making. Without this feature, providers would have no structured way to document and track the health status of respite residents during their stay.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Add Vitals" on the resident charting screen, Then the system must display the Add Vitals Form with all standard vitals fields.

2. If the provider enters valid vitals values and clicks Save, Then the system must store the record with timestamp and provider identity.
   Else if the provider leaves all fields empty and clicks Save, Then the system must display a validation error "At least one vital sign must be recorded".

3. If the provider enters a value outside the physiological range (e.g., heart rate > 250 or temperature > 110°F), Then the system must display a warning but still allow saving after confirmation.
   Else if the value is within normal range, Then the system must save without any warning.

4. If the vitals entry is saved successfully, Then the system must add the entry to the Vitals List in reverse chronological order.

5. If the provider enters decimal values for temperature or SpO2, Then the system must accept and store the decimal precision correctly.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Vitals" opens the Add Vitals Form with fields for BP, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, and pain level.
2. Verify that entering valid vitals (BP: 120/80, HR: 72, Temp: 98.6°F, RR: 16, SpO2: 98%, Weight: 150 lbs, Pain: 2) and saving creates the vitals record.
3. Verify that the saved vitals entry appears in the Vitals List with correct date/time and provider name.
4. Verify that recording only partial vitals (e.g., just BP and heart rate) is allowed and saves correctly.

Negative:
5. Verify that saving with all vitals fields empty displays a validation error.
6. Verify that entering non-numeric values in vitals fields is rejected.
7. Verify that entering negative values for heart rate or temperature displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Add Vitals Form displays clear labels and appropriate input types (numeric inputs) for each vital sign.
9. Verify the pain level field uses a visual scale (0-10) with clear markings.
10. Verify the date/time is auto-populated with the current date and time when the form opens.
11. Verify the Save and Cancel buttons are properly positioned on the form.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify that entering an unusually high BP value (e.g., 300/200) triggers a warning but allows saving after confirmation.
13. Verify behavior when adding vitals for a resident whose admission has expired but discharge has not been processed.

Security:
14. Verify that only authenticated clinical staff can add vitals for a resident.
15. Verify that vitals entries are associated with the logged-in provider and cannot be attributed to another user.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Add Vitals Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving a vitals entry completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to edit Vitals for a Respite Care Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to edit previously recorded vital signs for a Respite Care Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the Vitals List on the resident charting screen, the provider can select an existing vitals entry and click Edit to open the Edit Vitals Form, which is pre-populated with the current values. The provider can modify any vital sign measurement such as blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, or pain level, and save the updated record. Editing vitals is necessary when a recording error is identified, such as transposing systolic and diastolic pressure values or entering an incorrect temperature reading. The system maintains an audit trail of all changes, capturing the original value, modified value, timestamp of the change, and the identity of the provider who made the modification. Without this capability, incorrect vitals data would remain in the medical record, potentially leading to inappropriate clinical decisions during the respite stay.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Edit" on a vitals entry, Then the system must open the Edit Vitals Form pre-populated with the existing values.
   Else if the vitals entry is locked due to a finalized care period, Then the system must display a message "This entry cannot be edited after care period finalization".

2. If the provider modifies one or more vitals values and clicks Save, Then the system must update the record and log the changes in the audit trail with original values, new values, timestamp, and provider identity.
   Else if no changes were made and the provider clicks Save, Then the system must close the form without creating an audit entry.

3. If the provider enters a value outside the physiological range during editing, Then the system must display a warning and require confirmation before saving.

4. If the provider clears all vitals fields and clicks Save, Then the system must display a validation error and prevent the update.

5. If the provider clicks Cancel after making changes, Then the system must prompt a confirmation to discard changes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Edit" on a vitals entry opens the Edit Vitals Form pre-populated with current values.
2. Verify that changing the heart rate from 72 to 80 and saving updates the vitals record correctly.
3. Verify that the audit trail captures the original and modified values with timestamp and provider identity.
4. Verify that the Vitals List reflects the updated values after editing.

Negative:
5. Verify that clearing all vitals fields and attempting to save displays a validation error.
6. Verify that entering non-numeric values during editing is rejected.
7. Verify that editing a locked vitals entry (finalized care period) displays an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Edit Vitals Form layout matches the Add Vitals Form with all fields pre-populated.
9. Verify that modified fields are visually indicated before saving.
10. Verify the Save and Cancel buttons are clearly visible on the Edit Form.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify behavior when two providers attempt to edit the same vitals entry simultaneously.
12. Verify that editing vitals immediately after creation preserves the original timestamp.
13. Verify that editing a vitals entry does not affect other vitals entries for the same resident.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized clinical staff can edit vitals records.
15. Verify that all edit operations are captured in the audit trail and cannot be deleted.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Edit Vitals Form loads with pre-populated data within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving edited vitals completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to View list of Vitals for a Respite Care Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive list of all recorded vital signs for a Respite Care Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Vitals section to see a tabular list displaying each vitals entry with columns for date/time, blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, pain level, and recorded by (provider name). The list is sorted in reverse chronological order, showing the most recent readings at the top, and supports filtering by date range and pagination for residents with many entries. This view is critical for Respite Care residents because it provides the clinical team with a complete health monitoring history during the temporary stay, enabling trend identification and timely intervention for any deteriorating conditions. The provider can also access individual entries from this list to view full details or initiate edits. Without a consolidated vitals list, the provider would have to review each entry individually, making it impractical to assess health trends during the respite stay.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Vitals section for a Respite Care Resident, Then the system must display a list of all recorded vitals in reverse chronological order.
   Else if no vitals have been recorded for the resident, Then the system must display a message "No vitals recorded yet" with an option to add vitals.

2. If the provider applies a date range filter, Then the list must show only entries within the specified range.
   Else if no entries exist within the selected date range, Then the system must display a message "No vitals found for the selected date range".

3. If the list contains more than the page size limit (e.g., 20 entries per page), Then the system must display pagination controls.
   Else if all entries fit on a single page, Then pagination controls should not be displayed.

4. If the provider clicks on a vitals entry row, Then the system must display the full detail view with all recorded values.

5. If the provider clicks "Add Vitals" from the Vitals List view, Then the system must open the Add Vitals Form.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that navigating to the Vitals section displays a list of all recorded vitals for the Respite Care Resident.
2. Verify that the list shows columns: date/time, BP, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, pain level, and recorded by.
3. Verify that the list is sorted in reverse chronological order (most recent first).
4. Verify that applying a date range filter shows only entries within the selected range.
5. Verify that clicking on a vitals entry row displays the full details.

Negative:
6. Verify that the system displays "No vitals recorded yet" when no vitals exist for the resident.
7. Verify that applying a date range filter with no matching entries displays an appropriate message.
8. Verify that entering an invalid date range (end date before start date) displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the Vitals List table is properly formatted with clear column headers and readable data.
10. Verify pagination controls are displayed when entries exceed the page size limit.
11. Verify the date range filter controls are clearly labeled and easy to use.
12. Verify the "Add Vitals" button is visible and accessible from the Vitals List view.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify that the list correctly displays vitals entries recorded on the same date by different providers.
14. Verify behavior when the resident has only one vitals entry — the list should display it correctly without pagination.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can view the Vitals List for a resident.
16. Verify that the Vitals List does not expose data from other residents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Vitals List loads within 3 seconds for a resident with 100+ vitals entries.
2. Verify that applying a date range filter returns results within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to add & edit medications administered to a Respite Care Resident during their stay
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to add and edit medication records for a Respite Care Resident during their temporary stay within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident charting screen, the provider accesses the Medications module to add new medication entries including drug name, dosage, frequency, route of administration, prescribing physician, start date, and end date. The provider can also edit existing medication entries to update dosage, change frequency, correct errors, or discontinue a medication by setting an end date. Accurate medication tracking is critical for Respite Care residents because they often arrive with existing medication regimens from their primary caregiver, and the facility must ensure continuity of those medications as well as document any new prescriptions during the stay. Each medication entry and edit is logged with the provider identity and timestamp for regulatory compliance and care coordination. Without this feature, there would be no reliable way to track medications administered during the respite stay, creating risks for medication errors and gaps in care documentation.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Add Medication" and enters all required fields (drug name, dosage, frequency, route, start date) and clicks Save, Then the system must create the medication record and add it to the active Medications List.
   Else if the provider leaves mandatory fields empty and clicks Save, Then the system must display validation errors for each missing required field.

2. If the provider enters a drug name that matches an existing active medication for the same resident, Then the system must display a duplicate medication warning.
   Else if the drug name is unique, Then the system must proceed without warning.

3. If the provider edits a medication and changes the dosage or frequency, Then the system must update the record, log the change with original and new values, and display the updated entry in the Medications List.
   Else if the provider makes no changes and clicks Save, Then the system must close the form without creating an audit entry.

4. If the provider sets an end date on an active medication, Then the system must mark it as discontinued and move it to the inactive section.
   Else if no end date is set, Then the medication must remain in the active medications section.

5. If the provider enters a start date after the end date, Then the system must display a validation error "Start date must be before end date".
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Medication" opens the form with all required fields.
2. Verify that adding a medication with valid data creates the record in the active Medications List.
3. Verify that editing a medication and changing the dosage updates the record correctly.
4. Verify that setting an end date on an active medication marks it as discontinued.
5. Verify that discontinued medications appear in the inactive medications section.
6. Verify that the audit trail captures all add and edit operations with provider identity and timestamps.

Negative:
7. Verify that saving a medication without a drug name displays a validation error.
8. Verify that entering a start date after the end date displays a validation error.
9. Verify that adding a duplicate medication (same drug name for same resident) triggers a warning.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the Add Medication Form layout is organized with clear field labels and appropriate input types.
11. Verify the Medications List displays active and inactive medications in separate sections.
12. Verify the Edit button is accessible for each medication entry in the list.
13. Verify the drug name field supports autocomplete or search functionality.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when adding a medication for a resident whose respite stay has ended but discharge is not processed.
15. Verify that editing a medication immediately after creation retains all original data.
16. Verify behavior when the same medication is edited multiple times in quick succession.

Security:
17. Verify that only authorized clinical staff can add or edit medication records.
18. Verify that all medication changes are immutably logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Add Medication Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving a medication record completes within 3 seconds.
3. Verify the Medications List loads within 3 seconds for a resident with 20+ medication entries.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to discharge a Respite Care patient by recording the actual discharge date
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to discharge a Respite Care patient by recording the actual discharge date within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident profile or the Respite Care Resident List, the provider initiates the discharge process by clicking "Discharge" which opens a Discharge Form where they enter the actual discharge date, discharge reason, discharge disposition, and any discharge notes. The system validates that the discharge date is on or after the admission date and records the discharge, changing the resident status from "Active" to "Discharged". This is a critical workflow for Respite Care because these stays have a predetermined end date, and recording the actual discharge date is essential for accurate billing, occupancy tracking, and regulatory reporting. Upon discharge, the system also calculates the total length of stay based on admission and discharge dates for billing purposes. Without this feature, respite patients would remain in active status indefinitely, skewing occupancy reports and preventing proper room reallocation.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Discharge" on a Respite Care Resident, Then the system must open the Discharge Form with fields for actual discharge date, reason, disposition, and notes.
   Else if the resident is already discharged, Then the system must display a message "This resident has already been discharged".

2. If the provider enters a valid discharge date (on or after the admission date) and clicks "Confirm Discharge", Then the system must update the resident status to "Discharged" and calculate the length of stay.
   Else if the discharge date is before the admission date, Then the system must display a validation error and prevent the discharge.

3. If the discharge is confirmed, Then the system must trigger downstream processes including room release, billing finalization, and status update in the Resident List.

4. If the provider enters a discharge reason and notes, Then the system must store these details with the discharge record.
   Else if the discharge reason is left blank, Then the system must display a validation error "Discharge reason is required".

5. If the actual discharge date is after the originally planned expected discharge date, Then the system must flag the extended stay for billing review.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Discharge" opens the Discharge Form with required fields.
2. Verify that entering a valid discharge date, reason, and disposition and confirming discharge updates the resident status to "Discharged".
3. Verify that the Resident List shows the "Discharged" status badge after discharge.
4. Verify that the length of stay is correctly calculated from admission to discharge date.
5. Verify that discharge notes are stored and viewable on the resident profile.

Negative:
6. Verify that entering a discharge date before the admission date displays a validation error.
7. Verify that attempting to discharge an already discharged resident displays an appropriate message.
8. Verify that submitting the Discharge Form without a discharge reason displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the Discharge Form is clearly organized with date picker, reason dropdown, disposition field, and notes textarea.
10. Verify the "Confirm Discharge" button requires confirmation before proceeding.
11. Verify the "Discharged" status badge is visually distinct in the Resident List.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify behavior when the actual discharge date matches the admission date (same-day discharge).
13. Verify that discharging a resident with pending billing items triggers an appropriate warning.
14. Verify behavior when the actual discharge date is after the expected discharge date — the system should flag for billing review.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can initiate the discharge process.
16. Verify that discharge actions are recorded in the audit trail with provider identity and timestamp.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Discharge Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that confirming discharge and updating the resident status completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, when a Respite Care patient is discharged, the assigned room should automatically be marked as available again
Description of User Story: This story ensures that when a Respite Care patient is discharged, the room previously assigned to them is automatically marked as "Available" in the Room Management system within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Upon successful discharge confirmation, the system triggers an automated room release process that unlinks the room from the discharged resident and updates the room status from "Occupied" to "Available" on the Room Management dashboard. This automation eliminates the need for manual room status updates, reducing administrative overhead and preventing errors where rooms remain marked as occupied after a patient has left. For Respite Care, this is especially important because room turnover is frequent due to the short-term nature of respite stays, and timely room availability directly impacts the facility ability to admit new patients. The system also logs the room release event with the discharge timestamp and room details for audit and operational reporting purposes. Without this automated room release, staff would need to manually track discharges and update room statuses, leading to potential delays in new admissions and inaccurate occupancy data.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a Respite Care patient is successfully discharged, Then the system must automatically update the assigned room status from "Occupied" to "Available" without any manual intervention.
   Else if the discharge is cancelled or reversed, Then the room status must remain "Occupied" with the patient still linked.

2. If the automated room release is successful, Then the room must immediately appear in the available rooms list for new assignments.
   Else if the room release fails due to a system error, Then the system must log the failure, retain the room as "Occupied", and notify the administrator.

3. If the discharged patient had multiple room assignments (e.g., transfer during stay), Then only the current active room must be released.

4. If the room release is triggered, Then the system must create an audit log entry with room number, patient name, discharge date, and release timestamp.

5. If the room requires cleaning or maintenance after discharge, Then the facility can optionally set the status to "Under Maintenance" instead of "Available" based on facility configuration.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that discharging a Respite Care patient automatically changes the assigned room status to "Available".
2. Verify that the released room appears in the available rooms list for new admissions.
3. Verify that the Room Management dashboard no longer shows the discharged patient linked to the room.
4. Verify that an audit log entry is created for the room release with correct details.
5. Verify that a new patient can be assigned to the recently released room immediately.

Negative:
6. Verify that if the discharge is cancelled, the room status remains "Occupied".
7. Verify that a system error during room release retains the room as "Occupied" and triggers an admin notification.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Room Management dashboard updates the room status in real-time after discharge.
9. Verify the room status change is reflected with appropriate color coding (e.g., green for Available).
10. Verify that no manual "Release Room" action is required by the provider after discharge.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify behavior when a patient who was transferred between rooms during their stay is discharged — only the current room should be released.
12. Verify behavior when multiple Respite Care patients are discharged simultaneously — all their rooms should be released.
13. Verify that room release works correctly even if the discharge occurs after the expected discharge date.

Security:
14. Verify that room status changes triggered by discharge cannot be manually overridden by unauthorized users.
15. Verify that the audit log for room release events is immutable and accessible only to authorized administrators.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the room status update to "Available" occurs within 5 seconds of discharge confirmation.
2. Verify that simultaneous discharge of 5 respite patients releases all rooms within 10 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view a residents list filtered specifically by Independent Living
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a filtered list of residents that shows only those classified under the Independent Living (IL) care type within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. On the Resident List screen, the provider can apply a care type filter or navigate to a dedicated Independent Living section to see a table displaying IL residents with columns such as resident name, room number, admission date, status, and contact information. The filter ensures that ALF, Memory Care, Respite Care, and other care type residents are excluded from the view, providing a focused list for IL-specific operations. This capability is essential for facilities that manage multiple care levels simultaneously, as it allows the IL team to quickly access their resident population without scrolling through the entire facility census. The filtered list supports search within results, sorting by any column, and pagination for large IL populations. Without this filter, providers would need to manually scan through all residents to find IL-specific entries, which is time-consuming and error-prone in facilities with hundreds of residents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider selects "Independent Living" from the care type filter, Then the system must display only residents with care type "Independent Living".
   Else if no filter is applied, Then the system must display all residents across all care types.

2. If the IL-filtered list contains residents, Then the system must display the resident table with name, room, admission date, status, and contact columns.
   Else if no IL residents exist in the system, Then the system must display a message "No Independent Living residents found".

3. If the provider types a search query in the search box while the IL filter is active, Then the system must filter results within the IL population only.
   Else if the search query returns no matches, Then the system must display "No results found".

4. If the provider clicks a column header, Then the system must sort the list by that column and toggle between ascending and descending order on subsequent clicks.

5. If the filtered list exceeds the page size limit, Then the system must show pagination controls with the total count of IL residents.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that selecting "Independent Living" from the care type filter displays only IL residents.
2. Verify that the filtered list shows correct columns: name, room number, admission date, status, and contact.
3. Verify that searching within the IL-filtered list returns only matching IL residents.
4. Verify that sorting by admission date works correctly in both ascending and descending order.
5. Verify that the total count displayed matches the actual number of IL residents.

Negative:
6. Verify that ALF, MC, and Respite Care residents do not appear in the IL-filtered list.
7. Verify that searching for a non-existent resident name displays "No results found".
8. Verify that an empty IL population displays "No Independent Living residents found".

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the care type filter dropdown is clearly visible and accessible on the Resident List screen.
10. Verify the IL-filtered list table is properly formatted with readable column headers and data.
11. Verify pagination controls are displayed when the list exceeds the page size.
12. Verify the search box is positioned prominently and supports real-time filtering.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify behavior when the IL filter is applied and then the page is refreshed — the filter should persist or reset based on design.
14. Verify that switching between different care type filters updates the list correctly without stale data.

Security:
15. Verify that only authenticated providers can view the IL resident list.
16. Verify that the filtered list does not expose data from other facilities in a multi-facility setup.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the IL-filtered Resident List loads within 3 seconds for a facility with 500+ total residents.
2. Verify that search within the IL-filtered list returns results within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, if the facility offers IL along with ALF or MC, the medication module should be visible and available for all residents including IL residents who choose to use it
Description of User Story: This story ensures that when a facility offers Independent Living (IL) alongside Assisted Living Facility (ALF) or Memory Care (MC) services, the medication management module is visible and accessible for all residents, including IL residents who opt to use it, within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. In a multi-service facility, the medication module that is typically available for ALF and MC residents should also appear in the navigation and charting screens for IL residents, allowing providers to add, edit, and view medications for IL residents who voluntarily participate in medication management. This is an opt-in feature for IL residents since they generally self-manage medications, but some may choose facility-managed medication administration for convenience or health safety reasons. The facility configuration settings determine whether IL residents see the medication module based on the facility service offerings. The medication module for IL residents has the same functionality as for ALF/MC residents, including drug name, dosage, frequency, route, prescribing physician, and administration tracking. Without this conditional visibility, IL residents in multi-service facilities would be unable to access medication management services even when they need or want them.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the facility configuration includes IL along with ALF or MC, Then the system must display the Medication module for IL residents.
   Else if the facility offers only IL (no ALF or MC), Then the system must hide the Medication module for IL residents.

2. If the Medication module is visible for an IL resident, Then the provider must be able to add, edit, and view medications with the same functionality as ALF/MC.

3. If the facility configuration changes (e.g., ALF service is added), Then the Medication module visibility for IL residents must update accordingly without requiring a system restart.

4. If an IL resident has medication records and the facility later removes ALF/MC services, Then existing medication records must be retained and viewable in read-only mode.
   Else if no medication records exist, Then the Medication module should simply be hidden.

5. If the provider adds a medication for an IL resident, Then the system must store it with the same data fields and validations as ALF/MC medications.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Medication module is visible for IL residents when the facility offers IL + ALF services.
2. Verify that the Medication module is visible for IL residents when the facility offers IL + MC services.
3. Verify that the provider can add a medication for an IL resident with all standard fields.
4. Verify that the provider can edit an existing medication for an IL resident.
5. Verify that the provider can view the medications list for an IL resident.

Negative:
6. Verify that the Medication module is hidden for IL residents when the facility offers only IL (no ALF/MC).
7. Verify that adding a medication without required fields displays validation errors for IL residents, same as ALF/MC.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Medication module navigation item appears in the IL resident charting menu when conditions are met.
9. Verify the Medication interface for IL residents matches the layout and functionality of ALF/MC medication screens.
10. Verify that the Medication module is not shown in the navigation when the facility is IL-only.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify that when a facility adds ALF service to an existing IL-only facility, the Medication module becomes visible for IL residents dynamically.
12. Verify that when ALF/MC services are removed from a multi-service facility, existing IL medication records remain accessible in read-only mode.
13. Verify behavior when an IL resident is transferred to ALF — their medication records should carry over seamlessly.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized providers can access the Medication module for IL residents.
15. Verify that facility configuration changes affecting module visibility require administrator-level permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Medication module visibility check based on facility configuration completes within 1 second during page load.
2. Verify the Medication module for IL residents loads within 2 seconds, consistent with ALF/MC load times.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to add Vitals for a IL Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to add vital signs for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal patient charting section. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider accesses the Vitals module and records measurements including blood pressure (systolic/diastolic), heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation (SpO2), weight, and pain level. Each vitals entry is timestamped with the date and time of recording and linked to the logged-in provider who captured the readings. While IL residents are generally more independent, periodic vitals monitoring helps detect early signs of health changes that might require a transition to a higher level of care such as ALF or MC. The recorded vitals are stored in the resident medical record and accessible through the Vitals List for trending and clinical review. Without this feature, the facility would lack a standardized mechanism to document and track health indicators for IL residents, limiting proactive health management.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Add Vitals" for an IL Resident, Then the system must display the Add Vitals Form with all standard vitals fields.

2. If the provider enters valid vitals values and clicks Save, Then the system must store the entry with timestamp and provider information.
   Else if all fields are empty and the provider clicks Save, Then the system must display "At least one vital sign must be recorded".

3. If the provider enters a value outside physiological range (e.g., temperature > 110°F), Then the system must display a warning but allow saving upon confirmation.
   Else if all values are within normal range, Then the system must save without warning.

4. If the vitals entry saves successfully, Then it must appear in the Vitals List in reverse chronological order.

5. If the provider records only partial vitals (e.g., just blood pressure and weight), Then the system must accept and save only the entered fields.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Vitals" opens the form with fields for BP, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, and pain level.
2. Verify that entering valid vitals and saving creates a record with correct timestamp and provider name.
3. Verify that the saved entry appears at the top of the Vitals List.
4. Verify that recording only partial vitals (e.g., BP and weight only) saves correctly.

Negative:
5. Verify that saving with all fields empty displays a validation error.
6. Verify that entering non-numeric values in vitals fields is rejected.
7. Verify that entering negative values for heart rate or temperature displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Add Vitals Form has clear labels and numeric input fields for each vital sign.
9. Verify the pain level field uses a 0-10 visual scale.
10. Verify the date/time is auto-populated when the form opens.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify that entering a BP value like 300/200 triggers a warning but allows saving after confirmation.
12. Verify behavior when adding vitals for an IL resident who is in the process of being transferred to ALF.

Security:
13. Verify that only authenticated clinical staff can add vitals for an IL resident.
14. Verify that vitals entries are linked to the logged-in provider and cannot be attributed to another user.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Add Vitals Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving a vitals entry completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to edit Vitals for a IL Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to edit previously recorded vital signs for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the Vitals List on the IL resident charting screen, the provider selects an existing vitals entry and clicks Edit to open the Edit Vitals Form pre-populated with the current values. The provider can modify any vital sign measurement including blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, or pain level, and save the corrected record. Editing is necessary when a recording error is identified, such as an incorrect temperature reading or transposed blood pressure values captured during the assessment. The system maintains an audit trail of all edits, recording the original value, new value, timestamp of the change, and the identity of the modifying provider. Without this capability, erroneous vitals data would remain in the IL resident medical record, potentially misrepresenting their health status and affecting care decisions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Edit" on an IL resident vitals entry, Then the system must open the Edit Vitals Form pre-populated with existing values.
   Else if the entry is locked (finalized care period), Then the system must display "This entry cannot be edited".

2. If the provider modifies values and clicks Save, Then the system must update the record and log changes in the audit trail with original values, new values, timestamp, and provider.
   Else if no changes were made, Then the system must close without creating an audit entry.

3. If the provider enters values outside physiological range during editing, Then the system must display a warning and require confirmation.

4. If the provider clears all fields and clicks Save, Then the system must display a validation error and prevent the update.

5. If the provider clicks Cancel after modifications, Then the system must prompt a confirmation dialog to discard changes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Edit" on a vitals entry opens the form pre-populated with current values.
2. Verify that modifying the heart rate and saving updates the record correctly.
3. Verify that the audit trail captures original and modified values with timestamp and provider.
4. Verify that the Vitals List reflects updated values after editing.

Negative:
5. Verify that clearing all fields and saving displays a validation error.
6. Verify that entering non-numeric values during editing is rejected.
7. Verify that editing a locked entry displays an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the Edit Form layout matches the Add Form with pre-populated fields.
9. Verify Save and Cancel buttons are clearly visible.
10. Verify the confirmation dialog appears when cancelling after changes.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify behavior when two providers edit the same vitals entry simultaneously.
12. Verify that editing does not change the original recording timestamp.

Security:
13. Verify that only authorized clinical staff can edit IL resident vitals.
14. Verify that all edits are immutably logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Edit Vitals Form loads with pre-populated data within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving edited vitals completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to View list of Vitals for a IL Resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive list of all recorded vital signs for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Vitals section to see a tabular list showing each vitals entry with columns for date/time, blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, pain level, and recorded by. The list is sorted in reverse chronological order with the most recent readings at the top, and supports date range filtering and pagination for residents with extensive vitals history. This consolidated view is important for IL residents because it enables the clinical team to identify health trends over time, such as gradually increasing blood pressure or weight changes, which may signal the need for a care level transition. The provider can click on individual entries to view full details or initiate edits from this list view. Without a structured vitals list, the provider would lack a comprehensive picture of the IL resident health trajectory during their independent living stay.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Vitals section for an IL Resident, Then the system must display all recorded vitals in reverse chronological order.
   Else if no vitals exist, Then the system must display "No vitals recorded yet" with an option to add vitals.

2. If the provider applies a date range filter, Then only entries within that range must be shown.
   Else if no entries exist in the range, Then the system must display "No vitals found for the selected date range".

3. If the list exceeds the page size, Then the system must display pagination controls.
   Else if all entries fit on one page, Then pagination controls should not be shown.

4. If the provider clicks on a row, Then the system must display the full detail view.

5. If the provider clicks "Add Vitals" from the list view, Then the system must open the Add Vitals Form.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Vitals section displays a list of all recorded vitals for the IL Resident.
2. Verify correct columns: date/time, BP, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2, weight, pain level, recorded by.
3. Verify the list is sorted in reverse chronological order.
4. Verify date range filtering returns only entries within the selected range.
5. Verify clicking on a row displays full vitals details.

Negative:
6. Verify "No vitals recorded yet" is displayed when no entries exist.
7. Verify an invalid date range (end before start) shows a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the table is formatted with clear headers and readable data.
9. Verify pagination controls appear when entries exceed the page size.
10. Verify the "Add Vitals" button is accessible from the list view.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify correct display when multiple entries exist for the same date/time by different providers.
12. Verify the list displays correctly with only one vitals entry.

Security:
13. Verify only authorized providers can view the IL resident Vitals List.
14. Verify the list does not expose data from other residents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Vitals List loads within 3 seconds for 100+ entries.
2. Verify date range filtering returns results within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to add progress notes for an IL resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to add progress notes for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal charting section. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Progress Notes module and clicks "Add Note" to open a form where they can enter the note date, note type (e.g., General, Clinical, Social, Behavioral), subject, and detailed note content in a rich text area. Each progress note is timestamped and associated with the logged-in provider who authored the note, creating a documented record of observations, assessments, and care-related communications. For IL residents, progress notes capture periodic check-ins, wellness observations, social engagement notes, and any health concerns identified during routine interactions. The notes are stored chronologically in the resident record and accessible to all authorized care team members for continuity of documentation. Without this feature, the facility would lack a formal mechanism to document ongoing observations and interactions with IL residents, creating gaps in the care documentation chain.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Add Note" and fills all required fields (note type, subject, content) and clicks Save, Then the system must create the progress note with timestamp and provider identity.
   Else if the provider leaves the content field empty, Then the system must display "Note content is required" and prevent submission.

2. If the provider selects a note type from the dropdown, Then the system must accept and store the selected type.
   Else if no note type is selected, Then the system must display a validation error "Note type is required".

3. If the progress note is saved successfully, Then it must appear in the Progress Notes List in reverse chronological order.

4. If the provider enters a subject and content but no note date, Then the system must auto-populate the current date.

5. If the note content exceeds the maximum character limit, Then the system must display a warning indicating the character limit has been reached.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Note" opens the form with fields for date, type, subject, and content.
2. Verify that filling required fields and saving creates the progress note with correct timestamp and provider.
3. Verify that the saved note appears at the top of the Progress Notes List.
4. Verify that selecting each note type (General, Clinical, Social, Behavioral) is accepted.
5. Verify that the note date auto-populates with the current date.

Negative:
6. Verify that saving without note content displays "Note content is required".
7. Verify that saving without selecting a note type displays a validation error.
8. Verify that exceeding the character limit for content displays a warning.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the Add Note Form is clearly labeled with appropriate field types (dropdown for type, text area for content).
10. Verify the note content text area supports rich text formatting.
11. Verify the Save and Cancel buttons are properly positioned.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify behavior when adding a note with minimal content (e.g., a single character).
13. Verify that adding multiple notes in quick succession does not cause data loss.

Security:
14. Verify that only authenticated providers can add progress notes for IL residents.
15. Verify that progress notes are attributed to the logged-in provider and cannot be attributed to another user.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Add Progress Note Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving a progress note completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to edit progress notes for an IL resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to edit existing progress notes for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the Progress Notes List on the IL resident charting screen, the provider selects a note and clicks "Edit" to open the Edit Progress Note Form pre-populated with the current note type, subject, and content. The provider can modify any field including the note type, subject, or content text, and save the updated note. Editing progress notes is necessary when the provider needs to correct documentation errors, add additional observations that were missed in the original entry, or update the note with new information. The system maintains an audit trail of all modifications, capturing the original content, modified content, change timestamp, and the identity of the editing provider. Without this editing capability, any errors or omissions in progress notes would remain permanently, compromising the accuracy and reliability of the IL resident documentation.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Edit" on a progress note, Then the system must open the Edit Form pre-populated with existing values.
   Else if the note is locked (signed or finalized), Then the system must display "This note cannot be edited after signing".

2. If the provider modifies the content and clicks Save, Then the system must update the note and create an audit trail entry.
   Else if no changes were made, Then the system must close without creating an audit entry.

3. If the provider changes the note type, Then the system must update the type and log the change.

4. If the provider clears the content field and clicks Save, Then the system must display a validation error.

5. If the provider clicks Cancel after changes, Then the system must show a confirmation dialog.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Edit" opens the form pre-populated with current note data.
2. Verify that modifying the note content and saving updates the record.
3. Verify that the audit trail captures original and modified content.
4. Verify that changing the note type from "General" to "Clinical" updates correctly.

Negative:
5. Verify that clearing the note content and saving displays a validation error.
6. Verify that editing a locked (signed) note displays an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the Edit Form layout matches the Add Form with pre-populated fields.
8. Verify the Save and Cancel buttons are visible.
9. Verify the discard confirmation dialog appears when cancelling with unsaved changes.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify behavior when two providers edit the same note simultaneously.
11. Verify that editing does not change the original creation timestamp.
12. Verify rapid successive edits do not cause data loss.

Security:
13. Verify that only the authoring provider or an authorized supervisor can edit progress notes.
14. Verify that all edit actions are immutably recorded in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Edit Progress Note Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving edited progress notes completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view list of progress notes for an IL resident
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive list of all progress notes recorded for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Progress Notes section to see a tabular list with columns for date/time, note type, subject, author (provider name), and a preview of the note content. The list is sorted in reverse chronological order and supports filtering by note type and date range, as well as search functionality to find notes containing specific keywords. This consolidated view allows the care team to quickly review the documentation history of the IL resident, identify patterns in observations, and ensure continuity of care across provider shifts. The provider can click on any note in the list to view its full content or initiate edits. Without a structured list view, providers would have difficulty accessing historical documentation, leading to fragmented care coordination for IL residents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to Progress Notes for an IL Resident, Then the system must display all notes in reverse chronological order.
   Else if no notes exist, Then the system must display "No progress notes recorded yet" with an option to add a note.

2. If the provider filters by note type, Then only notes of that type must be displayed.
   Else if no notes match the filter, Then "No notes found for the selected type" must be displayed.

3. If the provider searches for a keyword, Then the system must return notes containing that keyword in the subject or content.
   Else if no notes match, Then "No results found" must be displayed.

4. If the provider clicks on a note, Then the full content must be displayed.

5. If the list exceeds the page size, Then pagination controls must be displayed.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Progress Notes section displays all notes for the IL resident.
2. Verify correct columns: date/time, note type, subject, author, and content preview.
3. Verify the list is sorted in reverse chronological order.
4. Verify filtering by note type shows only matching notes.
5. Verify searching by keyword returns relevant results.
6. Verify clicking on a note displays the full content.

Negative:
7. Verify "No progress notes recorded yet" is shown when no notes exist.
8. Verify searching for a non-existent keyword displays "No results found".

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the table is properly formatted with readable column headers.
10. Verify the note type filter dropdown is clearly labeled.
11. Verify the search box is visible and accessible.
12. Verify pagination controls appear when entries exceed the page size.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify the list displays correctly with only one progress note.
14. Verify that notes with very long content show a truncated preview in the list.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can view the IL resident progress notes list.
16. Verify the list does not expose notes from other residents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Progress Notes List loads within 3 seconds for 100+ notes.
2. Verify keyword search returns results within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to add allergy information for an IL resident.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to add allergy information for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal charting section. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Allergies module and clicks "Add Allergy" to open a form where they can enter the allergen name, allergy type (Drug, Food, Environmental, Other), reaction description, severity (Mild, Moderate, Severe, Life-Threatening), onset date, and any additional notes. Each allergy entry is timestamped and linked to the provider who documented it, creating a critical safety record for the resident. Accurate allergy documentation for IL residents is essential because even though they are more independent, the facility may still manage medications, prepare meals, or respond to medical emergencies where allergy awareness is critical. The allergy data is prominently displayed in the resident charting header for quick visibility during any clinical interaction. Without this feature, providers and staff would lack awareness of resident allergies, creating significant safety risks in medication administration, dietary management, and emergency response.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Add Allergy" and enters the allergen name, type, and severity and clicks Save, Then the system must create the allergy record and add it to the Allergies List.
   Else if the allergen name is missing, Then the system must display "Allergen name is required".

2. If the provider selects severity as "Life-Threatening", Then the system must display a prominent visual indicator (e.g., red badge) in the charting header.
   Else if severity is "Mild", Then a standard indicator must be shown.

3. If the provider enters an allergen that matches an existing allergy for the same resident, Then the system must warn about a potential duplicate entry.
   Else if the allergen is unique, Then the system must proceed without warning.

4. If the allergy record is saved, Then it must appear in both the Allergies List and the resident charting header.

5. If the provider enters a reaction description exceeding the character limit, Then the system must display a warning.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Allergy" opens the form with fields for allergen name, type, reaction, severity, onset date, and notes.
2. Verify that adding a drug allergy (e.g., Penicillin, Drug type, Hives reaction, Severe) saves correctly.
3. Verify that the allergy appears in the Allergies List with all entered details.
4. Verify that the allergy is displayed in the resident charting header.
5. Verify that multiple allergies can be added for the same resident.

Negative:
6. Verify that saving without an allergen name displays a validation error.
7. Verify that adding a duplicate allergen (same name for same resident) triggers a warning.
8. Verify that selecting no severity and saving displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the allergy type dropdown includes Drug, Food, Environmental, and Other options.
10. Verify the severity dropdown includes Mild, Moderate, Severe, and Life-Threatening options.
11. Verify Life-Threatening allergies display a prominent visual indicator in the charting header.
12. Verify the form layout is clean and organized with clear labels.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify behavior when adding an allergy with minimal data (allergen name and type only).
14. Verify that adding an allergy for a resident in the process of transfer is handled correctly.

Security:
15. Verify that only authorized providers can add allergy information.
16. Verify that allergy records cannot be deleted, only deactivated by authorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Add Allergy Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving an allergy record completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to edit allergy information for an IL resident.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to edit existing allergy information for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the Allergies List on the IL resident charting screen, the provider selects an allergy entry and clicks "Edit" to open the Edit Allergy Form pre-populated with the current allergen name, allergy type, reaction description, severity, onset date, and notes. The provider can update any field to correct errors, add newly discovered reaction details, or change the severity level based on updated clinical assessments. Editing allergy information is critical because allergy profiles can change over time — new reactions may be observed, severity levels may need upgrading after an adverse event, or initially recorded allergens may need correction. The system maintains a full audit trail of all allergy modifications for regulatory compliance and patient safety tracking. Without this capability, outdated or incorrect allergy information would persist in the resident record, potentially leading to unsafe medication or dietary decisions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Edit" on an allergy entry, Then the system must open the Edit Form pre-populated with existing values.

2. If the provider modifies fields and clicks Save, Then the system must update the record and create an audit trail entry with original and new values.
   Else if no changes were made, Then the system must close without creating an audit entry.

3. If the provider upgrades severity to "Life-Threatening", Then the charting header must update the visual indicator to a prominent alert (e.g., red badge).
   Else if severity is downgraded, Then the indicator must reflect the lower level.

4. If the provider clears the allergen name, Then the system must display "Allergen name is required" and prevent saving.

5. If the provider clicks Cancel after modifications, Then the system must prompt to discard or continue editing.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Edit" opens the form pre-populated with current allergy data.
2. Verify that changing the severity and saving updates the record correctly.
3. Verify that updating the reaction description is saved and reflected in the list.
4. Verify the audit trail captures the original and modified values.

Negative:
5. Verify that clearing the allergen name and saving displays a validation error.
6. Verify that changing the allergen name to match an existing allergy triggers a duplicate warning.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the Edit Form layout matches the Add Form with pre-populated fields.
8. Verify Save and Cancel buttons are clearly visible.
9. Verify the charting header updates immediately after saving a severity change.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify behavior when two providers edit the same allergy entry simultaneously.
11. Verify that editing does not change the original recording timestamp.
12. Verify behavior when editing an allergy immediately after creation.

Security:
13. Verify that only authorized providers can edit allergy information.
14. Verify that all allergy edits are immutably logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Edit Allergy Form loads with pre-populated data within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving edited allergy information completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view list of allergy information for an IL resident.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view a comprehensive list of all documented allergies for an Independent Living (IL) Resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the IL resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Allergies section to see a tabular list with columns for allergen name, allergy type (Drug, Food, Environmental, Other), reaction description, severity (Mild, Moderate, Severe, Life-Threatening), onset date, and documented by (provider name). The list shows both active and inactive allergies with clear visual differentiation, and supports filtering by allergy type and severity level. This consolidated allergy view is a critical patient safety feature as it provides immediate visibility into all known allergens for the IL resident, supporting safe medication prescribing, dietary planning, and emergency response. The provider can click on individual allergy entries to view full details or initiate edits directly from this list. Without a structured allergy list, providers and staff risk missing critical allergy information, which could result in adverse reactions or medical emergencies.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Allergies section for an IL Resident, Then the system must display all documented allergies.
   Else if no allergies are documented, Then the system must display "No allergies documented" with an option to add allergies.

2. If the provider filters by allergy type, Then only allergies of that type must be displayed.
   Else if no allergies match the filter, Then "No allergies found for the selected type" must be displayed.

3. If the provider filters by severity "Life-Threatening", Then only life-threatening allergies must be shown.

4. If the provider clicks on an allergy row, Then the full detail view must be displayed.

5. If the list contains both active and inactive allergies, Then they must be visually differentiated (e.g., inactive shown in gray or in a separate section).
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Allergies section displays a list of all documented allergies for the IL resident.
2. Verify correct columns: allergen name, type, reaction, severity, onset date, documented by.
3. Verify filtering by allergy type (Drug, Food, Environmental) returns correct results.
4. Verify filtering by severity shows only allergies of the selected severity.
5. Verify clicking on an allergy row displays full details.

Negative:
6. Verify "No allergies documented" is shown when no allergy records exist.
7. Verify that filtering with no matching results shows an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the table is formatted with clear headers and readable data.
9. Verify Life-Threatening severity allergies are highlighted with a prominent visual indicator (e.g., red text or icon).
10. Verify active and inactive allergies are visually differentiated.
11. Verify the "Add Allergy" button is accessible from the list view.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify the list displays correctly with only one allergy entry.
13. Verify behavior when the resident has allergies marked as both active and inactive.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized providers can view the IL resident allergy list.
15. Verify the allergy list does not expose data from other residents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Allergies List loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify filtering by allergy type returns results within 1 second.

User Story: As caregivers, I should be able to log behavior events such as wandering or aggression
Description of User Story: This story enables caregivers to log behavior events for Memory Care residents, such as wandering, aggression, agitation, sundowning, repetitive behaviors, or verbal outbursts, within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident charting screen, the caregiver navigates to the Behavior Tracking module and clicks "Log Behavior Event" to open a form where they record the behavior type, date/time of occurrence, duration, triggers (if identified), location within the facility, interventions applied, and outcome notes. Each behavior event is timestamped and linked to the caregiver who documented it, building a comprehensive behavioral history for the resident. For Memory Care residents, behavior tracking is a regulatory requirement and a clinical necessity, as it helps identify patterns, evaluate the effectiveness of interventions, and adjust care plans to reduce behavioral incidents. The logged events are accessible to the entire care team, physicians, and family members (where authorized) for coordinated behavior management. Without this feature, behavioral events would go undocumented, preventing pattern analysis and making it impossible to demonstrate regulatory compliance for behavior management in Memory Care.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the caregiver clicks "Log Behavior Event" and fills required fields (behavior type, date/time) and clicks Save, Then the system must create the behavior event record.
   Else if behavior type is not selected, Then the system must display "Behavior type is required".

2. If the caregiver selects "Aggression" as behavior type, Then the system must flag the event as high-priority in the behavior log.
   Else if a lower-risk behavior is selected (e.g., Repetitive behavior), Then the event must be logged without high-priority flagging.

3. If the caregiver enters trigger information, Then the system must store it with the event for pattern analysis.
   Else if no trigger is identified, Then the system must allow saving with trigger marked as "Unknown".

4. If the caregiver records an intervention applied, Then the system must link the intervention to the behavior event.

5. If multiple behavior events of the same type are logged within 24 hours, Then the system must flag this as an escalation pattern for clinical review.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Log Behavior Event" opens the form with behavior type, date/time, duration, triggers, location, interventions, and outcome notes fields.
2. Verify that logging a "Wandering" event with all details saves correctly.
3. Verify that logging an "Aggression" event flags it as high-priority.
4. Verify that the behavior event appears in the Behavior Events List after saving.
5. Verify that trigger and intervention information are correctly stored and displayed.

Negative:
6. Verify that saving without selecting a behavior type displays a validation error.
7. Verify that entering a future date/time for the behavior event is rejected or flagged.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the behavior type dropdown includes Wandering, Aggression, Agitation, Sundowning, Repetitive Behavior, and Verbal Outburst.
9. Verify the form layout is organized with clear sections for event details, triggers, and interventions.
10. Verify the date/time field auto-populates with the current date and time.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify that logging multiple behavior events in quick succession for the same resident is handled correctly.
12. Verify behavior when logging an event with minimal data (behavior type and date/time only).
13. Verify that multiple events of the same type within 24 hours triggers an escalation flag.

Security:
14. Verify that only authorized caregivers and clinical staff can log behavior events.
15. Verify that behavior event records cannot be deleted, only deactivated by authorized supervisors.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Log Behavior Event Form loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that saving a behavior event completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As providers, I should be able to record MMSE or memory assessments for residents
Description of User Story: This story enables providers to record Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) or other memory assessment scores for Memory Care residents within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. From the resident charting screen, the provider navigates to the Cognitive Assessment module and clicks "Record Assessment" to open a form where they can enter the assessment type (MMSE, MoCA, Clock Drawing Test, or other standardized tools), assessment date, total score, sub-scores for individual domains (orientation, registration, attention, recall, language), and clinical notes. Each assessment is timestamped, linked to the administering provider, and stored in the resident cognitive history for longitudinal tracking.
