   Else if no facilities are configured, Then the system must display "No facilities configured. Please contact the administrator."

2. If the provider clicks on a facility row, Then the system must expand the row to show program-level census breakdowns.
   Else if the facility has no active programs, Then the system must display "No active programs for this facility."

3. If a facility or program utilization reaches 90% or higher, Then the system must highlight the row with a warning color indicator (amber at 90%, red at 100%).
   Else if utilization is below 90%, Then the row must display with a standard green indicator.

4. If the provider clicks the Refresh button, Then the system must reload all census data and display the updated counts within 2 seconds.

5. If a new patient is admitted or discharged, Then the census dashboard must reflect the change on the next auto-refresh cycle.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Live Census dashboard loads with census data for all configured facilities showing occupied count, capacity, and utilization percentage.
2. Verify that clicking on a facility row expands to show program-level census (ADH, ALF, Respite, etc.).
3. Verify that clicking on a program row expands to show level-of-care breakdowns with occupied count, capacity, and available slots.
4. Verify that a new admission updates the census count on the next auto-refresh cycle.
5. Verify that a discharge updates the census count on the next auto-refresh cycle.
6. Verify that clicking the Refresh button immediately updates all census data.

Negative:
7. Verify that the dashboard displays "No facilities configured" when no facilities exist in the system.
8. Verify that a facility with no active programs shows "No active programs for this facility" when expanded.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify that utilization percentage is displayed as a color-coded progress bar (green < 90%, amber at 90%, red at 100%).
10. Verify the facility-level rows are expandable/collapsible with a clear expand/collapse icon.
11. Verify the dashboard layout is responsive and displays correctly on tablet-sized screens.
12. Verify the Refresh button displays a spinning indicator while data is being refreshed.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify census accuracy when a patient is transferred between programs within the same facility.
14. Verify dashboard behavior when the auto-refresh encounters a network timeout — it should retain the last known data with a "Last updated" timestamp.
15. Verify that the census correctly handles a facility at exactly 100% capacity.

Security:
16. Verify that providers can only see census data for facilities they are authorized to access.
17. Verify that the census API endpoint does not return data for unauthorized facilities when manipulated.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Live Census dashboard loads with data for 10+ facilities within 3 seconds.
2. Verify that the manual Refresh operation completes and updates all census data within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to track attendance and utilization metrics including average daily attendance, capacity percentage, and no-show rates.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track key utilization metrics including average daily attendance (ADA), capacity utilization percentage, and no-show rates through a dedicated Utilization Metrics dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard displays these metrics as summary cards with trend indicators, supported by interactive charts showing daily, weekly, and monthly trends over a configurable date range. Average daily attendance is calculated as the total patient-days divided by the number of operating days, capacity percentage reflects actual attendance against total program capacity, and no-show rate measures scheduled-but-unattended visits as a percentage of total scheduled visits. The provider can filter metrics by facility, program, and date range to drill into specific areas of interest. These utilization metrics are critical for identifying underperforming programs, optimizing scheduling, and justifying staffing levels to regulatory bodies. Without centralized utilization tracking, providers would rely on manual calculations from raw attendance logs, making it difficult to spot trends and respond proactively to capacity issues.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Utilization Metrics dashboard, Then the system must display summary cards for ADA, Capacity Percentage, and No-Show Rate with current values and trend indicators.
   Else if no attendance data exists for the current period, Then the system must display "No utilization data available for the selected period."

2. If the provider selects a date range, Then all metrics and charts must recalculate and display data for that range.
   Else if the selected date range has no data, Then the charts must display an empty state with a message.

3. If the no-show rate exceeds the configured threshold, Then the No-Show Rate card must be highlighted with a warning indicator (amber or red).
   Else if the no-show rate is within acceptable limits, Then the card must display with a standard green indicator.

4. If the provider applies facility and program filters, Then the metrics must recalculate for only the selected filters.

5. If the provider clicks a data point on the trend chart, Then a tooltip must display the exact values for that date.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Utilization Metrics dashboard loads with summary cards displaying ADA, Capacity Percentage, and No-Show Rate for the default period.
2. Verify that selecting a date range of "Last 30 Days" updates all metrics and charts to reflect that period.
3. Verify that filtering by a specific facility and program recalculates all metrics for that selection.
4. Verify the trend chart displays daily data points for ADA over the selected date range.
5. Verify that a no-show rate above 15% highlights the No-Show Rate card with a warning indicator.
6. Verify that clicking a data point on the chart shows a tooltip with the exact date, ADA, capacity percentage, and no-show count.

Negative:
7. Verify that selecting a date range with no attendance data displays "No utilization data available for the selected period."
8. Verify that selecting an invalid date range (end date before start date) shows a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the summary cards display trend arrows (up/down) compared to the previous period.
10. Verify the trend chart supports toggle between daily, weekly, and monthly views.
11. Verify the filter dropdowns for Facility and Program are clearly labeled and functional.
12. Verify the dashboard layout adjusts responsively on different screen sizes.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify metric calculations when a facility had zero operating days during a selected period (e.g., holiday closure).
14. Verify ADA calculation accuracy when the same patient checks in and out multiple times in one day.
15. Verify no-show rate calculation when all scheduled patients attend (rate should be 0%).

Security:
16. Verify that utilization data is restricted to the provider's authorized facilities only.
17. Verify that the metrics API does not expose patient-level data in the response payload.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Utilization Metrics dashboard loads with 90 days of data within 4 seconds.
2. Verify that changing date range filters recalculates and refreshes metrics within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to monitor staffing levels compared to census with metrics like hours per patient day to ensure appropriate care ratios.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to monitor and compare staffing levels against patient census through a Staffing vs Census Ratios dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard presents key metrics including Hours Per Patient Day (HPPD), staff-to-patient ratio, and total nursing hours segmented by shift and role (RN, LPN, CNA). The provider can view these ratios against the current census and compare them to regulatory minimum requirements and internal benchmarks displayed as reference lines on charts. A compliance indicator highlights whether current staffing meets state-mandated minimum ratios for each level of care. This visibility is essential for ensuring patient safety, meeting regulatory requirements, managing labor costs, and making real-time staffing adjustments when census fluctuates. Without this dashboard, providers would need to manually calculate staffing ratios from time-clock data and census records, risking non-compliance with state staffing mandates.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Staffing vs Census Ratios dashboard, Then the system must display HPPD, staff-to-patient ratio, and total nursing hours by role.
   Else if no staffing data is available, Then the system must display "No staffing data available for the selected criteria."

2. If the current staffing ratio meets or exceeds the regulatory minimum, Then the compliance indicator must display a green "Compliant" status.
   Else if the staffing ratio falls below the regulatory minimum, Then the compliance indicator must display a red "Non-Compliant" warning.

3. If the provider filters by a specific shift, Then the metrics must recalculate for that shift only.
   Else if no shift filter is selected, Then the metrics must display aggregate data across all shifts.

4. If the provider selects a date range, Then the dashboard must display historical trend data for staffing vs census over that period.

5. If staffing data is unavailable for certain roles on a given day, Then the system must display "N/A" for those role-specific metrics rather than zero.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Staffing vs Census dashboard loads with HPPD, staff-to-patient ratio, and total nursing hours by role (RN, LPN, CNA).
2. Verify the staffing ratio chart displays the current ratio with the regulatory minimum as a reference line.
3. Verify the compliance indicator shows green "Compliant" when the staffing ratio meets or exceeds the regulatory minimum.
4. Verify the compliance indicator shows red "Non-Compliant" when the staffing ratio falls below the regulatory minimum.
5. Verify filtering by Day shift recalculates all metrics for Day shift only.
6. Verify selecting a date range displays historical staffing-to-census trends.

Negative:
7. Verify that the dashboard displays "No staffing data available for the selected criteria" when no data exists for the selected filters.
8. Verify that missing role data for a specific day shows "N/A" instead of zero.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the compliance indicator is prominently displayed at the top of the dashboard with clear Compliant/Non-Compliant labeling.
10. Verify the staffing ratio chart includes a legend differentiating actual ratio from regulatory minimum.
11. Verify the shift filter (Day, Evening, Night) is accessible via toggle buttons or dropdown.
12. Verify the role breakdown (RN, LPN, CNA) is displayed in a structured table or segmented bar chart.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify HPPD calculation accuracy when census changes multiple times during a single day.
14. Verify behavior when a facility has zero census on a given day — HPPD should not cause a division-by-zero error.
15. Verify that overtime hours are correctly included in the total nursing hours calculation.

Security:
16. Verify that only authorized users with staffing management permissions can access the Staffing vs Census dashboard.
17. Verify that the staffing API does not expose individual employee payroll details in the response.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Staffing vs Census dashboard loads with 30 days of data for a multi-program facility within 4 seconds.
2. Verify that applying shift and date range filters recalculates metrics within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to analyze incident rates and trends for falls, medication errors, and behavioral incidents over time.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to analyze incident rates and identify trends for key incident types including falls, medication errors, and behavioral incidents through an Incident Rate Trending dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard presents incident counts and rates (per 1,000 patient days) in both summary cards and interactive trend charts, allowing the provider to visualize incident patterns over configurable time periods (weekly, monthly, quarterly). The provider can filter by incident type, facility, program, severity level, and date range to drill into specific areas of concern. Trend lines with moving averages help identify whether incidents are increasing, decreasing, or stable, and threshold-based alerts flag when incident rates exceed acceptable benchmarks. This trending capability is critical for quality improvement programs, state regulatory compliance reporting, and identifying systemic issues that require corrective action. Without trend analysis, providers would only react to individual incidents rather than proactively addressing patterns that indicate broader care quality concerns.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Incident Rate Trending dashboard, Then the system must display summary cards and trend charts for falls, medication errors, and behavioral incidents.
   Else if no incident data exists for the selected period, Then the system must display "No incident data available for the selected period."

2. If the incident rate for any category exceeds the configured threshold, Then the corresponding summary card must be highlighted with a warning indicator.
   Else if rates are within acceptable limits, Then the cards must display with standard styling.

3. If the provider selects a specific incident type filter, Then the dashboard must update to show only that incident type.
   Else if "All Types" is selected, Then the dashboard must show all incident categories together.

4. If the provider clicks on a data point, Then a tooltip must display date, incident count, rate per 1,000 patient days, and severity breakdown.

5. If the trend shows a consistent increase over three consecutive periods, Then the system must display a trending-up alert icon next to the relevant metric.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Incident Rate Trending dashboard loads with summary cards for total incidents, falls rate, medication error rate, and behavioral incident rate.
2. Verify the trend chart displays separate trend lines for falls, medication errors, and behavioral incidents.
3. Verify filtering by "Falls" updates the chart and cards to show only fall-related data.
4. Verify that an incident rate exceeding the threshold highlights the card with a warning indicator.
5. Verify that selecting a quarterly date range recalculates all metrics for that quarter.
6. Verify clicking a chart data point shows a tooltip with date, count, rate, and severity breakdown.

Negative:
7. Verify the dashboard displays "No incident data available for the selected period" when no incidents exist in the selected range.
8. Verify that selecting an invalid date range (end before start) shows a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the trend chart uses distinct colors for each incident type (e.g., blue for falls, orange for medication errors, red for behavioral).
10. Verify the chart includes a moving average line for smoothing trend visibility.
11. Verify the filter panel with incident type, facility, program, and severity dropdowns is clearly laid out.
12. Verify the summary cards show an upward or downward arrow based on the trend direction compared to the previous period.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify incident rate calculation when the census is very low (e.g., 1-2 patients) — rates per 1,000 patient days should still compute correctly.
14. Verify behavior when a single day has an unusually high spike in incidents — the moving average should smooth it appropriately.
15. Verify that backdated incidents entered after the reporting period are correctly reflected in historical trend data.

Security:
16. Verify that incident data is restricted to the provider's authorized facilities.
17. Verify that the incident trending API does not expose patient-identifiable information in the aggregate response.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Incident Rate Trending dashboard loads with 12 months of data within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that changing filters recalculates and re-renders the trend chart within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to track rates of hospitalization and emergency room usage to measure care effectiveness and identify high-risk members.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track hospitalization and emergency room (ER) visit rates among their patient population through a dedicated Hospitalization & ER Tracking dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard displays key metrics including hospitalization rate, ER visit rate, readmission rate (within 30 days), average length of hospital stay, and top reasons for hospitalization and ER visits. A high-risk member panel identifies patients with frequent hospitalizations or ER visits, enabling proactive care interventions. The provider can filter by facility, program, date range, and diagnosis category to analyze patterns across different patient populations. Tracking these metrics is essential for demonstrating care quality to payers and regulatory agencies, reducing preventable hospitalizations, and identifying members who may benefit from additional care coordination. Without this tracking, providers would lack visibility into downstream acute care utilization, making it impossible to measure the effectiveness of their community-based services.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Hospitalization & ER Tracking dashboard, Then the system must display summary cards for hospitalization rate, ER visit rate, readmission rate, and average length of stay.
   Else if no hospitalization or ER data exists, Then the system must display "No hospitalization or ER data available for the selected period."

2. If the High-Risk Members panel identifies patients with 2+ hospitalizations or ER visits in 90 days, Then it must list those patients ranked by frequency.
   Else if no patients meet the high-risk criteria, Then the panel must display "No high-risk members identified for the selected period."

3. If the provider clicks on a high-risk patient, Then the system must display a timeline of all hospitalizations and ER visits.

4. If the provider filters by diagnosis category, Then the dashboard must recalculate all metrics for that diagnosis only.
   Else if no data matches the diagnosis filter, Then the dashboard must display "No data found for the selected diagnosis."

5. If the 30-day readmission rate exceeds the configured benchmark, Then the readmission rate card must be highlighted with a warning indicator.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the dashboard loads with summary cards for hospitalization rate, ER visit rate, 30-day readmission rate, and average length of stay.
2. Verify the trend chart displays monthly hospitalization and ER visit trends.
3. Verify the High-Risk Members panel lists patients with 2+ hospitalizations or ER visits in the last 90 days.
4. Verify clicking a high-risk patient displays their hospitalization and ER visit timeline.
5. Verify the Top Reasons chart shows the most common reasons for hospitalization and ER visits as a ranked bar chart.
6. Verify filtering by diagnosis category updates all metrics and charts.

Negative:
7. Verify that the dashboard displays "No hospitalization or ER data available" when no data exists for the selected period.
8. Verify that the High-Risk Members panel displays "No high-risk members identified" when no patients meet the criteria.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the summary cards display rate values with trend arrows compared to the previous period.
10. Verify the High-Risk Members panel is scrollable when more than 10 patients are listed.
11. Verify the Top Reasons bar chart is horizontally oriented with readable labels.
12. Verify the patient timeline view uses a visual timeline with icons distinguishing hospitalizations from ER visits.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify that a patient with both hospitalization and ER visits on the same day is counted correctly in both categories.
14. Verify readmission rate calculation when a patient is readmitted exactly on the 30th day.
15. Verify behavior when a patient is transferred from ER to hospitalization — it should count as one hospitalization event, not both.

Security:
16. Verify that only authorized providers can view hospitalization and ER data for their facility's patients.
17. Verify that the High-Risk Members panel does not expose patient data to users without clinical access permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Hospitalization & ER Tracking dashboard loads with 12 months of data within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that the High-Risk Members panel populates within 3 seconds for a facility with 500+ patients.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to track progress toward service plan goals and measure outcomes to demonstrate care effectiveness.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track patient progress toward individualized service plan goals and measure care outcomes through a Service Plan Outcomes dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard presents an aggregated view of goal achievement rates across the patient population, broken down by goal category (e.g., ADL improvement, behavioral health, mobility, medication management). For each goal category, the provider can see the percentage of patients meeting, partially meeting, or not meeting their goals, along with trend data over time. The provider can drill down to individual patient-level goal tracking to see specific goal descriptions, target dates, current status, and progress notes from clinical staff. This outcomes tracking is critical for demonstrating care effectiveness to payers, families, and regulatory agencies, and for identifying areas where care plans need adjustment. Without aggregated outcome tracking, providers would need to review each patient's service plan individually, making it impossible to identify program-level trends or demonstrate overall care quality.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Service Plan Outcomes dashboard, Then the system must display aggregated goal achievement rates by category.
   Else if no service plan data exists, Then the system must display "No service plan outcomes data available."

2. If the provider clicks on a goal category, Then the system must display a list of patients with individual goal details, target dates, statuses, and progress notes.
   Else if no patients have goals in that category, Then the system must display "No patients with goals in this category."

3. If the provider applies facility and program filters, Then the metrics must recalculate for the selected filters only.

4. If a goal category shows more than 30% of patients "Not Meeting" goals, Then the category card must be highlighted with a warning indicator.
   Else if the "Not Meeting" percentage is below 30%, Then the card must display with standard styling.

5. If the provider selects a date range, Then the trend chart must show goal achievement progress over that period.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Service Plan Outcomes dashboard loads with aggregated goal achievement rates by category (ADL improvement, behavioral health, mobility, medication management).
2. Verify each category shows percentage breakdown: Meeting, Partially Meeting, Not Meeting.
3. Verify clicking a goal category shows a list of patients with goal descriptions, target dates, statuses, and progress notes.
4. Verify filtering by facility and program recalculates goal achievement metrics.
5. Verify the trend chart displays goal achievement improvement or decline over the selected date range.
6. Verify that updating a patient's goal status reflects in the aggregated dashboard metrics after refresh.

Negative:
7. Verify the dashboard displays "No service plan outcomes data available" when no data exists.
8. Verify that clicking a category with no patient goals shows "No patients with goals in this category."

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify goal achievement percentages are displayed as stacked bar charts or donut charts for each category.
10. Verify the patient drill-down list is sortable by goal status and target date.
11. Verify that categories with more than 30% "Not Meeting" are visually highlighted with a warning color.
12. Verify the trend chart uses clear labeling and accessible color schemes.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify goal achievement calculation when a patient has multiple goals in the same category with different statuses.
14. Verify behavior when a patient's service plan goal target date has passed without a status update.
15. Verify dashboard accuracy when new goals are added to a patient's service plan mid-period.

Security:
16. Verify that providers can only view service plan outcomes for patients within their authorized facilities.
17. Verify that the patient drill-down does not expose clinical notes to users without appropriate clinical permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Service Plan Outcomes dashboard loads with data for 500+ patients within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that drilling down into a goal category with 100+ patients loads the list within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to monitor charge volumes, claims submitted, and payments received segmented by payer and program.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to monitor financial volumes across the billing lifecycle — charges generated, claims submitted, and payments received — through a Charge/Claim/Payment Volumes dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard displays summary metrics for total charges, total claims submitted, total payments received, and the variance between charges and payments, all segmentable by payer (Medicaid, Medicare, Private Pay, Insurance) and program (ADH, ALF, Respite). Interactive bar charts and line graphs visualize these volumes over configurable time periods (monthly, quarterly, yearly), allowing the provider to identify trends and anomalies in billing performance. A payer mix analysis section shows the distribution of charges and payments by payer type as a pie or donut chart. This financial volume monitoring is essential for revenue cycle management, identifying payer-specific issues (e.g., low reimbursement rates or high denial rates), and forecasting revenue. Without centralized volume tracking, providers would rely on separate reports from billing systems, making it difficult to correlate charges, claims, and payments across payers and programs.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Charge/Claim/Payment Volumes dashboard, Then the system must display summary cards for Total Charges, Total Claims, Total Payments, and Variance.
   Else if no billing data exists, Then the system must display "No billing volume data available for the selected period."

2. If the provider filters by a specific payer, Then all metrics must recalculate for that payer only.
   Else if "All Payers" is selected, Then metrics must show aggregate data across all payers.

3. If the provider filters by a specific program, Then all metrics must recalculate for that program only.

4. If the Charge-to-Payment Variance exceeds a configured threshold (e.g., variance > 20%), Then the variance card must be highlighted with a warning indicator.
   Else if the variance is within acceptable range, Then the card must display with standard styling.

5. If the provider selects a date range, Then the volume trend chart and summary cards must update for that period.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the dashboard loads with summary cards for Total Charges, Total Claims Submitted, Total Payments Received, and Charge-to-Payment Variance.
2. Verify the volume trend chart displays monthly charge, claim, and payment data as grouped bars or lines.
3. Verify filtering by Medicaid payer updates all metrics to show only Medicaid-related volumes.
4. Verify filtering by ADH program recalculates all metrics for ADH only.
5. Verify the Payer Mix donut chart displays percentage distribution by payer type.
6. Verify selecting a yearly date range shows 12 months of charge, claim, and payment volumes.

Negative:
7. Verify the dashboard shows "No billing volume data available" when no data exists for the selected period.
8. Verify that selecting conflicting filters (e.g., a payer not associated with a program) returns no results with an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the volume trend chart uses distinct colors for charges (blue), claims (green), and payments (purple).
10. Verify the Payer Mix donut chart displays payer percentages and totals on hover.
11. Verify the variance card shows a positive or negative indicator based on whether payments exceed or fall short of charges.
12. Verify the filter dropdowns for Payer and Program are clearly labeled with a default "All" option.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify volume calculations when a claim spans two programs due to a patient transfer mid-month.
14. Verify accuracy when payments are received for claims submitted in a previous reporting period.
15. Verify that voided or reversed charges are excluded from the total charge volume.

Security:
16. Verify that financial volume data is restricted to users with billing or financial reporting permissions.
17. Verify that the billing volume API does not expose individual patient billing details in aggregate responses.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Charge/Claim/Payment Volumes dashboard loads with 12 months of data across multiple payers within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that payer and program filter changes recalculate and refresh the dashboard within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to track accounts receivable aging trends and cash collections over time to monitor financial health.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to track accounts receivable (AR) aging trends and cash collection performance over time through a Cash Collection Trends dashboard in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard displays AR aging buckets (Current, 30 days, 60 days, 90 days, 120+ days) with outstanding balances in each bucket, along with a trend chart showing how the AR distribution has shifted over time. Cash collection metrics include total collections, collection rate (collections as a percentage of charges), and days sales outstanding (DSO). The provider can segment these metrics by payer type to identify which payers have the slowest payment cycles. This financial health monitoring is essential for managing cash flow, identifying collection problems early, and making informed decisions about payer contract negotiations. Without AR trend tracking, providers would lack visibility into whether their collections are improving or deteriorating, making it difficult to anticipate cash flow issues before they become critical.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to the Cash Collection Trends dashboard, Then the system must display AR aging buckets with outstanding balances, collection metrics (Total Collections, Collection Rate, DSO), and a trend chart.
   Else if no AR data exists, Then the system must display "No accounts receivable data available."

2. If the provider filters by a specific payer, Then the AR aging and collection metrics must recalculate for that payer.
   Else if "All Payers" is selected, Then metrics must display aggregate data.

3. If DSO exceeds the configured threshold, Then the DSO card must display a warning indicator.
   Else if DSO is within the acceptable range, Then it must display with standard styling.

4. If the 120+ day bucket balance exceeds 20% of total AR, Then the 120+ bucket must be highlighted with a red warning.

5. If the provider selects a date range, Then all metrics and charts must update for the selected period.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the dashboard loads with AR aging buckets (Current, 30, 60, 90, 120+ days) and outstanding balances.
2. Verify the collection metrics section shows Total Collections, Collection Rate, and DSO.
3. Verify the AR trend chart displays a stacked area chart showing aging bucket trends over time.
4. Verify filtering by a specific payer recalculates AR aging and collection metrics.
5. Verify selecting a 6-month date range updates all metrics and trend charts.
6. Verify that DSO exceeding the threshold highlights the DSO card with a warning.

Negative:
7. Verify the dashboard shows "No accounts receivable data available" when no AR data exists.
8. Verify that filtering by a payer with zero outstanding balances shows all buckets at $0.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify AR aging buckets are displayed as a horizontal stacked bar with distinct colors for each bucket.
10. Verify the Collection Rate is displayed as a percentage with a visual gauge or progress indicator.
11. Verify the DSO value is prominently displayed with a comparison to the previous period.
12. Verify the payer filter dropdown includes all active payers plus an "All Payers" default option.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify AR aging calculation when a payment is received that partially satisfies an old claim, moving it between buckets.
14. Verify that credit balances (overpayments) are correctly reflected in the AR aging display.
15. Verify DSO calculation accuracy when collections in a period exceed charges (resulting in net negative AR movement).

Security:
16. Verify that only users with financial reporting permissions can access the Cash Collection Trends dashboard.
17. Verify that the AR data API does not expose individual patient account balances in the aggregate response.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Cash Collection Trends dashboard loads with 12 months of AR data within 4 seconds.
2. Verify that payer filter changes refresh the dashboard within 2 seconds.

User Story:
As a provider I should be able to export selected patient's report to file in the following formats-
-CSV File
-PDF file
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to export a selected patient's clinical report to either CSV or PDF file format from the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The provider navigates to the Clinical Reports section, selects a patient from the patient list or search, chooses the report type (e.g., clinical summary, medication history, vitals log, assessment history), and clicks an Export button that presents format options of CSV and PDF. When CSV is selected, the system generates a comma-separated file with structured data columns suitable for spreadsheet analysis or data import into external systems. When PDF is selected, the system generates a formatted, printable document with the facility logo, patient header information, and organized report sections. This export capability is essential for sharing patient information with external providers, submitting documentation for audits, and enabling offline review of patient records. Without export functionality, providers would need to manually transcribe or screenshot clinical data, which is inefficient and introduces the risk of transcription errors.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider selects a patient and report type and clicks Export, Then the system must present CSV and PDF format options.

2. If the provider selects CSV format, Then the system must generate and download a CSV file with properly structured columns and data.
   Else if the provider selects PDF format, Then the system must generate and download a formatted PDF with facility logo, patient header, and report sections.

3. If the selected patient has data for the chosen report type, Then the export must include all relevant data.
   Else if no data exists for the chosen report type, Then the system must display "No data available to export for the selected report" and not generate a file.

4. If the export process fails due to a system error, Then the system must display an error message "Export failed. Please try again." and allow retry.

5. If the provider exports a clinical summary, Then the PDF must include sections for demographics, diagnoses, medications, allergies, and recent notes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that selecting a patient on the Clinical Reports page displays available report types.
2. Verify that clicking Export shows format options: CSV File and PDF File.
3. Verify that selecting CSV downloads a CSV file with correct column headers and data rows matching the report.
4. Verify that selecting PDF downloads a formatted PDF with facility logo, patient header, and report sections.
5. Verify that the downloaded filename follows the naming convention: PatientName_ReportType_Date.
6. Verify exporting a medication history report includes all active and discontinued medications.

Negative:
7. Verify that exporting a report for a patient with no data for that report type shows "No data available to export for the selected report."
8. Verify that clicking Export without selecting a report type shows a validation message.
9. Verify that a simulated export failure displays "Export failed. Please try again."

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the Export button is clearly visible and accessible from the report view.
11. Verify the format selection dropdown (CSV, PDF) is styled consistently with other dropdowns in the portal.
12. Verify a loading indicator is displayed during the export generation process.
13. Verify the PDF output is properly paginated for reports that span multiple pages.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify export of a report with special characters in patient name or report data — characters should be properly encoded in both CSV and PDF.
15. Verify export behavior when the patient has an extremely large report (e.g., 2 years of daily vitals) — the file should generate without timeout.
16. Verify CSV export handles commas, quotes, and newlines within data fields using proper escaping.

Security:
17. Verify that only providers with clinical access to the selected patient can export their reports.
18. Verify that the export download URL is time-limited and cannot be shared or reused after expiration.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that exporting a clinical summary report to PDF for a patient with 1 year of data completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that exporting a vitals log to CSV with 365 daily entries completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to filter each type of report based on a respective filter.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to apply contextual filters to each type of report within the Analytics & Reports section of the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Each report type presents its own set of relevant filter criteria — for example, Attendance Reports can be filtered by month, facility, and program; Incident Reports by incident type, severity, and date range; Financial Reports by payer, program, and billing period. The filter panel is displayed alongside or above the report view, with dropdown menus, date pickers, and multi-select options as appropriate for each filter type. When filters are applied, the report data refreshes to show only matching records, and a filter summary bar displays the active filters with the ability to remove individual filters or clear all. This filtering capability is essential for providers to extract meaningful insights from large datasets by narrowing down to specific facilities, programs, time periods, or categories of interest. Without report-specific filtering, providers would need to scroll through unfiltered reports to find relevant data, severely limiting the practical utility of the reporting system.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider navigates to a report type, Then the system must display filter options specific to that report type.

2. If the provider applies one or more filters, Then the report must refresh to display only matching data, and a filter summary bar must show active filters.
   Else if no data matches the applied filters, Then the report must display "No results found for the selected filters."

3. If the provider removes a single filter from the summary bar, Then the report must refresh with the remaining filters still applied.

4. If the provider clicks "Clear All Filters," Then all filters must be removed and the report must display the full unfiltered dataset.

5. If the provider applies conflicting filters (e.g., a payer not associated with the selected program), Then the system must return no results with an appropriate message rather than displaying incorrect data.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that each report type displays its own relevant filter panel with appropriate filter options.
2. Verify that filtering an Attendance Report by month, facility, and program returns only matching attendance records.
3. Verify that filtering an Incident Report by incident type and severity returns only matching incidents.
4. Verify that filtering a Financial Report by payer and billing period returns only matching financial data.
5. Verify that the filter summary bar shows all active filters with individual remove buttons.
6. Verify that clicking "Clear All Filters" resets the report to unfiltered state.
7. Verify that removing a single filter from the summary bar refreshes the report with remaining filters.

Negative:
8. Verify that applying filters with no matching data displays "No results found for the selected filters."
9. Verify that applying conflicting filters returns no results with an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the filter panel is positioned consistently across all report types (above or alongside the report).
11. Verify that date range filters use a date picker component with calendar navigation.
12. Verify that multi-select filters (e.g., multiple programs) display selected values as chips or tags.
13. Verify the filter summary bar is sticky and remains visible when scrolling through the report.
14. Verify each filter has a clear label and placeholder text indicating expected input.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that filters persist when navigating away from and back to the same report within the same session.
16. Verify behavior when a filter option (e.g., a facility) is deactivated after the provider has applied it — the filter should be auto-cleared with a notification.
17. Verify that applying a date range filter with the same start and end date returns data for only that single day.

Security:
18. Verify that filter options only include facilities and programs the provider is authorized to access.
19. Verify that manipulating filter API parameters does not expose data from unauthorized facilities or programs.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that applying filters on a report with 10,000+ records refreshes the filtered results within 3 seconds.
2. Verify that the filter panel with all dropdowns loads within 2 seconds on any report page.

User Story: As a user, I should be able to ask the chatbot questions at any time so I can get quick answers
Description of User Story: This story enables any user of the Nemi Care Facility Portal to access an AI-powered chatbot at any time from any page within the application to ask questions and receive immediate answers. The chatbot is accessible through a persistent floating chat icon positioned at the bottom-right corner of every page, which expands into a chat window when clicked. Users can type natural language questions about system functionality, policies, procedures, or general information, and the chatbot responds with contextually relevant answers based on the portal's knowledge base and configured AI model. The chat window supports a conversational thread, allowing follow-up questions within the same context, and displays a timestamp for each message. This always-available chatbot reduces the need for users to search through help documentation or contact support for common questions, improving productivity and self-service capability. Without an accessible chatbot, users would need to interrupt their workflow to find answers through traditional support channels, leading to lost productivity and slower task completion.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks the floating chat icon, Then the chat window must open with a welcome message and input field.
   Else if the chat window is already open, Then clicking the icon must minimize the chat window.

2. If the user submits a question the chatbot can answer, Then the chatbot must display a relevant response within the chat window.
   Else if the chatbot cannot determine an answer, Then it must respond with "I'm not sure about that. Would you like me to connect you with support?" and provide a link to the support channel.

3. If the user submits a follow-up question, Then the chatbot must maintain the prior conversation context for the response.

4. If the user closes and reopens the chat window within the same session, Then the previous conversation must be preserved.
   Else if the user starts a new session (e.g., logs out and back in), Then the chat window must start with a fresh conversation.

5. If the chatbot service is unavailable, Then the chat icon must display a "temporarily unavailable" tooltip and the chat window must show "The chatbot is currently unavailable. Please try again later."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the floating chat icon is visible on the bottom-right corner of the Dashboard page.
2. Verify the floating chat icon is visible on the bottom-right corner of the Analytics & Reports page.
3. Verify clicking the chat icon opens the chat window with a welcome message and input field.
4. Verify typing a question and pressing Enter sends the message and displays a chatbot response.
5. Verify typing a question and clicking the Send button sends the message and displays a response.
6. Verify the chatbot maintains context for follow-up questions within the same conversation.
7. Verify minimizing and reopening the chat window preserves the conversation thread.

Negative:
8. Verify that submitting an empty message (pressing Enter with no text) does not send a message.
9. Verify the chatbot responds with a helpful fallback message when it cannot answer a question.
10. Verify the chat window shows "The chatbot is currently unavailable" when the chatbot service is down.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the floating chat icon has a recognizable chat bubble design and is consistently positioned.
12. Verify the chat window displays user messages on the right and chatbot responses on the left with distinct styling.
13. Verify each message displays a timestamp.
14. Verify the chat window has a visible close/minimize button.
15. Verify a typing indicator is displayed while the chatbot is processing the question.

Edge Cases:
16. Verify chatbot behavior when the user submits a very long question (500+ characters) — it should either process it or display a character limit message.
17. Verify that navigating between pages while the chat window is open does not close the window or lose the conversation.
18. Verify behavior when the user rapidly sends multiple messages — all should be queued and responded to in order.

Security:
19. Verify that only authenticated users can access the chatbot.
20. Verify that the chatbot does not expose sensitive patient data in responses unless the user has appropriate access permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the chatbot responds to a general question within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the chat window opens within 1 second of clicking the floating icon.

User Story: As staff, I should be able to ask the chatbot for quick details about a resident or patient to save time during care tasks
Description of User Story: This story enables clinical and care staff to use the AI chatbot to quickly retrieve patient or resident details by asking natural language questions, eliminating the need to navigate through multiple screens during care tasks. Staff can ask questions such as "What medications is John Smith on?" or "When is Mary Johnson's next appointment?" and the chatbot retrieves the relevant information from the patient's record and presents it in a concise, readable format within the chat window. The chatbot is context-aware, meaning staff can ask follow-up questions like "Does she have any allergies?" and it will maintain reference to the previously discussed patient. Results are presented with key identifiers (patient name, DOB, room/bed number) to confirm the correct patient before displaying clinical details. This capability significantly reduces the time staff spend navigating the portal during care delivery, especially in time-sensitive situations where quick access to patient information is critical. Without this feature, staff would need to stop their care task, navigate to the patient's chart, find the relevant section, and return to their work, costing valuable time during high-volume care periods.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the staff asks a patient-specific question and the patient is uniquely identified, Then the chatbot must display a patient confirmation header (name, DOB, room/bed) followed by the requested clinical details.
   Else if the patient name matches multiple records, Then the chatbot must display a disambiguation list for the staff to select the correct patient.

2. If the staff asks a follow-up question after a patient has been identified, Then the chatbot must maintain the patient context and return information for the same patient.
   Else if the staff explicitly asks about a different patient, Then the chatbot must switch context to the new patient.

3. If the staff has clinical access permissions for the requested patient, Then the chatbot must display the requested information.
   Else if the staff does not have permissions, Then the chatbot must respond with "You do not have permission to access this information."

4. If no patient matches the provided name, Then the chatbot must respond with "No patient found matching [name]. Please verify the name and try again."

5. If the chatbot retrieves patient data successfully, Then it must display data in a concise, formatted layout within the chat window.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify asking "What medications is [Patient Name] on?" returns the patient's current medication list.
2. Verify the chatbot displays a patient confirmation header (name, DOB, room/bed) before showing clinical details.
3. Verify asking "When is [Patient Name]'s next appointment?" returns the next scheduled appointment date and time.
4. Verify asking a follow-up like "Does she have allergies?" returns allergy information for the same patient.
5. Verify asking about a different patient explicitly (e.g., "What about Jane Doe?") switches the patient context.
6. Verify asking "What room is [Patient Name] in?" returns the current room and bed assignment.

Negative:
7. Verify that asking about a patient not in the system returns "No patient found matching [name]."
8. Verify that asking for information outside the staff's access permissions returns "You do not have permission to access this information."
9. Verify that a vague question like "Tell me about the patient" without specifying a name prompts the chatbot to ask for clarification.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the patient confirmation header is visually distinct from the clinical detail response.
11. Verify that medication lists are displayed in a structured format with drug name, dosage, and frequency.
12. Verify the disambiguation list for common names displays patient name, DOB, and room number for easy selection.
13. Verify that long responses (e.g., extensive medication lists) are scrollable within the chat window.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify disambiguation when two patients share the exact same first and last name — the list should include DOB and room for differentiation.
15. Verify chatbot behavior when a patient has been recently discharged — it should indicate the patient is no longer active.
16. Verify context retention after 5+ follow-up questions about the same patient.

Security:
17. Verify that the chatbot enforces role-based access control and does not return financial or administrative data to clinical-only staff.
18. Verify that patient data returned by the chatbot is not cached in the browser or accessible in browser developer tools after the session ends.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the chatbot retrieves and displays patient medication data within 3 seconds of the query.
2. Verify the disambiguation list for a common name appears within 2 seconds.

User Story: As staff, I should be able to ask the chatbot how to complete forms or documentation
Description of User Story: This story enables staff to ask the AI chatbot for step-by-step guidance on how to complete forms, documentation, and workflows within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Staff can ask questions like "How do I fill out the admission form?" or "What fields are required on the incident report?" and the chatbot responds with clear, step-by-step instructions tailored to the specific form or documentation task. The chatbot draws its guidance from the portal's built-in help content, form field descriptions, and workflow documentation to provide accurate and context-specific instructions. Responses may include field-by-field explanations, required vs. optional field indicators, tips for common mistakes, and links to the relevant page in the portal. This self-service guidance is essential for onboarding new staff, reducing training overhead, and ensuring consistent and accurate documentation across the organization. Without in-context guidance, staff would need to consult printed manuals, training materials, or ask supervisors, all of which slow down daily operations and introduce the risk of inconsistent documentation practices.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the staff asks how to complete a recognized form, Then the chatbot must respond with numbered step-by-step instructions including field names, expected inputs, and required/optional indicators.
   Else if the form is not recognized, Then the chatbot must respond with "I couldn't find guidance for that form. Please check the form name or contact support for assistance."

2. If the staff asks about required fields for a specific form, Then the chatbot must list all required fields with brief descriptions.

3. If the staff asks a follow-up about a specific field, Then the chatbot must provide a detailed explanation for that field including valid values, formatting, and common mistakes.

4. If the chatbot response includes a link to the form page, Then clicking the link must navigate the user to the correct page in the portal.
   Else if the user is not on a page where the link is accessible, Then the chatbot must display the navigation path (e.g., "Go to Patients > Admission > New Admission Form").

5. If the staff asks about a deprecated or removed form, Then the chatbot must inform the user that the form is no longer in use and suggest the replacement form if applicable.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify asking "How do I fill out the admission form?" returns step-by-step instructions with field names and input types.
2. Verify asking "What fields are required on the incident report?" returns a list of required fields with descriptions.
3. Verify asking "How do I complete the billing setup form?" returns accurate billing form guidance.
4. Verify asking a follow-up like "What goes in the Diagnosis Code field?" returns a specific field-level explanation.
5. Verify that the chatbot response includes a clickable link to the relevant form page.
6. Verify that clicking the form page link navigates to the correct page in the portal.

Negative:
7. Verify asking about a non-existent form returns "I couldn't find guidance for that form."
8. Verify asking an unrelated question (e.g., "What's the weather?") returns a helpful redirect message such as "I can help with portal forms and documentation. What form do you need help with?"

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the step-by-step instructions are displayed as a numbered list within the chat window.
10. Verify required fields are visually distinguished (e.g., bold or marked with an asterisk) in the chatbot response.
11. Verify that clickable links in the chatbot response are styled as hyperlinks and are distinguishable from plain text.
12. Verify long instructional responses are scrollable within the chat window without layout breaking.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify chatbot guidance when a form has been recently updated with new fields — the guidance should reflect the latest version.
14. Verify chatbot behavior when asked about a form in a different module than the user is currently viewing.
15. Verify that the chatbot handles form names with abbreviations (e.g., "How do I fill out the ISP?" for Individual Service Plan).

Security:
16. Verify that the chatbot does not provide guidance on admin-only forms to non-admin staff.
17. Verify that form navigation links respect the user's role-based access and do not navigate to pages the user cannot access.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the chatbot returns form guidance within 3 seconds of the query.
2. Verify that clicking a form navigation link opens the target page within 2 seconds.

User Story: As an authorized user (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse), I should be able to view a detailed resident's care plan or daily care routine, derived from completed assessments, with full editing capabilities
Description of User Story: This story enables authorized users such as Executive Directors, Residential Care Coordinators (RCC), and Nurses to view a comprehensive and detailed care plan for a specific resident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The care plan is automatically derived from completed assessments (e.g., ADL, cognitive, behavioral) and presents daily care routines, assistance levels, scheduled tasks, staff assignments, and special instructions in a structured and readable format. Users with edit privileges can directly modify care instructions, update schedules, change staff assignments, and adjust assistance levels from within the care plan view without navigating to a separate edit screen. The care plan consolidates all assessment-driven care needs into a single unified view, providing a holistic picture of the resident's daily routine across all shifts and care categories. This feature is essential for clinical staff to quickly review and act on a resident's care requirements during shift handoffs, family conferences, and regulatory audits. Without this capability, staff would need to review multiple individual assessments to piece together a resident's daily care needs, increasing the risk of missed tasks and inconsistent care delivery.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the logged-in user has an authorized role (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse) and navigates to a resident's Care Plan tab, Then the system must display the full care plan with editing capabilities enabled.
   Else if the user does not have an authorized role (e.g., Care Staff), Then the system must display the care plan in read-only mode without edit options.

2. If the resident has completed assessments linked to care plan generation, Then the system must display the care plan with all derived routines, instructions, and schedules.
   Else if no assessments have been completed, Then the system must display a message "No assessments completed. Care plan will be generated once assessments are finalized."

3. If the authorized user modifies a care instruction and clicks Save, Then the system must persist the changes, update the care plan view, and log the modification with a timestamp and user identity.
   Else if the user attempts to save with invalid data (e.g., empty instruction field), Then the system must display a validation error and prevent the save.

4. If multiple assessments contribute to the same care category, Then the system must merge and display the most recent instructions for that category.
   Else if only one assessment covers a category, Then the system must display instructions from that single assessment.

5. If the user clicks Cancel while editing a care instruction, Then the system must revert the instruction to its last saved state without persisting any changes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that an authorized user (Executive Director) can navigate to a resident's profile and view the care plan tab displaying all care routines derived from completed assessments.
2. Verify that the care plan displays daily routines grouped by shift (Morning, Afternoon, Night) with assistance levels and staff assignments.
3. Verify that an RCC can click on a specific care instruction within the care plan and edit the assistance level successfully.
4. Verify that a Nurse can modify care instructions, change scheduled times, and reassign staff within the care plan view.
5. Verify that saving an edited care instruction updates the care plan view immediately with the new data.
6. Verify that the care plan shows the source assessment name and date for each care instruction section.

Negative:
7. Verify that a user with an unauthorized role (e.g., Care Staff) can view the care plan but cannot access editing controls.
8. Verify that attempting to save a care instruction with an empty instruction field displays a validation error.
9. Verify that when no assessments are completed for a resident, the care plan tab shows an appropriate informational message instead of an empty page.
10. Verify that editing a care instruction and clicking Cancel reverts the instruction to its original state without saving.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the care plan layout organizes routines by category (ADL, Medication, Behavioral, etc.) with clear section headers.
12. Verify that edit icons or buttons are visible next to each editable care instruction for authorized users.
13. Verify that the care plan page is scrollable and sections are collapsible for easy navigation when there are many routines.
14. Verify that assistance level indicators (Independent, Supervision, Limited Assist, Full Assist) are displayed with distinct visual styling.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that the care plan correctly updates when an assessment is re-done and produces different care instructions than the original.
16. Verify behavior when two authorized users attempt to edit the same care instruction simultaneously.
17. Verify the care plan handles a resident with a large number of routines (20+) without layout or performance degradation.

Security:
18. Verify that only authenticated users with valid sessions can access the care plan view.
19. Verify that all care plan edits are logged in the audit trail with the user identity, timestamp, and changed fields.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the care plan page loads with all derived routines and instructions within 3 seconds for a resident with 10+ completed assessments.
2. Verify that saving an edited care instruction completes and reflects the update within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a user, I should be able to download the care summary or care plan in PDF format for printing, sharing with family, or record keeping
Description of User Story: This story enables any authorized user to download the resident's care summary or full care plan as a PDF document from the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The PDF generation captures all care plan sections including daily routines, assistance levels, care instructions, medication schedules, staff assignments, and any special notes in a formatted, print-ready layout. The downloaded PDF includes the facility logo, resident identifying information (name, room number, DOB), care plan effective date, and a generated timestamp for version tracking. This feature supports critical workflows such as sharing care details with a resident's family members during care conferences, providing printed copies for regulatory audits, and maintaining offline backup records. The PDF must be generated in a clean, professional format suitable for both digital sharing and physical printing on standard letter-size paper. Without this feature, staff would need to manually transcribe or screenshot care plan information for external sharing, which is time-consuming and error-prone.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks the "Download PDF" button and the resident has a populated care plan, Then the system must generate and download a formatted PDF with all care plan data.
   Else if the care plan has no data (no assessments completed), Then the system must display a notification "No care plan data available for PDF generation" and not generate a file.

2. If the PDF is generated successfully, Then the file must include the facility logo, resident details (name, room, DOB), care plan effective date, all routines and instructions, and a generation timestamp.
   Else if the PDF generation fails due to a server error, Then the system must display an error message "PDF generation failed. Please try again." and log the error.

3. If the care plan content spans multiple pages, Then the PDF must include proper page breaks, page numbers, and repeated headers on each page.
   Else if the content fits on a single page, Then the PDF must render on a single page with appropriate margins.

4. If the user clicks the download button while a PDF is already being generated, Then the system must ignore the duplicate request and continue the current generation.
   Else if no generation is in progress, Then the system must initiate a new PDF generation.

5. If the user has valid authentication and authorization, Then the PDF download must proceed.
   Else if the session has expired, Then the system must redirect the user to the login page.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Download PDF" on a populated care plan generates and downloads a PDF file successfully.
2. Verify the downloaded PDF contains the facility logo, resident name, room number, DOB, and care plan effective date.
3. Verify the PDF includes all care routines grouped by category with assistance levels, care instructions, and staff assignments.
4. Verify the PDF includes a generation timestamp in the footer or header.
5. Verify the PDF renders correctly when printed on standard letter-size paper with proper margins and no content cutoff.

Negative:
6. Verify that attempting to download a PDF when no care plan data exists displays an appropriate message and does not generate a file.
7. Verify that if PDF generation fails due to a server error, the user sees an error notification and can retry.
8. Verify that clicking the download button multiple times rapidly does not generate duplicate PDF files.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the "Download PDF" button is clearly visible and accessible on the care plan view.
10. Verify a loading spinner or progress indicator is displayed while the PDF is being generated.
11. Verify the download button is disabled during PDF generation and re-enabled after completion.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify PDF generation for a care plan with an extremely large number of routines (30+) produces a complete multi-page document.
13. Verify PDF generation when care instructions contain special characters, accented letters, or non-English text.
14. Verify behavior when the user's browser blocks the file download — the system should provide an alternative download link.

Security:
15. Verify that the generated PDF is served over HTTPS and contains no embedded scripts or executable content.
16. Verify that only authenticated users can trigger PDF generation and that the PDF URL cannot be accessed without a valid session.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that PDF generation for a standard care plan (10-15 routines) completes and initiates download within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that PDF generation for a large care plan (30+ routines, multi-page) completes within 10 seconds.

User Story: As an authorized user (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse only), I should be able to edit care instructions in the care plan, including modifying assistance levels, care instructions, schedules, and staff assignments
Description of User Story: This story enables authorized users (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse) to directly edit care instructions within a resident's care plan in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Editable fields include assistance levels (Independent, Supervision, Limited Assist, Extensive Assist, Full Assist), free-text care instructions, task schedules (time of day, shift, frequency), and staff type assignments (Care Staff, Med Tech). When editing, the system presents the current values in editable form fields, allowing the user to modify one or more aspects of a care instruction and save changes in a single action. All edits are tracked with an audit trail recording the previous value, new value, user who made the change, and timestamp for regulatory compliance and accountability. This feature is critical for keeping care plans current as a resident's condition changes between formal reassessment periods, ensuring staff always have the most accurate care instructions. Without this capability, care plan updates would require completing a new assessment, causing delays in reflecting urgent changes to a resident's care needs.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user has an authorized role (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse) and clicks Edit on a care instruction, Then the system must display editable form fields for assistance level, care instructions, schedule, and staff assignment.
   Else if the user does not have an authorized role, Then the Edit button must not be visible or clickable.

2. If the user modifies one or more fields and clicks Save with all required fields populated, Then the system must persist all changes, update the care plan display, and create an audit trail entry.
   Else if any required field is empty upon Save, Then the system must display validation errors and prevent the save.

3. If the user changes the assistance level, Then the system must update the visual indicator for that care instruction to reflect the new level.
   Else if the assistance level remains unchanged, Then the system must retain the existing visual indicator.

4. If the user modifies the schedule to a time that conflicts with another existing routine for the same resident, Then the system must display a warning "Schedule conflict detected with [routine name]. Please adjust."
   Else if there is no conflict, Then the system must accept the new schedule.

5. If the user clicks Cancel after making edits, Then the system must discard all unsaved changes and restore the original values.
   Else if the user clicks Cancel without making any changes, Then the system must close the edit mode silently.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that an Executive Director can click Edit on a care instruction and see editable fields for assistance level, instructions, schedule, and staff assignment.
2. Verify that an RCC can change the assistance level from "Supervision" to "Extensive Assist" and save successfully.
3. Verify that a Nurse can modify the care instructions text and save the updated text.
4. Verify that changing the schedule from "Morning Shift" to "Night Shift" updates the care plan correctly.
5. Verify that reassigning staff type from "Care Staff" to "Med Tech" is saved and reflected in the care plan.
6. Verify that modifying multiple fields (assistance level, instructions, schedule, staff) simultaneously and saving persists all changes.
7. Verify that the audit trail records the old value, new value, user identity, and timestamp for each edit.

Negative:
8. Verify that saving a care instruction with an empty care instructions text field displays a validation error.
9. Verify that a Care Staff user cannot see or click the Edit button on any care instruction.
10. Verify that scheduling a routine at a time that conflicts with another existing routine triggers a conflict warning.
11. Verify that clicking Cancel after editing reverts all fields to their original values.

UI Scenarios:
12. Verify the Edit button is positioned consistently next to each care instruction for authorized users.
13. Verify the assistance level field renders as a dropdown with all valid options (Independent, Supervision, Limited Assist, Extensive Assist, Full Assist).
14. Verify the schedule fields include shift selector and time picker controls.
15. Verify Save and Cancel buttons are clearly visible and properly positioned in the edit form.

Edge Cases:
16. Verify that editing a care instruction while another authorized user is also editing the same instruction handles concurrency gracefully.
17. Verify behavior when editing a care instruction for a resident who has been discharged — the system should prevent edits or show a warning.
18. Verify that rapid sequential edits to the same care instruction (save, edit, save again) do not corrupt the data or audit trail.

Security:
19. Verify that the edit API endpoint rejects requests from users without authorized roles even if the Edit button is bypassed via direct API calls.
20. Verify that all edit operations are transmitted over HTTPS and the audit trail cannot be tampered with by end users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that clicking Edit on a care instruction loads the editable form fields within 1 second.
2. Verify that saving an edited care instruction persists and updates the UI within 2 seconds.

User Story:
As an authorized user (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse), I should be able to add new routines or care tasks by entering:
Routine/Task Name, 
Instructions, 
Category, 
Schedule (repeat every day/week/specific days, select shift, select exact time), 
Start date & end date, and 
Assign to appropriate staff type (Care Staff or Med Tech), with system preventing scheduling conflicts
Description of User Story: This story enables authorized users (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse) to add entirely new care routines or tasks to a resident's care plan within the Nemi Care Facility Portal, beyond what is auto-generated from assessments. The add routine form captures essential details: Routine/Task Name, Instructions (free-text describing how to perform the task), Category (e.g., ADL, Medication, Behavioral, Therapy), Schedule configuration (repeat frequency — daily, weekly, or specific days; shift selection; exact time), Start date, End date, and Staff Type assignment (Care Staff or Med Tech). The system validates the scheduling configuration against existing routines for the same resident and prevents conflicts by alerting the user when a time slot is already occupied by another task. This capability is essential for handling ad-hoc care needs that arise outside of formal assessments, such as temporary wound care routines, short-term therapy exercises, or time-limited behavioral interventions. Added routines integrate seamlessly into the existing care plan view alongside assessment-derived routines, maintaining a unified schedule for care staff to follow. Without this feature, staff would have no structured way to add non-assessment-based care tasks, leading to informal workarounds like paper notes or verbal instructions that are prone to being missed.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user has an authorized role (Executive Director, RCC, or Nurse) and clicks "Add New Routine", Then the system must display the Add Routine form with all required fields.
   Else if the user does not have an authorized role, Then the "Add New Routine" button must not be visible.

2. If all required fields (Name, Instructions, Category, Schedule, Start date, End date, Staff Type) are filled and Save is clicked, Then the system must create the routine and add it to the care plan.
   Else if any required field is missing, Then the system must display validation errors indicating which fields are required.

3. If the selected schedule (day, shift, time) conflicts with an existing routine for the same resident, Then the system must display a conflict warning with the name of the conflicting routine and prevent saving until resolved.
   Else if no conflict exists, Then the system must accept the schedule.

4. If the Start date is before the End date, Then the system must accept the date range.
   Else if the Start date is on or after the End date, Then the system must display a validation error "Start date must be before End date."

5. If the user selects Staff Type as "Med Tech", Then the system must ensure only Med Tech-eligible tasks can be assigned.
   Else if the user selects "Care Staff", Then the system must allow general care tasks to be assigned.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that an authorized user can click "Add New Routine" and see a form with fields for Name, Instructions, Category, Schedule, Start date, End date, and Staff Type.
2. Verify that filling all required fields and clicking Save successfully creates the routine and adds it to the care plan.
3. Verify that the newly added routine appears under the correct category in the care plan view.
4. Verify that selecting "Repeat every day" with a specific shift and time creates daily recurring entries in the schedule.
5. Verify that selecting "Repeat every week" with specific days creates weekly recurring entries on the selected days.
6. Verify that assigning the routine to "Care Staff" correctly tags the routine for Care Staff visibility.
7. Verify that assigning the routine to "Med Tech" correctly tags the routine for Med Tech visibility.

Negative:
8. Verify that saving with the Routine/Task Name field empty displays a validation error.
9. Verify that saving with no Category selected displays a validation error.
10. Verify that entering a Start date after the End date triggers a validation error.
11. Verify that selecting a schedule time that conflicts with an existing routine displays a conflict warning and prevents save.
12. Verify that an unauthorized user (e.g., Care Staff) cannot see the "Add New Routine" button.

UI Scenarios:
13. Verify the Add Routine form is organized in a logical flow from top to bottom (Name > Instructions > Category > Schedule > Dates > Staff).
14. Verify that the Category field renders as a dropdown with predefined options (ADL, Medication, Behavioral, Therapy, etc.).
15. Verify the Schedule section has clear controls for repeat frequency, shift selection, and time picker.
16. Verify date pickers for Start date and End date are properly rendered and functional.

Edge Cases:
17. Verify adding a routine with a Start date and End date on the same day is handled appropriately (single-day routine).
18. Verify behavior when adding a routine with a very long instructions text (1000+ characters).
19. Verify that adding multiple routines in quick succession does not result in duplicate entries or data loss.

Security:
20. Verify that the Add Routine API endpoint rejects requests from unauthorized roles.
21. Verify that routine data including instructions and schedules are transmitted securely over HTTPS.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Add Routine form loads within 2 seconds after clicking "Add New Routine".
2. Verify that the scheduling conflict check and save operation complete within 3 seconds.

User Story:
