As a user, I should be able to schedule or unschedule care routines and tasks by:

- Repeat (every day, week)
- Select shift
- Select exact time
Description of User Story: This story enables users to schedule or unschedule existing care routines and tasks within a resident's care plan by configuring repeat frequency, shift, and exact time parameters in the Nemi Care Facility Portal. For scheduling, the user selects a repeat pattern (every day, every week, or specific days of the week), chooses the applicable shift (Morning, Afternoon, Night), and sets the exact time for the routine to occur. For unscheduling, the user can remove the schedule from an active routine, effectively pausing it without deleting the routine definition, allowing it to be rescheduled later. The system validates that the selected schedule does not conflict with existing scheduled routines for the same resident and time slot, preventing overlapping task assignments. This flexibility is essential for managing temporary changes in care needs, such as pausing a routine during a hospital stay or adjusting schedules for seasonal daylight changes. Without this feature, staff would need to delete and recreate routines to change their schedule, losing the associated care instructions and audit history.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks "Schedule" or "Edit Schedule" on a care routine, Then the system must display the scheduling options with Repeat, Shift, and Exact time fields.
   Else if the routine is locked or associated with a completed assessment that restricts schedule changes, Then the system must display a message "This routine's schedule is managed by the linked assessment and cannot be modified directly."

2. If the user configures a valid schedule (repeat, shift, time) with no conflicts and clicks Save, Then the system must apply the schedule and update the care plan.
   Else if a scheduling conflict is detected, Then the system must display the conflict details and prevent saving.

3. If the user clicks "Unschedule" on an active routine, Then the system must remove the schedule, mark the routine as "Unscheduled", and retain the routine definition.
   Else if the routine is already unscheduled, Then the "Unschedule" option must not be available.

4. If the user selects "Repeat every week" and chooses at least one day, Then the system must accept the weekly schedule.
   Else if the user selects "Repeat every week" without choosing any day, Then the system must display a validation error "Please select at least one day for weekly repeat."

5. If the user selects "Repeat every day", Then the system must schedule the routine for all seven days of the week at the specified shift and time.
   Else if the user selects specific days, Then the system must schedule only on those selected days.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Schedule" on an unscheduled routine displays scheduling options with Repeat, Shift, and Time fields.
2. Verify that selecting "Repeat every day", "Morning Shift", and "08:00 AM" and saving schedules the routine correctly.
3. Verify that selecting "Repeat every week" with Monday, Wednesday, and Friday schedules the routine on those days only.
4. Verify that clicking "Unschedule" on an active routine removes the schedule and marks the routine as "Unscheduled".
5. Verify that an unscheduled routine retains its name, instructions, and category for future rescheduling.
6. Verify that rescheduling a previously unscheduled routine applies the new schedule correctly.

Negative:
7. Verify that scheduling a routine at a time that conflicts with another existing routine triggers a conflict warning.
8. Verify that selecting "Repeat every week" without choosing any specific day displays a validation error.
9. Verify that attempting to unschedule an already unscheduled routine does not show the Unschedule option.
10. Verify that saving a schedule without selecting a shift displays a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the scheduling interface has clearly labeled controls for Repeat frequency, Shift selection, and Time picker.
12. Verify that the Shift dropdown contains options: Morning, Afternoon, Night.
13. Verify that unscheduled routines are visually distinct from scheduled ones (e.g., grayed out or tagged as "Unscheduled").
14. Verify the time picker allows selection in appropriate increments (e.g., 15-minute intervals).

Edge Cases:
15. Verify scheduling a routine with the same time as an existing routine for a different resident does not trigger a false conflict.
16. Verify behavior when scheduling a routine that spans across a shift boundary (e.g., scheduled at shift-end time).
17. Verify that unscheduling and rescheduling a routine multiple times preserves the audit history of all schedule changes.

Security:
18. Verify that schedule modifications are logged in the audit trail with user identity and timestamp.
19. Verify that scheduling API endpoints require valid authentication and appropriate authorization.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the scheduling options load within 1 second when clicking "Schedule" or "Edit Schedule".
2. Verify that saving a schedule and refreshing the care plan view completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a user, i should be able to view added incidents details
Description of User Story: This story enables users to view the detailed information of previously reported incidents within the Nemi Care Facility Portal's Incident Management module. When a user navigates to the Incident List and selects a specific incident, the system displays the full incident record including incident description, date and time, location, severity, involved staff and residents, tags, follow-up actions, uploaded documents, and notification history. The incident details view presents all information in a read-only format organized into logical sections for quick review, supporting workflows such as incident follow-up, regulatory reporting, and quality assurance analysis. Each section of the incident details is clearly labeled and any associated documents or images are accessible via download or inline preview. This feature is essential for staff, administrators, and compliance officers to review incident records during investigations, audits, and quality improvement meetings. Without this capability, users would have no structured way to review the full context of reported incidents, making follow-up actions and compliance tracking unreliable.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks on an incident record from the Incident List, Then the system must display the full Incident Details page with all sections populated from the stored record.
   Else if the incident record cannot be loaded (e.g., deleted or corrupted), Then the system must display an error message "Unable to load incident details. Please contact support."

2. If the incident has uploaded documents, Then the Documents section must display all files with names, dates, and download/preview links.
   Else if no documents were uploaded, Then the Documents section must display "No documents attached."

3. If the incident has follow-up actions, Then the Follow-Up Actions section must display each action with description, assignee, due date, and status.
   Else if no follow-up actions exist, Then the section must display "No follow-up actions assigned."

4. If the incident has notification records, Then the Notification History section must show each notification with the notified party, method, date/time, and status.
   Else if no notifications were sent, Then the section must display "No notifications recorded."

5. If the user has permission to view incidents, Then the system must allow access to the Incident Details page.
   Else if the user lacks incident viewing permissions, Then the system must display "You do not have permission to view this incident."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking on an incident record in the Incident List opens the Incident Details page with all sections populated.
2. Verify the Incident Details page displays the incident description, date/time, location, and severity correctly.
3. Verify the Involved Persons section shows all staff and residents associated with the incident.
4. Verify the Follow-Up Actions section displays each action with description, assignee, due date, and status.
5. Verify the Documents section lists all uploaded files with download links and inline preview for images.
6. Verify the Notification History section shows all notifications sent with party name, method, date, and status.
7. Verify that clicking "Back to List" returns the user to the Incident List with previous filters preserved.

Negative:
8. Verify that attempting to view a deleted or invalid incident ID displays an appropriate error message.
9. Verify that a user without incident viewing permissions is denied access to the Incident Details page.
10. Verify that the details page handles gracefully when certain optional sections have no data (e.g., "No documents attached").

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the Incident Details page is organized into clearly labeled sections with proper headings.
12. Verify the page layout is scrollable and all sections are accessible without horizontal scrolling.
13. Verify that tags are displayed as styled badges or chips for visual clarity.
14. Verify that document file names are clickable links that trigger download or preview.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify viewing an incident with a very long description (5000+ characters) renders correctly without layout breaking.
16. Verify viewing an incident with a large number of follow-up actions (10+) renders all actions without truncation.
17. Verify viewing an incident that was created by a user who no longer exists in the system still displays all incident data correctly.

Security:
18. Verify that incident details are served over HTTPS and sensitive information is not exposed in the URL.
19. Verify that direct URL access to an incident detail page without authentication redirects to the login page.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Incident Details page loads completely within 3 seconds for an incident with multiple documents and follow-up actions.
2. Verify that the Incident List page loads within 2 seconds when there are 100+ incident records.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to report various incident types including falls, medication errors, behavioral incidents, elopement attempts, and staff injuries using standardized forms.
Description of User Story: This story enables providers to report different categories of incidents using type-specific standardized forms within the Nemi Care Facility Portal's Incident Management module. Supported incident types include Falls (with fall-specific fields like fall location, activity at time of fall, footwear, assistive device usage), Medication Errors (wrong medication, wrong dose, wrong time, wrong route, wrong patient), Behavioral Incidents (aggression type, trigger, de-escalation method), Elopement Attempts (exit point, duration missing, how found), and Staff Injuries (injury type, body part, mechanism). Each incident type form inherits common fields (date, time, location, description, severity) while adding type-specific fields that capture the detailed information required for regulatory reporting and root cause analysis. The standardized forms ensure consistent data collection across incidents, enabling meaningful trend analysis and quality improvement reporting. This feature supports compliance with state reporting requirements that mandate specific data points for each incident category. Without standardized type-specific forms, providers would use generic free-text descriptions, making it impossible to perform structured analysis or generate required regulatory reports.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider selects an incident type from the dropdown (Fall, Medication Error, Behavioral Incident, Elopement Attempt, Staff Injury), Then the system must dynamically render the type-specific form fields in addition to the common fields.
   Else if no incident type is selected, Then the system must display only the common fields and show a prompt "Please select an incident type to see additional fields."

2. If the provider fills all required fields for the selected incident type and clicks Submit, Then the system must save the incident record with all common and type-specific data.
   Else if any required field is missing, Then the system must highlight the missing fields and display validation errors.

3. If the provider changes the incident type after partially filling the form, Then the system must warn "Changing incident type will clear type-specific fields. Continue?" and reset only the type-specific fields upon confirmation.
   Else if the provider cancels the type change, Then the system must retain the current form state.

4. If the selected incident type is "Fall" and the severity is marked as "Critical", Then the system must require additional fields such as "Immediate medical response taken" and "Physician notified".
   Else if the severity is "Low" or "Medium", Then these additional fields must be optional.

5. If the incident is submitted successfully, Then the system must assign a unique incident ID and make it available in the Incident List under the correct type filter.
   Else if submission fails, Then the system must display an error message and preserve the entered data for retry.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that selecting "Fall" as the incident type displays fall-specific fields (fall location, activity, footwear, assistive device).
2. Verify that selecting "Medication Error" displays medication-specific fields (wrong medication, dose, time, route, patient).
3. Verify that selecting "Behavioral Incident" displays behavioral-specific fields (aggression type, trigger, de-escalation method).
4. Verify that selecting "Elopement Attempt" displays elopement-specific fields (exit point, duration missing, how found).
5. Verify that selecting "Staff Injury" displays injury-specific fields (injury type, body part, mechanism).
6. Verify that filling all required fields for a Fall incident and submitting creates the record successfully.
7. Verify that each incident type form retains the common fields (date, time, location, description, severity) in addition to type-specific fields.

Negative:
8. Verify that submitting any incident type with missing required fields displays validation errors.
9. Verify that submitting without selecting an incident type is prevented by the system.
10. Verify that changing the incident type after partially filling the form warns the user before clearing type-specific fields.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the incident type dropdown lists all supported types (Fall, Medication Error, Behavioral Incident, Elopement Attempt, Staff Injury).
12. Verify that type-specific fields render dynamically below the common fields when a type is selected.
13. Verify the form layout is clean and organized with labeled field groups for common and type-specific sections.
14. Verify required fields are clearly marked with asterisks or visual indicators.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify behavior when rapidly switching between incident types — the form should correctly render the fields for the last selected type.
16. Verify that submitting an incident with maximum-length text in all text fields succeeds without truncation.
17. Verify that an incident form partially filled and then abandoned (browser closed) does not create a draft or partial record.

Security:
18. Verify that only authenticated providers can access the Add Incident page.
19. Verify that incident form submissions are transmitted securely over HTTPS and validated on the server side.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that switching incident types dynamically renders the type-specific fields within 1 second.
2. Verify that submitting a fully filled incident form completes and displays confirmation within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to code incident severity levels and injury types using standardized classification systems for consistent reporting and analysis.
Description of User Story: This story enables providers to assign standardized severity levels and injury type codes to incidents using recognized classification systems within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Severity levels follow a structured scale (e.g., Level 1 - Near Miss/No Injury, Level 2 - Minor Injury, Level 3 - Moderate Injury requiring treatment, Level 4 - Major Injury/Hospitalization, Level 5 - Death) ensuring consistency across all incident reports. Injury type codes follow standardized classification (e.g., Laceration, Contusion, Fracture, Sprain, Burn, Skin Tear, Head Injury) with the ability to select affected body regions from a predefined list. This standardized coding enables the facility to generate accurate reports for regulatory agencies, perform trend analysis by severity and injury type, and benchmark incident data across facilities. The classification system is configurable by administrators to align with state-specific regulatory requirements and facility policies. Without standardized coding, incident severity and injury descriptions would be subjective free-text entries, making trend analysis unreliable and regulatory reporting labor-intensive.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider selects a severity level from the predefined list, Then the system must store the severity code and description with the incident record.
   Else if the provider does not select a severity level and attempts to save, Then the system must display a validation error "Severity level is required."

2. If the provider selects an injury type from the standardized list, Then the system must enable the body region selector for further classification.
   Else if the provider selects "No Injury" (Level 1 - Near Miss), Then the system must skip the injury type and body region sections.

3. If the severity is Level 4 or Level 5, Then the system must mark the incident as "Critical" and trigger mandatory notification workflows.
   Else if the severity is Level 1 through Level 3, Then standard notification workflows apply.

4. If the administrator has configured state-specific severity scales, Then the system must display the configured scale instead of the default.
   Else if no custom configuration exists, Then the system must display the default severity scale.

5. If the provider changes the severity level after initial assignment, Then the system must log the change with the old value, new value, user, and timestamp in the audit trail.
   Else if the severity is unchanged, Then no audit entry is created for that field.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Severity section displays all standardized levels (Level 1 through Level 5) with descriptions.
2. Verify that selecting Level 3 - Moderate Injury correctly records the severity code in the incident record.
3. Verify that the Injury Type section displays standardized codes (Laceration, Contusion, Fracture, Sprain, Burn, Skin Tear, Head Injury, etc.).
4. Verify that selecting an injury type enables the body region selector with predefined options.
5. Verify that severity and injury codes are displayed with full descriptions on the Incident Details page.
6. Verify that selecting Level 1 - Near Miss skips the injury type and body region fields.
7. Verify that selecting Level 4 or Level 5 triggers the critical incident notification workflow.

Negative:
8. Verify that attempting to save an incident without selecting a severity level displays a validation error.
9. Verify that selecting an injury type without a body region (when body region is required) displays a validation error.
10. Verify that entering an invalid or unsupported severity code via API is rejected by the server.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the severity level options are displayed with clear descriptions (e.g., "Level 1 - Near Miss/No Injury").
12. Verify the body region selector provides a visual or list-based selection interface.
13. Verify that selected severity and injury codes are visually confirmed with labels or badges.
14. Verify the coding sections are positioned logically within the incident form.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that changing the severity level after initial coding logs the change in the audit trail.
16. Verify behavior when an administrator modifies the severity scale after incidents have already been coded with the old scale.
17. Verify that an incident with multiple injuries can have multiple injury type and body region entries.

Security:
18. Verify that severity and injury code modifications are tracked in the audit trail with user identity and timestamp.
19. Verify that the classification system configuration can only be modified by administrators.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that severity level and injury type dropdowns load with all options within 1 second.
2. Verify that saving severity and injury codes completes within 2 seconds.

User Story:
As a user, I should be able to add incidents via link or button, edit incident and include notified/informed person details.
1. Incident details (text area)
2. Tags
3. Date & time
4. Location
5. Incident related inquiries
6. Follow up actions
7. Document upload
8. Staff Involved
Description of User Story: This story enables users to create new incidents and edit existing ones within the Nemi Care Facility Portal via a clearly accessible link or button on the Incident List page or relevant resident profile sections. The incident form captures eight core data points: (1) Incident details as a free-text area for the narrative description, (2) Tags for categorization and filtering, (3) Date and time of the incident, (4) Location where the incident occurred, (5) Incident-related inquiries for tracking investigation questions, (6) Follow-up actions with assignees and due dates, (7) Document upload for attaching evidence files and photos, and (8) Staff Involved listing personnel present or relevant to the incident. Additionally, the form includes a Notified/Informed Person section where the user records who was informed about the incident (e.g., family, physician, state agency) with notification method and timestamp. Users can edit any previously submitted incident to update details, add follow-up information, or include additional notified persons as the incident investigation progresses. This comprehensive form ensures that all critical incident data is captured in a single structured record for regulatory compliance, internal review, and quality improvement purposes. Without this feature, incident reporting would rely on fragmented records across multiple systems, leading to incomplete documentation and compliance gaps.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks "Add Incident" from the Incident List or a resident profile, Then the system must open a blank Incident Form with all eight data fields and the Notified/Informed Person section.
   Else if the user clicks "Edit" on an existing incident, Then the system must open the form pre-populated with all existing incident data.

2. If the user fills all required fields (Incident details, Date & time, Location) and clicks Save, Then the system must create or update the incident record.
   Else if required fields are missing, Then the system must display validation errors and prevent saving.

3. If the user uploads a document, Then the system must validate the file type (allowed: PDF, JPG, PNG, DOCX) and size (max limit) before accepting.
   Else if the file type is unsupported or exceeds the size limit, Then the system must display an error "Invalid file type or file size exceeds limit."

4. If the user adds a notified/informed person with all required details (name, method, date/time), Then the system must add the person to the notification list.
   Else if required notification details are missing, Then the system must highlight the missing fields.

5. If the user edits an incident and saves, Then the system must update the record and log the edit in the audit trail.
   Else if the user clicks Cancel during editing, Then the system must discard unsaved changes and return to the incident details view.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Add Incident" opens the Incident Form with all eight fields and the Notified/Informed Person section.
2. Verify that filling all required fields and saving creates the incident with a unique incident ID.
3. Verify that the Incident details text area accepts free-text narrative descriptions.
4. Verify that Tags can be added via a tag input field with autocomplete from predefined tags.
5. Verify that Date & time fields accept valid date and time values via picker controls.
6. Verify that Location can be selected from a predefined list of facility locations.
7. Verify that Follow-up actions can be added with action description, assignee, and due date.
8. Verify that documents (PDF, JPG, PNG) can be uploaded and appear in the Documents section.
9. Verify that Staff Involved can be searched and selected from the staff directory.
10. Verify that notified/informed persons can be added with name, relationship, method, and timestamp.
11. Verify that editing an existing incident pre-populates all fields correctly.
12. Verify that editing and saving an incident updates the record without overwriting the notification list.

Negative:
13. Verify that saving without Incident details text displays a validation error.
14. Verify that saving without Date & time displays a validation error.
15. Verify that uploading an unsupported file type (e.g., .exe) displays an error message.
16. Verify that uploading a file exceeding the size limit is rejected with an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
17. Verify the "Add Incident" button or link is prominently visible on the Incident List page.
18. Verify the Incident Form is organized in a logical flow with clear section labels.
19. Verify the document upload area supports drag-and-drop as well as file browse.
20. Verify the Staff Involved field has a search/autocomplete interface.

Edge Cases:
21. Verify that adding multiple follow-up actions to a single incident works correctly.
22. Verify that uploading multiple documents simultaneously attaches all files correctly.
23. Verify behavior when editing an incident that another user is also editing simultaneously.

Security:
24. Verify that uploaded documents are scanned or validated for malicious content.
25. Verify that incident form data is transmitted securely over HTTPS.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Incident Form loads within 2 seconds when clicking "Add Incident".
2. Verify that saving an incident with multiple documents and staff involved completes within 4 seconds.

User Story: As a user, I should be able to complete tasks (mark as done)  for the respective notified person & notification should be sent
Description of User Story: This story enables users to mark tasks as done for each notified person associated with an incident within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When an incident is created, notification tasks are generated for each party that needs to be informed (e.g., Family, Physician, State Agency), and these tasks appear in the incident's notification tracking section with their current status (Pending, In Progress, Done). When a user marks a notification task as "Done", the system records the completion timestamp, the user who marked it, and triggers an automated notification (email or in-app alert) to confirm that the respective party has been successfully notified. This workflow ensures that all required notifications are tracked to completion, providing verifiable evidence of communication for regulatory compliance and internal auditing. The notification status is visible on the incident details page, giving administrators and compliance officers a real-time view of which notifications have been completed and which are still pending. Without this feature, notification tracking would be manual and unverifiable, creating compliance risk when regulators ask for proof that required parties were informed in a timely manner.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks "Mark as Done" on a Pending or In Progress notification task, Then the system must update the status to "Done", record the timestamp and user, and send a confirmation notification.
   Else if the task is already "Done", Then the "Mark as Done" action must be disabled.

2. If the confirmation notification is sent successfully, Then the system must log the notification delivery status as "Sent" in the notification history.
   Else if the notification fails to send (e.g., invalid email), Then the system must log it as "Failed" and display a warning to the user.

3. If all notification tasks for an incident are completed, Then the system must update the incident's notification status to "All Notified".
   Else if one or more tasks remain pending, Then the incident's notification status must remain "Partially Notified".

4. If the user marks a task as Done by mistake, Then the system must allow an authorized user (Admin or Supervisor) to revert the task to "In Progress" with a reason.
   Else if the user is not authorized to revert, Then the revert option must not be available.

5. If the notification method is "Email", Then the system must send an email to the recorded address.
   Else if the method is "Phone", Then the system must log the phone notification as manually confirmed by the user.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the Notification Tasks section displays all notification tasks with person name, relationship, method, and status.
2. Verify that clicking "Mark as Done" on a Pending task updates the status to "Done" with a timestamp.
3. Verify that marking a task as Done triggers a confirmation email notification to the relevant stakeholder.
4. Verify that the completion timestamp and user identity are recorded when marking a task as Done.
5. Verify that when all notification tasks are Done, the incident's notification status updates to "All Notified".
6. Verify that an authorized user can revert a Done task to "In Progress" with a reason.

Negative:
7. Verify that the "Mark as Done" button is disabled for tasks already marked as Done.
8. Verify that if the confirmation email fails to send, the system logs the failure and displays a warning.
9. Verify that an unauthorized user cannot revert a completed notification task.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that Pending tasks are visually distinct from Done tasks (e.g., color-coded status badges).
11. Verify that Done tasks display a green status indicator and completed timestamp.
12. Verify the "Mark as Done" button is clearly visible and positioned next to each pending task.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify behavior when two users attempt to mark the same notification task as Done simultaneously.
14. Verify that marking a task as Done when the system's email service is temporarily unavailable queues the notification for retry.
15. Verify that the notification history accurately reflects the sequence and timing of all task completions.

Security:
16. Verify that notification task status changes are logged in the audit trail.
17. Verify that notification emails do not contain full incident details to protect sensitive information.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that marking a notification task as Done and updating the status completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify that the confirmation notification email is queued and sent within 5 seconds of marking the task as Done.

User Story: As a user, I should be able to close incidents.
Description of User Story: This story enables users to close an incident record after all required follow-up actions, notifications, and investigations have been completed within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. Closing an incident transitions its status from "Open" or "Under Review" to "Closed", signaling that no further action is required and the incident has been fully resolved. The system requires the user to confirm that all associated follow-up actions are marked as complete and all notification tasks are done before allowing the incident to be closed, ensuring no steps are skipped. Upon closure, the system records the closure timestamp, the user who closed it, and optionally captures a closure summary or resolution notes describing the final outcome. Closed incidents remain accessible in read-only mode for historical reference, auditing, and reporting purposes but cannot be further edited without reopening. Without this feature, incidents would remain in an open state indefinitely, making it impossible to distinguish between active and resolved incidents and creating confusion for compliance tracking.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user clicks "Close Incident" and all follow-up actions and notification tasks are complete, Then the system must allow closure and change the status to "Closed".
   Else if any follow-up action or notification is incomplete, Then the system must block closure and display "Cannot close: [list of incomplete items]."

2. If the user provides resolution notes during closure, Then the system must store the notes with the incident record.
   Else if the user skips resolution notes, Then the system must close the incident without notes (notes are optional).

3. If the incident is closed, Then all fields must become read-only and the Edit button must be disabled.
   Else if the incident is still open, Then editing must remain available.

4. If an authorized user clicks "Reopen" on a closed incident, Then the system must change the status to "Open" and require a reason for reopening.
   Else if the user is not authorized to reopen, Then the "Reopen" button must not be visible.

5. If the incident is closed, Then the closure details (timestamp, user, resolution notes) must be visible on the Incident Details page.
   Else if the incident is open, Then no closure details section is displayed.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Close Incident" on a fully resolved incident (all actions and notifications done) allows closure.
2. Verify that the incident status changes to "Closed" and the closure timestamp and user are recorded.
3. Verify that the user can enter optional resolution notes during closure.
4. Verify that a closed incident appears with a "Closed" status badge in the Incident List.
5. Verify that a closed incident is displayed in read-only mode with editing disabled.
6. Verify that an authorized user can reopen a closed incident by providing a reason.

Negative:
7. Verify that attempting to close an incident with incomplete follow-up actions displays a blocking message with the list of incomplete items.
8. Verify that attempting to close an incident with pending notification tasks is prevented.
9. Verify that an unauthorized user cannot see or use the "Reopen" button on a closed incident.
10. Verify that editing a closed incident without reopening it first is not possible.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the "Close Incident" button is visible on open incidents and hidden on already-closed incidents.
12. Verify the closure confirmation dialog displays a summary of completed actions and a field for optional resolution notes.
13. Verify the "Closed" status badge is styled distinctly (e.g., gray or green) in the Incident List.
14. Verify the "Reopen" button is visible only on closed incidents for authorized users.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that closing an incident that was just reopened works correctly if all actions are now complete.
16. Verify behavior when the last follow-up action is completed and the user immediately attempts to close the incident.
17. Verify that an incident with no follow-up actions and no notification tasks can still be closed.

Security:
18. Verify that incident closure and reopening events are logged in the audit trail.
19. Verify that the Close Incident API endpoint validates user permissions server-side.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that clicking "Close Incident" and processing the closure (validation + status update) completes within 3 seconds.
2. Verify that the Incident List correctly filters and displays closed incidents within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a user, I should be able to upload documents/ photos to incident reports, including state-approved images (like body diagrams or injury templates), so that I can clearly mark the incident details such as the location of injury and what happened
Description of User Story: This story enables users to upload documents and photos to incident reports within the Nemi Care Facility Portal, with specific support for state-approved images such as body diagrams and injury templates that can be annotated to indicate injury locations and incident details. The upload feature supports common file types including PDF, JPG, PNG, and DOCX, with file size validation to prevent excessively large uploads that could impact system performance. State-approved body diagrams and injury templates are pre-loaded in the system and available for selection; once selected, the user can annotate the diagram by marking the location of injury using drawing or pin-drop tools directly within the portal. Uploaded documents and annotated images are attached to the incident record and displayed in the Documents section of the Incident Details page with file names, upload dates, and preview thumbnails. This capability is critical for creating visual documentation that regulatory agencies and legal teams require when reviewing incident reports, particularly for fall-related injuries and behavioral incidents. Without this feature, injury location documentation would rely on free-text descriptions which are ambiguous and insufficient for regulatory compliance and legal proceedings.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user selects a file with a supported type (PDF, JPG, PNG, DOCX) and within the size limit, Then the system must upload and attach the file to the incident.
   Else if the file type is unsupported or exceeds the size limit, Then the system must reject the upload and display "File type not supported" or "File size exceeds the maximum limit."

2. If the user selects a body diagram from the template gallery, Then the system must open the diagram with annotation tools enabled.
   Else if no templates are available (configuration missing), Then the system must display "No templates configured. Please contact your administrator."

3. If the user annotates a body diagram and clicks Save, Then the system must save the annotated version as a new image attached to the incident.
   Else if the user closes the annotation tool without saving, Then the system must discard the annotations and not attach the diagram.

4. If the incident has multiple uploaded documents, Then the system must display all documents in a list or grid with preview capabilities.
   Else if no documents are uploaded, Then the Documents section must display "No documents attached."

5. If the user deletes an uploaded document, Then the system must remove the document from the incident record and log the deletion in the audit trail.
   Else if the document is referenced by a regulatory report, Then the system must prevent deletion and display "This document is referenced by a regulatory report and cannot be deleted."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Upload Document" opens a file selector that accepts PDF, JPG, PNG, and DOCX files.
2. Verify that a valid file uploads successfully and appears in the Documents section with name, type, and upload date.
3. Verify that image files display a thumbnail preview in the Documents section.
4. Verify that clicking "Use Body Diagram" displays a gallery of pre-loaded state-approved templates.
5. Verify that selecting a body diagram opens it with annotation tools (pin-drop, drawing, text labels).
6. Verify that annotating a body diagram and saving attaches the annotated image to the incident.
7. Verify that documents can be downloaded from the Incident Details page.
8. Verify that documents can be previewed inline (especially images and PDFs).

Negative:
9. Verify that uploading an unsupported file type (e.g., .exe, .bat) is rejected with an error message.
10. Verify that uploading a file exceeding the size limit is rejected with an appropriate message.
11. Verify that closing the annotation tool without saving discards the annotations.
12. Verify that deleting a document referenced by a regulatory report is prevented.

UI Scenarios:
13. Verify the upload area supports both click-to-browse and drag-and-drop interactions.
14. Verify a progress indicator is shown during file upload.
15. Verify the body diagram template gallery displays templates with preview thumbnails.
16. Verify annotation tools are intuitive with undo/redo capability.

Edge Cases:
17. Verify uploading multiple files simultaneously attaches all files correctly.
18. Verify behavior when the user uploads a very large image (e.g., 10MB) that is within the size limit.
19. Verify that annotating a diagram multiple times (save, reopen, re-annotate) preserves all annotation layers.

Security:
20. Verify that uploaded files are scanned for malicious content before being stored.
21. Verify that document download URLs require authentication and cannot be accessed by unauthenticated users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that a 5MB file upload completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that the body diagram template gallery loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As an Administrator/QA Lead, I want to view a dynamic dashboard that displays incident trends by type, time of day, and location, so that I can identify systemic risks and prioritize quality improvement (PI) initiatives.
Description of User Story: This story provides Administrators and QA Leads with a dynamic, interactive dashboard that visualizes incident trends across multiple dimensions within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The dashboard displays incident data segmented by incident type (Falls, Medication Errors, Behavioral, Elopement, Staff Injuries), time of day (Morning, Afternoon, Night shifts), and location (specific facility areas such as dining room, bedroom, hallway, common area). Charts and graphs include bar charts for type distribution, heat maps for time-of-day patterns, and location-based incident maps, all with drill-down capability to view underlying incident records. Filters allow the user to adjust the date range, select specific incident types, choose severity levels, and narrow by resident or staff member. This analytical tool is essential for identifying systemic risks (e.g., recurring falls in the same hallway during night shifts) and prioritizing quality improvement initiatives based on data-driven insights rather than anecdotal observations. Without this dashboard, administrators would need to manually compile and analyze incident reports in spreadsheets, a time-consuming process that delays identification of emerging risk patterns.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Administrator/QA Lead has access to the Incident Dashboard, Then the system must load all dashboard widgets and charts with current incident data.
   Else if the user does not have the Administrator or QA Lead role, Then the system must deny access and display "You do not have permission to view the Incident Dashboard."

2. If the selected date range contains incidents, Then the charts must display populated visualizations with accurate counts.
   Else if no incidents exist in the selected date range, Then the dashboard must display "No incidents found for the selected period."

3. If the user clicks on a chart data point, Then the system must drill down to show the list of incident records contributing to that data point.
   Else if drill-down is not applicable for a specific widget, Then the click should have no effect.

4. If the user applies multiple filters (date range + type + severity), Then the dashboard must apply all filters simultaneously and update all charts.
   Else if no filters are applied, Then the dashboard must show data for the default date range (e.g., last 30 days).

5. If the dashboard data is being loaded, Then the system must display loading indicators for each chart.
   Else if data loading fails, Then the system must show an error message per affected chart.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that navigating to the Incident Dashboard loads summary widgets with total incident counts.
2. Verify the "Incidents by Type" chart displays a breakdown by Falls, Medication Errors, Behavioral, Elopement, and Staff Injuries.
3. Verify the "Incidents by Time of Day" chart shows counts by Morning, Afternoon, and Night shifts.
4. Verify the "Incidents by Location" chart maps incidents to specific facility areas.
5. Verify that applying a date range filter updates all charts and widgets accordingly.
6. Verify that clicking on a chart data point drills down to show the underlying incident records.
7. Verify that combining multiple filters (type + severity + date range) narrows the dashboard data correctly.

Negative:
8. Verify that a user without Administrator or QA Lead role is denied access to the dashboard.
9. Verify that selecting a date range with no incidents displays a "No incidents found" message.
10. Verify that an invalid date range (end before start) is rejected with a validation error.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the dashboard layout is clean with charts arranged in a logical grid or card format.
12. Verify charts are interactive with hover tooltips showing exact values.
13. Verify filter controls (date pickers, type checkboxes, severity dropdown) are clearly accessible.
14. Verify the dashboard is responsive and displays correctly on different screen sizes.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify dashboard behavior when there is only one incident in the system — charts should still render correctly.
16. Verify dashboard behavior with a very large dataset (1000+ incidents) — charts should render without freezing.
17. Verify that the drill-down view handles a data point with 100+ underlying incidents with pagination.

Security:
18. Verify that dashboard data access is restricted to authorized roles only.
19. Verify that dashboard API endpoints do not expose raw incident data beyond what is displayed in the charts.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Incident Dashboard loads all charts and widgets within 5 seconds for a dataset of 500+ incidents.
2. Verify that applying a filter and refreshing the dashboard completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As an Administrator, I want to be able to electronically track and document when and how key parties were notified (Family, Provider, State Agency), so that regulatory compliance is verifiable and communication records are centralized.
Description of User Story: This story provides administrators with the ability to electronically track and document all notifications sent to key parties (Family, Provider, State Agency) related to incidents within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. For each notification, the system records the notified party type, the specific contact (e.g., family member name, physician name, state agency), the notification method (Email, Phone, Fax, In-Person), the date and time of notification, the user who initiated the notification, and the acknowledgment status. The centralized notification log is accessible from the Incident Details page and from a dedicated Communication Log view that aggregates all notifications across incidents for compliance review. This electronic tracking eliminates the need for paper-based notification logs, providing auditable digital records that can be quickly retrieved during regulatory surveys and inspections. The system supports both manual logging (for phone and in-person notifications) and automated tracking (for email notifications sent through the system). Without this feature, facilities would struggle to produce evidence of timely and complete notifications during regulatory audits, risking citations and penalties.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Administrator clicks "Add Notification Record" and fills all required fields (party type, contact, method, date/time), Then the system must save the record and add it to the Notification Log.
   Else if required fields are missing, Then the system must display validation errors.

2. If the notification was sent via system email, Then the system must automatically capture the delivery status (Sent, Delivered, Failed) and timestamp.
   Else if the notification was manual (Phone, In-Person, Fax), Then the system must rely on the user's manual entry for the date/time and method.

3. If the Administrator filters the Communication Log by party type and date range, Then only matching notification records must be displayed.
   Else if no records match the filters, Then the system must display "No notification records found for the selected criteria."

4. If the notified party acknowledges the notification (e.g., email read receipt, verbal confirmation logged), Then the acknowledgment status must be updated to "Acknowledged".
   Else if no acknowledgment is received, Then the status must remain "Awaiting Acknowledgment".

5. If the Administrator exports the Communication Log, Then the system must generate a downloadable file (PDF or CSV) with all displayed records.
   Else if no records exist to export, Then the export button must be disabled.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Notification Log section on the Incident Details page displays all notification records for that incident.
2. Verify that adding a notification record with all required fields saves successfully.
3. Verify that system-sent email notifications automatically log delivery status and timestamp.
4. Verify that the Communication Log view displays all notifications across incidents.
5. Verify that filtering by party type (Family, Provider, State Agency) correctly narrows the log.
6. Verify that filtering by date range shows only notifications within the specified period.
7. Verify that the acknowledgment status can be updated from "Awaiting Acknowledgment" to "Acknowledged".
8. Verify that the Communication Log can be exported as PDF or CSV.

Negative:
9. Verify that adding a notification record without a party type displays a validation error.
10. Verify that adding a notification record without a date/time displays a validation error.
11. Verify that filtering with criteria that match no records displays "No notification records found."

UI Scenarios:
12. Verify the Notification Log is presented as a sortable table with columns for party type, contact, method, date/time, notifier, and status.
13. Verify filter controls on the Communication Log view are intuitive and clearly labeled.
14. Verify the "Add Notification Record" form is organized logically with dropdowns for party type and method.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that logging notifications for an incident that has been closed still allows adding records (for late notifications).
16. Verify that the Communication Log handles a large number of records (500+) with pagination.
17. Verify behavior when a system email notification bounces — the delivery status should reflect "Failed".

Security:
18. Verify that only Administrators can access the Communication Log view and add/edit notification records.
19. Verify that notification records containing personal contact information are transmitted securely over HTTPS.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the Communication Log view loads within 3 seconds with 500+ notification records.
2. Verify that adding a notification record saves and displays within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Nurse Manager, I want the system to require confirmation that all associated corrective actions are complete and notifications are tracked before an incident can be marked as closed, so that no step in the resolution process is overlooked.
Description of User Story: This story implements a mandatory closure validation workflow for the Nurse Manager role within the Nemi Care Facility Portal, ensuring that incidents cannot be marked as closed until all corrective actions are confirmed complete and all required notifications are documented. When a Nurse Manager attempts to close an incident, the system performs an automated pre-closure checklist verification that scans the incident record for: (1) all follow-up/corrective actions marked as "Complete", (2) all required notification tasks marked as "Done", and (3) all mandatory fields populated. If any item fails the verification, the system blocks closure and presents a detailed list of incomplete items with links to each one for quick resolution. This workflow prevents premature closure of incidents that still have outstanding corrective actions or undocumented notifications, which is a common regulatory finding during facility audits. The pre-closure checklist is configurable by administrators to include additional facility-specific requirements beyond the standard corrective action and notification checks. Without this automated enforcement, the closure of incidents would rely on manual diligence, and incomplete closures could expose the facility to regulatory citations and liability.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If all corrective actions are "Complete" and all notification tasks are "Done", Then the system must allow the Nurse Manager to close the incident.
   Else if any corrective action is not "Complete", Then the system must block closure and display "The following corrective actions are incomplete: [list with links]."

2. If all notification tasks are "Done", Then the notification verification passes.
   Else if any notification task is "Pending" or "In Progress", Then the system must block closure and display "The following notifications are pending: [list with links]."

3. If the administrator has configured additional custom closure requirements, Then the system must verify those requirements in addition to the standard checks.
   Else if no custom requirements are configured, Then only the standard corrective action and notification checks apply.

4. If the Nurse Manager resolves all incomplete items and re-attempts closure, Then the system must re-run the validation and allow closure if all checks pass.
   Else if items remain incomplete after the second attempt, Then closure must be blocked again with the updated list.

5. If the incident is successfully closed, Then the system must record the closure with timestamp, closing user, resolution notes, and the pre-closure checklist results.
   Else if closure is blocked, Then no closure record is created and the incident remains open.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the pre-closure validation checks all corrective actions for "Complete" status.
2. Verify that the pre-closure validation checks all notification tasks for "Done" status.
3. Verify that when all checks pass, the Nurse Manager can close the incident successfully.
4. Verify that the closure record includes timestamp, closing user, and resolution notes.
5. Verify that resolving all incomplete items and re-attempting closure succeeds.
6. Verify that administrator-configured custom closure requirements are also validated.

Negative:
7. Verify that closure is blocked when one or more corrective actions are incomplete, with the incomplete items listed.
8. Verify that closure is blocked when one or more notification tasks are pending, with the pending items listed.
9. Verify that closure is blocked when both corrective actions and notifications are incomplete, with all items listed.
10. Verify that a user without the Nurse Manager or authorized role cannot close an incident.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the pre-closure validation result is displayed in a clear, organized format with links to incomplete items.
12. Verify the closure confirmation dialog shows a summary of the pre-closure checklist results (all items green/passed).
13. Verify that incomplete items in the validation result are highlighted with red or warning styling.
14. Verify the resolution notes field is clearly labeled and supports multi-line text entry.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify that an incident with zero corrective actions and zero notification tasks can still be closed (no items to validate).
16. Verify that the validation correctly handles an incident with a large number of corrective actions (20+) and notifications (10+).
17. Verify that if a corrective action is marked complete and then reverted to incomplete between validation and closure confirmation, the system re-validates.

Security:
18. Verify that the closure validation logic runs server-side and cannot be bypassed by client-side manipulation.
19. Verify that pre-closure checklist results and closure records are included in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the pre-closure validation completes within 3 seconds for an incident with 10+ corrective actions and notifications.
2. Verify that the incident closure process (validation + save + status update) completes within 4 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view list of patients previous intake forms.
Description of User Story: This story enables providers to view a comprehensive list of all previous intake forms associated with a patient within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When the provider navigates to a patient's Intake section, the system displays a chronological list of all historically submitted intake forms with key metadata including intake date, program type (ADHS, ALF, Home Care, BH, NEMT), intake status (Complete, Draft, Archived), and the user who created the intake. Each list entry is clickable, allowing the provider to open and review the full intake form details in read-only mode. This feature supports continuity of care by giving providers visibility into a patient's intake history, enabling them to review prior admissions, program enrollments, and the information captured at each point of entry. The list supports sorting by date and filtering by program type and status for easy navigation when a patient has multiple intake records. Without this capability, providers would have no centralized view of a patient's intake history, requiring them to search through paper files or separate system modules to find prior intake information.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the patient has one or more previous intake forms, Then the system must display all intake records in a list with date, program type, status, and created by.
   Else if the patient has no intake records, Then the system must display "No previous intake forms found for this patient."

2. If the provider clicks on an intake form entry, Then the system must open the form in read-only mode with all fields populated.
   Else if the intake form data is corrupted or missing, Then the system must display "Unable to load this intake form. Please contact support."

3. If the provider sorts the list by date, Then the system must reorder the entries chronologically (ascending or descending based on the sort direction).
   Else if no sort is applied, Then the default order must be most recent intake first.

4. If the provider filters by program type, Then only matching intake forms must be displayed.
   Else if the filter matches no records, Then the system must display "No intake forms found for the selected filter."

5. If the provider has permission to view patient intake records, Then the Intake section must be accessible.
   Else if the provider lacks permission, Then the Intake section must display "You do not have permission to view intake records."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that navigating to a patient's Intake section displays a list of all previous intake forms.
2. Verify that each intake form entry shows the intake date, program type, status, and created by user.
3. Verify that clicking on an intake form entry opens the full form in read-only mode.
4. Verify that the list can be sorted by date in ascending and descending order.
5. Verify that filtering by program type (e.g., ALF, ADHS, Home Care) correctly narrows the list.
6. Verify that the default sort order shows the most recent intake form first.

Negative:
7. Verify that when a patient has no previous intake forms, the system displays "No previous intake forms found."
8. Verify that attempting to open a corrupted or missing intake form displays an error message.
9. Verify that a provider without intake viewing permissions is denied access.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the intake list is displayed as a table with clear column headers (Date, Program, Status, Created By).
11. Verify that sort indicators (arrows) are visible on sortable column headers.
12. Verify that filter controls are accessible above or alongside the list.
13. Verify that the list supports pagination if there are many intake records.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that a patient with a large number of intake forms (20+) renders the list correctly with pagination.
15. Verify that intake forms created across different programs are all shown in the unified list.
16. Verify behavior when the provider navigates directly to the Intake section via URL for a patient who does not exist.

Security:
17. Verify that intake form data is served over HTTPS and patient PII is protected.
18. Verify that direct URL access to another patient's intake records without authorization is denied.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the intake forms list loads within 2 seconds for a patient with 10+ previous intake forms.
2. Verify that opening a read-only intake form loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to import previous intake into the current encounter.
Description of User Story: This story enables providers to import data from a previously completed intake form into a current intake encounter within the Nemi Care Facility Portal, eliminating the need to re-enter information that has not changed since the last intake. When creating a new intake for a patient who has existing intake records, the provider can select a previous intake from a list and click "Import" to auto-populate the current intake form with the historical data. After import, all fields are editable so the provider can review and update any information that has changed since the previous intake, such as medication lists, insurance details, emergency contacts, or clinical conditions. The import feature is particularly valuable for patients returning for readmission or transferring between programs (e.g., from ALF to Home Care) where much of the baseline demographic and medical information remains the same. The system clearly marks imported fields to distinguish pre-populated data from newly entered information, supporting audit trail clarity. Without this feature, providers would need to manually re-enter all intake information for returning patients, a time-consuming and error-prone process that delays the admission workflow.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the patient has previous intake records, Then the system must display the "Import Previous Intake" option with a selectable list of prior intakes.
   Else if no previous intake exists, Then the "Import Previous Intake" option must be hidden or disabled.

2. If the provider selects a prior intake and clicks Import, Then the system must auto-populate all current intake fields with the selected intake's data.
   Else if the import fails (e.g., data format mismatch between old and new intake versions), Then the system must display "Import failed. The selected intake may have an incompatible format." and leave the form blank.

3. If the provider modifies any imported field and saves, Then the system must save the current intake with the modified data and preserve the original imported intake unchanged.
   Else if the provider saves without modifying any imported data, Then the system must save the imported data as-is for the current intake.

4. If the imported intake was from a different program (e.g., ALF import into Home Care), Then the system must import only the fields that are common to both programs and leave program-specific fields blank.
   Else if the programs are the same, Then all fields must be imported.

5. If the import process takes longer than expected, Then the system must display a loading indicator and not allow form interaction until the import is complete.
   Else if the import completes quickly, Then the form must be immediately editable.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the "Import Previous Intake" option is visible when the patient has prior intake records.
2. Verify that selecting a previous intake and clicking Import auto-populates the current intake form.
3. Verify that all imported fields are editable after import.
4. Verify that imported fields are marked with an "Imported" indicator for audit clarity.
5. Verify that modifying imported data and saving creates the current intake without altering the original.
6. Verify that importing from a different program populates only common fields.

Negative:
7. Verify that the "Import Previous Intake" option is hidden when the patient has no prior intake records.
8. Verify that an incompatible intake format displays an import failure message.
9. Verify that importing and then canceling the intake does not create a new record.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the "Import Previous Intake" option is clearly positioned on the new intake form page.
11. Verify the list of available prior intakes shows date, program, and status for selection.
12. Verify a loading indicator is displayed during the import process.
13. Verify imported fields have a distinct visual indicator (e.g., badge or background color).

Edge Cases:
14. Verify importing from a very old intake record (1+ years) that may have deprecated fields handles gracefully.
15. Verify behavior when importing from a Draft intake (incomplete) — the system should import available fields and leave missing fields blank.
16. Verify that importing twice (selecting another intake after the first import) replaces the data with the second import.

Security:
17. Verify that the import process does not expose data from intakes the provider is not authorized to view.
18. Verify that the import action is logged in the audit trail with the source intake ID.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that importing a previous intake auto-populates the form within 3 seconds.
2. Verify that the list of previous intakes loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As an Admin, I need a multi-step intake wizard so that I can efficiently and accurately capture all required initial patient/resident information across various care programs (ADHS, ALF, Home Care, BH, NEMT).
Description of User Story: This story provides administrators with a multi-step intake wizard that guides them through the complete patient/resident intake process in a structured, step-by-step format within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. The wizard breaks the intake form into logical sections (steps) such as Demographics, Insurance, Medical History, Emergency Contacts, Program-Specific Information, Consents, and Document Uploads, with navigation controls to move forward and backward between steps. The wizard dynamically adjusts the number and content of steps based on the selected care program (ADHS, ALF, Home Care, BH, NEMT), ensuring that only relevant sections are presented for each program type. Each step validates required fields before allowing progression to the next step, preventing incomplete data entry while allowing the user to save progress as a draft and resume later. This wizard approach reduces data entry errors by focusing the user on one section at a time and providing contextual help and field descriptions within each step. Without this multi-step wizard, the intake process would present all fields on a single long form, overwhelming users and increasing the likelihood of missed required fields and data entry errors.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Admin selects a care program from the dropdown, Then the wizard must configure steps specific to that program.
   Else if no program is selected, Then the wizard must not proceed and display "Please select a care program to begin the intake."

2. If all required fields on the current step are filled and validated, Then the "Next" button must advance to the next step.
   Else if any required field is missing or invalid, Then the system must display validation errors and prevent advancement.

3. If the Admin clicks "Previous", Then the system must navigate to the prior step with all data retained.
   Else if the Admin is on the first step, Then the "Previous" button must be disabled.

4. If the Admin clicks "Save as Draft" at any step, Then the system must save all entered data as a draft and allow the user to resume later.
   Else if saving the draft fails, Then the system must display an error and preserve the form data in memory.

5. If the Admin clicks "Submit" on the final step with all required fields across all steps completed, Then the system must create the intake record with "Complete" status.
   Else if any previous step has missing required fields, Then the system must redirect the Admin to the first incomplete step with errors highlighted.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that selecting a care program (ALF) loads the wizard with program-specific steps.
2. Verify that selecting a different program (NEMT) loads different steps appropriate for that program.
3. Verify that filling all required fields on Step 1 and clicking Next advances to Step 2.
4. Verify that clicking Previous on Step 2 returns to Step 1 with all data intact.
5. Verify that clicking "Save as Draft" saves the current progress and the draft can be resumed later.
6. Verify that completing all steps and clicking Submit creates a Complete intake record.
7. Verify that the wizard step indicator shows the current step and total steps.

Negative:
8. Verify that clicking Next without filling required fields displays validation errors and blocks advancement.
9. Verify that not selecting a care program prevents the wizard from starting.
10. Verify that clicking Submit on the final step when a previous step has missing required data redirects to the incomplete step.
11. Verify that the Previous button is disabled on the first step.

UI Scenarios:
12. Verify the wizard displays a step progress indicator (e.g., Step 2 of 6) at the top.
13. Verify each step has a clear title and description of what information is being captured.
14. Verify the Next, Previous, Save as Draft, and Submit buttons are clearly labeled and positioned.
15. Verify required fields are marked with asterisks or visual indicators.

Edge Cases:
16. Verify that resuming a draft intake restores all previously entered data across all steps.
17. Verify behavior when the Admin switches the care program after partially completing the wizard — the system should warn about data loss for program-specific fields.
18. Verify that the wizard handles a program with only 2 steps (e.g., NEMT) as well as one with 6+ steps (e.g., ALF).

Security:
19. Verify that only Admins can initiate and submit intake wizards.
20. Verify that draft intake data is stored securely and not accessible to unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the wizard loads the first step within 2 seconds after selecting a care program.
2. Verify that advancing between wizard steps completes within 1 second.

User Story: As an Admin, I need to be able to configure the set of required intake form fields and sections based on the selected Program (e.g., ALF vs. NEMT) and state-specific regulatory requirements, so that the system ensures compliance and gathers only relevant data for each intake.
Description of User Story: This story enables administrators to configure which intake form fields and sections are required, optional, or hidden based on the selected care program and state-specific regulatory requirements within the Nemi Care Facility Portal's configuration module. The configuration interface allows the Admin to select a program (ADHS, ALF, Home Care, BH, NEMT) and a state, then customize the intake form by toggling sections on or off and marking individual fields as required, optional, or hidden. For example, the ALF program in Arizona may require a Level of Care assessment section with specific mandatory fields, while the NEMT program in the same state only requires transportation-related fields and does not need a medical history section. The configured rules are applied dynamically when any user initiates an intake for the specified program and state, ensuring that the form presented to the user matches the configured requirements without requiring code changes. This configurability is essential for multi-state facilities that must comply with varying regulatory requirements across different states and programs. Without this feature, intake forms would be static and one-size-fits-all, either collecting unnecessary data for simpler programs or missing required fields for more regulated programs, creating compliance gaps.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Admin selects a program and state on the Intake Configuration page, Then the system must display all intake sections and fields with their current status (Required, Optional, Hidden).
   Else if no program or state is selected, Then the system must display "Please select a program and state to view the configuration."

2. If the Admin changes a field status from "Optional" to "Required" and saves, Then the configuration must be persisted and applied to all future intakes for that program/state.
   Else if the Admin changes a field but does not save, Then the changes must not be persisted.

3. If a section is configured as "Hidden" for a program/state, Then the intake form for that program/state must not display that section.
   Else if a section is "Required" or "Optional", Then it must be displayed accordingly.

4. If a field is configured as "Required", Then the intake form must validate that field is not empty on submission.
   Else if a field is "Optional", Then the intake form must allow empty submission for that field.

5. If the Admin copies configuration from one state to another, Then the system must duplicate the configuration for the target state and allow the Admin to modify it.
   Else if no copy source is available, Then the Admin must configure from scratch.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Intake Configuration page lists all available programs and states for selection.
2. Verify that selecting ALF and Arizona displays all intake sections and fields with their current configuration.
3. Verify that toggling a section from "Optional" to "Required" and saving persists the change.
4. Verify that hiding a section for NEMT removes it from the NEMT intake form.
5. Verify that marking a field as "Required" triggers validation on the intake form when the field is empty.
6. Verify that different states for the same program can have different configurations.
7. Verify that copying a configuration from one state to another creates a duplicate that can be independently modified.

Negative:
8. Verify that navigating to the configuration page without selecting a program and state shows a prompt message.
9. Verify that making changes without saving does not persist the configuration.
10. Verify that attempting to hide all sections for a program is blocked with a warning "At least one section must be visible."
11. Verify that a non-Admin user cannot access the Intake Configuration page.

UI Scenarios:
12. Verify the configuration page displays sections and fields in a clear, hierarchical layout.
13. Verify that Required, Optional, and Hidden statuses are represented by distinct visual controls (e.g., toggle switches or dropdown selectors).
14. Verify the Save and Cancel buttons are clearly visible on the configuration page.
15. Verify that changes since last save are visually indicated (e.g., unsaved changes warning).

Edge Cases:
16. Verify that changing a section from "Required" to "Hidden" after existing intakes have been submitted with that section does not affect the historical records.
17. Verify that adding a new required field to a program does not retroactively invalidate previously completed intakes.
18. Verify the configuration handles a program with a large number of sections (15+) and fields (100+) without layout issues.

Security:
19. Verify that only Admin users can view and modify intake form configurations.
20. Verify that configuration changes are logged in the audit trail with the Admin identity, timestamp, and what was changed.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Intake Configuration page loads within 3 seconds with all sections and fields displayed.
2. Verify that saving a configuration update persists within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to generate draft progress notes with one click by leveraging structured data including vitals, ADLs, and patient history.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to generate a complete draft progress note with a single click, leveraging structured clinical data already captured in the system including vitals, Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), and the patient's historical health records. On the Progress Notes screen, a "Generate Draft" button is available that triggers the AI engine to compile and synthesize relevant patient data into a coherent clinical narrative following standard progress note formats (e.g., SOAP or DAP). The generated draft pulls the most recent vitals (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, weight), ADL scores, attendance records, incident history, and active care plan goals to produce a contextually accurate note. The provider can review the generated draft, make edits or additions as needed, and then finalize and sign the note for submission. This capability significantly reduces the time spent on repetitive documentation tasks, allowing providers to focus more on direct patient care rather than manual note writing. Without this feature, providers must manually compile data from multiple sections of the patient chart and compose each progress note from scratch, which is time-consuming and prone to omissions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Generate Draft" and the patient has complete recent vitals and ADL data, Then the system must generate a fully populated draft progress note within the expected format.
   Else if the patient has partial data (e.g., vitals present but no ADL scores), Then the system must generate a partial draft and highlight the missing sections with a warning indicator.

2. If the provider edits the generated draft and clicks "Finalize and Sign," Then the system must save the edited version as the final progress note.
   Else if the provider clicks "Discard Draft," Then the system must discard the draft without saving any changes.

3. If the patient has no data recorded in the system (new patient with no vitals, ADLs, or history), Then the system must display a message "Insufficient data to generate a draft note" and prevent draft generation.

4. If the AI engine encounters an error during draft generation, Then the system must display an error message "Unable to generate draft at this time. Please try again." and log the error for troubleshooting.

5. If the provider generates a draft for a patient who already has an unsigned draft note, Then the system must prompt "An existing draft already exists. Do you want to overwrite it?" with options to overwrite or cancel.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
