1. Verify that clicking "Generate Draft" on the Progress Notes screen generates a draft note populated with the patient's latest vitals, ADL scores, and care plan goals.
2. Verify that the generated draft follows the standard progress note format (e.g., SOAP: Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan).
3. Verify that the provider can edit any section of the generated draft before finalizing.
4. Verify that clicking "Finalize and Sign" saves the note to the patient's chart with the provider's signature and timestamp.
5. Verify that generating a draft for a patient with extensive history includes the most relevant recent data (last 30 days).
6. Verify that the generated draft includes a reference to active medications and allergies when available.

Negative:
7. Verify that clicking "Generate Draft" for a patient with no recorded data displays "Insufficient data to generate a draft note."
8. Verify that if the AI engine is unavailable, clicking "Generate Draft" shows an appropriate error message.
9. Verify that a provider cannot finalize and sign a completely empty draft note.
10. Verify that clicking "Generate Draft" when an unsigned draft already exists prompts the overwrite confirmation dialog.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the "Generate Draft" button is clearly visible and labeled on the Progress Notes screen.
12. Verify a loading spinner or progress indicator is displayed while the draft is being generated.
13. Verify the generated draft is displayed in a readable, structured format with clear section headings.
14. Verify the "Finalize and Sign" and "Discard Draft" buttons are prominently displayed after draft generation.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify behavior when the provider clicks "Generate Draft" multiple times rapidly — only one draft should be generated.
16. Verify that generating a draft for a patient with only vitals but no ADLs produces a partial draft with a warning for the missing ADL section.
17. Verify behavior when the provider's session expires while reviewing the draft — the draft should be auto-saved.

Security:
18. Verify that only authenticated and authorized providers can generate and sign draft progress notes.
19. Verify that the generated draft content cannot be accessed or intercepted by unauthorized users via API calls.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the AI draft generation completes and displays the draft within 5 seconds for a patient with 6 months of clinical data.
2. Verify that the "Finalize and Sign" action saves the note and returns confirmation within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive AI suggestions for documentation phrasing that aligns with regulatory standards and best practices.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive real-time AI-powered phrasing suggestions while composing or editing clinical documentation, ensuring that the language used aligns with regulatory standards such as CMS guidelines, state-specific requirements, and industry best practices. As the provider types in a documentation field (e.g., progress notes, assessments, care plans), the AI engine analyzes the text and suggests alternative phrasing that meets compliance requirements, replacing vague or non-standard language with precise, audit-ready terminology. Suggestions appear as inline recommendations or a side panel, and the provider can accept, modify, or dismiss each suggestion. The system cross-references the documentation against a library of regulatory-compliant phrases updated periodically to reflect the latest standards. This capability helps providers avoid common documentation pitfalls that can lead to claim denials, audit findings, or regulatory penalties. Without AI-assisted phrasing, providers may inadvertently use language that does not meet regulatory scrutiny, increasing compliance risk for the facility.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider types a phrase flagged as non-compliant, Then the AI must highlight the phrase with a visual indicator and display a suggested compliant alternative.
   Else if the phrase is already compliant, Then no suggestion should be shown for that phrase.

2. If the provider clicks "Accept" on a suggestion, Then the system must replace the original phrase with the suggested text.
   Else if the provider clicks "Dismiss," Then the system must remove the suggestion and retain the original phrase.

3. If the provider modifies the suggested text before accepting, Then the system must use the provider's modified version in the document.

4. If the AI engine is temporarily unavailable, Then the system must allow the provider to continue documenting without suggestions and display a non-blocking notification "AI suggestions temporarily unavailable."

5. If the documentation contains multiple non-compliant phrases, Then the system must display suggestions for each flagged phrase independently.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that typing a non-standard phrase (e.g., "patient seems better") triggers an AI suggestion with a regulatory-aligned alternative (e.g., "patient demonstrates measurable improvement in...").
2. Verify that clicking "Accept" on a suggestion replaces the original text with the compliant alternative.
3. Verify that clicking "Dismiss" removes the suggestion and retains the original text.
4. Verify that multiple suggestions can be displayed simultaneously for different phrases in the same document.
5. Verify that accepted suggestions persist after saving and reopening the document.

Negative:
6. Verify that compliant phrasing does not trigger unnecessary suggestions.
7. Verify that if the AI engine is unavailable, the provider can continue documenting without interruption and sees a "AI suggestions temporarily unavailable" notification.
8. Verify that dismissing all suggestions does not affect the ability to finalize the document.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify that flagged phrases are visually highlighted (e.g., underlined in orange or yellow) to distinguish them from regular text.
10. Verify that suggestions appear in a non-obstructive manner (inline tooltip or side panel) without blocking the documentation workflow.
11. Verify that "Accept" and "Dismiss" buttons are clearly labeled and accessible for each suggestion.
12. Verify that the suggestion panel is scrollable when multiple suggestions are present.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify behavior when the provider types very rapidly — suggestions should still appear without significant lag.
14. Verify behavior when the documentation field contains a mix of compliant and non-compliant phrases — only non-compliant phrases should be flagged.
15. Verify that undoing an accepted suggestion (Ctrl+Z) restores the original text.

Security:
16. Verify that AI suggestion requests are transmitted securely and do not expose patient data in logs or network requests.
17. Verify that only authorized providers can access the AI suggestion feature.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that AI phrasing suggestions appear within 2 seconds of the provider pausing after typing a non-compliant phrase.
2. Verify that accepting or dismissing a suggestion updates the document in under 1 second.

User Story: As a provider, I should be alerted when required elements are missing from clinical documentation before submission.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive automated alerts when required elements are missing from clinical documentation prior to submission, ensuring completeness and reducing the risk of rejected claims or audit deficiencies. When the provider clicks "Submit" or "Review" on a clinical document (progress note, assessment, care plan), the system performs a validation check against a checklist of required elements such as patient demographics, diagnosis codes, service dates, provider signatures, and mandated clinical observations. Any missing elements are highlighted with clear visual indicators (e.g., red borders, warning icons) and listed in a summary panel showing what needs to be completed. The provider can click on a missing element in the summary to navigate directly to the corresponding field in the document. This pre-submission validation prevents incomplete documentation from being submitted to payers or regulatory bodies, saving time on rework and resubmission. Without this feature, missing elements may go unnoticed until a claim denial or audit finding surfaces, leading to delayed revenue and compliance risk.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Submit" and required elements are missing, Then the system must block submission and display a summary of missing elements with visual highlighting on the document fields.
   Else if all required elements are present, Then the system must submit the document successfully.

2. If the provider clicks on a missing element in the summary panel, Then the system must navigate to and focus the cursor on the corresponding field.

3. If the provider fills in a previously missing element, Then the system must remove the highlight and update the summary panel to reflect the change in real-time.

4. If the provider clicks "Save Draft" with missing elements, Then the system must save the document as a draft without blocking or requiring all elements.
   Else if the provider clicks "Submit" with missing elements, Then submission must be blocked.

5. If the required elements checklist is updated by an administrator, Then the validation should reflect the updated checklist for all subsequent document submissions.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Submit" on a document with missing required elements displays a validation summary listing all missing items.
2. Verify that each missing element is highlighted with a red border or warning icon in the document form.
3. Verify that clicking a missing element in the summary panel navigates to the corresponding field.
4. Verify that filling in a missing element removes its highlight and updates the summary panel in real-time.
5. Verify that a fully completed document passes validation and submits successfully on the first attempt.
6. Verify that saving as draft is allowed even when required elements are missing.

Negative:
7. Verify that submission is blocked when any required element is missing.
8. Verify that the system does not allow bypassing validation by directly calling the submission API.
9. Verify that an empty document with no fields filled triggers validation errors for all required elements.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the missing elements summary panel is displayed prominently at the top or side of the document.
11. Verify that highlighted missing fields have clear visual distinction (red border, warning icon) from completed fields.
12. Verify that the validation summary includes descriptive labels for each missing element (e.g., "Diagnosis Code (ICD-10) is required").
13. Verify that the summary panel is dismissible but re-accessible via a "View Issues" button.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a required field is filled with only whitespace — it should still be flagged as missing.
15. Verify behavior when the required elements checklist is updated mid-session — the updated checklist should apply on the next "Submit" click.
16. Verify that validation works correctly for documents with conditional required fields (e.g., a field required only when a specific diagnosis is selected).

Security:
17. Verify that validation cannot be bypassed through direct API submission without proper authorization.
18. Verify that the missing elements details are not exposed in client-side console logs or network responses to unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the validation check completes and displays results within 3 seconds for a document with 50+ fields.
2. Verify that navigating to a missing element from the summary panel focuses the field within 1 second.

User Story: As a provider, I should be alerted when there are sudden changes in a member's attendance patterns that may indicate health or social issues.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive automated AI-generated alerts when a member exhibits sudden or significant changes in their attendance patterns, which may signal underlying health deterioration, social isolation, transportation problems, or other issues requiring intervention. The AI engine continuously monitors attendance records for each member, establishing a baseline pattern (e.g., attends 4 days per week) and flagging deviations such as unexpected absences, reduced frequency, or irregular check-in/check-out times. Alerts are displayed on the provider's dashboard with details including the member's name, the nature of the anomaly (e.g., "3 consecutive absences," "attendance dropped from 4 to 1 day/week"), and a recommended action. The provider can acknowledge the alert, add notes, or escalate it to the care team for follow-up. Early detection of attendance anomalies allows proactive outreach to the member or their family, potentially preventing adverse health events or disengagement from care programs. Without this automated detection, attendance changes may go unnoticed until they become critical, especially in facilities managing large member populations.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a member has 3 or more consecutive unexplained absences, Then the system must generate an attendance anomaly alert categorized as "High Severity."
   Else if a member's weekly attendance drops by 50% or more compared to their baseline, Then the system must generate an alert categorized as "Medium Severity."

2. If the provider acknowledges the alert and adds notes, Then the system must save the notes and mark the alert as acknowledged with a timestamp.
   Else if the provider escalates the alert, Then the system must notify the assigned care team and log the escalation.

3. If the member's attendance returns to baseline within 2 weeks after an alert, Then the system must auto-resolve the alert and update the status to "Resolved."
   Else if the anomaly persists beyond 2 weeks, Then the system must escalate the alert priority.

4. If no attendance data exists for a new member (less than 2 weeks enrolled), Then the system must not generate anomaly alerts until a baseline is established.

5. If multiple anomaly alerts exist for the same member, Then the system must consolidate them into a single alert with a combined severity assessment.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the AI generates an alert when a member has 3 consecutive unexplained absences.
2. Verify that the alert displays the member's name, anomaly type, attendance trend, and recommended action on the dashboard.
3. Verify that clicking "Acknowledge" marks the alert as acknowledged with a timestamp.
4. Verify that clicking "Escalate to Care Team" forwards the alert to the assigned care team with details and notes.
5. Verify that an alert is auto-resolved when the member returns to baseline attendance within 2 weeks.
6. Verify that alerts are sorted by severity and recency on the dashboard.

Negative:
7. Verify that no anomaly alert is generated for a new member with less than 2 weeks of enrollment (no baseline established).
8. Verify that a planned absence (pre-notified) does not trigger an anomaly alert.
9. Verify that dismissing an alert without acknowledgment logs a warning for compliance tracking.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that attendance anomaly alerts are displayed with a distinct color or icon (e.g., orange for medium, red for high severity).
11. Verify that the alert detail view shows a visual attendance trend chart for the member.
12. Verify that the "Acknowledge" and "Escalate" buttons are clearly labeled and accessible in the alert detail view.
13. Verify that the dashboard notification count reflects the number of unacknowledged attendance anomaly alerts.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a member has a sporadic attendance pattern as their baseline — alerts should only trigger for deviations from their established irregular pattern.
15. Verify that multiple anomaly alerts for the same member are consolidated into a single alert with combined severity.
16. Verify behavior when the AI engine is unavailable — existing alerts should remain visible, and a notification should indicate that real-time anomaly detection is paused.

Security:
17. Verify that attendance anomaly alerts are only visible to authorized providers and care team members assigned to the member.
18. Verify that alert escalation emails or notifications do not expose sensitive member data to unauthorized recipients.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that attendance anomaly detection processes and generates alerts within 10 minutes of the attendance data being recorded.
2. Verify that the dashboard loads and displays up to 100 active anomaly alerts within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive automatic flags when a member exhibits patterns of frequent falls or emergency room visits.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive automated AI-generated flags when a member shows recurring patterns of falls or emergency room (ER) visits, which may indicate a deteriorating health condition, medication side effects, environmental hazards, or inadequate care planning. The AI engine analyzes incident reports, ER visit records, and health event logs for each member, detecting patterns such as two or more falls within a 30-day period or multiple ER visits within a quarter. Flags are presented on the provider's dashboard and on the member's profile page with contextual information including the number and dates of incidents, potential contributing factors identified by the AI, and suggested interventions (e.g., fall risk protocol activation, medication review). The provider can review the flag, initiate a care plan update, or escalate to a specialist. Early identification of fall and ER visit patterns enables timely interventions that can reduce hospitalizations, improve member safety, and lower care costs. Without this automated pattern recognition, providers may not detect escalating trends until a serious adverse event occurs.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a member has 2 or more falls within 30 days, Then the system must generate a "Fall Pattern" flag with "High" priority.
   Else if a member has 1 fall with contributing risk factors (e.g., new medication, mobility decline), Then the system must generate a "Fall Risk Watch" flag with "Medium" priority.

2. If a member has 2 or more ER visits within 90 days, Then the system must generate an "ER Visit Pattern" flag with "High" priority.
   Else if a member has 1 ER visit with a related prior incident, Then the system must generate an "ER Watch" flag with "Medium" priority.

3. If the provider clicks "Update Care Plan" from the flag, Then the system must open the care plan editor with AI-suggested interventions pre-populated.
   Else if the provider clicks "Escalate to Specialist," Then the system must create a referral with the flag details.

4. If no new incidents occur for 60 days after the flag was raised, Then the system must auto-resolve the flag.
   Else if new incidents occur within the 60-day window, Then the system must escalate the flag priority.

5. If both fall and ER patterns are detected for the same member, Then the system must display a combined "Critical Health Pattern" flag.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the system generates a "Fall Pattern" flag when a member has 2 falls within 30 days.
2. Verify that the system generates an "ER Visit Pattern" flag when a member has 2 ER visits within 90 days.
3. Verify that clicking the flag displays incident dates, descriptions, contributing factors, and suggested interventions.
4. Verify that clicking "Update Care Plan" opens the care plan with pre-populated AI-suggested interventions.
5. Verify that clicking "Escalate to Specialist" creates a referral with flag details attached.
6. Verify that the flag auto-resolves when no new incidents occur for 60 days.

Negative:
7. Verify that a single isolated fall without contributing risk factors does not generate a flag.
8. Verify that a single ER visit for an unrelated cause does not trigger an "ER Visit Pattern" flag.
9. Verify that resolved flags are not displayed on the active dashboard but are accessible in the flag history.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that fall pattern flags are displayed with a distinct icon and color (e.g., red fall hazard icon).
11. Verify that ER visit flags are displayed with a different icon and color (e.g., blue emergency icon).
12. Verify that the flag detail view includes a timeline visualization of incidents.
13. Verify that the "Update Care Plan" and "Escalate to Specialist" buttons are clearly visible in the flag detail view.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a member has both fall and ER patterns — a combined "Critical Health Pattern" flag should be displayed.
15. Verify that incidents recorded retroactively (e.g., late-entry incident reports) are included in the AI pattern analysis.
16. Verify behavior when the same incident is recorded in both the fall log and the ER visit log — it should not be double-counted.

Security:
17. Verify that pattern flags are only visible to authorized providers with access to the member's profile.
18. Verify that escalation referrals do not expose sensitive information to unauthorized specialists.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the AI pattern detection engine processes new incidents and generates flags within 15 minutes of incident recording.
2. Verify that the flag detail view with timeline visualization loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to view AI-generated risk scores predicting likelihood of hospitalization or institutionalization.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to view AI-generated predictive risk scores for each member that assess the likelihood of hospitalization or institutionalization based on a comprehensive analysis of clinical, behavioral, and social data. The AI engine evaluates factors including chronic conditions, recent vital trends, fall history, ER visits, medication complexity, ADL decline, attendance patterns, and social determinants of health to produce a composite risk score on a scale (e.g., Low, Medium, High, Critical). The risk score is displayed on the member's profile summary, the provider's dashboard as part of the high-risk member list, and within the care plan view to inform clinical decision-making. Each score includes a breakdown of contributing factors and recommended preventive actions. Predictive risk scores empower providers to allocate resources proactively, prioritize at-risk members for interventions, and reduce avoidable hospitalizations, which is a key quality metric for value-based care programs. Without predictive risk scoring, providers must rely on reactive care models that address problems only after they escalate.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the AI calculates a member's risk score as "Critical," Then the system must display the score with a red indicator and generate an immediate alert to the assigned provider and care team.
   Else if the score is "High," Then the system must display an orange indicator and add the member to the high-risk dashboard list.
   Else if the score is "Medium," Then the system must display a yellow indicator.
   Else if the score is "Low," Then the system must display a green indicator.

2. If the provider clicks "View Details" on a risk score, Then the system must display the contributing factors with percentage weights and trend indicators (improving, stable, worsening).

3. If a member's risk score changes category (e.g., Medium to High), Then the system must send a notification to the assigned provider.
   Else if the score changes within the same category (e.g., from 65 to 70, both Medium), Then no notification is needed.

4. If insufficient data is available to calculate a risk score (new member, less than 30 days), Then the system must display "Insufficient Data" instead of a score.

5. If the AI engine encounters an error during score calculation, Then the system must retain the last known valid score and display a "Last updated: [date]" timestamp.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the member's profile displays an AI-generated risk score with the correct severity level (Low, Medium, High, Critical).
2. Verify that clicking "View Details" on a risk score shows contributing factors with percentage weights and trend indicators.
3. Verify that the "High-Risk Members" dashboard widget displays members with High and Critical risk scores.
4. Verify that clicking "View Recommended Actions" shows AI-suggested preventive interventions tailored to the member.
5. Verify that the risk score updates when new clinical data (vitals, incidents) is recorded for the member.
6. Verify that a notification is sent when a member's risk score category changes (e.g., Medium to High).

Negative:
7. Verify that "Insufficient Data" is displayed for new members with less than 30 days of enrollment.
8. Verify that the system retains the last valid score and shows a "Last updated" timestamp when the AI engine encounters an error.
9. Verify that low-risk members are not displayed in the "High-Risk Members" dashboard widget.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the risk score is displayed with color-coded indicators: green (Low), yellow (Medium), orange (High), red (Critical).
11. Verify that the risk score is prominently visible on the member's profile summary without requiring scrolling.
12. Verify that the contributing factors breakdown uses a clear visual format (e.g., bar chart or weighted list).
13. Verify that the "High-Risk Members" widget displays member name, risk score, and primary contributing factor.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a member's risk score oscillates between two categories within a short period — notifications should not be sent excessively.
15. Verify that retroactively entered clinical data triggers a risk score recalculation.
16. Verify that the risk score considers data from all relevant modules (vitals, incidents, ADLs, medications, attendance).

Security:
17. Verify that risk scores are only visible to authorized providers with appropriate role-based access.
18. Verify that the AI risk score calculation algorithm and contributing factor weights are not exposed via client-side API responses.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that risk score recalculation completes within 30 seconds after new clinical data is recorded.
2. Verify that the "High-Risk Members" dashboard widget loads and displays up to 200 members within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to run claims through an AI-based scrubber before submission that validates CPT/ICD-10 code pairings, checks for missing required fields, identifies inconsistencies against PA records, assigns a denial risk score based on historical rejection patterns, and flags high-risk claims for manual review with suggested corrective actions
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to run claims through a comprehensive AI-based claim scrubber before submission, performing multiple layers of validation to reduce claim denials and improve first-pass acceptance rates. The scrubber validates CPT/ICD-10 code pairings to ensure medical necessity alignment, checks for missing required fields (member demographics, service dates, provider NPI, authorization numbers), and cross-references claims against Prior Authorization (PA) records for consistency in approved services, units, and date ranges. Additionally, the AI assigns a denial risk score (Low, Medium, High) based on historical rejection patterns from the payer and flags high-risk claims for manual review with specific suggested corrective actions (e.g., "CPT 97110 typically denied without modifier GP for this payer"). The scrubber results are displayed in a Claim Review dashboard showing the claim number, status (Pass, Warning, Fail), risk score, and a list of issues found with recommended fixes. This multi-layered claim scrubbing significantly reduces revenue cycle disruptions by catching errors before they reach the payer, saving staff time spent on denial management and appeals. Without this capability, claims are submitted with undetected errors, leading to high denial rates, delayed reimbursements, and increased administrative burden.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the claim has invalid CPT/ICD-10 pairings, Then the system must flag the claim as "Fail" and display specific pairing issues with suggested corrections.
   Else if the pairings are valid, Then the system must pass the code validation check.

2. If the claim has missing required fields, Then the system must flag the claim as "Fail" and list the missing fields.
   Else if all required fields are present, Then the system must pass the completeness check.

3. If the claim details are inconsistent with the PA record (e.g., units exceed authorized amount, service outside PA date range), Then the system must flag the inconsistency and display the PA-authorized values.
   Else if the claim is consistent with the PA, Then the PA consistency check must pass.

4. If the denial risk score is "High," Then the system must flag the claim for mandatory manual review and display suggested corrective actions.
   Else if the risk score is "Medium," Then the system must display a warning with recommendations but allow submission.
   Else if the risk score is "Low," Then the system must mark the claim as "Pass."

5. If the provider corrects all flagged issues and re-runs the scrubber, Then the updated claim must be re-evaluated and the results must reflect the corrections.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that clicking "Run Scrubber" validates the claim and displays results including code validation, missing fields, PA consistency, and risk score.
2. Verify that invalid CPT/ICD-10 pairings are listed with explanations and suggested corrections.
3. Verify that missing required fields are listed with direct links to the corresponding fields.
4. Verify that PA inconsistencies are detailed with both claimed and authorized values.
5. Verify that a "High" risk score claim requires manual review before submission is allowed.
6. Verify that a "Low" risk score claim with no issues is marked "Pass" and can be submitted immediately.
7. Verify that correcting all flagged issues and re-running the scrubber updates the results and risk score.

Negative:
8. Verify that a claim with a "Fail" status cannot be submitted without resolving the flagged issues.
9. Verify that running the scrubber on a completely empty claim form flags all required fields as missing.
10. Verify that submitting a claim without running the scrubber first displays a prompt "Run the claim scrubber before submission."

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify that the Claim Review dashboard displays claim number, status (Pass/Warning/Fail), risk score badge, and issues list.
12. Verify that "Pass" status is shown in green, "Warning" in yellow, and "Fail" in red.
13. Verify that the suggested corrective actions are displayed in an actionable format (clickable links to fields).
14. Verify that a loading indicator is shown while the scrubber is processing the claim.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify behavior when the claim references a PA record that has expired — the system should flag it as an inconsistency.
16. Verify behavior when the payer's historical rejection data is insufficient — the risk score should default to "Medium" with a note about limited data.
17. Verify that running the scrubber on a batch of claims processes each claim independently.

Security:
18. Verify that only authorized billing staff and providers can run the claim scrubber.
19. Verify that claim data transmitted to the AI scrubber engine is encrypted and not stored beyond the session.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the claim scrubber completes processing and displays results for a single claim within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that batch scrubbing of 50 claims completes within 60 seconds and displays results for all claims.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive AI suggestions for appropriate modifiers and diagnostic codes based on encounter documentation details.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive AI-generated suggestions for CPT modifiers and ICD-10 diagnostic codes based on the clinical encounter documentation, ensuring accurate coding that maximizes reimbursement while maintaining compliance. When the provider completes an encounter note or documentation form, the AI engine analyzes the clinical content — including documented procedures, patient conditions, treatment details, and clinical findings — and suggests appropriate CPT modifiers (e.g., GP for physical therapy, 25 for significant E/M service) and ICD-10 codes that accurately reflect the encounter. Suggestions are displayed in a dedicated panel adjacent to the coding fields, showing the suggested code, its description, confidence level, and the documentation excerpt that supports the suggestion. The provider can accept, modify, or reject each suggestion before finalizing the encounter coding. This AI-assisted coding reduces the risk of under-coding (lost revenue), over-coding (compliance risk), and incorrect code pairing that leads to claim denials. Without this feature, providers must rely on manual code lookup and their own coding knowledge, which is error-prone and time-consuming, especially for complex encounters.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the encounter documentation contains sufficient clinical detail to determine codes, Then the AI must display code suggestions with confidence levels.
   Else if the documentation is vague or insufficient, Then the AI must display a message "Additional clinical detail needed for accurate code suggestions" without generating suggestions.

2. If the provider accepts a suggested ICD-10 code, Then the system must apply the code to the diagnosis field and validate the pairing with the selected CPT code.
   Else if the provider accepts a modifier, Then the system must append the modifier to the CPT code.

3. If the AI suggests multiple codes for the same condition, Then the system must display them ranked by confidence level with the highest confidence suggestion first.

4. If the provider manually enters a code that conflicts with the AI suggestion, Then the system must display a warning "Manually entered code differs from AI recommendation. Please verify."

5. If the AI engine is unavailable, Then the system must allow manual code entry without suggestions and display "AI code suggestions are currently unavailable."
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that completing an encounter note and navigating to the coding section displays AI-suggested CPT modifiers and ICD-10 codes.
2. Verify that each suggestion shows the code, description, confidence level, and supporting documentation excerpt.
3. Verify that clicking "Accept" on a suggested ICD-10 code populates the diagnosis field.
4. Verify that clicking "Accept" on a suggested modifier appends it to the CPT code.
5. Verify that rejecting a suggestion dismisses it without changing the coding fields.
6. Verify that updating the encounter documentation refreshes the AI suggestions.

Negative:
7. Verify that vague or insufficient documentation displays "Additional clinical detail needed for accurate code suggestions" without generating suggestions.
8. Verify that manually entering a code conflicting with the AI suggestion displays a verification warning.
9. Verify that when the AI engine is unavailable, manual code entry is still possible with an "AI code suggestions are currently unavailable" message.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the suggestions panel is positioned adjacent to the coding fields for easy reference.
11. Verify that confidence levels are visually indicated (e.g., green for High, yellow for Medium).
12. Verify that hovering over a modifier displays a tooltip with the modifier's definition and usage guidelines.
13. Verify that the "Accept" and "Reject" buttons are clearly labeled for each suggestion.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that the AI handles encounters with multiple diagnoses by suggesting codes for each documented condition.
15. Verify behavior when the documentation is in free-text format versus structured templates — suggestions should work for both.
16. Verify that previously accepted codes are not re-suggested if the documentation is refreshed.

Security:
17. Verify that AI code suggestion requests do not log or expose patient-identifiable information outside the system.
18. Verify that only authorized providers and billing staff can view and accept AI code suggestions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that AI code suggestions are generated and displayed within 3 seconds of navigating to the coding section.
2. Verify that refreshing suggestions after documentation update completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive AI-powered staffing recommendations based on census projections, patient acuity levels, and required skill mix.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive AI-powered staffing recommendations that optimize workforce allocation based on projected census data, patient acuity levels, and required skill mix for the facility. The AI engine analyzes historical census trends, current enrollment, patient acuity scores (derived from care needs, medical complexity, and ADL dependencies), and regulatory staffing ratios to generate daily and weekly staffing recommendations specifying the number and type of staff needed (e.g., RNs, CNAs, therapists, activity coordinators). Recommendations are displayed on a Staffing Dashboard showing projected census, required staff by role, current scheduled staff, and any staffing gaps or surpluses. The provider can adjust recommendations manually, approve the suggested schedule, or request the AI to generate alternative scenarios (e.g., what-if analysis for increased census). Smart staffing reduces overtime costs, ensures regulatory compliance with mandated staffing ratios, and improves care quality by matching staff skills to patient needs. Without AI-assisted staffing, providers often rely on static schedules that do not account for fluctuating census and acuity, leading to understaffing on high-acuity days or overstaffing on low-census days.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the projected census exceeds current staffing capacity, Then the system must highlight the gap and suggest specific additional staff roles needed.
   Else if the current staffing meets or exceeds recommendations, Then the system must show a "Staffing Adequate" indicator.

2. If patient acuity is "High" for more than 30% of the projected census, Then the system must recommend additional RN coverage and flag the acuity concern.
   Else if acuity levels are predominantly "Low," Then the system must recommend standard staffing ratios.

3. If the provider manually adjusts the recommended staffing, Then the system must recalculate and display whether the adjusted staffing meets minimum regulatory requirements.
   Else if the manual adjustment falls below regulatory minimums, Then the system must display a warning "Below minimum staffing ratio."

4. If the provider approves the recommended schedule, Then the system must lock the schedule and push it to the scheduling module.

5. If historical data is insufficient for projections (e.g., new facility), Then the system must use industry-standard staffing ratios as the baseline recommendation.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Staffing Dashboard displays AI-generated recommendations with projected census, required staff by role, scheduled staff, and gaps.
2. Verify that the recommendation includes specific staff types (RNs, CNAs, therapists) and counts for each projected day.
3. Verify that staffing gaps are highlighted in red with suggested corrective actions.
4. Verify that manually adjusting staff counts recalculates the adequacy indicators.
5. Verify that clicking "Approve Schedule" saves the schedule and pushes it to the scheduling module.
6. Verify that the "What-If Analysis" generates revised recommendations when the provider inputs an adjusted census number.

Negative:
7. Verify that adjusting staff below regulatory minimums displays a "Below minimum staffing ratio" warning.
8. Verify that the system does not allow approving a schedule that falls below minimum regulatory staffing requirements.
9. Verify that if the AI engine is unavailable, a fallback recommendation based on standard ratios is displayed with a disclaimer.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the Staffing Dashboard displays a clear weekly view with daily breakdowns.
11. Verify that gaps are visually distinct (red) and surpluses are marked differently (blue or green).
12. Verify that the "What-If Analysis" input field accepts only numeric values for census projections.
13. Verify that the "Approve Schedule" button is disabled until the provider has reviewed all gaps.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior for a new facility with less than 30 days of census data — the system should use industry-standard ratios.
15. Verify that sudden census changes (e.g., 20% increase in admissions) trigger a real-time recommendation update.
16. Verify behavior when all staff roles are fully staffed and no gaps exist — the system should show "Staffing Optimal."

Security:
17. Verify that only authorized management-level providers can view and approve staffing recommendations.
18. Verify that staffing data and AI recommendations are not accessible via unauthorized API endpoints.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the Staffing Dashboard loads AI recommendations for a 7-day forecast within 5 seconds.
2. Verify that the "What-If Analysis" generates revised recommendations within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive AI suggestions for optimal transportation routes considering traffic patterns, member locations, and time windows.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive AI-generated route optimization suggestions for member transportation, factoring in real-time and historical traffic patterns, member pickup/drop-off locations, scheduled appointment time windows, and vehicle capacity constraints. The AI engine calculates the most efficient routes for transportation vehicles by grouping geographically proximate members, sequencing stops to minimize total travel time, and accounting for time-window constraints (e.g., members must arrive at the facility by 9:00 AM). The optimized routes are displayed on a Route Planning dashboard showing a map view with member locations, suggested route paths, estimated travel times, and vehicle assignments. The provider can adjust routes manually (e.g., add/remove a member from a route), regenerate routes with updated constraints, or approve routes for the transportation team. Route optimization reduces transportation costs, minimizes member wait times, and ensures timely arrivals, which is particularly critical for Adult Day Health programs where attendance affects billing and care delivery. Without AI route optimization, transportation coordinators must plan routes manually, often resulting in suboptimal paths, longer travel times, and higher fuel costs.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the AI generates routes that fit within all time-window constraints, Then the system must display the routes as "Optimized" with green status indicators.
   Else if one or more time constraints cannot be met, Then the system must flag the affected routes as "At Risk" with yellow indicators and suggest adding a vehicle or adjusting pickup times.

2. If the provider manually adjusts a route, Then the system must recalculate the route metrics and display updated estimated times.
   Else if the manual adjustment makes the route infeasible (e.g., exceeds vehicle capacity), Then the system must display a warning.

3. If real-time traffic data indicates a significant delay on an approved route, Then the system must suggest an alternative path and notify the driver.
   Else if the delay is minor (under 5 minutes), Then the system must update the ETA without generating an alert.

4. If a member is added to a route after approval, Then the system must recalculate the route with the new stop and update the driver's device.

5. If no traffic data is available (API outage), Then the system must use historical average travel times for route optimization.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the Route Planning dashboard displays AI-optimized routes on a map with member locations and route paths.
2. Verify that reviewing a specific route shows the sequence of stops, estimated pickup times, and total route duration.
3. Verify that adding a member to a route triggers AI recalculation and updates the route.
4. Verify that removing a member from a route triggers AI recalculation and optimizes the remaining stops.
5. Verify that clicking "Approve Routes" pushes the routes to the transportation team's mobile devices.
6. Verify that real-time traffic changes trigger alternative route suggestions.

Negative:
7. Verify that adding a member that exceeds vehicle capacity displays a "Vehicle capacity exceeded" warning.
8. Verify that a route with unachievable time constraints is flagged as "At Risk" with a suggested resolution.
9. Verify that an empty route (no members assigned) cannot be approved.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the map view clearly shows member locations as pins and routes as colored paths.
11. Verify that each route is displayed in a distinct color for easy differentiation.
12. Verify that estimated pickup times are displayed next to each member pin on the map.
13. Verify that the "Approve Routes" button is disabled until all "At Risk" routes have been addressed.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a member's address is not geocodable — the system should flag the member for manual address verification.
15. Verify behavior when traffic data API is unavailable — routes should be calculated using historical averages.
16. Verify that routes are recalculated correctly when a member's pickup address changes on the same day.

Security:
17. Verify that member addresses and route data are only accessible to authorized transportation staff and providers.
18. Verify that route data pushed to mobile devices is encrypted in transit and at rest.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that AI route optimization for 10 vehicles and 100 members completes within 10 seconds.
2. Verify that re-routing after a member addition or removal completes within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive automated reminders for expiring credentials, authorizations, and compliance tasks with configurable advance notice.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive automated reminders for upcoming expirations and compliance deadlines, including staff credentials (licenses, certifications), member authorizations (Prior Authorizations, service authorizations), and compliance tasks (training renewals, policy acknowledgments), with the ability to configure the advance notice period. The system tracks expiration dates for all credentialed items and authorization records, and generates reminders at configurable intervals (e.g., 90 days, 60 days, 30 days, 7 days before expiration) based on the provider's settings. Reminders are delivered through the in-app notification center, the provider's dashboard, and optionally via email. Each reminder includes the item type, person/member affected, expiration date, days remaining, and a link to take action (e.g., renew credential, request PA extension). Configurable automated reminders prevent lapses in credentials and authorizations that can halt service delivery, expose the facility to regulatory penalties, and disrupt billing. Without this feature, providers must manually track expiration dates across spreadsheets or calendars, which is error-prone and often results in last-minute scrambles or missed deadlines.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a credential expires within the configured advance notice period, Then the system must generate a reminder notification with the credential details and an action link.
   Else if the credential is not within the advance notice period, Then no reminder should be generated.

2. If the provider has configured multiple reminder intervals (e.g., 90, 60, 30, 7 days), Then the system must generate a separate reminder at each interval.
   Else if only one interval is configured, Then only one reminder should be generated.

3. If a credential or authorization is renewed before expiration, Then the system must dismiss pending reminders and schedule new ones based on the updated expiration date.
   Else if the item expires without renewal, Then the system must generate an "Expired" alert with urgent priority.

4. If the provider enables email reminders, Then the system must send email notifications in addition to in-app notifications.
   Else if email reminders are disabled, Then only in-app notifications should be generated.

5. If multiple items expire on the same date, Then the system must generate individual reminders for each item rather than a single combined notification.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that a reminder is generated at 90 days before a staff credential expiration when the 90-day interval is configured.
2. Verify that the reminder displays item type, person name, expiration date, days remaining, and an action link.
3. Verify that clicking the action link navigates to the credential renewal page.
4. Verify that configuring custom intervals (e.g., 45 days, 15 days) generates reminders at those specific thresholds.
5. Verify that renewing a credential dismisses pending reminders and schedules new ones based on the updated expiration.
6. Verify that email reminders are sent when the email notification option is enabled.

Negative:
7. Verify that no reminder is generated for items outside the configured advance notice period.
8. Verify that setting an invalid interval (e.g., 0 days, negative number) displays a validation error.
9. Verify that disabling email reminders stops email delivery while in-app notifications continue.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that reminders appear in the in-app notification center with a clear badge count.
11. Verify that the Reminder Settings page allows separate configuration for credentials, authorizations, and compliance tasks.
12. Verify that expired items are displayed with an "Expired" badge in red on the dashboard.
13. Verify that the notification displays a countdown ("7 days remaining") in an easy-to-read format.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that multiple items expiring on the same date generate individual reminders for each item.
15. Verify behavior when a reminder is generated but the provider has already initiated the renewal process — the reminder should still appear until the renewal is completed.
16. Verify that reminders for items expiring today are generated with "Expires Today" urgency.

Security:
17. Verify that reminder notifications are only visible to authorized providers and administrators responsible for the item.
18. Verify that email reminders do not include sensitive credential details in the email body (only a link to view in-app).
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the reminder engine processes and generates notifications for 1000+ tracked items within 60 seconds during the daily batch run.
2. Verify that the notification center loads and displays up to 200 active reminders within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I want calls made through the RingCentral integration to be automatically transcribed in real-time, with the transcript attached to the lead's or resident's communication timeline, so that no verbal information is lost and all call content is searchable and reviewable.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to have calls made through the RingCentral integration automatically transcribed in real-time and attached to the respective lead's or resident's communication timeline within the Nemi Care Facility Portal. When a provider initiates or receives a call via the RingCentral integration, the system activates real-time speech-to-text transcription, converting the conversation into a text transcript that appears live during the call and is saved upon call completion. The completed transcript is automatically linked to the lead or resident record, appearing in their communication timeline alongside emails, notes, and other interactions. The transcript is fully searchable, allowing providers to search for specific keywords or phrases across all call transcripts to quickly locate relevant information. This feature ensures that no verbal information from phone conversations is lost, reducing reliance on manual note-taking and improving the accuracy of communication records. Without real-time transcription, providers must manually document call details after each conversation, which is time-consuming, prone to omissions, and delays the availability of call information for the care team.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider initiates or receives a call via RingCentral and the transcription service is available, Then the system must activate real-time transcription and display live text.
   Else if the transcription service is unavailable, Then the system must notify the provider "Transcription unavailable. Call will proceed without transcript." and allow the call to continue.

2. If the call ends successfully, Then the system must save the complete transcript and attach it to the lead/resident communication timeline.
   Else if the call is disconnected unexpectedly, Then the system must save the partial transcript up to the disconnection point.

3. If the provider searches for a keyword, Then the system must return matching transcripts with highlighted keyword occurrences.
   Else if no transcripts match the keyword, Then the system must display "No results found."

4. If the call involves a lead, Then the transcript must be attached to the lead's communication timeline.
   Else if the call involves a resident, Then the transcript must be attached to the resident's communication timeline.

5. If the transcription detects multiple speakers, Then the system must label each speaker's segments (e.g., "Provider:", "Caller:").
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that initiating a call via RingCentral activates real-time transcription and displays live text on the provider's screen.
2. Verify that the transcription accurately identifies and labels multiple speakers (Provider, Caller).
3. Verify that upon call completion, the transcript is automatically attached to the lead's communication timeline.
4. Verify that the transcript entry on the timeline shows call date, duration, participants, and full text.
5. Verify that searching for a keyword returns matching transcripts with highlighted keyword occurrences.
6. Verify that receiving an incoming call via RingCentral also triggers automatic transcription.

Negative:
7. Verify that if the transcription service is unavailable, the call proceeds without transcription and the provider sees a notification.
8. Verify that an unexpectedly disconnected call saves a partial transcript up to the disconnection point.
9. Verify that searching for a keyword with no matching transcripts displays "No results found."

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the live transcription panel is displayed alongside the call interface without obstructing call controls.
11. Verify that the transcript entry in the communication timeline is visually distinct from other entry types (emails, notes).
12. Verify that the live transcription text scrolls automatically to show the most recent text.
13. Verify that search result highlights are visually prominent (e.g., bold or background color) within transcript text.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that a call with poor audio quality produces a transcript with "[inaudible]" markers for unclear segments.
15. Verify behavior during a very long call (60+ minutes) — transcription should continue without interruption.
16. Verify that concurrent calls by different providers each generate independent transcripts attached to the correct records.

Security:
17. Verify that call transcripts are encrypted at rest and in transit.
18. Verify that only authorized providers with access to the lead/resident record can view the associated transcripts.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that real-time transcription has a latency of less than 2 seconds between speech and text display.
2. Verify that searching across 10,000+ call transcripts returns results within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I want AI to generate a structured call summary after each inquiry or follow-up call, extracting key information including: patient condition discussed, medical history highlights, family concerns raised, decision outcome, and recommended next action items
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive an AI-generated structured call summary automatically after each inquiry or follow-up call, extracting and organizing key information from the call transcript into predefined sections for quick review and documentation. After the call ends and the transcript is finalized, the AI engine processes the transcript and generates a summary with sections: Patient Condition Discussed (clinical topics mentioned), Medical History Highlights (relevant past conditions or treatments referenced), Family Concerns Raised (worries or questions from family members), Decision Outcome (decisions made during the call), and Recommended Next Action Items (follow-up tasks identified). The structured summary is displayed as a collapsible section within the call record on the lead's or resident's communication timeline, allowing the provider to quickly review without reading the full transcript. The provider can edit the AI-generated summary to correct or supplement any information before marking it as final. This feature eliminates the manual effort of summarizing call content and ensures that critical information from conversations is captured consistently and completely. Without AI-generated summaries, providers must manually write call notes, which are often incomplete, inconsistent in format, and delayed.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the call transcript contains sufficient content, Then the AI must generate a structured summary with all five predefined sections populated.
   Else if the transcript is very short (under 30 seconds of content), Then the AI must display "Transcript too short for structured summary" and show the raw transcript instead.

2. If the provider edits and saves the AI summary, Then the system must save the provider's version as the final summary.
   Else if the provider does not edit the summary, Then the AI-generated version must remain as the default.

3. If the AI identifies action items from the call, Then the provider must be able to convert them into follow-up tasks with a single click.
   Else if no action items are identified, Then the Next Action Items section must display "No action items identified."

4. If a section has no relevant information in the transcript, Then the section must display "No relevant information identified" rather than being removed.

5. If the AI engine fails to generate a summary, Then the system must display "Summary generation failed. Please review the transcript manually." and allow the provider to manually write a summary.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that after a call ends, the AI generates a structured summary with all five sections: Patient Condition, Medical History, Family Concerns, Decision Outcome, Next Action Items.
2. Verify that the summary appears as a collapsible section at the top of the call record in the communication timeline.
3. Verify that clicking "Edit Summary" makes all sections editable.
4. Verify that saving an edited summary persists the provider's changes.
5. Verify that clicking "Create Tasks" from the Next Action Items section creates linked follow-up tasks.
6. Verify that sections with no relevant information display "No relevant information identified."

Negative:
7. Verify that a very short transcript (under 30 seconds) displays "Transcript too short for structured summary" instead of a summary.
8. Verify that if the AI engine fails, the system displays "Summary generation failed. Please review the transcript manually."
9. Verify that an empty transcript (no speech detected) does not trigger summary generation.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the summary is displayed in a clean, collapsible format with clear section headers.
11. Verify that the "Edit Summary" and "Create Tasks" buttons are clearly labeled and accessible.
12. Verify that the collapsible summary defaults to expanded view for the most recent call and collapsed for older calls.
13. Verify that each section of the summary is visually separated with headers and consistent formatting.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that the AI correctly identifies family concerns even when raised by a non-family member (e.g., social worker acting on behalf of the family).
15. Verify behavior when the call covers multiple patients — the summary should be associated with the primary lead/resident and note the involvement of others.
16. Verify that the AI summary handles multilingual calls gracefully, noting sections in the detected language.

Security:
17. Verify that AI-generated summaries are stored with the same access controls as the parent call transcript.
18. Verify that editing a summary maintains an audit trail showing the original AI version and the provider's edits.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the AI-generated call summary is available within 30 seconds of call completion for a 15-minute call.
2. Verify that the communication timeline loads with call summaries and transcripts within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should receive AI-generated, patient-specific clinical recommendations (e.g., medication review needed, fall risk protocol activation, therapy adjustment, hydration monitoring) based on the individual resident's health history, recent vitals, incident patterns, and care activity trends, so that I can make proactive care decisions rather than only reacting to events.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive AI-generated, patient-specific clinical recommendations tailored to each resident's unique health profile, empowering proactive care decision-making rather than reactive event-based responses. The AI engine continuously analyzes the resident's health history, recent vital sign trends, incident patterns (falls, ER visits, behavioral events), medication regimen, ADL scores, and care activity trends to generate actionable clinical recommendations such as medication review needed, fall risk protocol activation, therapy adjustment, or hydration monitoring. Recommendations are displayed on the resident's profile and the provider's clinical dashboard with priority levels (Urgent, Recommended, Informational) and supporting data points that justify the recommendation. The provider can accept a recommendation (which initiates the corresponding care action), dismiss it with a documented reason, or defer it for review at a later date. This proactive recommendation engine helps providers identify early warning signs and take preventive actions that can reduce hospitalizations, improve outcomes, and enhance quality of care metrics. Without AI-driven clinical recommendations, providers must manually review large volumes of patient data across multiple screens to identify potential issues, which is impractical for facilities managing dozens or hundreds of residents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the AI detects a concerning trend (e.g., consecutive BP readings above threshold), Then the system must generate an "Urgent" priority recommendation with the specific action (e.g., "Schedule medication review").
   Else if the trend is moderate, Then the system must generate a "Recommended" priority recommendation.
   Else if the observation is informational, Then the system must generate an "Informational" recommendation.

2. If the provider accepts a recommendation, Then the system must initiate the linked care action and mark the recommendation as "Accepted."
   Else if the provider dismisses with a reason, Then the system must log the reason and mark the recommendation as "Dismissed."
   Else if the provider defers, Then the system must schedule a follow-up reminder.

3. If a recommendation is dismissed but the underlying data trend continues to worsen, Then the system must regenerate the recommendation with escalated priority.

4. If insufficient data exists to generate recommendations for a resident (new admission, less than 7 days), Then the system must display "Recommendations will be available after sufficient data collection."

5. If multiple recommendations exist for the same resident, Then the system must display them in order of priority (Urgent first).
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that the resident's profile displays AI-generated clinical recommendations with priority levels (Urgent, Recommended, Informational).
2. Verify that clicking "View Details" on a recommendation shows supporting data points, trend charts, and rationale.
3. Verify that clicking "Accept" on a recommendation initiates the corresponding care action (e.g., opens medication review form).
4. Verify that clicking "Dismiss" prompts for a reason and logs the dismissal.
5. Verify that clicking "Defer" schedules a reminder for the specified future date.
6. Verify that the clinical dashboard recommendations widget shows all active recommendations sorted by priority.

Negative:
7. Verify that "Recommendations will be available after sufficient data collection" is displayed for a new resident with less than 7 days of data.
8. Verify that dismissing a recommendation without providing a reason displays a validation error.
9. Verify that a dismissed recommendation is regenerated with escalated priority if the underlying trend worsens.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that Urgent recommendations are displayed with a red indicator, Recommended with orange, and Informational with blue.
11. Verify that the recommendations section on the resident profile is prominently positioned and does not require excessive scrolling.
12. Verify that the "Accept," "Dismiss," and "Defer" buttons are clearly labeled and accessible for each recommendation.
13. Verify that trend charts in the recommendation details are clear and include labeled axes.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that the AI generates recommendations across multiple clinical domains (vitals, incidents, ADLs, medications) for a single resident.
15. Verify behavior when the same recommendation type is generated multiple times within a week — duplicates should be consolidated.
16. Verify that recommendations are updated when new clinical data is recorded (e.g., a new vital reading changes the trend).

Security:
17. Verify that clinical recommendations are only visible to authorized providers with access to the resident's record.
18. Verify that the AI recommendation engine does not expose the underlying algorithm weights or model parameters via API responses.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that clinical recommendations are generated and updated within 5 minutes of new clinical data being recorded.
2. Verify that the clinical dashboard loads recommendations for all assigned residents within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, after recording a pain scale entry for a resident, AI should automatically compare the score against the resident's established baseline and flag any sudden or significant increase, alerting the assigned RN and facility director so that pain escalation is caught early and intervention can be initiated.
Description of User Story: This story enables the system to automatically compare a newly recorded pain scale entry against a resident's established baseline pain score and flag any sudden or significant increase for immediate attention by the assigned RN and facility director. When a provider records a pain scale score for a resident, the AI engine immediately compares the new score against the resident's baseline (calculated as the average of the last 5 recorded scores or a clinically set baseline) and evaluates whether the change meets the escalation threshold (e.g., increase of 2 or more points on a 0-10 scale). If a significant increase is detected, the system generates an alert that is sent to the assigned RN and facility director via in-app notification and optional email/SMS, including the resident's name, new score, baseline score, magnitude of change, and recommended immediate actions (e.g., pain assessment, medication review). The alert is also displayed on the clinical dashboard and the resident's profile for visibility. Early detection of pain escalation is critical for timely intervention, preventing unnecessary suffering, and identifying potential underlying conditions (infections, fractures, medication changes). Without automated comparison and alerting, pain score increases may not be noticed until the next scheduled assessment, delaying intervention and potentially worsening the resident's condition.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the new pain score exceeds the baseline by 2 or more points, Then the system must generate a "Pain Escalation" alert and notify the assigned RN and facility director.
   Else if the new score is within 1 point of the baseline, Then no alert should be generated.

2. If the resident has no established baseline (fewer than 5 recorded scores and no clinically set baseline), Then the system must record the score without comparison and display "Baseline not yet established."

3. If the pain escalation alert is generated, Then the system must include recommended immediate actions (pain assessment, medication review, physician notification).

4. If the RN acknowledges the alert and records a follow-up pain assessment, Then the system must update the alert with the follow-up details and adjust the alert status.
   Else if the alert is not acknowledged within 30 minutes, Then the system must re-alert with escalated urgency.

5. If the resident has a clinically set baseline (override), Then the system must use the clinical baseline for comparison instead of the calculated average.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that recording a pain score triggers an immediate AI comparison against the resident's baseline.
2. Verify that a pain score increase of 2+ points generates an alert to the assigned RN and facility director.
3. Verify that the alert includes the resident's name, new score, baseline score, magnitude of change, and recommended actions.
4. Verify that the alert appears in the in-app notification center for both the RN and the facility director.
5. Verify that a pain score within 1 point of the baseline does not generate an alert.
6. Verify that the RN can acknowledge the alert and record follow-up notes directly from the alert.

Negative:
7. Verify that "Baseline not yet established" is displayed for a new resident with fewer than 5 recorded pain scores and no clinical override.
8. Verify that recording a pain score of 0 (no pain) when the baseline is also 0 does not generate an alert.
9. Verify that an alert not acknowledged within 30 minutes is re-sent with escalated urgency.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the pain escalation alert is displayed with a prominent red indicator and urgent priority styling.
11. Verify that the alert notification includes a direct link to the resident's profile and pain assessment history.
12. Verify that the follow-up assessment form is accessible directly from the alert without navigating away.
13. Verify that the resident's profile shows a visual pain trend chart with the baseline and the flagged score highlighted.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify that a clinically set baseline override takes precedence over the calculated average for comparison.
15. Verify behavior when the pain score decreases significantly (e.g., from 8 to 2) — no escalation alert should be generated, but the change should be logged.
16. Verify that concurrent pain entries by different providers for the same resident each trigger independent AI comparisons.

Security:
17. Verify that pain escalation alerts are only sent to authorized personnel (assigned RN and facility director).
18. Verify that alert details (resident name, clinical data) are not exposed in push notification previews on lock screens.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the AI comparison and alert generation completes within 5 seconds of recording the pain score.
2. Verify that the alert notification is delivered to the RN and facility director within 10 seconds of generation.

User Story: As a Nurse/RN, I want the AI to analyze a new resident's captured medical conditions and ICD-10 codes so that it can suggest the most relevant care plan templates from the master library of 30-50 templates, reducing manual search time and ensuring the most appropriate templates are selected at onboarding.
Description of User Story: This story enables the Nurse/RN to leverage AI analysis of a new resident's captured medical conditions and ICD-10 codes to receive automated suggestions for the most relevant care plan templates from the facility's master library of 30-50 templates during the onboarding process. When a new resident is admitted and their medical conditions and ICD-10 codes are recorded in the system, the AI engine analyzes the clinical profile and matches it against the care plan template library to identify the most appropriate templates based on diagnosis relevance, comorbidity coverage, and clinical best practices. The suggested templates are displayed in a ranked list with relevance scores, showing the template name, the matched conditions/codes, and a brief description of what the template covers. The Nurse/RN can review the suggestions, select one or more templates to apply to the resident's care plan, and customize them as needed. This AI-assisted template matching significantly reduces the time spent manually searching through 30-50 templates and ensures that the most clinically appropriate templates are selected, reducing the risk of missing relevant care protocols. Without this feature, nurses must manually review the entire template library for each new admission, which is time-consuming and may result in suboptimal template selection.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the resident's ICD-10 codes match one or more templates with high relevance, Then the system must display those templates at the top of the suggestion list with "Recommended" labels.
   Else if no high-relevance matches are found, Then the system must display the best available matches with "Possible Match" labels and prompt the Nurse/RN to browse the full library.

2. If the Nurse/RN selects and applies templates, Then the system must add them to the resident's care plan and make all fields editable.
   Else if the Nurse/RN does not select any templates and proceeds, Then the system must display a warning "No care plan templates selected. Are you sure you want to continue?"

3. If the resident has multiple comorbidities requiring different templates, Then the system must suggest multiple templates covering each condition.

4. If the template library is updated with new templates, Then the AI must include the new templates in future suggestions.

5. If the resident's ICD-10 codes are not recognized by the AI, Then the system must display "Unable to match ICD-10 codes to templates. Please select templates manually." and show the full library.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify that after recording a new resident's ICD-10 codes, the Care Plan setup screen displays AI-suggested templates ranked by relevance.
2. Verify that each suggested template shows the template name, relevance score, matched ICD-10 codes, and description.
3. Verify that selecting and clicking "Apply Templates" adds the selected templates to the resident's care plan.
4. Verify that applied templates are fully editable for resident-specific customization.
5. Verify that clicking "Browse All Templates" displays the full master library of 30-50 templates.
6. Verify that residents with multiple comorbidities receive suggestions covering each condition.

Negative:
7. Verify that proceeding without selecting any template displays "No care plan templates selected. Are you sure you want to continue?"
8. Verify that unrecognized ICD-10 codes display "Unable to match ICD-10 codes to templates. Please select templates manually."
9. Verify that no duplicate template suggestions are shown for overlapping ICD-10 codes.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify that the suggestion list displays "Recommended" badges on high-confidence matches and "Possible Match" on lower-confidence ones.
11. Verify that the relevance score is displayed as a percentage or star rating for clarity.
12. Verify that the template suggestion list is scrollable and displays up to all 50 templates if needed.
13. Verify that the "Apply Templates" and "Browse All Templates" buttons are prominently displayed.

Edge Cases:
14. Verify behavior when a resident has a rare condition with no matching templates — the system should show "Possible Match" templates and prompt manual selection.
15. Verify that updating the template library includes new templates in AI suggestions for subsequent admissions.
16. Verify that removing an ICD-10 code from the resident's record after template suggestion refreshes the suggestions if revisited.

Security:
17. Verify that only authorized Nurses/RNs and clinical staff can view and apply care plan template suggestions.
18. Verify that the AI template matching engine does not expose the master template library structure via client-side responses.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify that the AI template suggestions are generated and displayed within 3 seconds of navigating to the Care Plan setup screen.
2. Verify that applying 5 templates simultaneously to a resident's care plan completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I want the system to suggest specific safety interventions (e.g., fall protocols or diabetic monitoring) based on flagged risk scores and vital sign baselines captured during the initial assessment.
Description of User Story: This story enables the provider to receive system-suggested safety interventions tailored to a resident's specific risk profile, derived from flagged risk scores and vital sign baselines captured during the initial assessment upon admission. When a new resident completes their initial assessment (including vital sign baselines, medical conditions, fall risk score, diabetes risk, mobility assessment, and cognitive status), the AI engine evaluates the assessment data to identify applicable safety interventions from a predefined intervention library (e.g., fall prevention protocol, diabetic monitoring schedule, seizure precautions, pressure ulcer prevention, wandering alert activation). The suggested interventions are displayed on the Resident Safety Plan screen with the intervention name, the triggering risk factor, priority level, and a brief protocol description. The provider can review each suggestion, accept it to activate the intervention in the resident's care plan, modify the intervention parameters, or decline with a documented reason.
