8. UI Scenario: The language indicator appears as a globe icon with the current language abbreviation (e.g., "EN", "ES", "FR") in the application header.
9. UI Scenario: The language selector dropdown displays all supported languages with their native names (e.g., "Espanol", "Francais", "Deutsch").
10. UI Scenario: When switching languages, a brief loading overlay appears while the interface re-renders in the new language.
11. Edge Case: User switches language mid-session while filling out a form; the form labels change but user-entered data is preserved.
12. Edge Case: User's browser has multiple preferred languages configured; the system selects the highest-priority supported language.
13. Security: Language preference changes are logged in the user activity audit trail to track profile modifications.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Automatic language detection and interface rendering in the detected language completes within 1 second of page load.
2. Switching language via the language selector re-renders the entire interface within 2 seconds without a full page reload.

User Story: As a user, I should see all app content (labels, menus, notifications, instructions) translated accurately into my preferred language.
Description of User Story: The Content Translation feature ensures that all user-facing text elements in the application are accurately translated into the user's selected preferred language. This includes navigation menu items, page headings, field labels, button texts, tooltip messages, placeholder texts, instructional paragraphs, validation error messages, success/failure notifications, and system-generated content. The translations are managed through a centralized localization resource system that supports adding new languages and updating existing translations without code deployments. Each translated string undergoes contextual accuracy review to ensure medical and healthcare terminology is correctly localized. The system supports right-to-left (RTL) layout rendering for applicable languages such as Arabic or Hebrew, automatically adjusting the interface layout direction.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user's preferred language has complete translations for all UI elements, Then every visible text element appears in the selected language.
2. If a specific string does not have a translation available in the selected language, Then the system falls back to the English version of that string and logs the missing translation for administrators.
3. If the user's language requires RTL layout, Then the system automatically mirrors the interface layout including navigation, text alignment, and icon positioning.
4. If a translation contains medical terminology, Then the system uses the approved healthcare-specific translation rather than a generic translation.
5. If a translation update is deployed, Then users see the updated translations on their next page load without needing to log out and back in.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: User sets language to Spanish and navigates to the Dashboard; all menu items, headings, and labels display in Spanish.
2. Positive Functional: User in French locale submits a form with validation errors; all error messages appear in French.
3. Positive Functional: User receives a success notification after saving a record; the notification text is in the user's preferred language.
4. Positive Functional: User selects Arabic and the entire interface layout mirrors to RTL with proper text alignment and navigation positioning.
5. Negative: A specific tooltip string is missing its Spanish translation; the system displays the English fallback text and logs the missing translation.
6. Negative: User selects a recently added language with partial translations; untranslated strings show in English while translated ones display correctly.
7. Negative: A translation contains a typo in medical terminology; the system displays the stored translation as-is until an administrator updates it.
8. UI Scenario: All translated labels maintain proper text length accommodation, with the UI expanding or wrapping gracefully for languages with longer text (e.g., German).
9. UI Scenario: Buttons and navigation items resize appropriately to accommodate translated text without truncation or overflow.
10. UI Scenario: RTL layout properly mirrors form field layouts, breadcrumbs, and sidebar navigation.
11. Edge Case: A translated string contains HTML entities or special characters; the system renders them correctly without raw HTML display.
12. Edge Case: User switches language on a page with dynamic content (e.g., charts with labels); chart labels update to the new language.
13. Security: Translation resource files are served from the application server and cannot be modified by end users; unauthorized translation API access is blocked.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Page navigation with full content translation renders within 2 seconds regardless of the selected language.
2. Translation resource files for a language load within 1 second on first access and are cached for subsequent page loads.

User Story: As a user, I should receive notifications, reminders, and alerts in my selected language so that I understand system actions clearly.
Description of User Story: The Multilingual Notifications feature ensures that all system-generated notifications, reminders, and alerts are delivered to users in their selected preferred language. This covers in-app notification banners, push notifications, email notifications, SMS alerts, and scheduled reminders for tasks, appointments, credential expirations, and other system events. The notification templates are maintained in a centralized template management system with translations for each supported language. When a notification is triggered, the system looks up the recipient's language preference and renders the notification content from the appropriate language template. Dynamic content within notifications (such as patient names, dates, and record references) is inserted into the localized template while respecting locale-specific date, time, and number formatting conventions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the user's preferred language has a translated template for the notification type, Then the notification is delivered in that language.
2. If a notification template does not have a translation for the user's preferred language, Then the system sends the notification in English and logs the missing template for administrators.
3. If the user changes their language preference after a notification was queued but before delivery, Then the system uses the updated preference for delivery.
4. If a notification contains dynamic dates, Then the system formats dates according to the user's locale (e.g., DD/MM/YYYY for European locales, MM/DD/YYYY for US).
5. If a batch notification is sent to multiple users with different language preferences, Then each user receives the notification in their own preferred language.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: User with Spanish preference receives an in-app credential expiration alert in Spanish with the date formatted as DD/MM/YYYY.
2. Positive Functional: User with French preference receives a task reminder email with subject and body text in French.
3. Positive Functional: Batch notification sent to 10 users with mixed language preferences; each user receives it in their respective language.
4. Positive Functional: User changes language from German to English and the next triggered notification arrives in English.
5. Negative: A notification template for "Appointment Reminder" does not have a Japanese translation; user receives the notification in English as a fallback.
6. Negative: Notification delivery service encounters an error; the system retries delivery and logs the failure without losing the notification.
7. Negative: User has no language preference set in their profile; the system defaults to English for all notifications.
8. UI Scenario: In-app notification banners display the translated text with proper font rendering for the selected language including non-Latin scripts.
9. UI Scenario: Email notifications include a localized header, body, and footer with the organization branding intact across all languages.
10. UI Scenario: Push notifications display the translated text within mobile OS character limits, truncating gracefully if the translated text is longer.
11. Edge Case: User receives a notification while switching languages; the notification uses the language that was active at the time of delivery.
12. Edge Case: A notification template includes placeholders for dynamic data (patient name, date); the placeholders are correctly replaced with actual values in the localized output.
13. Security: Notification content in any language does not expose sensitive patient data in push notification previews; sensitive details are masked or require authentication to view.
Performance Scenarios:
1. In-app notifications render in the user's preferred language within 1 second of the triggering event.
2. Batch notifications for 100 users with mixed language preferences are queued and dispatched within 10 seconds.

User Story: As an Provider, I should be able to create forms using form builder
Description of User Story: The Create Form screen provides Providers with a drag-and-drop form builder interface to design custom forms for various clinical, administrative, and operational purposes. The builder offers a component palette on the left side containing field types such as text input, text area, dropdown, checkbox, radio button, date picker, file upload, signature pad, number input, and section headers. Providers drag components from the palette onto a central canvas area to construct the form layout, and each dropped component opens a configuration panel on the right for setting properties like label, placeholder, required/optional, validation rules, and conditional visibility. The form builder supports multi-page forms with page navigation, form naming, and description fields at the top. A live preview mode allows Providers to see how the form will appear to end users before publishing.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Provider drags a component to an invalid position on the canvas, Then the system shows a visual indicator that the drop is not allowed and the component returns to the palette.
2. If the Provider attempts to save a form without a name, Then the system displays a validation error "Form name is required" and prevents saving.
3. If the Provider adds a conditional visibility rule to a field (e.g., show field B only if field A is "Yes"), Then the preview mode demonstrates the conditional behavior.
4. If the Provider closes the form builder without saving, Then the system displays an unsaved changes confirmation dialog.
5. If the form save operation fails due to a server error, Then the system displays an error message and retains all form builder state so the Provider can retry.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider creates a new form with 5 fields (text input, dropdown, date picker, checkbox, signature), names it "Patient Intake Form", and saves it successfully.
2. Positive Functional: Provider drags a dropdown field onto the canvas, configures it with 4 options (Option A, B, C, D), and the preview shows the dropdown with all options.
3. Positive Functional: Provider adds a conditional visibility rule to show a "Specify Other" text field only when the dropdown value is "Other"; preview demonstrates the rule correctly.
4. Positive Functional: Provider creates a multi-page form with 3 pages and navigation buttons; preview shows page navigation working correctly.
5. Negative: Provider attempts to save a form without entering a form name; system displays "Form name is required" validation error.
6. Negative: Provider drags a component to an invalid position (e.g., inside another component that does not support nesting); system rejects the drop with a visual cue.
7. Negative: Provider navigates away from the form builder with unsaved changes; system displays an unsaved changes confirmation dialog.
8. UI Scenario: The component palette on the left displays categorized field types (Basic, Advanced, Layout) with icons and labels for each component.
9. UI Scenario: The canvas area shows a dotted grid background with drop zones highlighted when a component is being dragged.
10. UI Scenario: The configuration panel on the right slides in when a component is selected, showing tabs for Properties, Validation, and Conditional Logic.
11. Edge Case: Provider creates a form with 50+ fields; the canvas scrolls smoothly and all fields are accessible.
12. Edge Case: Provider rapidly drags and drops multiple components; the system handles each drop correctly without lag or duplication.
13. Security: Only authenticated Providers with form management permissions can access the form builder; unauthorized users are denied access.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The form builder interface loads with the component palette and empty canvas within 2 seconds of clicking "Create Form".
2. Drag-and-drop operations respond within 200 milliseconds with smooth visual feedback during the drag.

User Story: As an Provider, I should be able to archive forms
Description of User Story: The Archive Form feature allows Providers to archive forms that are no longer actively needed, removing them from the active forms list without permanently deleting them. The archive action is available from the forms list via a context menu or action button on each form row. When a Provider initiates an archive action, the system displays a confirmation dialog explaining that the form will be moved to the archived state and will no longer be available for new submissions but existing submissions will be preserved. Archived forms are accessible through an "Archived" filter or tab on the forms list page, allowing Providers to review, restore, or permanently delete archived forms if needed. The archive operation updates the form status and records the archiving user and timestamp for audit purposes.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Provider confirms the archive action, Then the form status changes to "Archived", it is removed from the active list, and the archive timestamp is recorded.
2. If the Provider cancels the archive confirmation dialog, Then the form remains in active status and no changes are made.
3. If the archived form has existing submissions, Then those submissions remain accessible and viewable but no new submissions can be created.
4. If the Provider restores an archived form, Then it returns to active status and becomes available for new submissions.
5. If the Provider attempts to archive a form that is currently being filled out by another user, Then the system completes the archive after the active session ends or warns the Provider about active sessions.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks Archive on "Patient Intake Form", confirms the dialog, and the form disappears from the active list.
2. Positive Functional: Provider navigates to the Archived tab and sees "Patient Intake Form" with the archive date and their username.
3. Positive Functional: Provider restores an archived form; it reappears in the active forms list with "Active" status.
4. Positive Functional: Provider archives a form with 50 existing submissions; all submissions remain viewable under the archived form.
5. Negative: Provider clicks Archive but cancels the confirmation dialog; the form remains active with no status change.
6. Negative: User attempts to submit a new entry for an archived form via a bookmarked URL; system displays "This form is no longer active" message.
7. Negative: Provider attempts to archive a form that is already archived; the archive action is disabled or hidden for that form.
8. UI Scenario: The archive confirmation dialog displays the form name, a warning message about the impact, and "Confirm Archive" and "Cancel" buttons.
9. UI Scenario: The forms list includes a filter toggle or tabs for "Active" and "Archived" forms with a count badge on each.
10. UI Scenario: Archived forms in the list display with a muted visual style and an "Archived" status badge with the archive date.
11. Edge Case: Provider archives the last remaining active form; the active list shows an empty state message "No active forms. Create a new form or restore an archived one."
12. Edge Case: Provider archives and restores the same form multiple times; each action is logged in the audit trail with timestamps.
13. Security: Only Providers with form management permissions can archive or restore forms; the archive action is hidden for users without this permission.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The archive operation completes and the forms list refreshes within 2 seconds of confirming the archive action.
2. Switching between Active and Archived tabs loads the respective forms list within 1 second.

User Story: As an Provider, I should be able to duplicate form
Description of User Story: The Duplicate Form feature allows Providers to create an exact copy of an existing form, enabling rapid form creation based on previously built designs. The duplicate action is accessible from the forms list via a context menu or action button on each form row. When a Provider initiates the duplicate action, the system creates a new form with all fields, configurations, validation rules, conditional logic, and layout settings copied from the original form. The duplicated form is automatically named with a "Copy of" prefix followed by the original form name, and it opens in the form builder for the Provider to make modifications before saving. The duplicated form is independent of the original, so changes to one do not affect the other. This feature significantly reduces the time needed to create similar forms for different departments or use cases.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Provider duplicates a form successfully, Then the form builder opens with the complete copy and a "Copy of" prefix on the form name.
2. If the Provider duplicates a form and saves it without changing the name, Then the system saves it with the "Copy of" prefix as the form name.
3. If the Provider duplicates a form and then cancels without saving, Then no new form is created and the forms list remains unchanged.
4. If the original form is archived after a duplicate is created, Then the duplicated form remains active and unaffected.
5. If the duplication process fails due to a server error, Then the system displays an error message and the original form remains unchanged.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider duplicates "Patient Intake Form" and sees "Copy of Patient Intake Form" open in the form builder with all 10 fields from the original.
2. Positive Functional: Provider duplicates a form, changes the name to "Facility B Intake Form", adds 2 new fields, and saves; both forms exist independently.
3. Positive Functional: Provider duplicates a form with conditional logic; the copied form retains all conditional rules working correctly.
4. Positive Functional: Provider duplicates a multi-page form; all pages, navigation, and page-specific fields are copied accurately.
5. Negative: Provider clicks Duplicate but the server encounters an error; system displays an error message and the original form is unaffected.
6. Negative: Provider duplicates a form and cancels without saving; no new form appears in the forms list.
7. Negative: Provider attempts to duplicate an archived form; the system either allows duplication into an active state or displays a message indicating the form must be restored first.
8. UI Scenario: The "Duplicate" action appears as an icon or menu item in the actions column of the forms list alongside Edit and Archive options.
9. UI Scenario: The form builder opens with a banner indicating "This is a duplicate of [Original Form Name]" for clarity.
10. UI Scenario: The duplicated form name field is highlighted or focused so the Provider can immediately rename it.
11. Edge Case: Provider duplicates a form with 50+ fields and complex conditional logic; all fields and rules are copied without data loss.
12. Edge Case: Provider duplicates a form whose name is already at the maximum character limit; the system truncates the "Copy of" prefix or handles the overflow gracefully.
13. Security: Only Providers with form management permissions can duplicate forms; the duplicate action is hidden for users without this permission.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The form duplication operation completes and the form builder opens with the copied form within 3 seconds for forms with up to 50 fields.
2. Saving the duplicated form as a new entry completes within 2 seconds.

User Story: As an Provider, I should be able to save a form as a template, view the template library, edit, delete and assign templates to specific facilities
Description of User Story: The Manage Templates feature provides Providers with a complete template lifecycle management system within the Form Builder module. Providers can save any created or existing form as a reusable template, which is stored in a centralized Template Library accessible from the Form Builder settings. The Template Library displays all saved templates in a list or grid view with the template name, description, creation date, creator name, and facility assignments. From the library, Providers can edit a template to update its fields and configuration, delete templates that are no longer needed (with a confirmation dialog), and assign templates to one or more specific facilities to control which locations can use each template. Facility assignment ensures that only relevant templates appear when users at a particular facility create new forms, maintaining organizational consistency across multi-facility operations.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Provider saves a form as a template with a unique name, Then the template is added to the Template Library and is immediately available for use.
2. If the Provider attempts to save a template with a name that already exists, Then the system displays a validation error "A template with this name already exists" and prevents saving.
3. If the Provider deletes a template that is currently assigned to facilities, Then the system warns about the assignment and upon confirmation removes the template and its facility associations.
4. If the Provider assigns a template to no facilities, Then the template is available to all facilities by default (global template).
5. If the Provider edits a template that has been used to create existing forms, Then the existing forms are not affected and only new forms created from the template use the updated version.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider saves a completed form as a template named "Standard Intake Template" with a description; it appears in the Template Library.
2. Positive Functional: Provider navigates to the Template Library and sees 5 templates with names, descriptions, dates, creators, and facility assignments displayed.
3. Positive Functional: Provider edits a template by adding a new field and saves; the updated template reflects the change in the library.
4. Positive Functional: Provider assigns a template to "Facility A" and "Facility B"; users at Facility C no longer see this template when creating forms.
5. Positive Functional: Provider deletes an unused template after confirming the deletion dialog; the template is removed from the library.
6. Negative: Provider attempts to save a template with a duplicate name; system displays "A template with this name already exists" error.
7. Negative: Provider attempts to delete a template assigned to 3 facilities; system warns about active assignments and requires confirmation.
8. Negative: Provider attempts to save a template without entering a name; system displays a required field validation error.
9. UI Scenario: The Template Library displays templates in a list view with columns for Name, Description, Created Date, Creator, and Facilities, with action buttons for Edit, Delete, and Assign.
10. UI Scenario: The "Assign Facilities" interface presents a multi-select dropdown or checkbox list of all available facilities with currently assigned ones pre-checked.
11. UI Scenario: The "Save as Template" dialog includes fields for template name, description text area, and an optional facility assignment section.
12. Edge Case: Provider deletes all templates from the library; the library shows an empty state message "No templates available. Save a form as a template to get started."
13. Edge Case: Provider assigns a template to a facility that is later deactivated; the template assignment shows the deactivated facility with a visual indicator.
14. Security: Only Providers with template management permissions can create, edit, delete, or assign templates; other users can only view and use assigned templates.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Template Library loads and displays all templates within 2 seconds including facility assignment details.
2. Saving a form as a template completes within 2 seconds and the template immediately appears in the library.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to save and submit a form
Description of User Story: The Save and Submit Form feature provides Providers with two distinct actions when working with forms: saving a form as a draft for later completion and submitting a finalized form for processing. The save (draft) action persists all entered data without triggering validation of required fields, allowing Providers to partially complete a form and return to it later. The submit action performs comprehensive validation of all required fields, data formats, and business rules before accepting the form as a final submission. Upon successful submission, the form transitions to a "Submitted" status, becomes read-only, a confirmation message with a reference number is displayed, and relevant stakeholders are notified. The forms list displays the status of each form entry (Draft, Submitted) with the last modified date, enabling Providers to track their in-progress and completed forms.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Provider clicks "Save as Draft" with partially completed fields, Then the system saves the data without enforcing required field validation.
2. If the Provider clicks "Submit" and all validations pass, Then the form status changes to "Submitted", it becomes read-only, and a reference number is generated.
3. If the Provider clicks "Submit" with validation errors, Then the system scrolls to the first error, highlights all invalid fields, and displays inline error messages.
4. If the Provider attempts to edit a submitted form, Then the system prevents editing and displays a message "This form has been submitted and cannot be modified."
5. If the save or submit operation fails due to a server error, Then the system displays an error message and retains all form data so the Provider can retry.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider fills out 3 of 10 fields, clicks "Save as Draft", and sees a confirmation; the form appears as "Draft" in the forms list.
2. Positive Functional: Provider opens a saved draft, completes all remaining fields, clicks "Submit", and receives a submission confirmation with reference number REF-2026-001.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the forms list and sees two entries: one with "Draft" status and one with "Submitted" status with their respective last modified dates.
4. Positive Functional: Provider opens a submitted form and sees all data in read-only mode with no editable fields.
5. Negative: Provider clicks "Submit" with 3 required fields empty; system highlights all 3 fields with inline errors and scrolls to the first one.
6. Negative: Provider attempts to edit a submitted form; system displays "This form has been submitted and cannot be modified" message.
7. Negative: Provider clicks "Save as Draft" but the server encounters an error; system displays an error message and retains all entered data.
8. UI Scenario: The form footer displays two buttons: "Save as Draft" styled as a secondary button and "Submit" styled as a primary button.
9. UI Scenario: Draft forms in the list display a yellow "Draft" badge while submitted forms display a green "Submitted" badge.
10. UI Scenario: The submission confirmation displays a success icon, reference number, and options to "View Submission" or "Return to Forms List".
11. Edge Case: Provider saves a draft, another user is assigned to complete it; the second user can open and continue from where the first left off.
12. Edge Case: Provider clicks "Submit" exactly when a session timeout occurs; the system either completes the submission or preserves the form data for retry after re-authentication.
13. Security: Form submissions are validated server-side to prevent bypassing client-side validation; submitted data is encrypted in transit and at rest.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Saving a form as a draft completes within 2 seconds regardless of the number of fields populated.
2. Form submission with full validation of all fields completes and returns the confirmation with reference number within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to create custom roles with fine-grained permissions to control which features and data each staff member can access based on their job function.
Description of User Story: The Role-Based Access Control screen allows the provider administrator to define custom roles and assign granular permissions that govern which features, data, and actions each staff member can access. The interface presents a list of existing roles with their names, descriptions, and the number of assigned users, along with an option to create a new role. When creating or editing a role, the provider selects from a categorized permission tree covering modules such as patient records, billing, scheduling, and reporting, with read, write, and delete toggles for each. The system enforces that at least one admin role with full permissions exists at all times to prevent lockout scenarios. Changes to role permissions take effect immediately for all users assigned to the modified role, and an audit log entry is recorded for every permission change. The screen includes a search and filter capability to quickly locate specific roles or permissions within the hierarchy.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider creates a new role with a name that already exists, Then the system displays a validation error "A role with this name already exists" and prevents saving.
2. If the provider attempts to delete a role that has active users assigned, Then the system blocks deletion and displays a message listing the number of users who must be reassigned first.
3. If the provider selects a parent category in the permission tree, Then all child permissions under that category are automatically selected.
4. If the provider deselects a parent category, Then all child permissions under that category are deselected.
5. If the permission data fails to load, Then the system displays an error message with a Retry option and does not allow role creation or editing until data is loaded.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider administrator creates a new role "Front Desk" with permissions for scheduling (read/write) and patient demographics (read only), saves, and sees the role in the roles list.
2. Positive Functional: Provider administrator edits the "Nurse" role to add billing read permission, saves, and a nurse user immediately gains access to billing reports.
3. Positive Functional: Provider administrator searches for "billing" in the permissions tree and the system filters to show only billing-related permissions.
4. Positive Functional: Provider administrator views the roles list showing five roles with correct user counts and descriptions.
5. Negative: Provider administrator attempts to create a role with an empty name field; system displays a validation error preventing submission.
6. Negative: Provider administrator tries to delete the only admin role; system blocks the action with a lockout prevention warning.
7. Negative: Provider administrator attempts to delete a role assigned to 10 users; system prevents deletion and prompts reassignment.
8. UI Scenario: The permission tree displays categories (Patient Records, Billing, Scheduling, Reporting) as expandable sections with read/write/delete toggle switches for each sub-permission.
9. UI Scenario: Roles list table displays columns for Role Name, Description, Users Assigned, Last Modified, and Actions with consistent styling.
10. UI Scenario: The Create/Edit Role form organizes the permission tree in a scrollable panel with select-all and deselect-all options at the top.
11. Edge Case: Provider administrator creates a role with no permissions selected; system saves the role but displays a warning that users assigned to this role will have no access.
12. Edge Case: Two administrators edit the same role simultaneously; the system applies the last save and notifies the other administrator of the conflict.
13. Security: Only users with the "manage_roles" permission can access the RBAC configuration; unauthorized users are redirected to an access-denied page and the attempt is logged.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The RBAC roles list page loads with all roles and user counts within 2 seconds.
2. Saving a new or modified role with permission changes completes and propagates to all assigned users within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to require multi-factor authentication for all staff accounts using methods like SMS codes, authenticator apps, or email verification to enhance security.
Description of User Story: The Multi-Factor Authentication configuration screen enables the provider administrator to enforce MFA policies across all staff accounts within the organization. The settings page presents toggles for enabling or disabling MFA enforcement globally, along with options to configure allowed authentication methods including SMS one-time codes, authenticator app (TOTP) integration, and email verification codes. The administrator can set grace periods for new users to set up MFA after their first login and configure backup recovery options such as recovery codes. Each staff member receives guided setup instructions when MFA is enabled, walking them through linking their preferred authentication method. The system maintains a status dashboard showing which users have completed MFA setup and which are still pending, allowing administrators to send reminder notifications to non-compliant users.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the administrator enables MFA with a 7-day grace period, Then new users can log in without MFA for up to 7 days after account creation before being required to set it up.
2. If a staff member loses access to their MFA device, Then they can use a backup recovery code to regain access and reconfigure their MFA method.
3. If all MFA methods are disabled while MFA enforcement is on, Then the system prevents saving and displays an error that at least one method must be enabled.
4. If the administrator disables MFA enforcement, Then existing MFA configurations for users are retained but not required at login.
5. If a staff member enters an incorrect MFA code three consecutive times, Then the system locks the account temporarily and notifies the administrator.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Administrator enables MFA enforcement with SMS and authenticator app methods, saves, and all staff receive MFA setup notification emails.
2. Positive Functional: Staff member completes MFA setup by scanning a QR code with an authenticator app and verifying with a generated code.
3. Positive Functional: Staff member logs in, enters correct credentials, receives an SMS code, enters it correctly, and gains access to the system.
4. Positive Functional: Administrator views MFA status dashboard showing 15 of 20 users have completed setup and sends reminders to the remaining 5.
5. Negative: Staff member enters an expired MFA code; system rejects the code and prompts for a new one.
6. Negative: Staff member enters incorrect MFA codes three consecutive times; system locks the account and displays a lockout message.
7. Negative: Administrator attempts to save MFA settings with enforcement enabled but all methods disabled; system displays validation error.
8. UI Scenario: MFA settings page displays a global enforcement toggle at the top, followed by method-specific configuration cards for SMS, Authenticator App, and Email with individual enable/disable toggles.
9. UI Scenario: The MFA status dashboard displays a table with columns for User Name, Email, MFA Method, Setup Status (Complete/Pending), and Actions (Send Reminder).
10. UI Scenario: The MFA setup wizard for staff presents step-by-step instructions with visual guides for scanning QR codes or entering phone numbers.
11. Edge Case: Staff member sets up MFA with an authenticator app, then changes their phone; they use a recovery code to log in and reconfigure MFA on the new device.
12. Edge Case: Administrator enables MFA for the first time on an organization with 500 users; system queues and sends all notification emails without timeout or failure.
13. Security: MFA codes expire after 5 minutes, recovery codes are single-use and hashed in the database, and all MFA-related events are recorded in the security audit log.
Performance Scenarios:
1. MFA verification (SMS delivery and code validation) completes within 5 seconds of the user submitting their credentials.
2. The MFA status dashboard loads the compliance status of up to 500 users within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a provider, I should be able to log every instance of data being viewed, edited, or deleted with details on who accessed it, when, from which device, and what changes were made to maintain HIPAA compliance.
Description of User Story: The Comprehensive Audit Logs screen provides a detailed, searchable record of every data access and modification event within the system to support HIPAA compliance requirements. Each log entry captures the user who performed the action, the timestamp, the action type (view, create, edit, delete), the affected record or module, the device and IP address from which the action was performed, and a before-and-after snapshot of any data changes. The log viewer presents entries in a sortable table with columns for each attribute and supports filtering by date range, user, action type, module, and severity level. An export function allows administrators to download filtered log data in CSV or PDF format for compliance audits. The system retains audit logs for a configurable retention period and prevents any user, including administrators, from modifying or deleting log entries to ensure data integrity.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the administrator filters audit logs by action type "Delete", Then only log entries for delete operations are displayed in the table.
2. If the administrator applies multiple filters simultaneously (date range, user, and module), Then the table displays entries matching all selected filter criteria.
3. If the audit log contains more than 1000 entries for the selected filters, Then the system paginates results and displays a total count indicator.
4. If the administrator attempts to modify or delete an audit log entry, Then the system denies the action and displays a message that audit logs are immutable.
5. If the export request involves more than 10,000 entries, Then the system processes the export asynchronously and notifies the administrator when the file is ready for download.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Administrator views audit logs and sees entries for a nurse viewing patient records, a doctor editing a prescription, and an admin deleting an old lead.
2. Positive Functional: Administrator filters logs by "Edit" action type and "Patient Records" module, seeing only edit events for patient records.
3. Positive Functional: Administrator clicks on an edit log entry and views before/after field values showing a patient's phone number changed from "555-0100" to "555-0200".
4. Positive Functional: Administrator exports filtered audit logs as a CSV file containing 500 entries with all columns properly formatted.
5. Negative: Administrator attempts to delete an audit log entry; system denies the action with an immutability warning message.
6. Negative: Administrator applies filters that return no matching entries; system displays "No audit log entries found for the selected criteria" message.
7. Negative: Audit log export for 50,000 records is requested; system queues the export and notifies when ready rather than timing out.
8. UI Scenario: The audit log table displays sortable columns for User, Timestamp, Action, Module, Record ID, Device/IP with alternating row colors for readability.
9. UI Scenario: Filter controls are displayed in a collapsible panel above the table with dropdowns for action type, module, and user, plus a date range picker.
10. UI Scenario: The log detail panel opens as a side drawer showing before/after values in a diff-style format with changed fields highlighted.
11. Edge Case: A single record is edited 100 times in rapid succession; each edit generates a separate audit log entry with accurate timestamps and change details.
12. Edge Case: A user accesses the system from two different devices simultaneously; both sessions generate distinct audit log entries with correct device and IP information.
13. Security: Audit logs are stored in an append-only data store that prevents tampering; access to audit logs is restricted to users with the "audit_log_viewer" permission, and all access to the audit log viewer is itself logged.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The audit log viewer loads the most recent 100 entries within 3 seconds of navigation, including all column data.
2. Filtering audit logs by date range and action type returns results within 2 seconds for datasets up to 1 million entries.

User Story:
As a Provider, I should be able to view below cards on dashboard home screen.
- Total Members
- New Leads
- Attendance (MTD)
- Todays visit count
Description of User Story: The Dashboard Home Screen displays a set of key metric cards at the top of the page providing the provider with an at-a-glance overview of critical operational data. The cards include Total Members showing the count of all active residents or patients, New Leads showing recently added leads within the current period, Attendance (MTD) displaying the month-to-date attendance percentage or count, and Today's Visit Count showing the number of visits scheduled or completed for the current day. Each card is styled as a distinct visual block with a metric label, a large numeric value, and an optional trend indicator or icon. The cards refresh automatically when the dashboard is loaded and can reflect real-time data based on the current system state. The layout is responsive and adjusts card positioning for different screen sizes.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If all data sources are available, Then all four metric cards display their respective values with correct formatting.
2. If a specific data source fails to load (e.g., leads service is down), Then the affected card displays a "Data unavailable" message while other cards load normally.
3. If the Total Members count is zero, Then the card displays "0" rather than being hidden or showing an error.
4. If the provider accesses the dashboard on a mobile device, Then the metric cards stack vertically in a single-column layout for readability.
5. If the data has not been updated since the last load, Then the cards display the most recently cached values with a "Last updated" timestamp.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider navigates to the dashboard and sees all four metric cards with correct values: Total Members = 45, New Leads = 8, Attendance MTD = 92%, Today's Visits = 12.
2. Positive Functional: Provider refreshes the dashboard after a new member is added and the Total Members card updates to reflect the new count.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the dashboard at the start of a new month and Attendance MTD resets to reflect current month data only.
4. Positive Functional: Provider views Today's Visit Count and the number matches the actual scheduled visits for the current date.
5. Negative: One of the backend services is unavailable; the affected card shows "Data unavailable" while the other three cards display correctly.
6. Negative: Provider accesses the dashboard with no members, no leads, and no visits; all cards display "0" with proper formatting.
7. Negative: Network connectivity is lost after initial load; dashboard displays cached values with a stale data indicator.
8. UI Scenario: Each metric card displays a descriptive label at the top, a large bold numeric value in the center, and a subtle icon or trend arrow on the right.
9. UI Scenario: The four cards are arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop with equal spacing and consistent card dimensions.
10. UI Scenario: Cards use distinct but complementary background colors or accent borders to differentiate each metric visually.
11. Edge Case: Total Members count exceeds 99,999; the card displays the number with proper comma formatting (e.g., "100,000").
12. Edge Case: Provider views the dashboard exactly at midnight when the day rolls over; Today's Visit Count resets to the new day's data.
13. Security: Dashboard metric cards only display data for facilities and members the logged-in provider has permission to view, based on their RBAC role.
Performance Scenarios:
1. All four dashboard metric cards load and render with accurate data within 2 seconds of navigating to the dashboard.
2. Individual card data API calls complete within 1 second each, enabling progressive rendering.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view Today's residents list showing scheduled members for current day.
Description of User Story: The Today's Residents List widget on the dashboard displays all members who are scheduled to attend or receive services for the current day. The list is presented in a tabular or card format showing essential information for each resident including their name, scheduled time, and service type. The widget pulls data from the scheduling system and filters it to show only today's date, refreshing automatically when the dashboard loads. The list is sorted by scheduled arrival time by default, allowing providers to quickly see who is expected next. A count indicator at the top of the widget shows the total number of scheduled residents for the day. The widget integrates with the attendance system to reflect check-in status as residents arrive throughout the day.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If there are scheduled residents for today, Then the widget displays the list with all resident details and a total count.
2. If no residents are scheduled for today, Then the widget displays a message "No residents scheduled for today" with an empty state illustration.
3. If a resident's scheduled time has passed and they have not checked in, Then their entry is visually flagged as overdue or missed.
4. If the scheduling data fails to load, Then the widget displays an error message with a Retry button.
5. If a new resident is scheduled for today after the dashboard was loaded, Then the list updates upon page refresh to include the newly scheduled resident.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the Today's Residents widget and sees 10 residents listed with names, scheduled times, and service types.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees the count indicator showing "10 Residents Scheduled" matching the actual entries in the list.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the list sorted by arrival time, with 8:00 AM resident at the top and 4:00 PM resident at the bottom.
4. Positive Functional: A resident checks in at 9:00 AM and their status updates to "Checked In" in the list.
5. Negative: No residents are scheduled for today; widget displays "No residents scheduled for today" with an empty state visual.
6. Negative: Scheduling service is temporarily unavailable; widget displays an error message with a Retry button.
7. Negative: A resident was scheduled but their appointment was cancelled; the list reflects only active non-cancelled schedules.
8. UI Scenario: The widget displays a header "Today's Residents" with a count badge, followed by a scrollable list of resident entries.
9. UI Scenario: Each resident entry shows a profile avatar, name, scheduled time, and service type badge in a consistent row layout.
10. UI Scenario: Checked-in residents display a green status indicator while not-yet-arrived residents show a neutral gray indicator.
11. Edge Case: A facility has 100 residents scheduled for today; the widget displays a scrollable list with all entries without performance degradation.
12. Edge Case: A resident is scheduled for exactly midnight (12:00 AM); they appear in today's list and not in the previous day's list.
13. Security: The Today's Residents list only displays residents from facilities the provider has access to, preventing cross-facility data exposure.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Today's Residents widget loads and renders all scheduled residents for the day within 2 seconds of dashboard navigation.
2. Check-in status updates are reflected in the widget within 5 seconds of the check-in event.

User Story:
As a Provider, I should be able to view 
- Member arrival time
- Name
- Service type (ADH/Home Care) 
- Due ( vitals, progress notes, care plan )
- Transport ( verida, family) 
- Status
- Action in Today's residents list.
Description of User Story: The Today's Residents detailed view expands the resident list to show comprehensive information columns for each scheduled member. Each row displays the member's arrival time (actual or expected), their full name, service type indicating whether they are ADH or Home Care, a due column highlighting pending tasks such as vitals, progress notes, or care plan updates, transport information showing the mode of transportation such as Verida or family, current status reflecting check-in state, and an action column with quick-access buttons. The due column uses visual indicators like badges or icons to flag overdue items versus upcoming ones. The transport column helps staff coordinate arrivals and departures. The action column provides contextual buttons such as Check-in, View Profile, or Record Vitals enabling providers to take immediate action without navigating away from the dashboard.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a resident has multiple due items (vitals, progress notes, and care plan), Then all due items are displayed as separate badges or icons in the due column.
2. If a resident has no due items for today, Then the due column displays "None" or remains empty with no indicators.
3. If transport information is not recorded for a resident, Then the transport column displays "Not Specified".
4. If a resident is marked as "Absent", Then the action column shows limited options such as "Mark Excused" instead of "Check-in".
5. If the resident's arrival time has not been recorded yet, Then the arrival time column shows the scheduled time with a "Scheduled" label.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views a resident row showing arrival time "9:15 AM", name "John Smith", service type "ADH", due "Vitals, Progress Notes", transport "Verida", status "Checked In", and a "View Profile" action button.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees a Home Care resident with transport "Family" and due item "Care Plan" displayed with an upcoming badge.
3. Positive Functional: Provider clicks the "Check-in" action button for a resident and the status updates to "Checked In" in real time.
4. Positive Functional: Provider views due column for a resident with overdue vitals showing a red badge and upcoming progress notes showing an orange badge.
5. Negative: A resident has no transport information recorded; the transport column shows "Not Specified" without breaking the layout.
6. Negative: A resident has no due items; the due column displays "None" cleanly without empty space artifacts.
7. Negative: Provider clicks "Check-in" but the backend fails; system shows an error toast and retains the current status.
8. UI Scenario: The table columns are properly aligned with headers: Arrival Time, Name, Service Type, Due, Transport, Status, Action, each with consistent width and text alignment.
9. UI Scenario: Due items display as small colored badges (red for overdue, orange for due today, green for completed) within the due column.
10. UI Scenario: The action column contains compact icon buttons with tooltips for Check-in, View Profile, and Record Vitals.
11. Edge Case: A resident has five due items; the due column displays them without overflowing, using a "+N more" indicator if space is limited.
12. Edge Case: A resident's name is very long; the name column truncates with an ellipsis and shows the full name on hover.
13. Security: Action buttons are conditionally rendered based on the provider's RBAC permissions; a provider without vitals recording permission does not see the "Record Vitals" action.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The detailed Today's Residents table with all columns loads within 2 seconds for up to 50 scheduled residents.
2. Clicking an action button (Check-in) processes the request and updates the status within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click "Check-in" button directly from Today's residents list for quick attendance marking.
Description of User Story: The Quick Check-in feature allows providers to mark a resident's attendance directly from the Today's Residents list on the dashboard without navigating to a separate attendance module. Each resident entry in the list includes a prominently placed "Check-in" button that, when clicked, records the current timestamp as the resident's arrival time and updates their status to "Checked In" in real time. The button is only displayed for residents who have not yet been checked in for the current day. Upon successful check-in, a brief success notification appears, the button transforms into a "Checked In" status indicator, and the arrival time column updates with the actual check-in time. The system prevents duplicate check-ins for the same resident on the same day.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks "Check-in" and the request succeeds, Then the arrival time is set to the current timestamp and the status changes to "Checked In".
2. If the provider clicks "Check-in" but the server request fails, Then the system displays an error notification and the button remains active for retry.
3. If the resident was already checked in by another provider simultaneously, Then the system detects the duplicate and displays a message that the resident is already checked in.
4. If the provider does not have attendance marking permissions, Then the "Check-in" button is not displayed in their view.
5. If the check-in occurs outside the resident's scheduled time window, Then the system allows the check-in but flags it as an early or late arrival.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Check-in" for resident "Jane Doe" at 9:05 AM; status updates to "Checked In", arrival time shows "9:05 AM", and a success toast appears.
2. Positive Functional: Provider checks in three residents consecutively; each check-in updates independently and correctly.
3. Positive Functional: Provider checks in a resident who arrives 30 minutes early; system records the check-in with an "Early Arrival" flag.
4. Positive Functional: After check-in, the "Check-in" button is replaced by a green "Checked In" badge and is no longer clickable.
5. Negative: Provider clicks "Check-in" but the network request fails; an error toast displays and the button remains active for retry.
6. Negative: Two providers attempt to check in the same resident simultaneously; the second attempt receives a "Already checked in" message.
7. Negative: Provider without attendance permissions views the list; the Check-in button is not rendered.
8. UI Scenario: The "Check-in" button is a compact, distinctly colored (e.g., blue) button within the action column that is easily identifiable.
9. UI Scenario: After check-in, the button smoothly transitions to a "Checked In" badge with a green checkmark icon.
10. UI Scenario: The success notification toast appears at the top-right corner, auto-dismisses after 3 seconds, and does not obstruct the table.
11. Edge Case: Provider rapidly double-clicks the "Check-in" button; the system processes only one check-in request and prevents duplicate entries.
12. Edge Case: Provider checks in the last remaining unchecked resident; all entries now show "Checked In" status and the pending count updates to zero.
13. Security: Check-in actions are recorded in the audit log with the provider's identity, timestamp, and the resident checked in.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Clicking the "Check-in" button processes the attendance marking and updates the UI within 2 seconds.
2. The success notification renders within 500 milliseconds of the server confirming the check-in.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to filter Today's Members list by "All" or specific criteria.
Description of User Story: The Filter Today's Members feature provides providers with the ability to narrow down the Today's Residents list using predefined filter options. A filter control bar or dropdown is positioned above the residents list offering options such as "All" to display all scheduled members, and specific criteria filters such as by service type, check-in status, transport mode, or due items. Selecting a filter immediately updates the list to show only matching residents, with the active filter visually highlighted. The "All" option serves as the default selection and resets any active filter. Multiple filters can potentially be combined to create more specific views. The filtered count updates to reflect the number of matching residents, and the filter state persists during the current dashboard session.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider selects "Checked In" status filter, Then only residents who have already checked in are displayed in the list.
2. If the provider selects a filter that matches no residents, Then the list displays "No residents match the selected filter" with a suggestion to clear the filter.
3. If the provider combines multiple filter criteria, Then only residents matching all selected criteria are shown.
4. If the provider navigates away from the dashboard and returns, Then the filter resets to "All" as the default.
5. If filter data fails to apply due to an error, Then the system retains the previous list state and displays an error notification.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider selects "All" filter and sees all 15 scheduled residents for today.
2. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "ADH" service type and sees 8 residents with ADH service.
3. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "Not Checked In" status and sees only residents who have not yet arrived.
4. Positive Functional: Provider filters by transport "Verida" and sees residents using Verida transport services.
5. Negative: Provider applies a filter that matches no residents; the list shows "No residents match the selected filter" with a clear filter option.
6. Negative: Provider applies a filter while the data is loading; the system queues the filter and applies it once data is ready.
7. Negative: Filter API request fails; system retains the current list and shows an error toast.
8. UI Scenario: The filter bar displays options as clickable chips or a dropdown positioned directly above the residents list.
9. UI Scenario: The active filter chip or option is highlighted with a distinct color or border to indicate the current selection.
10. UI Scenario: The resident count badge next to the filter bar updates dynamically to show the count of matching residents.
11. Edge Case: Provider rapidly switches between filters; each filter application completes before the next one processes, preventing race conditions.
12. Edge Case: Provider applies a filter then checks in a resident who no longer matches the filter; the list updates to remove that resident from the filtered view.
13. Security: Filter options are restricted based on the provider's data access permissions; providers cannot filter to see residents from facilities they do not manage.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Applying a filter updates the Today's Residents list within 1 second for lists of up to 100 residents.
2. Switching between filter options renders the updated list within 500 milliseconds for client-side filtering.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click "View All" to see expanded resident list with more details.
Description of User Story: The View All Expanded Resident List feature allows providers to click a "View All" link or button on the Today's Residents widget to navigate to a full-page view of the resident list with additional detail columns and enhanced functionality. The expanded view presents all scheduled residents for the day in a comprehensive table that includes additional fields not shown in the compact dashboard widget, such as detailed contact information, assigned caregiver, room or unit number, and comprehensive due item breakdowns. The expanded list supports advanced sorting across all columns, a more robust search bar, and enhanced filtering capabilities. Pagination controls allow providers to navigate through large lists efficiently. A "Back to Dashboard" link is provided for easy navigation back to the main dashboard view.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the expanded list has more residents than the page size, Then pagination controls are displayed and function correctly.
2. If the provider searches for a resident name that does not exist, Then the list displays "No matching residents found" with a clear search option.
3. If the expanded list fails to load, Then the system displays an error message with a Retry button.
4. If the provider applies a sort and then navigates to the next page, Then the sort order persists across pages.
5. If the provider was viewing a filtered list on the dashboard widget, Then the expanded view opens with the same filter applied.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "View All" on the Today's Residents widget and navigates to a full-page list showing 25 residents with expanded details.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views the expanded list showing additional columns for caregiver, room number, and a detailed breakdown of due items for each resident.
3. Positive Functional: Provider sorts the expanded list by room number and sees residents organized by their assigned rooms.
4. Positive Functional: Provider searches for "Maria" in the search bar and the list filters to show only residents with "Maria" in their name.
5. Negative: Provider clicks "View All" but the expanded list fails to load; an error message with a Retry button is displayed.
6. Negative: Provider searches for a name that does not match any resident; the list shows "No matching residents found".
7. Negative: Provider navigates to the expanded list while the scheduling data is being updated; the system shows a loading state and then renders the latest data.
8. UI Scenario: The expanded list page has a clear header "Today's Residents - Full View" with the date and total count displayed prominently.
9. UI Scenario: The table is wider than the dashboard widget and includes columns for Arrival Time, Name, Service Type, Caregiver, Room, Due Items, Transport, Status, and Actions.
10. UI Scenario: Pagination controls at the bottom show page numbers, previous/next buttons, and a "rows per page" selector.
11. Edge Case: A facility has 200 residents scheduled for today; the expanded list paginates with 25 per page and handles all pages without degradation.
12. Edge Case: Provider clicks "View All" and immediately clicks "Back to Dashboard" before the list loads; the system navigates back cleanly without errors.
13. Security: The expanded resident list only displays residents from facilities the provider is authorized to access.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The expanded resident list page loads and renders the first page of results within 3 seconds of clicking "View All".
2. Sorting and searching within the expanded list return results within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view weekly, monthly and day attendance bar chart showing Present (blue) vs Absent (light blue) for each day.
Description of User Story: The Attendance Graph Widget on the dashboard displays a bar chart visualization showing the attendance pattern over a configurable time period. The chart plots daily attendance data with dual-color bars where blue represents present (attended) members and light blue represents absent members for each day. The widget supports three view modes: daily view showing hourly breakdowns, weekly view showing each day of the current week, and monthly view showing each day of the current month. The x-axis represents time units (days or hours depending on the view), and the y-axis represents the count of members. Hovering over a bar segment displays a tooltip with the exact count and percentage. The widget includes a toggle control to switch between the different time period views and updates the chart dynamically upon selection.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If attendance data is available for the selected period, Then the chart renders with blue (Present) and light blue (Absent) bar segments for each time unit.
2. If no attendance data exists for a particular day, Then that day shows empty or zero-height bars with the day label still displayed on the x-axis.
3. If the provider switches between view modes rapidly, Then the chart debounces updates and renders only the final selected view.
4. If the attendance data fails to load, Then the widget displays an error message with a Retry button in place of the chart.
5. If the monthly view has more than 28 days, Then the chart adjusts the x-axis to accommodate all days without overlapping labels.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the weekly attendance chart showing 5 weekdays with blue present bars and light blue absent bars, each with accurate counts.
2. Positive Functional: Provider hovers over the Wednesday bar and sees a tooltip reading "Present: 38 (86%), Absent: 6 (14%)".
3. Positive Functional: Provider switches to monthly view and sees 31 days of attendance data with consistent bar coloring.
4. Positive Functional: Provider switches to day view and sees hourly attendance breakdown for the current day.
5. Negative: No attendance data exists for the selected period; the chart shows empty bars with day labels and a message "No attendance data available".
6. Negative: Attendance data service is unavailable; the widget shows an error state with a Retry button.
7. Negative: Provider switches to monthly view for a month with only partial data (mid-month); chart shows bars for available days and empty spaces for future days.
8. UI Scenario: The bar chart uses a clear blue color for Present and a lighter blue for Absent with a legend at the top or bottom identifying each color.
9. UI Scenario: The time period toggle (Day/Week/Month) is positioned at the top-right corner of the widget as segmented buttons.
10. UI Scenario: The chart has a clean grid background with horizontal guide lines and properly spaced axis labels.
11. Edge Case: A day has 100% attendance with zero absences; the Absent bar is not rendered and only the blue Present bar is shown.
12. Edge Case: A facility has 500 members; the y-axis scales appropriately and bar heights remain proportional and readable.
13. Security: The attendance chart displays data only for facilities the provider has permission to monitor.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Attendance Graph Widget renders the weekly bar chart within 2 seconds of dashboard load.
2. Switching between Day, Week, and Month views updates the chart within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to switch attendance view between Week and other time periods.
Description of User Story: The Switch Attendance View feature provides a toggle control within the Attendance Graph Widget that allows providers to change the time period displayed in the attendance bar chart. The toggle offers options for Day, Week, and Month views, presented as segmented buttons or tabs positioned prominently within the widget header. Clicking a different time period option immediately reloads the attendance data for the selected period and re-renders the chart with the appropriate time axis. The currently selected view is visually highlighted to indicate the active period. The system remembers the last selected view during the current dashboard session so that if the widget is refreshed, it retains the provider's preference. The transition between views includes a smooth animation or loading indicator to provide visual feedback during the data fetch.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks the already-active view option, Then no reload occurs and the chart remains unchanged.
2. If data for the selected time period is still loading, Then a spinner or skeleton chart is displayed until rendering completes.
3. If the selected time period has no data available, Then the chart shows an empty state with a descriptive message.
4. If the provider's session expires and they log back in, Then the view resets to the default (Weekly) selection.
5. If network connectivity is lost during a view switch, Then the system displays an error and retains the previously loaded chart.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider switches from Week to Month view; the chart updates to show 30 days of attendance bars.
2. Positive Functional: Provider switches from Month to Day view; the chart shows hourly attendance breakdown for today.
3. Positive Functional: Provider switches back to Week view from Day view; the chart reverts to the weekly display.
4. Positive Functional: Provider selects Month view and the chart retains this selection when other dashboard widgets are interacted with.
5. Negative: Provider clicks the already selected "Week" toggle; nothing changes and no unnecessary API call is made.
6. Negative: Network error during view switch; the system shows an error toast and retains the previous chart.
7. Negative: Provider switches to Day view for a day with no attendance data; chart displays an empty state.
8. UI Scenario: The toggle control displays three options (Day, Week, Month) as equally sized segmented buttons with the active one filled or highlighted.
9. UI Scenario: During view transition, a brief loading spinner overlays the chart area providing visual feedback.
10. UI Scenario: The toggle buttons have hover effects and are keyboard-accessible for accessibility compliance.
11. Edge Case: Provider rapidly clicks through all three view options in quick succession; only the final selection renders without intermediate flicker.
12. Edge Case: Provider switches to Month view at the very start of a new month with only one day of data; chart shows one bar with remaining days empty.
13. Security: View switching does not expose attendance data from unauthorized facilities regardless of the time period selected.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Switching between attendance views fetches and renders the new chart data within 2 seconds.
2. The toggle interaction responds visually within 200 milliseconds of clicking, even before data loads.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view calendar widget showing PA Authorization and Standing Order expirations by date.
Description of User Story: The PA and Standing Order Expiration Calendar widget on the dashboard displays a calendar-style view highlighting dates on which Prior Authorization (PA) and Standing Order documents are set to expire for residents. The calendar presents the current month by default with dates containing expirations visually marked using color-coded dots or badges indicating the number and type of expirations. Clicking on a specific date reveals the list of residents whose PA authorizations or standing orders expire on that date. The widget helps providers proactively manage renewals and avoid lapses in authorization coverage. Navigation controls allow providers to move between months to view upcoming expiration dates. The calendar distinguishes between PA expirations and Standing Order expirations using different colors or icons.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a date has both PA and Standing Order expirations, Then the date cell shows two distinct colored indicators.
2. If no expirations exist for the current month, Then the calendar displays normally with no marked dates and a message "No expirations this month".
3. If the provider clicks on a date with no expirations, Then no dropdown appears and the click has no effect.
4. If expiration data fails to load, Then the calendar renders with an error message and a Retry button overlaying the widget.
5. If an expiration is within the next 7 days, Then the date indicator uses an urgent color (e.g., red) to flag the approaching deadline.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the calendar and sees 5 dates with PA expiration markers and 3 dates with Standing Order expiration markers for the current month.
2. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on March 15 and sees a panel listing two residents with PA expirations on that date.
3. Positive Functional: Provider navigates to next month and sees upcoming April expiration dates.
4. Positive Functional: Provider sees a date with a red urgent indicator for a PA expiration occurring in 3 days.
5. Negative: No expirations exist for the selected month; the calendar shows clean dates with a "No expirations this month" message.
6. Negative: Expiration data service is unavailable; the calendar widget shows an error overlay with a Retry option.
7. Negative: Provider clicks on a date with no expirations; nothing happens and no empty panel is displayed.
8. UI Scenario: The calendar widget displays a monthly grid with day labels (Sun-Sat) and date numbers, with colored dots beneath dates that have expirations.
9. UI Scenario: A legend at the bottom of the widget shows PA expirations in one color and Standing Order expirations in another.
10. UI Scenario: Navigation arrows (left/right) are positioned at the top of the calendar beside the current month/year label.
11. Edge Case: A single date has 20 expiring authorizations; the detail panel supports scrolling to display all affected residents.
12. Edge Case: Provider navigates backward to a past month; the calendar shows historical expirations that have already passed with a distinct muted visual style.
13. Security: The expiration calendar only shows data for residents within facilities the provider is authorized to manage.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The PA & Standing Order Expiration Calendar widget loads with all marked dates within 2 seconds of dashboard navigation.
2. Clicking on a date with expirations opens the detail panel with resident names within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view resident names listed under each expiration date in calendar.
Description of User Story: The Resident Expiration Details feature enhances the PA and Standing Order Expiration Calendar by displaying resident names directly under or within each expiration date cell in the calendar view. When a date has expiring authorizations, the calendar cell shows the names of affected residents, making it immediately visible which residents need attention without requiring an additional click. For dates with a small number of expirations, all resident names are displayed inline within the date cell. For dates with many expirations, the first few names are shown with a "+N more" indicator to view the full list. Each resident name is clickable, enabling the provider to navigate directly to that resident's authorization details. The display differentiates between PA authorization expirations and Standing Order expirations through labels or color coding next to each name.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a date cell has space for only one or two names, Then the remaining names are hidden behind a "+N more" link.
2. If a resident has both a PA and a Standing Order expiring on the same date, Then their name appears twice (or once with both labels) in the date cell.
3. If the provider clicks "+N more", Then a popover or expanded panel shows the complete list of residents expiring on that date.
4. If the provider clicks a resident name and their authorization page fails to load, Then an error message is displayed with a back navigation option.
5. If no residents have expirations on a date, Then the date cell remains empty without any names or indicators.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views March 20 in the calendar and sees "John Smith - PA" and "Mary Johnson - Standing Order" listed in the date cell.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views a date with 5 expirations; the cell shows 3 names and "+2 more" indicator.
3. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "+2 more" and a popover displays all 5 resident names with their expiration types.
4. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on "John Smith" in the calendar cell and is navigated to John's PA authorization details page.
5. Negative: Provider clicks on a resident name but the authorization page is unavailable; an error message displays with a return link.
6. Negative: A date has no expirations; the cell is empty and clean with no placeholder text.
7. Negative: Resident name data fails to load for the calendar; date cells show expiration count badges instead of names as a fallback.
8. UI Scenario: Resident names within date cells are displayed in a small font with PA names in one color and Standing Order names in another for visual distinction.
9. UI Scenario: The "+N more" indicator is styled as a clickable link with a count badge.
10. UI Scenario: Clickable resident names have an underline or hover effect indicating they are interactive links.
11. Edge Case: A resident has a very long name; the calendar cell truncates the name with an ellipsis and shows the full name on hover.
12. Edge Case: A single date has 50 expirations; the "+47 more" popover panel supports scrolling to display all names.
13. Security: Resident names in the calendar are visible only to providers with permission to view those residents' records.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The calendar renders with resident names in all date cells within 3 seconds for a month with up to 100 total expirations.
2. Clicking "+N more" opens the full resident list popover within 500 milliseconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click "View All" to see complete list of upcoming expirations.
Description of User Story: The View All Upcoming Expirations feature provides a "View All" link within the PA and Standing Order Expiration Calendar widget that navigates the provider to a full-page comprehensive list of all upcoming authorization and standing order expirations. The expanded view displays a detailed table with columns for resident name, expiration type (PA Authorization or Standing Order), expiration date, days remaining until expiration, current status, and action buttons for initiating renewals. The list is sorted by expiration date in ascending order by default, prioritizing the most urgent expirations.
