Advanced filtering options allow providers to filter by expiration type, date range, and urgency level. A search bar enables finding specific residents quickly. Color-coded urgency indicators highlight critical expirations (within 7 days), upcoming expirations (within 30 days), and future expirations.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If there are no upcoming expirations, Then the page displays a message "No upcoming expirations" with a positive confirmation that all authorizations are current.
2. If an expiration is within 7 days, Then the row is highlighted in red with a "Critical" urgency badge.
3. If an expiration is within 30 days, Then the row displays an orange "Upcoming" urgency badge.
4. If the provider clicks an action button to initiate a renewal, Then the system navigates to the renewal workflow for that authorization.
5. If the expirations list exceeds the page size, Then pagination controls are provided for navigating through the full list.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "View All" and navigates to a page showing 25 upcoming expirations with all detail columns populated.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees the top entry as a PA Authorization expiring in 2 days with a red "Critical" badge.
3. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "Standing Order" type and sees only standing order expirations.
4. Positive Functional: Provider searches for "Jane Doe" and sees her two upcoming expirations listed.
5. Negative: No upcoming expirations exist; the page displays "No upcoming expirations" with a confirmation message.
6. Negative: Expirations data fails to load; an error page with a Retry button is displayed.
7. Negative: Provider searches for a resident with no upcoming expirations; the list shows "No matching expirations found".
8. UI Scenario: The expirations list page has a header "Upcoming Expirations" with total count, filter dropdowns, and a search bar.
9. UI Scenario: Row urgency is indicated by a colored left-border or badge: red for critical, orange for upcoming, and green for future.
10. UI Scenario: The "Days Remaining" column shows a countdown number with color matching the urgency level.
11. Edge Case: An expiration has already passed (negative days remaining); the row shows "Overdue by N days" in red and appears at the very top of the list.
12. Edge Case: A resident has both a PA and Standing Order expiring on the same date; both appear as separate rows in the list.
13. Security: The expirations list only shows data for residents within the provider's authorized facilities.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The full expirations list page loads and renders within 3 seconds for up to 500 expiration entries.
2. Filtering and searching within the list returns results within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to apply filter on view calendar widget for PA & standing order expiration Calendar
Description of User Story: The Filter Expiration Calendar feature adds filtering controls to the PA and Standing Order Expiration Calendar widget, allowing providers to narrow the displayed expirations by specific criteria. Filter options include expiration type (PA Authorization, Standing Order, or both), urgency level (critical within 7 days, upcoming within 30 days, all), facility selection for multi-facility providers, and service type. The filter controls are positioned as dropdown menus or chip selectors above or beside the calendar widget. Applying a filter immediately updates the calendar to show only dates and residents matching the selected criteria. The active filters are displayed as removable tags, and a "Clear All" option resets the calendar to its unfiltered state. The filter state persists during the current dashboard session.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider applies a type filter for "Standing Order" only, Then PA Authorization expirations are hidden from the calendar.
2. If the combined filters result in no matching expirations, Then the calendar displays with no marked dates and a message "No expirations match the selected filters".
3. If the provider removes one filter chip while others remain active, Then the calendar updates to reflect the remaining active filters.
4. If the provider navigates to a different month with filters active, Then the filters persist and apply to the new month's data.
5. If the filter controls fail to load, Then the calendar displays unfiltered data with a notice that filters are temporarily unavailable.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "PA Authorization" and the calendar shows only PA expiration dates.
2. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "Critical" urgency and sees only dates within the next 7 days with expirations highlighted.
3. Positive Functional: Provider applies both type and urgency filters simultaneously; only matching expirations are shown.
4. Positive Functional: Provider removes one filter chip; the calendar updates to reflect the remaining filters.
5. Negative: Filters result in no matching expirations; the calendar shows a "No expirations match the selected filters" message.
6. Negative: Filter controls fail to load; the calendar displays unfiltered data with a notice about filter unavailability.
7. Negative: Provider applies a filter and navigates to a month with no matching data; the clean calendar is displayed for that month.
8. UI Scenario: Filter controls are displayed as dropdown menus with clear labels positioned above the calendar widget.
9. UI Scenario: Active filters appear as removable chips/tags between the filter controls and the calendar, each with an "x" close button.
10. UI Scenario: A "Clear All" button is visible when any filters are active, styled as a text link next to the filter chips.
11. Edge Case: Provider applies all available filters in a combination that results in exactly one expiration; the calendar shows that single date marked.
12. Edge Case: Provider opens filters, selects options, but then clicks away without applying; the filters do not change the calendar state.
13. Security: Facility filter options only include facilities the provider has authorization to manage.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Applying or removing a calendar filter updates the display within 1 second.
2. Loading filter options (facilities, types) completes within 500 milliseconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on resident name in calendar to view their full authorization details.
Description of User Story: The View Resident Authorization Details feature enables providers to click on a resident's name within the PA and Standing Order Expiration Calendar to navigate directly to that resident's comprehensive authorization details page. The authorization details page displays the resident's current PA authorization information including authorization number, authorized service type, approved units or hours, start date, end date, authorizing agency, and current utilization against the authorized amount. For standing orders, the page shows the order details, prescribing physician, order date, renewal date, and associated medications or treatments. The page also includes a history section showing past authorizations and standing orders for the resident. Action buttons allow the provider to initiate a renewal, request an extension, or print the authorization documentation directly from the details page.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the resident has an active PA authorization, Then all authorization details are displayed with a green "Active" status badge.
2. If the resident's PA authorization has expired, Then the status shows "Expired" in red and a prominent "Renew Now" button is displayed.
3. If the resident has no standing orders, Then the standing order section displays "No standing orders on file" message.
4. If the authorization details page fails to load, Then the system displays an error with a "Back to Calendar" link and a Retry option.
5. If the resident has utilization exceeding 80% of authorized units, Then a warning indicator is displayed showing the utilization is approaching the limit.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "John Smith" on March 15 in the calendar and navigates to John's authorization page showing PA #12345, ADH service, 120 approved hours, active status.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views the standing order section showing Dr. Wilson as prescribing physician with a renewal date of April 1.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the history section showing three previous authorizations spanning the last two years.
4. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Initiate Renewal" and the renewal form opens with pre-populated data from the current authorization.
5. Negative: Authorization details fail to load; system displays an error with a "Back to Calendar" link.
6. Negative: Resident has an expired authorization; the page shows "Expired" status with a "Renew Now" call-to-action button.
7. Negative: Resident has no authorization history; the history section shows "No previous authorizations" message.
8. UI Scenario: The authorization details page is organized into card-style sections: Current PA Authorization, Standing Orders, Utilization Summary, and Authorization History.
9. UI Scenario: Utilization is displayed as a progress bar showing consumed versus approved units with percentage label.
10. UI Scenario: Action buttons (Initiate Renewal, Request Extension, Print) are positioned in a fixed action bar at the top or bottom of the page.
11. Edge Case: A resident has multiple concurrent PA authorizations for different service types; each is displayed in its own card within the current authorization section.
12. Edge Case: Authorization data includes very long agency names or authorization numbers; the layout handles text wrapping without breaking.
13. Security: Authorization details are accessible only to providers with "view_authorization" permission for the specific resident's facility.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The authorization details page loads with all sections within 3 seconds of clicking a resident name in the calendar.
2. The authorization history section loads previous records within 2 seconds, even for residents with extensive authorization histories.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view Vitals widget showing residents who need vitals checks.
Description of User Story: The Vitals Due Widget on the dashboard displays a list of residents who have pending vitals checks that need to be completed. The widget presents each resident's name along with the type of vitals due (blood pressure, temperature, pulse, oxygen saturation, weight, etc.) and the urgency status indicating whether the vitals check is upcoming, due today, or overdue. The list is sorted by urgency with overdue checks at the top to prioritize immediate attention. A count indicator shows the total number of residents with pending vitals checks. The widget is designed as a compact dashboard component with a scrollable list and includes quick-action functionality to navigate directly to the vitals entry screen for any listed resident. A "View All" link expands the list to a full page view for comprehensive review.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If residents have pending vitals checks, Then the widget displays each resident with their name, vitals type, and urgency status.
2. If no residents require vitals checks currently, Then the widget displays "All vitals are up to date" with a checkmark icon.
3. If a resident has multiple types of vitals due, Then all due vitals types are listed as badges within their entry.
4. If the vitals data fails to load, Then the widget displays an error state with a Retry button.
5. If a vitals check was just completed for a listed resident, Then the widget updates upon dashboard refresh to remove or update that resident's entry.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the Vitals Due Widget showing 8 residents with pending vitals, sorted by urgency.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Maria Lopez - Blood Pressure, Temperature - Overdue" as the first entry in the list.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the count indicator showing "8 Residents Need Vitals" at the widget header.
4. Positive Functional: Provider scrolls within the widget to see all 8 residents when the widget initially displays only 5.
5. Negative: No residents need vitals checks; the widget shows "All vitals are up to date" with a green checkmark.
6. Negative: Vitals data service is unavailable; the widget shows an error state with a Retry button.
7. Negative: A resident's vitals data is partially missing; the widget shows available information and flags the incomplete data.
8. UI Scenario: The widget has a compact card design with a header "Vitals Due" and a count badge, followed by a scrollable resident list.
9. UI Scenario: Each resident entry shows a profile avatar, name, vitals type badges, and an urgency indicator (green/orange/red).
10. UI Scenario: Overdue entries have a red urgency indicator and border accent, while due-today entries use orange.
11. Edge Case: A facility has 100 residents all with overdue vitals; the widget displays a scrollable list with all entries and the count shows "100".
12. Edge Case: A resident is scheduled for vitals at the exact current time; the system correctly categorizes them as "Due Now" rather than upcoming or overdue.
13. Security: The Vitals Due Widget only shows residents from facilities the provider has access to, filtered by RBAC permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Vitals Due Widget loads and renders the list of residents with pending vitals within 2 seconds of dashboard load.
2. Scrolling within the widget to reveal additional residents renders smoothly without lag.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to see resident names in Vitals due list.
Description of User Story: The Resident Names in Vitals Due List feature ensures that each entry in the Vitals Due Widget prominently displays the full name of the resident who requires a vitals check. The resident name is the primary identifier in each list entry, displayed in a bold or prominent font at the beginning of the row. Alongside the name, the entry shows supplementary information such as the resident's profile photo thumbnail, room or unit number for quick identification, and the specific vitals that are due. The names are presented in a consistent format (first name, last name) and support proper display of names with special characters, suffixes, or preferred names. If the resident list is long, a search capability allows providers to find a specific resident by typing their name.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the resident has a profile photo uploaded, Then the photo thumbnail is displayed next to their name.
2. If the resident does not have a profile photo, Then a default avatar with their initials is displayed.
3. If the resident name contains special characters or accents, Then the name renders correctly without encoding issues.
4. If two residents share the same name, Then additional identifiers like room number or date of birth are shown to differentiate them.
5. If the search returns no matching residents, Then the widget displays "No matching residents found" with a clear search option.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the Vitals Due list and sees "John Smith" with a profile photo, Room 204, and "Blood Pressure" as the due vital.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Maria Garcia-Lopez" displayed correctly with the hyphenated last name.
3. Positive Functional: Provider uses search to find "Robert" and the list filters to show only residents named Robert.
4. Positive Functional: Two residents named "James Wilson" are differentiated by their room numbers (Room 101 and Room 305).
5. Negative: Search for "Xyz" returns no results; the widget shows "No matching residents found".
6. Negative: A resident's name data is missing; the entry displays "Unknown Resident" with a data issue flag.
7. Negative: Profile photo fails to load for a resident; the default initials avatar is displayed instead.
8. UI Scenario: Resident names are displayed in bold font with a consistent size, left-aligned within each list entry.
9. UI Scenario: Profile photo thumbnails are circular and uniformly sized, positioned to the left of the resident name.
10. UI Scenario: Room numbers are displayed as a subtle secondary text below or beside the resident name.
11. Edge Case: A resident has an extremely long name (40+ characters); the name truncates with an ellipsis in the widget and shows full name on hover.
12. Edge Case: A resident's name includes a suffix like "Jr." or "III"; the suffix is displayed as part of the full name.
13. Security: Resident names and photos are only visible to providers with appropriate access permissions for those residents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Vitals Due list with resident names and photos loads within 2 seconds for up to 50 residents.
2. Name search filtering responds within 500 milliseconds as the provider types.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on resident name in Vitals widget to navigate to vitals entry screen.
Description of User Story: The Navigate to Vitals Entry feature makes each resident name in the Vitals Due Widget a clickable link that navigates the provider directly to the vitals entry screen for the selected resident. When a provider clicks on a resident's name, the system opens the vitals recording form pre-configured for that specific resident with the due vitals types already indicated. The vitals entry screen displays the resident's identifying information at the top and presents input fields for the relevant vital signs such as blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, and weight. After recording the vitals, the provider can save and either return to the dashboard or proceed to the next resident with pending vitals. The navigation preserves the dashboard context so the provider can easily return to where they were.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks a resident name and the vitals entry screen loads successfully, Then all relevant vital sign input fields are displayed for the resident.
2. If the vitals entry screen fails to load, Then the system displays an error message with options to Retry or return to the dashboard.
3. If the provider enters a vital sign value outside the normal range, Then the system highlights the field with a warning but allows saving.
4. If the provider attempts to save without entering any vitals data, Then the system displays a validation message requiring at least one vital sign entry.
5. If the provider clicks "Back to Dashboard" with unsaved vitals entries, Then the system displays a confirmation dialog asking if they want to discard unsaved data.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "John Smith" in the Vitals Due Widget and navigates to John's vitals entry screen showing due vitals for blood pressure and temperature.
2. Positive Functional: Provider records blood pressure as 120/80, temperature as 98.6°F, and saves successfully with a confirmation message.
3. Positive Functional: Provider saves vitals and clicks "Next Resident" to proceed to the next pending resident's vitals entry.
4. Positive Functional: Provider saves vitals and returns to the dashboard; the Vitals Due Widget no longer shows the just-completed resident.
5. Negative: Vitals entry screen fails to load; error message displays with Retry and Back to Dashboard options.
6. Negative: Provider enters a blood pressure value of 300/200 (abnormally high); system highlights the field with a warning but allows the save.
7. Negative: Provider attempts to save with no vitals entered; system shows a validation error.
8. UI Scenario: The vitals entry screen displays the resident name and photo at the top, followed by labeled input fields for each vital sign type.
9. UI Scenario: Each vital sign input field includes the measurement unit label (mmHg, °F, bpm, %, breaths/min, lbs) and acceptable range hint.
10. UI Scenario: Save button is styled as a primary action, with "Back to Dashboard" and "Next Resident" as secondary navigation options.
11. Edge Case: Provider navigates to the vitals entry screen for the last remaining resident with pending vitals; "Next Resident" button is disabled or hidden.
12. Edge Case: Provider opens vitals entry for a resident, then another provider completes the same resident's vitals simultaneously; system handles the conflict gracefully.
13. Security: Vitals entry is restricted to providers with "record_vitals" permission; clicking the name without this permission navigates to a read-only vitals view.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The vitals entry screen loads within 2 seconds of clicking a resident name in the Vitals Due Widget.
2. Saving recorded vitals processes and confirms within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view Progress Note widget showing residents who need progress notes.
Description of User Story: The Progress Notes Due Widget on the dashboard displays a list of residents who have pending progress notes that need to be completed. The widget shows each resident's name along with the due date or frequency of the required progress note and the current urgency status. Progress notes may be due on a monthly, weekly, or custom schedule depending on the resident's care plan requirements. The list is sorted by urgency with overdue progress notes at the top to ensure immediate attention. A count indicator in the widget header shows the total number of residents with pending progress notes. The widget supports scrolling for longer lists and includes a "View All" option for navigating to a detailed full-page view. The urgency indicators use color coding to differentiate between overdue, due today, and upcoming progress notes.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If residents have pending progress notes, Then the widget displays each resident with their name, due date, and urgency indicator.
2. If no residents require progress notes currently, Then the widget displays "All progress notes are up to date" with a checkmark icon.
3. If a resident's progress note is overdue by more than 7 days, Then their entry is flagged with a critical urgency indicator.
4. If the progress notes data fails to load, Then the widget displays an error message with a Retry button.
5. If a progress note was just submitted for a resident, Then the widget updates upon refresh to remove or update that resident's entry.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the Progress Notes Due Widget showing 6 residents with pending notes, sorted by urgency.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Robert Chen - Monthly Note Due March 25 - Due Today" as the first non-overdue entry.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the count indicator showing "6 Progress Notes Due".
4. Positive Functional: Provider scrolls through the widget to see all 6 entries.
5. Negative: No progress notes are due; the widget shows "All progress notes are up to date" with a green checkmark.
6. Negative: Progress notes data service is unavailable; the widget shows an error with Retry.
7. Negative: A resident's progress note schedule is not configured; they do not appear in the widget.
8. UI Scenario: The widget has a header "Progress Notes Due" with a count badge, followed by a scrollable list of resident entries.
9. UI Scenario: Each entry displays the resident name, due date, and a color-coded urgency badge (red for overdue, orange for due today, gray for upcoming).
10. UI Scenario: The widget footer contains a "View All" link for expanding to a full-page progress notes due list.
11. Edge Case: A facility has 50 residents all with overdue notes; the widget scrolls smoothly through all entries.
12. Edge Case: A resident's progress note is due at exactly midnight; the system correctly categorizes it as due today when viewed that day.
13. Security: The Progress Notes Due Widget only shows residents the provider is authorized to manage based on facility and role permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Progress Notes Due Widget loads and renders the list within 2 seconds of dashboard navigation.
2. Scrolling through a long list of 50+ resident entries renders smoothly without visible lag.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to see resident names and profile pictures in Progress Notes due list.
Description of User Story: The Resident Names and Profile Pictures in Progress Notes Due List feature ensures that each entry in the Progress Notes Due Widget prominently displays the resident's full name accompanied by their profile photograph for quick visual identification. The profile picture is displayed as a circular thumbnail positioned to the left of the resident's name, creating a consistent visual pattern across all entries. The name is shown in a bold, easily readable font and follows a "First Name Last Name" format for consistency. For residents without an uploaded profile photo, the system displays a default avatar generated from the resident's initials with a color assigned based on their name. The combination of name and photo helps providers quickly identify residents in a busy care environment, reducing the chance of errors when selecting a resident for progress note documentation.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the resident has a profile photo, Then it is displayed as a circular thumbnail with consistent dimensions across all entries.
2. If the resident does not have a profile photo, Then a colored circle with the resident's initials is displayed in the same dimensions.
3. If the profile photo fails to load due to a network error, Then the initials avatar is shown as a fallback.
4. If a resident's name contains special characters or Unicode, Then the name renders correctly without display issues.
5. If two residents have the same name, Then their profile photos help differentiate them visually.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Jane Doe" with her profile photo displayed as a circular thumbnail in the Progress Notes list.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Alex Johnson" with an initials avatar "AJ" in blue, as no profile photo is uploaded.
3. Positive Functional: All entries in the list display names in consistent "First Last" format with aligned photo thumbnails.
4. Positive Functional: Provider identifies the correct resident from the photo when two residents share similar names.
5. Negative: A resident's profile photo fails to load; the system displays the initials avatar as a fallback.
6. Negative: A resident's name data is corrupted; the entry shows "Unknown Resident" with a default avatar.
7. Negative: Network timeout prevents photos from loading for all residents; all entries show initials avatars.
8. UI Scenario: Profile photos are 32x32 pixel circular thumbnails positioned to the left of each resident name with 8px spacing.
9. UI Scenario: Default initials avatars use a consistent color scheme based on the first letter of the resident's last name.
10. UI Scenario: Resident names are displayed in a semi-bold font, left-aligned, with consistent vertical spacing between entries.
11. Edge Case: A resident's name is a single character or a very short name; the avatar and name display correctly without misalignment.
12. Edge Case: A profile photo is extremely large (5MB); the system serves a compressed thumbnail without impacting widget load time.
13. Security: Profile photos and names are only displayed for residents the provider has authorization to view.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Progress Notes Due list with names and profile photos loads within 2 seconds for up to 50 residents.
2. Profile photo thumbnails load progressively without blocking the rendering of names and other widget content.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on resident name in Progress Notes widget to navigate to notes entry screen.
Description of User Story: The Navigate to Progress Notes Entry feature makes each resident name in the Progress Notes Due Widget a clickable link that navigates the provider directly to the progress notes entry screen for the selected resident. Upon clicking, the system opens the progress note form pre-populated with the resident's identifying information and configured for the type of progress note that is due (monthly, weekly, or custom). The progress note entry screen includes structured sections for clinical observations, activities participated in, behavioral notes, medication adherence, and the provider's assessment and plan. After completing and saving the note, the provider can return to the dashboard or proceed to the next resident with pending notes. The navigation preserves context so the provider can efficiently work through multiple pending notes in sequence.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks a resident name and the notes entry screen loads successfully, Then all structured sections of the progress note form are displayed.
2. If the notes entry screen fails to load, Then the system displays an error with Retry and Back to Dashboard options.
3. If the provider attempts to save a progress note with required sections empty, Then the system highlights the empty sections with validation errors.
4. If the provider navigates away with unsaved note content, Then a confirmation dialog asks whether to discard or save the draft.
5. If the progress note for the resident was completed by another provider concurrently, Then the system notifies the provider and shows the already-submitted note.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Sarah Williams" in the Progress Notes Widget and navigates to her monthly progress note entry screen.
2. Positive Functional: Provider completes all sections of the progress note, saves, and sees a success confirmation message.
3. Positive Functional: Provider saves a note and clicks "Next Resident" to continue with the next pending progress note.
4. Positive Functional: Provider returns to the dashboard after saving; "Sarah Williams" is no longer listed in the Progress Notes Due Widget.
5. Negative: Progress note entry screen fails to load; error displays with Retry and Back options.
6. Negative: Provider tries to save with the clinical observations section empty; validation error highlights the required field.
7. Negative: Provider navigates away with unsaved content; confirmation dialog appears asking to save draft or discard.
8. UI Scenario: The progress note entry screen displays the resident header followed by expandable sections: Clinical Observations, Activities, Behavioral Notes, Medication Adherence, Assessment & Plan.
9. UI Scenario: Each section contains appropriate input fields (text areas, checkboxes, dropdowns) with clear labels and character count indicators.
10. UI Scenario: Save, Back to Dashboard, and Next Resident buttons are positioned in a fixed footer bar for easy access.
11. Edge Case: Provider is documenting the last remaining pending progress note; "Next Resident" button is disabled or hidden.
12. Edge Case: Provider opens a progress note entry, is interrupted, and returns 30 minutes later; the auto-saved draft is still available.
13. Security: Progress note entry is restricted to providers with "create_progress_notes" permission; read-only users can view but not edit notes.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The progress notes entry screen loads within 2 seconds of clicking a resident name.
2. Saving a completed progress note processes and confirms within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on Quick Links to navigate to the respective page.
Description of User Story: The Dashboard Quick Links feature provides a set of shortcut links on the dashboard that allow providers to navigate directly to frequently used pages or modules within the platform. The Quick Links section is displayed as a row or grid of icon-labeled buttons or tiles, each representing a key area such as Attendance, Vitals, Progress Notes, Residents, Scheduling, Billing, or Reports. Clicking a Quick Link immediately navigates the provider to the corresponding page without intermediate steps. The Quick Links are positioned in a consistent location on the dashboard for easy discovery and can be customized by the administrator to show the most relevant links for the facility's workflow. Each link includes a descriptive icon and label for quick identification, and the section remains visible regardless of dashboard scroll position.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider clicks a Quick Link and the target page loads successfully, Then the provider is navigated to the correct page.
2. If the target page fails to load, Then the system displays an error message with options to retry or return to the dashboard.
3. If a Quick Link leads to a page the provider does not have permission to access, Then the link is either hidden or clicking it displays an access denied message.
4. If the administrator has not configured any Quick Links, Then the section displays a default set of commonly used links.
5. If the provider is on a mobile device, Then the Quick Links section adjusts its layout to a scrollable horizontal row or a compact grid.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Attendance" Quick Link and navigates to the Attendance management page.
2. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Residents" Quick Link and is taken to the Residents list page.
3. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "Reports" Quick Link and navigates to the Reports dashboard.
4. Positive Functional: Dashboard displays 6 Quick Links in a horizontal row with icons for Attendance, Vitals, Progress Notes, Residents, Scheduling, and Reports.
5. Negative: Provider clicks a Quick Link to a page that is temporarily unavailable; system shows an error with a Retry option.
6. Negative: Provider without billing access clicks "Billing" Quick Link; system displays an access denied message.
7. Negative: Quick Links fail to render; the section shows a fallback message "Quick Links unavailable" with a page reload suggestion.
8. UI Scenario: Quick Links are displayed as square or rectangular tiles with a descriptive icon above a text label, arranged in a horizontal row.
9. UI Scenario: Each Quick Link tile has a hover effect (subtle shadow or color change) indicating it is interactive.
10. UI Scenario: On mobile, Quick Links display as a horizontally scrollable row with touch-friendly tile sizes.
11. Edge Case: An administrator configures 15 Quick Links; the section provides scroll or pagination to accommodate all links without cluttering.
12. Edge Case: A Quick Link target page URL is changed during a platform update; the Quick Link automatically resolves to the new URL.
13. Security: Quick Links are filtered based on the provider's RBAC permissions, ensuring only accessible modules are shown as shortcuts.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Quick Links render on the dashboard within 1 second of page load as they are lightweight navigation elements.
2. Clicking a Quick Link initiates navigation within 500 milliseconds with the target page loading per its own performance standards.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view weekly vitals schedule for all active residents in month, week and day view.
Description of User Story: The Vitals Schedule Calendar View displays a comprehensive calendar showing scheduled vitals checks for all active residents across configurable time periods. The calendar supports three view modes: day view showing hourly slots with residents scheduled for vitals at each time, week view showing daily columns with vitals schedules for each day, and month view showing a monthly grid with daily vitals counts and resident names. Each calendar entry shows the resident name, the type of vitals due, and the scheduled time. The calendar uses color coding to distinguish between different vitals types (blood pressure, temperature, etc.) and indicates completion status for past entries. Navigation controls allow providers to move forward and backward through time periods, and a "Today" button quickly returns to the current date.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the weekly view has residents with vitals at the same time, Then they are stacked within the same time slot with individual entries.
2. If a day has no scheduled vitals, Then the day cell or column appears empty with no placeholder entries.
3. If a past vitals entry has been completed, Then it is marked with a completed indicator (checkmark or strikethrough).
4. If the schedule data fails to load, Then the calendar displays an error overlay with a Retry button.
5. If the provider clicks "Today", Then the calendar navigates to the current date regardless of which month or week was being viewed.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the weekly schedule and sees vitals scheduled for 10 residents across five weekdays with names and vitals types.
2. Positive Functional: Provider switches to month view and sees daily vitals counts for each day of the month.
3. Positive Functional: Provider switches to day view for today and sees hourly slots with 3 residents scheduled at 9 AM and 2 at 2 PM.
4. Positive Functional: Provider navigates forward one week and sees next week's scheduled vitals.
5. Negative: No vitals are scheduled for the selected week; the calendar shows empty day columns.
6. Negative: Schedule data service is unavailable; the calendar shows an error with a Retry button.
7. Negative: Provider navigates to a past week; completed vitals show checkmarks and missed ones show overdue indicators.
8. UI Scenario: The calendar header displays the current date range, navigation arrows (left/right), a "Today" button, and view mode toggle (Day/Week/Month).
9. UI Scenario: Calendar entries are color-coded: blue for blood pressure, green for temperature, orange for oxygen saturation, etc., with a legend.
10. UI Scenario: Completed entries display with a subtle checkmark overlay and reduced opacity compared to pending entries.
11. Edge Case: A day has 20 residents scheduled for vitals at the same time; the calendar cell shows a scrollable list or "+N more" indicator.
12. Edge Case: Provider navigates to a month 6 months in the future; the system shows scheduled recurring vitals based on care plan configurations.
13. Security: The vitals schedule only displays data for active residents within the provider's authorized facilities.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Vitals Schedule Calendar loads the weekly view with all resident schedules within 3 seconds.
2. Switching between Day, Week, and Month views updates the calendar within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to click on the “View All” so that it opens a larger (expanded) view of the scheduled calendar
Description of User Story: The Expand Vitals Calendar feature provides a "View All" button within the Vitals Schedule Calendar widget that opens a full-page expanded version of the calendar. The expanded view offers more screen real estate, allowing providers to see more time slots, more resident entries per slot, and more detailed information without scrolling within a compact widget. The expanded calendar maintains all functionality of the widget version including view mode switching between day, week, and month, navigation controls, and color-coded entries. Additional features in the expanded view include a sidebar with a list of all active residents and their vitals schedule summary, advanced filtering by vitals type or resident, and the ability to click on entries to navigate to the vitals recording form. A "Back to Dashboard" link enables easy return to the compact widget view.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the expanded calendar loads successfully, Then all resident entries, color coding, and navigation controls are fully functional.
2. If the expanded view data fails to load, Then an error page is displayed with a Retry button and a Back to Dashboard link.
3. If the provider applies a filter in the expanded view, Then only matching calendar entries are shown and a clear filter option is available.
4. If the provider clicks on a calendar entry in the expanded view, Then they are navigated to the vitals recording form for that resident.
5. If the expanded view was opened from a specific date in the widget, Then the expanded calendar opens centered on that same date.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider clicks "View All" and navigates to the expanded vitals calendar showing the full week with all resident entries visible.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views the expanded month view and sees daily vitals entries without the "+N more" truncation that appears in the widget.
3. Positive Functional: Provider filters the expanded calendar by "Blood Pressure" and sees only blood pressure check entries.
4. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on a resident entry in the expanded calendar and is navigated to their vitals recording form.
5. Negative: Expanded calendar fails to load; error page shows with Retry and Back to Dashboard options.
6. Negative: Provider applies a filter that matches no entries; calendar shows empty with a "No matching entries" message.
7. Negative: Provider clicks "View All" on a slow connection; a loading indicator is shown until the expanded view renders.
8. UI Scenario: The expanded calendar fills the full page width and height, with a sticky header containing view toggles, navigation, and filters.
9. UI Scenario: A sidebar on the expanded view lists all active residents with their next scheduled vitals date for quick reference.
10. UI Scenario: Calendar cells in the expanded view are larger with room to display full resident names and vitals type labels without truncation.
11. Edge Case: The widget was scrolled to a specific week; clicking "View All" opens the expanded view at the same week.
12. Edge Case: Provider resizes the browser window while viewing the expanded calendar; the layout adjusts responsively.
13. Security: The expanded vitals calendar enforces the same permission-based data filtering as the widget version.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The expanded vitals calendar page loads within 3 seconds of clicking "View All".
2. Filtering and view switching within the expanded calendar respond within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to filter the Vitals Schedule Calendar from the dashboard
Description of User Story: The Filter Vitals Calendar feature adds filtering controls to the Vitals Schedule Calendar widget on the dashboard, enabling providers to narrow the displayed schedule by specific criteria. Available filter options include vitals type (blood pressure, temperature, pulse, oxygen saturation, weight, respiratory rate), completion status (pending, completed, overdue), resident name search, and service type. The filter controls are positioned as dropdown menus or interactive chips within the widget header area. Applying a filter immediately updates the calendar to show only matching entries while maintaining the current view mode and date range. Active filters are displayed as removable tags, and a "Clear All Filters" option resets the calendar to its unfiltered state. The filter selections persist during the current dashboard session but reset when the provider logs out or refreshes the page.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the provider filters by "Temperature" vitals type, Then only temperature-related calendar entries are displayed.
2. If the combined filters result in no matching entries, Then the calendar shows empty slots with a message "No vitals match the selected filters".
3. If the provider removes one filter while others remain active, Then the calendar updates to reflect the remaining filters.
4. If the provider switches view modes (Day/Week/Month) while filters are active, Then the filters persist in the new view.
5. If the filter controls fail to render, Then the calendar displays in unfiltered mode with a notice that filters are temporarily unavailable.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "Blood Pressure" and the calendar shows only blood pressure check schedules.
2. Positive Functional: Provider filters by "Overdue" status and sees only overdue vitals highlighted in the calendar.
3. Positive Functional: Provider applies both vitals type and status filters; only matching entries are shown.
4. Positive Functional: Provider removes the status filter chip; the calendar updates to show all entries for the selected vitals type.
5. Negative: Filters return no matching entries; the calendar shows "No vitals match the selected filters".
6. Negative: Filter controls fail to load; the calendar displays unfiltered with a notice about filter unavailability.
7. Negative: Provider applies a filter and switches from Week to Month view; the filter persists correctly in the month view.
8. UI Scenario: Filter controls appear as compact dropdown menus or chips within the calendar widget header, not consuming excessive space.
9. UI Scenario: Active filter chips display the filter name with an "x" button for individual removal.
10. UI Scenario: A "Clear All Filters" text link appears next to the active filter chips when any filter is active.
11. Edge Case: Provider applies all available filter types simultaneously; the calendar correctly intersects all criteria.
12. Edge Case: Provider searches for a resident by partial name; the calendar shows entries for all residents matching the partial search.
13. Security: Filter options only expose vitals types and residents the provider is authorized to view.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Applying a vitals calendar filter updates the display within 1 second.
2. Clearing all filters restores the full calendar within 1 second.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view vitals history and trends for each resident with graphical display.
Description of User Story: The Vitals History and Trends feature provides a graphical visualization of each resident's vitals data over time, enabling providers to identify patterns, trends, and anomalies in a resident's health metrics. The feature displays line charts or trend graphs for each vitals type (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, oxygen saturation, weight, respiratory rate) plotted against a configurable time axis. Each chart shows data points connected by trend lines with normal range bands shaded in the background for quick visual assessment of whether readings fall within healthy parameters. The provider can select a specific resident and choose the time range (7 days, 30 days, 90 days, or custom) to view their vitals history. Interactive tooltips on data points display the exact reading value, date, time, and the provider who recorded it. The feature also highlights significant deviations from the normal range with visual markers.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the resident has vitals data for the selected time range, Then the charts render with connected data points and trend lines.
2. If the resident has no vitals data for a selected period, Then the chart shows an empty state with a message "No vitals data available for this period".
3. If a vitals reading is significantly above the normal range, Then the data point is highlighted in red and a deviation marker is shown.
4. If the provider selects a custom date range, Then the charts render data between the specified start and end dates.
5. If vitals data is sparse with large gaps, Then the chart plots available data points and shows dashed lines for estimated gaps.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views John Smith's blood pressure trend over 30 days showing daily readings with a clear downward trend within normal range.
2. Positive Functional: Provider hovers over a data point and sees "120/80 mmHg - March 15, 9:00 AM - Recorded by Nurse Davis".
3. Positive Functional: Provider switches to 90-day view and sees a broader trend of temperature readings with a fever spike highlighted on March 5.
4. Positive Functional: Provider views weight trend over 90 days and identifies a gradual 5-pound weight loss pattern.
5. Negative: Resident has no vitals recorded for the past 30 days; the chart shows "No vitals data available for this period".
6. Negative: Vitals history data fails to load; an error message displays with a Retry option.
7. Negative: Resident has only one data point in the selected range; a single dot is plotted without a trend line.
8. UI Scenario: Each vitals type (BP, HR, Temp, SpO2, Weight, RR) is displayed as a separate chart panel arranged vertically or in a tab layout.
9. UI Scenario: Normal range bands are displayed as light-shaded horizontal areas on each chart, making it easy to see if readings fall outside.
10. UI Scenario: The time range selector offers preset options (7 days, 30 days, 90 days) and a custom date range picker.
11. Edge Case: A resident has been monitored for 2 years; the provider selects a custom range of the full duration and the chart handles the large dataset without performance issues.
12. Edge Case: A vitals reading was recorded with an incorrect value and later corrected; the chart shows the corrected value with an annotation indicating the correction.
13. Security: Vitals history and trends are accessible only to providers with "view_vitals" permission for the specific resident.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Vitals trend charts render with up to 90 days of daily data points within 3 seconds.
2. Switching between time ranges updates the charts within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view list of resident requiring monthly progress notes in month, week and day view.
Description of User Story: The Progress Notes Schedule View displays a calendar-based list of residents who require monthly progress notes, presented in configurable day, week, and month view modes. The month view shows a grid where each day cell lists the residents whose monthly progress notes are due on that date. The week view provides a detailed daily breakdown for the current week showing residents and their progress note due dates. The day view focuses on a single day listing all residents with notes due that day along with their status. Each entry shows the resident name, note type, and completion status. The calendar uses color coding to indicate whether a progress note is pending, due today, overdue, or completed. View mode toggles allow switching between Day, Week, and Month perspectives, and navigation controls enable moving forward and backward through time periods.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a resident's progress note has been completed, Then their entry shows a "Completed" status with a green checkmark.
2. If a resident's progress note is overdue, Then their entry is highlighted in red with an "Overdue" label.
3. If no progress notes are due on a particular day, Then the day cell or slot is empty.
4. If the schedule data fails to load, Then the view displays an error message with a Retry button.
5. If the provider clicks on a resident name in the calendar, Then the system navigates to the progress note entry screen for that resident.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider views the month view and sees 20 residents distributed across different dates with their monthly progress note due dates.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views the week view showing 5 residents with notes due on Wednesday and 3 on Friday.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views the day view for today showing 4 residents with progress notes due, 2 completed and 2 pending.
4. Positive Functional: Provider navigates to next month and sees the upcoming progress note schedule.
5. Negative: No progress notes are scheduled for the selected week; the view shows empty day columns.
6. Negative: Schedule data fails to load; error message with Retry is displayed.
7. Negative: Provider navigates to a past month; completed and overdue notes are shown with appropriate status indicators.
8. UI Scenario: View mode toggle (Day/Week/Month) is positioned at the top of the widget as segmented buttons with the active mode highlighted.
9. UI Scenario: Resident entries in the calendar are color-coded: green for completed, orange for due today, red for overdue, gray for upcoming.
10. UI Scenario: The month view calendar grid shows day numbers with resident names listed under each date that has due notes.
11. Edge Case: A single day has 15 residents with due progress notes; the day cell in month view shows 3 names and "+12 more" with a clickable indicator.
12. Edge Case: A resident's progress note frequency changes mid-month; the calendar updates to reflect the new schedule.
13. Security: The progress notes schedule only shows residents the provider has permission to document for.
Performance Scenarios:
1. The Progress Notes Schedule View loads the month view within 3 seconds with all resident entries.
2. Switching between Day, Week, and Month views renders the updated calendar within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view dashboard alert for PA expirations within 90 days.
Description of User Story: The Automated Compliance Alert for PA Expirations displays a prominent notification on the dashboard when any resident's Prior Authorization is set to expire within the next 90 days. The alert appears as a banner or card in the dashboard alert section showing the count of upcoming PA expirations categorized by urgency: critical (within 30 days), warning (within 60 days), and informational (within 90 days). Each alert entry lists the resident name, authorization number, expiration date, and days remaining. The alert is automatically generated by the system based on expiration date calculations and requires no manual configuration. Providers can click on individual alerts to navigate to the resident's authorization details for initiating a renewal. The alert persists until the PA is renewed or the authorization expires, at which point it escalates to an "Expired" alert.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a PA expires within 30 days, Then the alert is displayed with a critical (red) urgency indicator.
2. If a PA expires within 31-60 days, Then the alert is displayed with a warning (orange) urgency indicator.
3. If a PA expires within 61-90 days, Then the alert is displayed with an informational (yellow) urgency indicator.
4. If no PAs are expiring within 90 days, Then no PA expiration alerts are shown and the alerts section indicates all PAs are current.
5. If a PA has already expired, Then the alert escalates to an "Expired" status with a red badge and is prioritized at the top.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider sees a dashboard alert "PA #12345 for John Smith expires in 15 days" with a critical red indicator.
2. Positive Functional: Provider sees 3 PA alerts categorized: 1 critical (12 days), 1 warning (45 days), 1 informational (78 days).
3. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on a PA alert and navigates to the resident's authorization details page.
4. Positive Functional: A PA is renewed; upon dashboard refresh, the alert for that PA disappears.
5. Negative: No PAs are expiring within 90 days; the PA alerts section shows "All Prior Authorizations are current".
6. Negative: A PA has expired; the alert shows "Expired" in red and is prioritized above upcoming expirations.
7. Negative: PA data service is unavailable; alerts section shows "Unable to check PA expirations - Retry".
8. UI Scenario: PA expiration alerts are displayed as cards with urgency color-coding on the left border (red/orange/yellow) and details in the body.
9. UI Scenario: Each alert card shows the resident name, PA number, expiration date, and a countdown showing "X days remaining".
10. UI Scenario: A count badge next to the "PA Expirations" header shows the total number of upcoming expirations.
11. Edge Case: A PA expires today (0 days remaining); the alert shows "Expires Today" with critical urgency.
12. Edge Case: 50 PAs are expiring within 90 days; the alerts section paginates or provides a "View All" option.
13. Security: PA expiration alerts are shown only for residents within the provider's authorized facilities.
Performance Scenarios:
1. PA expiration alerts load and render within 2 seconds as part of the dashboard load.
2. The system calculates PA expiration dates and generates alerts in real time without noticeable delay.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view dashboard alert for Standing Order renewals within 60 days.
Description of User Story: The Standing Order Renewal Alert displays a compliance notification on the dashboard when any resident's standing order is approaching its renewal date within the next 60 days. The alert follows the same visual pattern as other dashboard compliance alerts, showing the count of upcoming renewals categorized by urgency: critical (within 14 days), warning (within 30 days), and informational (within 60 days). Each alert entry includes the resident name, standing order details, prescribing physician, renewal date, and days remaining until renewal is due. The alert helps providers proactively manage standing order renewals to maintain continuous care coverage. Clicking on an alert navigates the provider to the resident's standing order details where they can initiate the renewal process. The alert automatically clears when the standing order is renewed.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a standing order renewal is within 14 days, Then the alert shows a critical (red) urgency indicator.
2. If a standing order renewal is within 15-30 days, Then the alert shows a warning (orange) urgency indicator.
3. If a standing order renewal is within 31-60 days, Then the alert shows an informational (yellow) urgency indicator.
4. If no standing orders need renewal within 60 days, Then the section shows "All Standing Orders are current".
5. If a standing order has passed its renewal date without being renewed, Then the alert escalates to "Overdue Renewal" with a critical red indicator.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Standing Order for Mary Johnson - Dr. Wilson - Renewal due in 10 days" with a critical red indicator.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views 4 standing order alerts categorized by urgency levels.
3. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on a standing order alert and navigates to Mary's standing order details.
4. Positive Functional: Standing order is renewed; the alert disappears from the dashboard.
5. Negative: No standing orders need renewal; the section shows "All Standing Orders are current".
6. Negative: A standing order renewal is overdue by 5 days; alert shows "Overdue by 5 days" in red.
7. Negative: Standing order data fails to load; alerts section shows an error with Retry.
8. UI Scenario: Standing order alerts are visually consistent with PA alerts, using color-coded borders and a structured card layout.
9. UI Scenario: Each alert card displays the standing order name, prescribing physician, renewal date, and countdown days.
10. UI Scenario: An icon distinguishes standing order alerts from PA alerts for quick visual identification.
11. Edge Case: A standing order renewal is due today; the alert shows "Renewal Due Today" with critical urgency.
12. Edge Case: A resident has multiple standing orders expiring at different times; each generates a separate alert.
13. Security: Standing order alerts are restricted to providers authorized to view the specific residents' medical records.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Standing order renewal alerts load within 2 seconds as part of the dashboard rendering.
2. Alert status updates (after renewal) are reflected on the next dashboard load within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view dashboard alert for overdue vitals checks.
Description of User Story: The Overdue Vitals Alert displays a compliance notification on the dashboard highlighting residents who have missed their scheduled vitals checks. The alert appears as a prominent card or banner in the dashboard alerts section showing the total count of overdue vitals checks and listing individual residents with details about which vitals are overdue and by how many hours or days. The alert categorizes overdue vitals by severity: recently overdue (within the last few hours), moderately overdue (1-2 days), and critically overdue (more than 2 days). Each entry shows the resident name, the specific vitals type that is overdue, the originally scheduled time, and how long it has been overdue. The alert serves as an immediate action prompt for providers to address missed vitals and maintain care quality standards. Clicking an individual overdue entry navigates directly to the vitals recording form for that resident.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If vitals are overdue by less than 4 hours, Then the entry shows a yellow "Recently Overdue" indicator.
2. If vitals are overdue by 1-2 days, Then the entry shows an orange "Moderately Overdue" indicator.
3. If vitals are overdue by more than 2 days, Then the entry shows a red "Critically Overdue" indicator.
4. If no vitals are currently overdue, Then the alert section shows "All vitals checks are on schedule" with a positive confirmation.
5. If the vitals data service is unavailable, Then the alert displays an error state with a Retry option.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Overdue Vitals: 5 checks" alert with individual entries for each overdue resident.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views an entry "John Smith - Blood Pressure - Scheduled 9:00 AM - Overdue by 3 hours" with a yellow indicator.
3. Positive Functional: Provider sees a critically overdue entry "Mary Johnson - Temperature - Overdue by 3 days" in red.
4. Positive Functional: Provider clicks an overdue entry and navigates to the vitals recording form.
5. Negative: No vitals are overdue; alert section shows "All vitals checks are on schedule".
6. Negative: Vitals data service is down; alert shows error with Retry button.
7. Negative: Provider records the overdue vitals; upon return to dashboard the alert is updated.
8. UI Scenario: Overdue vitals alerts are displayed as a list within an alert card with a header showing the total count.
9. UI Scenario: Each entry has a color-coded urgency indicator, resident name, vitals type, and time overdue displayed in a compact row.
10. UI Scenario: A bell or warning icon accompanies the overdue vitals alert header to draw attention.
11. Edge Case: A resident has 5 different vitals all overdue; each overdue vital is listed as a separate entry.
12. Edge Case: Vitals become overdue while the provider is viewing the dashboard; the alert updates on the next refresh cycle.
13. Security: Overdue vitals alerts only display for residents within the provider's authorized facilities and care scope.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Overdue vitals alerts load and render within 2 seconds as part of the dashboard load.
2. Clicking an overdue entry navigates to the vitals form within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a Provider, I should be able to view dashboard alert for overdue progress notes.
Description of User Story: The Overdue Progress Notes Alert displays a compliance notification on the dashboard when residents have progress notes that are past their due date and have not been completed. The alert shows the total count of overdue progress notes and lists individual residents with details about the note type, original due date, and how long the note has been overdue. The alert categorizes entries by severity: recently overdue (1-3 days), moderately overdue (4-7 days), and critically overdue (more than 7 days). Each entry includes the resident name, progress note type (monthly, weekly, etc.), due date, and the number of days overdue. This alert is essential for maintaining regulatory compliance as progress notes are often required by state health departments and Medicaid programs. Clicking on an entry navigates directly to the progress note entry form for that resident. The alert clears for a resident once their overdue note is submitted.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a progress note is overdue by 1-3 days, Then the entry shows a yellow "Recently Overdue" indicator.
2. If a progress note is overdue by 4-7 days, Then the entry shows an orange "Moderately Overdue" indicator.
3. If a progress note is overdue by more than 7 days, Then the entry shows a red "Critically Overdue" indicator.
4. If no progress notes are overdue, Then the alert section shows "All progress notes are up to date" with a positive indicator.
5. If the progress notes service is unavailable, Then the alert displays an error state with a Retry option.
Test Scenarios:
1. Positive Functional: Provider sees "Overdue Progress Notes: 4" alert with individual entries for each resident.
2. Positive Functional: Provider views "Sarah Williams - Monthly Note - Due March 15 - Overdue by 10 days" with a red critically overdue indicator.
3. Positive Functional: Provider views "Alex Brown - Monthly Note - Due March 23 - Overdue by 2 days" with a yellow recently overdue indicator.
4. Positive Functional: Provider clicks on an overdue entry and navigates to the progress note form for that resident.
5. Negative: No progress notes are overdue; alert section shows "All progress notes are up to date".
6. Negative: Progress notes data service is unavailable; alert shows error with Retry.
7. Negative: Provider submits the overdue note and returns to the dashboard; that resident is removed from the overdue list.
8. UI Scenario: Overdue progress notes alerts display in a card layout consistent with other compliance alerts, with urgency color coding.
9. UI Scenario: Each entry shows resident name, note type, due date, and "Overdue by X days" in a compact row format.
10. UI Scenario: A document icon or progress notes icon accompanies the alert header for visual identification.
11. Edge Case: A resident has overdue progress notes from two consecutive months; both appear as separate alert entries.
12. Edge Case: A progress note was due today and it is now past midnight; the note is classified as "Overdue by 1 day" on the new day.
13. Security: Overdue progress notes alerts are displayed only for residents the provider is responsible for and authorized to document.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Overdue progress notes alerts load and render within 2 seconds as part of the dashboard load.
2. Clicking an overdue entry navigates to the progress note form within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to create folders and subfolders to organize documents across organizations and facilities.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to create new folders and subfolders within the Document Management module to establish a structured directory for organizing documents. The SuperAdmin can create top-level folders as well as nested subfolders under any existing folder, allowing a multi-level hierarchy that mirrors organizational and facility structures. When creating a folder, the SuperAdmin provides a folder name and selects the parent location within the directory tree. The system validates that no duplicate folder names exist at the same level and enforces naming conventions such as maximum character limits and disallowed special characters. This capability is foundational to the entire Document Management system because without an organized folder structure, documents cannot be logically categorized or permissioned. All subsequent document operations such as uploading, sharing, permissioning, and auditing depend on this folder hierarchy being in place.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin enters a valid and unique folder name at the selected directory level, Then the system must create the folder and display it in the tree.
   Else if the name already exists at that level, Then the system must show a duplicate name error and not create the folder.

2. If the folder name contains only allowed characters and is within the character limit, Then the folder is created successfully.
   Else if the name contains disallowed special characters or exceeds the limit, Then the system must display a validation error.

3. If the SuperAdmin submits the create folder form with an empty name field, Then the system must show a required field validation error.

4. If the SuperAdmin creates a subfolder under an existing folder, Then the subfolder must appear nested under the correct parent in the directory hierarchy.

5. If the folder creation is successful, Then the folder must be immediately available for document uploads and permission assignments.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can create a new top-level folder with a valid name and it appears in the directory tree.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can create a subfolder under an existing folder and it appears nested correctly.
3. Verify multiple subfolders can be created under the same parent folder with different names.
4. Verify a newly created folder is immediately available for uploading documents.
5. Verify the folder creation success confirmation message is displayed.

Negative:
6. Verify that creating a folder with a duplicate name at the same directory level shows a duplicate error.
7. Verify that submitting with an empty folder name shows a required field error.
8. Verify that a folder name exceeding the maximum character limit is rejected with a validation error.
9. Verify that folder names with disallowed special characters (e.g., /, \, <, >) are rejected.
10. Verify that a folder name consisting only of spaces is rejected.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the "Create Folder" button is visible and accessible on the Document Management screen.
12. Verify the folder creation dialog displays with a name input field and submit/cancel buttons.
13. Verify the newly created folder appears in the correct position in the tree view with the correct folder icon.
14. Verify the folder creation form is responsive on tablet and mobile screen sizes.

Edge Cases:
15. Verify creating deeply nested subfolders (e.g., 10 levels deep) works correctly.
16. Verify creating a folder with the maximum allowed character length succeeds.
17. Verify concurrent folder creation by two SuperAdmins at the same level handles duplicates correctly.

Security:
18. Verify that non-SuperAdmin users cannot access the create folder functionality.
19. Verify folder creation actions are logged in the audit trail with user details and timestamp.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify folder creation completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the directory tree refreshes and displays the new folder within 3 seconds after creation.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to rename any folder or subfolder.
Description of User Story: This story allows the SuperAdmin to rename any existing folder or subfolder within the Document Management directory structure. The SuperAdmin selects a folder from the tree view and invokes the rename action, which presents the current name in an editable field. After entering the new name, the system validates it against naming rules and checks for duplicates at the same directory level. Upon successful rename, all references to the folder including breadcrumb navigation and permission assignments are updated automatically. This feature is important for maintaining an organized and intuitive directory structure as organizational naming conventions evolve over time. Without the ability to rename, SuperAdmins would need to create new folders, move all contents, and delete the old folder, which is error-prone.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin enters a valid unique new name for the folder, Then the system must rename the folder and update all references.
   Else if the new name duplicates an existing folder at the same level, Then the system must show a duplicate error and keep the original name.

2. If the new name is empty or contains only spaces, Then the system must show a validation error and not rename the folder.

3. If the SuperAdmin cancels the rename action, Then the folder must retain its original name with no changes.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can rename a top-level folder with a valid new name.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can rename a subfolder with a valid new name.
3. Verify breadcrumb navigation updates to reflect the renamed folder.
4. Verify documents inside the renamed folder remain accessible.
5. Verify folder permissions remain intact after renaming.

Negative:
6. Verify renaming to a duplicate name at the same level shows a duplicate error.
7. Verify renaming with an empty name shows a required field error.
8. Verify renaming with only spaces is rejected.
9. Verify renaming with disallowed special characters is rejected.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the rename option is available in the folder context menu.
11. Verify the rename dialog pre-fills the current folder name.
12. Verify the updated name is immediately reflected in the tree view.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify renaming a folder with many nested subfolders does not affect child items.
14. Verify renaming to the same name (no change) is handled gracefully.

Security:
15. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot rename folders.
16. Verify rename actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify folder rename completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the tree view refreshes with the new name within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to delete a folder and all its contents when it is no longer needed.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to delete folders that are no longer needed from the Document Management directory structure. When a folder is selected for deletion, the system displays a confirmation prompt warning that all contents including subfolders and documents will also be affected. Deleted folders and their contents are moved to the trash folder as part of the soft-delete policy, allowing recovery within a defined retention period. The system prevents deletion of folders that have active shared links unless those are revoked first. This feature is critical for maintaining a clean and relevant directory structure and preventing clutter from accumulating over time. Without folder deletion, the document management system would become increasingly difficult to navigate as obsolete folders pile up.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin confirms folder deletion, Then the system must move the folder and all its contents to the trash folder.
   Else if the SuperAdmin cancels, Then the folder must remain in its current location unchanged.

2. If the folder contains active shared links, Then the system must warn the SuperAdmin before proceeding.

3. If the folder is empty, Then the system must still show a confirmation prompt before deletion.

4. If the folder has been soft-deleted, Then it must be recoverable within the defined retention period.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can delete an empty folder after confirmation.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can delete a folder with documents and subfolders, all moved to trash.
3. Verify the deleted folder appears in the trash folder.
4. Verify the deleted folder is no longer visible in the directory tree.

Negative:
5. Verify cancelling the deletion confirmation keeps the folder intact.
6. Verify attempting to delete a folder with active shared links shows a warning.
7. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot delete folders.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the delete option is available in the folder context menu.
9. Verify the confirmation dialog clearly states the folder name and warns about contents.
10. Verify the tree view updates immediately after folder deletion.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify deleting a deeply nested folder moves the entire subtree to trash.
12. Verify deleting a folder that another user is currently browsing is handled gracefully.

Security:
13. Verify folder deletion is logged in the audit trail with user details and timestamp.
14. Verify deleted folder contents cannot be accessed via direct URL.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify deleting a folder with up to 100 documents completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify the directory tree refreshes within 3 seconds after deletion.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to move a folder from one location to another within the directory structure.
Description of User Story: This story allows the SuperAdmin to move an existing folder from its current location to a different location within the directory hierarchy. The SuperAdmin selects a folder and invokes the move action, which presents a tree view of available destination folders. Upon selecting a destination and confirming, the folder along with all its subfolders, documents, and permissions is relocated to the new parent. The system validates that the move does not create circular references or duplicate folder names at the destination level. This feature is essential for reorganizing the document structure as business needs change without losing any content or access configurations. Without folder move capability, reorganization would require manually recreating folders, moving individual documents, and reassigning all permissions.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin selects a valid destination without naming conflicts, Then the system must move the folder and all contents to the new location.
   Else if the destination has a folder with the same name, Then the system must display a duplicate error.

2. If the SuperAdmin attempts to move a folder into its own subtree, Then the system must block the action and show a circular reference error.

3. If the move is successful, Then all permissions and shared links associated with the folder must be preserved.

4. If the SuperAdmin cancels the move, Then no changes must be made to the folder location.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can move a folder to a different parent folder.
2. Verify all subfolders and documents inside the moved folder are preserved.
3. Verify folder permissions are retained after the move.
4. Verify breadcrumb navigation reflects the new folder location.

Negative:
5. Verify moving a folder to a destination with a duplicate name shows an error.
6. Verify moving a folder into its own subfolder is blocked with a circular reference error.
7. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot move folders.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the move dialog displays a tree view for selecting the destination.
9. Verify the current location of the folder is highlighted in the tree.
10. Verify the directory tree updates to reflect the new location after the move.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify moving a folder with many nested levels works correctly.
12. Verify moving a folder to the root level works correctly.

Security:
13. Verify folder move actions are logged in the audit trail.
14. Verify document access via old bookmarked URLs redirects or shows an appropriate message.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify moving a folder with 50+ documents completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify the tree view refreshes within 3 seconds after the move.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to view the complete folder hierarchy across all organizations and facilities in a tree view.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to view the entire folder hierarchy across all organizations and facilities in a visually organized tree view within the Document Management module. The tree view displays folders and subfolders in an expandable and collapsible format, allowing drill-down into any level of the directory structure. Each folder node shows its name and may display metadata such as document count and last modified date. The SuperAdmin can expand or collapse branches to focus on specific areas of the hierarchy without losing context.
