This comprehensive view is essential for understanding how documents are organized, identifying gaps, and making informed decisions about folder management. Without this tree view, navigating a complex multi-organization multi-facility document repository would be disorienting and inefficient.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the Document Management module is accessed by a SuperAdmin, Then the system must display the complete folder hierarchy in a tree view.

2. If a folder has subfolders, Then it must display an expand/collapse indicator.
   Else if the folder has no subfolders, Then no expand/collapse indicator should be shown.

3. If the SuperAdmin clicks a folder, Then the system must load and display the documents contained in that folder.

4. If the folder structure spans multiple organizations, Then all folders must be visible in the tree.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the tree view loads and displays all top-level folders when the SuperAdmin opens Document Management.
2. Verify clicking a collapsed folder expands it to show subfolders.
3. Verify clicking an expanded folder collapses it to hide subfolders.
4. Verify selecting a folder displays its documents in the content area.
5. Verify folders from all organizations and facilities are visible.

Negative:
6. Verify the tree view shows an empty state message when no folders exist.
7. Verify non-SuperAdmin users see only folders they have permission to access.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the tree view uses expand/collapse icons for folders with children.
9. Verify the selected folder is visually highlighted in the tree.
10. Verify the tree view is scrollable when the hierarchy is large.
11. Verify the tree view is responsive on different screen sizes.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify the tree view handles a deeply nested hierarchy without breaking layout.
13. Verify the tree view handles a large number of folders at the same level.

Security:
14. Verify folder visibility respects organizational access controls.
15. Verify the tree view does not expose folder metadata to unauthorized users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the tree view loads within 3 seconds for a hierarchy of 1000+ folders.
2. Verify expanding a folder with 100 subfolders renders within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to upload single or multiple documents at once to any folder.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to upload one or more documents simultaneously to any selected folder within the Document Management system. The SuperAdmin navigates to the desired folder, invokes the upload action, and selects files from their local device using a file picker or drag-and-drop interface. The system accepts multiple files in a single operation and displays upload progress for each file with status indicators. File size limits and allowed file type restrictions are enforced during upload, and violations are reported immediately without blocking the remaining valid files. This bulk upload capability is critical for efficiency when onboarding large sets of compliance documents, contracts, or facility records. Without multi-file upload, administrators would need to upload documents one at a time, which is impractical for facilities managing hundreds of documents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin selects valid files within size and type limits, Then the system must upload all files to the selected folder.
   Else if any file exceeds the size limit, Then the system must reject that file and continue uploading the remaining valid files.

2. If the upload is successful, Then each file must appear in the folder document list immediately.

3. If the SuperAdmin cancels the upload mid-progress, Then files already uploaded must remain and pending files must not be uploaded.

4. If the upload fails due to a network error, Then the system must display an error and allow retry.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can upload a single document to a selected folder.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can upload multiple documents simultaneously.
3. Verify uploaded documents appear in the folder document list immediately.
4. Verify upload progress indicators are displayed for each file.
5. Verify a success message is shown after all files are uploaded.

Negative:
6. Verify a file exceeding the maximum size limit is rejected with a clear error message.
7. Verify uploading a file with an unsupported file type is rejected.
8. Verify uploading with no file selected shows an appropriate message.
9. Verify that a network interruption during upload shows an error with retry option.

UI Scenarios:
10. Verify the upload button is clearly visible within the folder view.
11. Verify drag-and-drop upload area is available and functional.
12. Verify progress bars or percentage indicators are shown during upload.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify uploading a file with a very long filename is handled correctly.
14. Verify uploading a 0-byte file is handled gracefully.
15. Verify uploading duplicate-named files to the same folder is handled appropriately.

Security:
16. Verify uploaded files are scanned for malware or malicious content.
17. Verify upload actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify uploading a 10MB document completes within 10 seconds on a standard connection.
2. Verify batch uploading 10 documents completes within 30 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, the system should support upload of standard file types (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PNG, JPG) so that all common document formats are accepted.
Description of User Story: This story ensures the Document Management system supports uploading standard file types including PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PNG, and JPG formats. When the SuperAdmin uploads a document, the system validates the file extension and MIME type to confirm it matches one of the accepted formats. If a file with an unsupported type is selected, the system displays a clear error message indicating the allowed formats and rejects the file. This validation occurs both on the client side for immediate feedback and on the server side for security. Supporting these standard file types is essential because healthcare facilities regularly work with PDF compliance documents, DOCX policies, XLSX reports, and PNG/JPG images. Without file type validation, the system could accept potentially harmful or unusable file formats that pose security risks or cannot be previewed.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the uploaded file type is PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PNG, or JPG, Then the system must accept and process the upload.
   Else if the file type is not in the supported list, Then the system must reject the file with an error listing supported formats.

2. If a batch upload contains a mix of supported and unsupported types, Then supported files must be uploaded while unsupported files are rejected individually.

3. If a file has a supported extension but an invalid MIME type, Then the system must reject the file for security reasons.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify a PDF file can be uploaded successfully.
2. Verify a DOCX file can be uploaded successfully.
3. Verify an XLSX file can be uploaded successfully.
4. Verify a PNG file can be uploaded successfully.
5. Verify a JPG file can be uploaded successfully.
6. Verify a batch upload with all supported types succeeds.

Negative:
7. Verify an .exe file is rejected with the correct error message.
8. Verify a .bat file is rejected with the correct error message.
9. Verify a .zip file is rejected if not in the allowed list.
10. Verify a file with a renamed extension (e.g., .exe renamed to .pdf) is rejected by server-side validation.

UI Scenarios:
11. Verify the file picker filters to show only supported file types by default.
12. Verify the error message for unsupported types clearly lists all accepted formats.

Edge Cases:
13. Verify uploading a file with mixed case extension (e.g., .PDF, .Docx) is accepted.
14. Verify uploading a file with no extension is rejected.

Security:
15. Verify server-side MIME type validation prevents disguised malicious files.
16. Verify file type validation cannot be bypassed via API calls.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify file type validation completes within 1 second before upload begins.
2. Verify uploading a large supported file (50MB) completes without timeout.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to view/preview a document directly in the system without downloading it.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to view or preview documents directly within the Document Management interface without needing to download them. When the SuperAdmin clicks on a document, the system renders an inline preview using a built-in viewer that supports PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PNG, and JPG formats. The viewer provides standard navigation controls including page scrolling, zoom in/out, and page navigation for multi-page documents. Image files are displayed with fit-to-screen capability and optional zoom controls. This in-browser preview is critical for workflow efficiency as it allows SuperAdmins to quickly review document contents without downloading and opening in external applications. It also enhances security by reducing the number of document copies stored on local devices.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the document is a supported preview format (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PNG, JPG), Then the system must render it inline in the document viewer.
   Else if the format does not support preview, Then the system must show a message and offer a download option.

2. If the document is multi-page, Then the viewer must provide page navigation controls.

3. If the document fails to load for preview, Then the system must display an error with an option to download instead.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify a PDF document opens in the inline viewer with page navigation.
2. Verify a DOCX document opens in the inline viewer.
3. Verify an XLSX document opens in the inline viewer.
4. Verify a PNG image opens in the viewer with zoom capability.
5. Verify a JPG image opens in the viewer with zoom capability.
6. Verify zoom in and zoom out controls work correctly.

Negative:
7. Verify a corrupted file shows an appropriate error message in the viewer.
8. Verify an unsupported preview format shows a message with download option.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify the document viewer has a close button to return to the folder view.
10. Verify the viewer displays the document name in the header.
11. Verify the viewer is responsive on different screen sizes.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify previewing a very large document (100+ pages) works without crashing.
13. Verify previewing a high-resolution image renders correctly.

Security:
14. Verify document preview does not cache sensitive content in the browser.
15. Verify document view actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document preview loads within 3 seconds for a 10-page PDF.
2. Verify image preview loads within 2 seconds for a 5MB image.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to download any document to my local device.
Description of User Story: This story allows the SuperAdmin to download any document from the Document Management system to their local device. The SuperAdmin can initiate a download from the document list, document preview, or document context menu. The system serves the file in its original format with the correct filename and extension, triggering browser default download behavior. The download preserves the exact content and formatting of the stored document without any alterations. This feature is necessary for SuperAdmins who need to work with documents offline, share them via external channels, or print physical copies. Without download capability, documents stored in the system would be accessible only through the web interface, limiting their practical utility.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin clicks the download action for a document, Then the system must serve the file in its original format for download.

2. If the download fails due to a network error, Then the system must show an error message and allow retry.

3. If the document has been deleted or is unavailable, Then the system must show an appropriate error message.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can download a PDF document from the document list.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can download a document from the preview viewer.
3. Verify the downloaded file has the correct filename and extension.
4. Verify the downloaded file content matches the original uploaded document.

Negative:
5. Verify downloading a deleted document shows an appropriate error.
6. Verify download behavior when network connectivity is lost mid-download.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the download button is visible in the document list and preview.
8. Verify a download progress indicator is shown for large files.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify downloading a very large file works correctly.
10. Verify downloading a file with special characters in the name works correctly.

Security:
11. Verify download actions are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify that only authorized users can download documents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify download initiation begins within 2 seconds of clicking the button.
2. Verify a 10MB file downloads within 15 seconds on a standard connection.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to delete a document from the system with a confirmation prompt.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to delete individual documents from the Document Management system with a mandatory confirmation step. When the SuperAdmin selects the delete action, the system presents a confirmation dialog displaying the document name and a warning message. Upon confirmation, the document is soft-deleted and moved to the trash folder where it can be recovered within the defined retention period. The confirmation prompt is a critical safeguard as deleted documents may contain irreplaceable compliance records or patient-related materials. The system also checks for active shared links or dependencies before proceeding with deletion. Without this confirmation mechanism, a single misclick could result in the loss of important documents, creating compliance and operational risks.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin confirms document deletion, Then the document must be moved to the trash folder.
   Else if the SuperAdmin cancels, Then the document must remain in its current location.

2. If the document has active shared links, Then the system must warn the SuperAdmin before proceeding.

3. If the document is in the trash, Then it must be recoverable within the retention period.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can delete a document after confirming in the dialog.
2. Verify the deleted document appears in the trash folder.
3. Verify the deleted document is removed from the original folder list.

Negative:
4. Verify cancelling the confirmation dialog keeps the document unchanged.
5. Verify non-SuperAdmin users without delete permission cannot delete documents.

UI Scenarios:
6. Verify the confirmation dialog shows the document name and a clear warning.
7. Verify the confirmation dialog has confirm and cancel buttons.
8. Verify the document list updates after deletion.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify deleting a document another user is currently previewing is handled gracefully.
10. Verify deleting a document with active shared links shows a warning.

Security:
11. Verify document deletion is logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify deleted documents are not accessible via direct URL.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document deletion completes within 2 seconds after confirmation.
2. Verify the document list refreshes within 2 seconds after deletion.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to rename any document in the system.
Description of User Story: This story allows the SuperAdmin to rename any document stored in the Document Management system. The SuperAdmin selects a document and invokes the rename action, which presents the current document name in an editable field while preserving the file extension. After entering the new name, the system validates it for uniqueness within the same folder, character limits, and disallowed characters. The rename operation updates the document name throughout the system including search results, recent documents, and shared links. This capability is important for maintaining consistent naming conventions across facilities and organizations as standards evolve. Without document renaming, files uploaded with incorrect or inconsistent names would permanently clutter the system and reduce findability.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin enters a valid unique name, Then the system must rename the document.
   Else if the name duplicates another document in the same folder, Then the system must show an error.

2. If the name field is empty, Then the system must show a required field error.

3. If the SuperAdmin cancels, Then no changes must be made to the document name.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can rename a document successfully.
2. Verify the renamed document appears with the new name in the folder list.
3. Verify the file extension is preserved after renaming.
4. Verify the renamed document is searchable by the new name.

Negative:
5. Verify duplicate name in the same folder shows an error.
6. Verify empty name shows a required field error.
7. Verify disallowed characters in the name are rejected.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the rename dialog pre-fills the current document name.
9. Verify the rename option is accessible from the document context menu.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify renaming a document with a very long name at the character limit.
11. Verify renaming a shared document updates the shared link reference.

Security:
12. Verify rename actions are logged in the audit trail.
13. Verify non-SuperAdmin users without edit permission cannot rename documents.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document rename completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify search results reflect the new name within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to move a document from one folder to another.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to relocate a document from its current folder to a different folder within the Document Management directory structure. The SuperAdmin selects a document and invokes the move action, which presents a folder tree view for choosing the destination. Upon selecting the destination and confirming, the document is transferred to the new folder while preserving all metadata, version history, and audit trail. The system validates that no document with the same name exists in the destination folder. This feature is essential for organizing documents that may have been uploaded to the wrong folder or need to be reclassified. Without document move capability, users would need to download, re-upload, and delete the original, losing version history and audit continuity.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin selects a valid destination without naming conflicts, Then the system must move the document.
   Else if a document with the same name exists at the destination, Then the system must show a duplicate error.

2. If the move is successful, Then version history and audit trail must be preserved.

3. If the SuperAdmin cancels, Then the document must remain in its original location.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify a document can be moved to a different folder.
2. Verify the document appears in the destination folder after move.
3. Verify the document is removed from the original folder.
4. Verify version history is preserved after the move.

Negative:
5. Verify moving to a folder with a duplicate name shows an error.
6. Verify non-SuperAdmin users without permission cannot move documents.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the move dialog displays a folder tree for destination selection.
8. Verify the current folder is indicated in the tree view.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify moving a document with active shared links preserves the links.
10. Verify moving a document to a restricted folder is handled appropriately.

Security:
11. Verify document move actions are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify document permissions are updated to reflect the destination folder rules.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document move completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify both source and destination folder lists refresh within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to upload a new version of an existing document to replace the old one while maintaining version history.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to upload a new version of an existing document, effectively replacing the current version while automatically preserving the full version history. When the SuperAdmin selects the replace action on a document, the system allows them to upload a new file which becomes the current version while the previous version is retained in history. Each version is timestamped with the upload date and the user who uploaded it, creating a complete audit trail. The system validates that the replacement file is of a supported type and does not exceed size limits. This versioning capability is critical for compliance-heavy healthcare environments where regulatory documents and consent forms are periodically updated. Without version history, there would be no way to track document changes over time or roll back to a previous version if errors are discovered.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin uploads a valid replacement file, Then the system must set it as the current version and retain the previous version in history.
   Else if the file is invalid, Then the system must reject it and keep the current version.

2. If the replacement is successful, Then the version history must show both old and new versions with timestamps.

3. If the SuperAdmin cancels the replacement, Then no changes must be made.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify uploading a new version replaces the current document.
2. Verify the previous version is preserved in version history.
3. Verify version history shows uploader name and timestamp for each version.
4. Verify the new version is immediately viewable and downloadable.

Negative:
5. Verify an unsupported file type is rejected as a replacement.
6. Verify a file exceeding size limits is rejected.
7. Verify cancelling the replacement keeps the current version.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the replace/update option is accessible from the document context menu.
9. Verify upload progress is shown during replacement.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify replacing a document multiple times maintains all versions in history.
11. Verify replacing with a different file format (e.g., PDF to DOCX) is handled.

Security:
12. Verify replacement actions are logged in the audit trail.
13. Verify the replacement file undergoes security scanning.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document replacement completes within 5 seconds for a 10MB file.
2. Verify version history loads within 2 seconds after replacement.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to set access permissions (View / Download / Upload / Edit / Delete) at the folder level for specific roles or organizations.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to configure granular access permissions on folders, controlling which roles or organizations can View, Download, Upload, Edit, or Delete documents within those folders. The SuperAdmin selects a folder and opens the permissions panel, where they can assign specific permission levels to individual roles, organizations, or facilities. Permissions are inherited by all documents within the folder unless overridden at the document level, providing a hierarchical permission model. The system displays a clear matrix of current permissions allowing the SuperAdmin to review and modify access at any time. This granular access control is critical in healthcare environments where compliance documents, financial records, and clinical policies have different audiences. Without folder-level permissions, all documents would be accessible to all users, violating HIPAA requirements and organizational confidentiality standards.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin assigns permissions to a role for a folder, Then all users with that role must gain the specified access.

2. If the SuperAdmin removes a permission, Then the affected users must immediately lose that access.

3. If a document within the folder has overridden permissions, Then the document-level permissions must take precedence.

4. If no permissions are assigned to a folder, Then only SuperAdmins must have access.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can assign View permission to a role for a folder.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can assign multiple permissions (View, Download, Upload) to a role.
3. Verify permissions are inherited by documents within the folder.
4. Verify the SuperAdmin can remove a previously assigned permission.
5. Verify permissions can be assigned to organizations and facilities.

Negative:
6. Verify a user without assigned permission cannot access the folder documents.
7. Verify removing all permissions from a role blocks that role from the folder.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the permissions panel displays a clear matrix of roles and permissions.
9. Verify checkboxes or toggles are used for assigning/removing permissions.
10. Verify changes are saved with a success confirmation.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify assigning permissions to a role with no active users is handled gracefully.
12. Verify permission changes propagate to deeply nested subfolders.

Security:
13. Verify permission changes are logged in the audit trail.
14. Verify that permission assignments cannot be modified by non-SuperAdmin users.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify permission changes take effect within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the permissions panel loads within 2 seconds for folders with many role assignments.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to set access permissions on individual documents, overriding folder-level permissions when needed.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to set access permissions on individual documents that can override the permissions inherited from the parent folder. The SuperAdmin selects a document and opens its permissions panel, where they can assign or restrict View, Download, Edit, and Delete access for specific roles, organizations, or users. Document-level permissions take precedence over folder-level permissions, allowing fine-grained control for sensitive documents. The permissions panel clearly indicates which permissions are inherited from the folder and which have been explicitly set. This capability is essential for scenarios where a single sensitive document in a general folder needs restricted access, such as a financial audit report in a shared compliance folder. Without document-level permission overrides, all documents in a folder would share the same access level, making it impossible to protect individual sensitive documents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If document-level permissions are set, Then they must take precedence over folder-level permissions.
   Else if no document-level permissions are set, Then folder-level permissions must apply.

2. If the SuperAdmin restricts access at the document level, Then affected users must immediately lose access.

3. If the SuperAdmin removes the document-level override, Then folder permissions must be re-applied automatically.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can set document-level permissions that override folder permissions.
2. Verify removing a document-level override restores folder-level permissions.
3. Verify document-level restrictions prevent access even when folder allows it.
4. Verify document-level grants allow access even when folder restricts it.

Negative:
5. Verify a user without document-level or folder-level permission cannot access the document.
6. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot modify document permissions.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the permissions panel distinguishes inherited vs. overridden permissions.
8. Verify the document permissions panel is accessible from the document context menu.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify setting contradictory permissions (folder allows, document denies) works as expected.
10. Verify moving a document with overridden permissions preserves the overrides.

Security:
11. Verify permission changes are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify document-level permissions cannot be bypassed via direct URL.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document permission changes take effect within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the document permissions panel loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to restrict a folder or document to a specific organization so that only users of that organization can access it.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to restrict folder or document access to a specific organization, ensuring that only users belonging to that organization can view or interact with the content. The SuperAdmin selects a folder or document and assigns an organization-level restriction from the access control settings. Once restricted, the folder or document becomes invisible to users from other organizations, even if they have role-based permissions. The system enforces this restriction at both the UI and API levels to prevent unauthorized access. This organizational isolation is critical in multi-tenant healthcare environments where multiple organizations share the same platform but must maintain strict data separation. Without organization-level access control, sensitive documents from one organization could be inadvertently exposed to users of another organization.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a folder or document is restricted to a specific organization, Then only users from that organization must have access.
   Else if no organization restriction is set, Then the standard permission rules must apply.

2. If the SuperAdmin removes the organization restriction, Then access must revert to the default permission settings.

3. If a restricted folder contains subfolders and documents, Then all children must inherit the organization restriction.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can restrict a folder to a specific organization.
2. Verify users from the restricted organization can access the folder.
3. Verify removing the restriction restores access based on other permissions.
4. Verify subfolders inherit the organization restriction.

Negative:
5. Verify users from other organizations cannot see the restricted folder.
6. Verify users from other organizations cannot access restricted documents via direct URL.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the organization restriction option is available in the folder/document settings.
8. Verify a visual indicator shows the folder is organization-restricted.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify restricting a folder that was previously accessible to multiple organizations immediately removes access for excluded organizations.
10. Verify a SuperAdmin from any organization can still manage the restriction.

Security:
11. Verify organization restrictions are enforced at the API level.
12. Verify restriction changes are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify organization restriction changes take effect within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the folder tree filters correctly within 2 seconds for organization-restricted views.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to restrict a folder or document to a specific facility so that only users of that facility can access it.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to restrict folder or document access to a specific facility within an organization. Only users associated with that facility can view or interact with the restricted content. The SuperAdmin selects a folder or document and assigns a facility-level restriction, which takes priority over broader organization-level access. This granular control ensures that facility-specific documents such as local compliance records, staffing documents, and operational manuals are accessible only to relevant personnel. The restriction is enforced at both the UI and API levels to prevent unauthorized cross-facility access. Without facility-level access control, users at one facility could view sensitive documents belonging to another facility within the same organization.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a folder or document is restricted to a specific facility, Then only users from that facility must have access.
   Else if no facility restriction is set, Then the standard permission rules must apply.

2. If the SuperAdmin removes the facility restriction, Then access must revert to the default permission settings.

3. If a restricted folder contains subfolders, Then all children must inherit the facility restriction.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can restrict a folder to a specific facility.
2. Verify users from the restricted facility can access the folder.
3. Verify removing the restriction restores default access.
4. Verify subfolders inherit the facility restriction.

Negative:
5. Verify users from other facilities cannot see the restricted folder.
6. Verify users from other facilities cannot access restricted documents via direct URL.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the facility restriction option is available in folder/document settings.
8. Verify a visual indicator shows the folder is facility-restricted.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify applying both organization and facility restrictions works correctly with the most restrictive rule taking effect.
10. Verify changing a user from one facility to another updates their access immediately.

Security:
11. Verify facility restrictions are enforced at the API level.
12. Verify restriction changes are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify facility restriction changes take effect within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the folder tree filters correctly within 2 seconds for facility-restricted views.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to assign document access based on user roles (e.g., only Facility Directors can access compliance documents).
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to assign document and folder access based on user roles defined in the system, such as Facility Director, Nurse, Billing Coordinator, or Administrative Staff. The SuperAdmin selects a folder or document and assigns view, download, edit, or delete permissions to specific roles. Users are granted access based on their assigned role, and role changes automatically update access accordingly. This role-based access control (RBAC) is fundamental to maintaining data security and compliance in healthcare environments where different staff members have different information needs. For example, compliance documents may be accessible only to Facility Directors while clinical forms are accessible to nursing staff. Without RBAC, managing individual user permissions would be impractical in large facilities with high staff turnover.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a role is assigned View permission for a folder, Then all users with that role must be able to view the folder and its documents.

2. If a user role is changed, Then the system must update their access to reflect the new role permissions immediately.

3. If a user has multiple roles, Then they must receive the union of all permissions from all their roles.

4. If the SuperAdmin removes a role from a folder, Then all users with only that role must lose access immediately.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can assign permissions to a specific role.
2. Verify users with the assigned role can access the folder.
3. Verify role changes update document access automatically.
4. Verify a user with multiple roles gets combined permissions.

Negative:
5. Verify users without the assigned role cannot access the restricted folder.
6. Verify removing a role from folder access blocks all users with only that role.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the role selection is available as a dropdown or searchable list in the permissions panel.
8. Verify current role assignments are clearly displayed.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify a user with no assigned roles cannot access any restricted folders.
10. Verify creating a new role and assigning it to a folder works immediately.

Security:
11. Verify role-based access is enforced at the API level.
12. Verify role assignment changes are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify role-based permission changes take effect within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the permissions panel with 20+ roles loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to revoke access to a document or folder from any user, role, or organization at any time.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to immediately revoke access to any document or folder from specific users, roles, or entire organizations. The SuperAdmin navigates to the permissions panel of the target folder or document and removes the access for the selected entity. Upon revocation, the affected users lose access immediately and can no longer view, download, or interact with the content. The system provides a confirmation step before executing the revocation to prevent accidental access removal. This capability is critical for responding to security incidents, employee terminations, organizational restructuring, or compliance requirements that demand immediate access removal. Without the ability to revoke access in real time, sensitive documents could remain exposed to unauthorized users indefinitely.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin revokes access for a user, role, or organization, Then the affected entities must immediately lose access.

2. If the revocation is confirmed, Then the system must remove the permission and update the access list.
   Else if the SuperAdmin cancels, Then no changes must be made.

3. If a revoked user attempts to access the content, Then the system must display an access denied message.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can revoke access for a specific role.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can revoke access for an entire organization.
3. Verify revoked users immediately lose access.
4. Verify the permissions panel updates to remove the revoked entity.

Negative:
5. Verify cancelling the revocation keeps the permissions unchanged.
6. Verify a revoked user cannot access the content via direct URL.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify a confirmation prompt is shown before revoking access.
8. Verify the revoke action is available in the permissions panel.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify revoking access from a user who is currently viewing the document handles gracefully.
10. Verify revoking organization access also revokes facility-level access within that organization.

Security:
11. Verify revocation is enforced at the API level immediately.
12. Verify revocation actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify access revocation takes effect within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the permissions panel refreshes within 2 seconds after revocation.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to mark a document as public (visible to all facilities) or private (restricted to specific org/facility).
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to classify documents as either public or private within the Document Management system. Public documents are visible and accessible to all facilities and organizations on the platform, making them suitable for system-wide policies, announcements, or shared templates. Private documents are restricted to specific organizations or facilities as configured by the SuperAdmin. The SuperAdmin can toggle between public and private status from the document settings panel at any time. This classification provides a simple high-level access control that works alongside the more granular role-based and folder-level permissions. Without this distinction, every document would require individual permission configuration, making it cumbersome to share system-wide content or restrict facility-specific documents.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a document is marked as public, Then all users across all facilities must have access based on their role permissions.
   Else if the document is marked as private, Then only users from the specified organization or facility must have access.

2. If the SuperAdmin changes a document from public to private, Then users outside the restriction must immediately lose access.

3. If the SuperAdmin changes a document from private to public, Then all users must immediately gain access.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can mark a document as public.
2. Verify a public document is visible to users from all facilities.
3. Verify the SuperAdmin can mark a document as private.
4. Verify a private document is only visible to users from the specified org/facility.

Negative:
5. Verify changing from public to private immediately restricts access.
6. Verify private documents are not visible to users outside the restriction.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the public/private toggle is available in document settings.
8. Verify a visual indicator (e.g., icon or badge) shows the document status.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify toggling public/private on a document with active shared links handles the links appropriately.
10. Verify bulk-changing multiple documents between public and private.

Security:
11. Verify private documents cannot be accessed via direct URL by unauthorized users.
12. Verify public/private status changes are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify public/private status changes take effect within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the document list filters by public/private status within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to search for documents by name, file type, date, or organization across the entire system.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to search for documents across the entire Document Management system using criteria such as document name, file type, upload date, or organization. The search functionality provides a global search bar accessible from any Document Management screen. Search results display matching documents with key metadata including name, location, file type, upload date, and organization. The SuperAdmin can click on a result to navigate directly to the document in its folder. This cross-system search is essential for quickly locating specific documents in a large repository spanning multiple organizations and facilities. Without global search, the SuperAdmin would need to manually browse through the entire folder hierarchy to find a specific document, which is impractical at scale.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the search query matches one or more documents, Then the system must display all matching results with metadata.
   Else if no documents match, Then the system must display a "No results found" message.

2. If the SuperAdmin combines multiple search criteria, Then the results must match all specified criteria.

3. If the SuperAdmin clicks a search result, Then the system must navigate to the document in its folder location.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify searching by document name returns matching results.
2. Verify searching by file type returns only documents of that type.
3. Verify searching by date range returns documents within the range.
4. Verify searching by organization returns documents from that organization.
5. Verify clicking a search result navigates to the document location.

Negative:
6. Verify searching with no matching criteria shows "No results found".
7. Verify an empty search query shows an appropriate prompt.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the search bar is visible and accessible on the Document Management screen.
9. Verify search results display document name, type, date, and organization.
10. Verify search results are paginated for large result sets.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify partial name search returns relevant results.
12. Verify search with special characters is handled correctly.

Security:
13. Verify search results respect the user permission model.
14. Verify search queries are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify search results are returned within 3 seconds for repositories with 10000+ documents.
2. Verify incremental search (type-ahead) responds within 1 second.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to filter documents by date uploaded, file type, organization, facility, or document category.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to filter the document list within a folder or across the system using multiple filter criteria including date uploaded, file type, organization, facility, and document category. The filter panel provides dropdown selectors and date pickers for each criterion, and multiple filters can be applied simultaneously for a combined result. Applied filters are visually indicated with tags or chips that can be individually removed. The filtered view updates in real time as filters are applied or removed. This filtering capability is essential for managing large document collections where the SuperAdmin needs to quickly narrow down to specific subsets based on operational or compliance needs. Without filters, the SuperAdmin would need to scroll through potentially thousands of documents to find relevant ones.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If one or more filters are applied, Then the document list must display only documents matching all applied filters.

2. If a filter is removed, Then the list must update to reflect the remaining active filters.

3. If all filters are cleared, Then the full unfiltered list must be displayed.

4. If no documents match the applied filters, Then the system must display a "No documents found" message.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify filtering by file type shows only documents of that type.
2. Verify filtering by date range shows documents within the range.
3. Verify filtering by organization shows documents from that organization.
4. Verify filtering by facility shows documents from that facility.
5. Verify combining multiple filters narrows results correctly.
6. Verify removing a filter updates the list appropriately.

Negative:
7. Verify no results message is shown when filters match no documents.
8. Verify invalid date range (end before start) is rejected.

UI Scenarios:
9. Verify filter panel is accessible and displays all filter options.
10. Verify applied filters are shown as removable tags or chips.
11. Verify the clear all filters option is available.

Edge Cases:
12. Verify applying the same filter twice does not duplicate results.
13. Verify filters persist when navigating between folders.

Security:
14. Verify filtered results respect the user permission model.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify filter application updates the list within 2 seconds.
2. Verify filtering across 10000+ documents completes within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to sort documents by name, date modified, file size, or uploaded by.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to sort the document list within a folder by various criteria including document name, date modified, file size, and the user who uploaded the document. The sort functionality is accessible via clickable column headers in the document list view, with toggle capability for ascending and descending order. A visual indicator (arrow) shows the current sort direction on the active column. The default sort order is by date modified (newest first) to show the most recently updated documents at the top. This sorting capability helps the SuperAdmin quickly organize and locate documents based on their current workflow needs, whether finding the latest uploads, the largest files consuming storage, or alphabetically scanning for a specific document. Without sorting, documents would appear in an arbitrary order, making manual scanning impractical.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin clicks a column header, Then the document list must sort by that column in ascending order.
   If they click the same header again, Then the sort must toggle to descending order.

2. If no sort is applied, Then the default sort must be by date modified (newest first).

3. If the SuperAdmin switches to a different folder, Then the sort preference should be maintained.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify sorting by name in ascending order.
2. Verify sorting by name in descending order.
3. Verify sorting by date modified (newest first and oldest first).
4. Verify sorting by file size (largest first and smallest first).
5. Verify sorting by uploaded by user name.

Negative:
6. Verify sorting an empty folder shows the empty state message.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify clickable column headers are visually indicated as sortable.
8. Verify an arrow icon shows the current sort direction.
9. Verify the active sort column is visually highlighted.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify sorting documents with identical names sorts stably by a secondary criterion.
11. Verify sorting a folder with 1000+ documents works correctly.

Security:
12. Verify sort operations do not expose documents the user does not have permission to view.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify sorting 1000+ documents completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify toggling sort direction responds within 1 second.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to navigate through the folder structure using breadcrumb navigation so that I always know my current location in the directory.
Description of User Story: This story provides breadcrumb navigation within the Document Management module so the SuperAdmin always knows their current location in the folder hierarchy. The breadcrumb trail displays the full path from the root to the current folder, with each segment being a clickable link that navigates directly to that level. As the SuperAdmin navigates deeper into subfolders, the breadcrumb trail grows to reflect the full path. Clicking any intermediate breadcrumb segment navigates directly to that folder without needing to use the back button or tree view. This navigation aid is essential in deep folder hierarchies where the SuperAdmin may be several levels deep and needs to quickly jump to a parent folder. Without breadcrumbs, users would lose context of their position in the hierarchy and waste time navigating back through multiple levels.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin is in a nested folder, Then the breadcrumb must show the complete path from root to the current folder.

2. If the SuperAdmin clicks a breadcrumb segment, Then the system must navigate to that folder and update the content area.

3. If the SuperAdmin is at the root level, Then only the root breadcrumb element must be displayed.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the breadcrumb displays the full path from root to current folder.
2. Verify clicking a parent breadcrumb navigates to that folder.
3. Verify the breadcrumb extends when navigating deeper.
4. Verify clicking the root breadcrumb navigates to the root.

Negative:
5. Verify the breadcrumb handles renamed folders correctly.

UI Scenarios:
6. Verify breadcrumb segments are visually clickable with hover effects.
7. Verify separator characters (e.g., >) are displayed between segments.
8. Verify the breadcrumb is responsive and handles long paths with truncation or wrapping.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify breadcrumb for a deeply nested folder (10+ levels) is displayed correctly.
10. Verify breadcrumb updates when a folder in the path is renamed.

Security:
11. Verify breadcrumb does not expose folder names the user does not have permission to see.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify breadcrumb navigation responds within 1 second.
2. Verify breadcrumb rendering for a 15-level deep path completes within 1 second.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to view a list of recently uploaded or accessed documents for quick reference.
Description of User Story: This story provides the SuperAdmin with a Recent Documents view that lists documents recently uploaded or accessed, sorted by most recent activity. This view serves as a quick-access dashboard, displaying document name, folder location, file type, last activity timestamp, and the user who performed the activity. The SuperAdmin can click on any document in the recent list to navigate directly to it in its folder location. The recent documents list can show the last 50 or configurable number of recent items. This feature significantly improves workflow efficiency by eliminating the need to navigate through the folder hierarchy to find documents the SuperAdmin was recently working with. Without a recent documents view, the SuperAdmin would need to remember and manually navigate to the exact folder of every document they interacted with.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If there are recent documents, Then the system must display them sorted by most recent activity.
   Else if there are no recent documents, Then the system must display an empty state message.

2. If the SuperAdmin clicks a document in the list, Then the system must navigate to the document in its folder.

3. If a document is deleted, Then it must be removed from the recent documents list.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify recently uploaded documents appear in the recent documents list.
2. Verify recently viewed documents appear in the list.
3. Verify the list is sorted by most recent activity.
4. Verify clicking a document navigates to its folder location.

Negative:
5. Verify deleted documents are removed from the recent list.
6. Verify an empty state message is shown when there are no recent documents.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify each entry shows document name, type, location, timestamp, and user.
8. Verify the recent documents view is accessible from the Document Management navigation.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify the list handles the maximum number of entries correctly.
10. Verify accessing a document whose folder has been moved updates the location correctly.

Security:
11. Verify recent documents list respects the user permission model.
12. Verify recent document access is logged.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the recent documents list loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify the list updates within 3 seconds after a new activity.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to view the complete version history of a document showing who uploaded each version and when.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to view the complete version history of any document, showing a chronological list of all versions with details about who uploaded each version and when. Each version entry displays the version number, upload timestamp, uploader name, file size, and optionally a comment about the changes. The SuperAdmin can access the version history panel from the document context menu or detail view. The current (latest) version is highlighted, and previous versions can be previewed or downloaded individually. This version tracking is critical for compliance and audit purposes in healthcare environments where document changes must be traceable. Without version history, there would be no way to determine who changed a document, when, or what the previous content was.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the document has multiple versions, Then the system must display all versions in reverse chronological order.
   Else if the document has only one version, Then the system must display that single version.

2. If the SuperAdmin clicks a previous version, Then the system must allow preview or download of that specific version.

3. If a version was uploaded by a user who is no longer active, Then the uploader name must still be displayed.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the version history panel displays all document versions.
2. Verify each version shows version number, date, uploader, and file size.
3. Verify the current version is clearly highlighted.
4. Verify a previous version can be previewed.
5. Verify a previous version can be downloaded.

Negative:
6. Verify a document with only one version shows that single version.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the version history panel is accessible from the document context menu.
8. Verify version entries are displayed in a clear, scrollable list.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify a document with 50+ versions displays all versions correctly.
10. Verify version history for a deleted and restored document is intact.

Security:
11. Verify version history access respects document permissions.
12. Verify version history access is logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify version history loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify downloading a previous version completes within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to restore a previous version of a document when the current version needs to be rolled back.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to restore a previous version of a document, making it the current active version when the latest version is found to be incorrect, corrupted, or needs to be rolled back. From the version history panel, the SuperAdmin selects a previous version and clicks the restore action, which creates a new version entry based on the selected previous version while preserving the full version history. The restore action does not delete any existing versions but rather creates a new current version that is a copy of the selected historical version. A confirmation prompt is displayed before the restore is executed. This rollback capability is critical in environments where document errors can have compliance or patient safety implications. Without version restore, the only remedy for an incorrect document would be to manually re-upload the correct file.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin confirms the restore of a previous version, Then the system must create a new current version based on the selected historical version.
   Else if the SuperAdmin cancels, Then no changes must be made.

2. If a restore is executed, Then the complete version history must be preserved with the restore noted.

3. If the restored version is of a different file format, Then the system must handle this appropriately.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify restoring a previous version makes it the current active version.
2. Verify the restored document content matches the selected historical version.
3. Verify all previous versions are preserved after restore.
4. Verify the version history shows the restore as a new version entry.

Negative:
5. Verify cancelling the restore keeps the current version.
6. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot restore versions.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the restore button is available on each version in the history panel.
8. Verify a confirmation prompt is shown before restoring.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify restoring the very first version of a document with many versions.
10. Verify restoring a version of a document that has active shared links.

Security:
11. Verify restore actions are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify version restore respects document permissions.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify version restore completes within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the document view updates within 2 seconds after restore.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to compare two versions of the same document to identify what changed between versions.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to compare two versions of the same document side by side to identify differences between them. The SuperAdmin selects two versions from the version history panel and invokes the comparison action. The system displays both versions in a split-screen view highlighting additions, deletions, and modifications between the two versions. For text-based documents (PDF, DOCX), the comparison shows textual differences with color-coded highlights. For images, the comparison may show both images side by side for visual inspection. This comparison capability is essential for regulatory compliance reviews where auditors need to understand exactly what changed between document revisions. Without version comparison, reviewers would need to manually read through both versions to identify changes, which is error-prone and time-consuming.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If two text-based versions are selected for comparison, Then the system must display a diff view with color-coded highlights.

2. If two image versions are selected, Then the system must display them side by side for visual comparison.

3. If the document format does not support comparison, Then the system must display both versions for manual review with a note.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify selecting two versions and clicking compare opens the comparison view.
2. Verify differences are highlighted with color coding.
3. Verify additions and deletions are distinguishable.
4. Verify image versions are displayed side by side.

Negative:
5. Verify selecting only one version disables the compare button.
6. Verify comparing identical versions shows a "No differences" message.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the split-screen layout displays both versions clearly.
8. Verify the comparison view has a close button to return to version history.
9. Verify color legend for additions and deletions is displayed.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify comparing versions of very large documents (100+ pages).
11. Verify comparing versions of different file formats.

Security:
12. Verify comparison access respects document permissions.
13. Verify comparison actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify document comparison loads within 5 seconds for 20-page documents.
2. Verify image comparison loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to share a document with specific organizations, facilities, or individual users directly from the system.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to share documents directly with specific organizations, facilities, or individual users from within the Document Management system. The SuperAdmin selects a document and invokes the share action, which opens a share dialog where they can search for and select recipients. Recipients can be organizations, facilities, or individual users, and the SuperAdmin can specify the access level (view only, download, or edit). Shared documents appear in the recipient notification area and are accessible from their Document Management view. The share action creates a record that can be tracked and revoked. This internal sharing mechanism is more secure than email-based sharing as it keeps documents within the platform boundary. Without internal sharing, users would need to download documents and share them through external channels, creating security risks and version control issues.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin shares a document with an organization, Then all users in that organization must gain access.

2. If the SuperAdmin specifies view-only access, Then the recipient must be able to view but not download or edit.
   Else if download access is specified, Then the recipient must be able to view and download.

3. If the share is successful, Then the recipient must receive a notification.

4. If the SuperAdmin revokes the share, Then the recipient must immediately lose access.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can share a document with an organization.
2. Verify the SuperAdmin can share a document with a specific facility.
3. Verify the SuperAdmin can share a document with an individual user.
4. Verify the recipient receives a notification.
5. Verify view-only access restricts download and edit.

Negative:
6. Verify sharing with a non-existent user shows an error.
7. Verify sharing an already-shared document to the same recipient shows appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the share dialog has a searchable recipient field.
9. Verify the access level dropdown shows available options.
10. Verify shared status is indicated on the document.

Edge Cases:
11. Verify sharing a document that is subsequently deleted revokes shared access.
12. Verify sharing a document with a user who already has folder-level access.

Security:
13. Verify share actions are logged in the audit trail.
14. Verify shared documents cannot be accessed by unintended recipients.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify the share action completes within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the recipient notification is delivered within 5 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to generate a secure shareable link for a document with an optional expiry date so that external parties can access it temporarily.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to generate a secure, shareable link for a document that can be shared with external parties who may not have system accounts. The generated link provides temporary access to the document, and the SuperAdmin can set an optional expiry date after which the link becomes inactive. The link uses a cryptographically secure token that cannot be guessed or brute-forced. When an external party clicks the link, they can view or download the document without logging in. The SuperAdmin can track link usage and revoke it at any time. This feature is essential for sharing documents with external auditors, regulatory agencies, or partner organizations who need temporary access to specific documents. Without shareable links, external sharing would require downloading and emailing documents, losing audit trail visibility and version control.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin generates a shareable link with an expiry date, Then the link must become inactive after the expiry.
   Else if no expiry date is set, Then the link must remain active until manually revoked.

2. If an external party clicks a valid link, Then they must be able to view or download the document without logging in.

3. If the link has expired or been revoked, Then the system must display an appropriate message.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can generate a shareable link for a document.
2. Verify the link can be copied to clipboard.
3. Verify an external party can access the document via the link.
4. Verify the link with an expiry date becomes inactive after expiry.
5. Verify a link without an expiry date remains active until revoked.

Negative:
6. Verify an expired link shows an expiry message.
7. Verify a revoked link shows an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
8. Verify the generate link dialog shows the link and a copy button.
9. Verify the expiry date picker is available and optional.

Edge Cases:
10. Verify generating multiple links for the same document creates unique links.
11. Verify the link works across different browsers and devices.

Security:
12. Verify the link token is cryptographically secure and cannot be guessed.
13. Verify link access is logged in the audit trail.
14. Verify the link uses HTTPS protocol.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify link generation completes within 2 seconds.
2. Verify document access via the shareable link loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to revoke a previously generated shareable link at any time.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to revoke any previously generated shareable link, immediately disabling access for anyone who has the link. The SuperAdmin can view a list of all active shared links for a document and revoke individual links or all links at once. Upon revocation, any subsequent clicks on the link display an access denied or link revoked message. The revocation takes effect immediately without any delay. This capability is critical for security incident response when a link has been shared with unintended parties or when the document content is no longer approved for external distribution. Without link revocation, shared links would remain active indefinitely, creating persistent security exposure that cannot be remediated.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin confirms link revocation, Then the link must become inactive immediately.
   Else if the SuperAdmin cancels, Then the link must remain active.

2. If a revoked link is accessed, Then the system must display a "Link has been revoked" message.

3. If the SuperAdmin revokes all links, Then every shared link for the document must be invalidated.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can revoke a single shared link.
2. Verify the revoked link immediately becomes inactive.
3. Verify accessing a revoked link shows a revocation message.
4. Verify the SuperAdmin can revoke all links for a document.

Negative:
5. Verify cancelling the revocation keeps the link active.

UI Scenarios:
6. Verify the shared links panel shows all active links with details.
7. Verify a confirmation prompt appears before revocation.

Edge Cases:
8. Verify revoking a link that is currently being accessed handles gracefully.
9. Verify revoking an already expired link does not cause errors.

Security:
10. Verify revocation is immediate and cannot be circumvented.
11. Verify revocation actions are logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify link revocation takes effect within 1 second.
2. Verify the shared links panel loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to view who a document has been shared with and when it was shared.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to view a comprehensive sharing history for any document, showing who the document has been shared with, when it was shared, and the access level granted. The sharing history panel is accessible from the document context menu and displays a chronological list of all share actions including internal shares to users, organizations, and facilities, as well as external shareable links. Each entry shows the recipient name, share date, access level, and current status (active or revoked). This tracking is essential for compliance audits where organizations must demonstrate who had access to specific documents at specific times. Without share tracking, there would be no accountability for document distribution, creating gaps in audit trails.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the document has sharing history, Then the system must display all share entries with full details.
   Else if the document has never been shared, Then the system must show a "Not shared" message.

2. If a share has been revoked, Then the entry must show "Revoked" status with the revocation date.

3. If the document has shareable links, Then the links must appear in the sharing history with their status.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify sharing history shows all internal shares with details.
2. Verify sharing history shows all external shareable links.
3. Verify revoked shares show "Revoked" status.
4. Verify entries are sorted chronologically.

Negative:
5. Verify a document with no sharing history shows an appropriate message.

UI Scenarios:
6. Verify the sharing history panel is accessible from the document context menu.
7. Verify each entry clearly displays recipient, date, access level, and status.

Edge Cases:
8. Verify sharing history for a document with 100+ share entries loads correctly.
9. Verify sharing history persists after the document is moved to a different folder.

Security:
10. Verify sharing history access respects document permissions.
11. Verify sharing history access is logged in the audit trail.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify sharing history loads within 2 seconds.
2. Verify sharing history with 100+ entries paginates and loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to upload and manage system-wide document templates (intake forms, contracts, consent forms, state-specific forms) that are made available to all facilities.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to upload and manage system-wide document templates that serve as standardized forms across all facilities. Templates can include intake forms, contracts, consent forms, state-specific regulatory forms, and other reusable documents. The SuperAdmin uploads template files to a dedicated templates area and assigns metadata such as template name, category, and description. Once uploaded, templates become available to all facilities for use in their document workflows. The SuperAdmin can manage the template library by updating, deactivating, or replacing templates. This centralized template management ensures consistency across facilities and reduces the risk of outdated or incorrect forms being used. Without template management, each facility would need to maintain its own copies of standard forms, leading to inconsistency and compliance risk.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin uploads a valid template file with metadata, Then the system must add it to the template library and make it available to facilities.

2. If the uploaded file type is unsupported, Then the system must reject it with an error.

3. If the template name already exists, Then the system must warn the SuperAdmin about the duplicate.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can upload a template with name, category, and description.
2. Verify the template appears in the template library.
3. Verify facility users can access the uploaded template.
4. Verify templates are searchable by name and category.

Negative:
5. Verify unsupported file types are rejected.
6. Verify duplicate template names show a warning.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the template upload form includes name, category, description, and file upload fields.
8. Verify the template library displays templates in a grid or list view.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify uploading a large template file (50MB) succeeds.
10. Verify uploading templates with special characters in the name.

Security:
11. Verify template uploads are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify uploaded templates undergo security scanning.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify template upload completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify the template library loads within 3 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to assign specific templates to specific facilities or service types so that each facility only sees relevant templates.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to assign specific document templates to specific facilities or service types, ensuring that each facility only sees templates relevant to their operations. The SuperAdmin selects a template from the library and assigns it to one or more facilities or service types through an assignment panel. Facilities only see templates that have been explicitly assigned to them, preventing confusion from irrelevant templates. This targeted assignment is important because different facility types have different regulatory requirements and operational needs. For example, an adult day health services facility needs different forms than an assisted living facility. Without template assignment, all facilities would see all templates, making it difficult to identify the correct forms for their specific regulatory context.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If a template is assigned to a facility, Then users of that facility must see it in their template list.
   Else if the template is not assigned, Then the facility must not see it.

2. If a template is assigned to a service type, Then all facilities of that type must have access.

3. If the assignment is removed, Then the facility must immediately lose access to the template.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can assign a template to a specific facility.
2. Verify the assigned facility can see the template.
3. Verify assigning to a service type makes it available to all facilities of that type.
4. Verify removing an assignment hides the template from the facility.

Negative:
5. Verify non-assigned facilities cannot see the template.
6. Verify assigning an inactive template shows a warning.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the assignment panel shows a list of facilities and service types.
8. Verify assigned facilities are clearly indicated.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify assigning a template to all facilities and then removing one.
10. Verify template visibility updates immediately after assignment change.

Security:
11. Verify assignment changes are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify non-SuperAdmin users cannot modify template assignments.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify template assignment changes take effect within 3 seconds.
2. Verify the assignment panel loads within 2 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to update an existing template and push the updated version to all facilities using that template.
Description of User Story: This story enables the SuperAdmin to update an existing document template by uploading a new version, which is then automatically pushed to all facilities that have been assigned the template. The SuperAdmin selects the template from the library, uploads the updated file, and the system replaces the current version while maintaining version history. All facilities using the template automatically receive the updated version on their next access. The system can optionally notify facility administrators about the template update. This centralized update mechanism ensures that all facilities always have access to the latest version of regulatory and operational forms. Without centralized template updates, the SuperAdmin would need to manually distribute updated forms to each facility individually, risking inconsistent versions across the organization.
Acceptance Criteria:
1. If the SuperAdmin uploads a valid new version, Then the system must update the template and preserve version history.

2. If the uploaded file is invalid, Then the system must reject it and keep the current version.

3. If the template is assigned to multiple facilities, Then all facilities must receive the updated version automatically.
Test Scenarios:
Positive Functional:
1. Verify the SuperAdmin can upload a new version of a template.
2. Verify the updated template is immediately available to assigned facilities.
3. Verify version history is preserved.
4. Verify a success message is shown after update.

Negative:
5. Verify uploading an unsupported file type is rejected.
6. Verify cancelling the update keeps the current version.

UI Scenarios:
7. Verify the update option is accessible from the template management view.
8. Verify upload progress is shown during the update.

Edge Cases:
9. Verify updating a template that is currently being viewed by a facility user.
10. Verify updating a template assigned to 100+ facilities.

Security:
11. Verify template updates are logged in the audit trail.
12. Verify the updated file undergoes security scanning.
Performance Scenarios:
1. Verify template update completes within 5 seconds.
2. Verify the updated template is available to facilities within 10 seconds.

User Story: As a SuperAdmin, I should be able to deactivate a template so that it is no longer available to facilities without permanently deleting it.
